title: OCAP Principles (First Nations Data Governance)
abstract: The OCAP principles (Ownership, Control, Access, and Possession) establish that First Nations communities own, control, access, and possess information about their peoples and territories. Originally articulated in 1998 and institutionalized by the First Nations Information Governance Centre, OCAP is the foundational data governance framework for First Nations communities in Canada.

title: Russian Orthodox Church - Basis of the Social Concept (Technology Principles)
abstract: Adopted by the Bishops Council in Moscow (August 13-16, 2000) with 146 bishops present. The first Orthodox Church to formulate an official position on social, economic, and cultural issues. While not AI-specific, it establishes foundational principles that technologies must serve humanity, not subjugate it, and that it is unacceptable to create systems that infringe on the freedom and dignity of the personality. Covers church-state relations, human rights, mass media, healthcare, and environmental concerns. Serves as the doctrinal foundation for subsequent ROC technology positions.

title: FHI Existential Risk and AI Safety
abstract: A multidisciplinary research program at the University of Oxford founded by Nick Bostrom in 2005 focused on existential risks from advanced AI, including alignment, AI governance, and global catastrophic risk. FHI produced foundational work on AI safety, effective altruism, and longtermism that shaped government biosecurity policy and a generation of AI safety researchers. The Institute closed on April 16, 2024.

title: UK EPSRC Principles of Robotics
abstract: Five principles for responsible robotics covering safety, transparency, human responsibility, legal personhood, and prevention of harm.

title: Local Contexts TK and BC Labels
abstract: Local Contexts provides Traditional Knowledge (TK) Labels and Biocultural (BC) Labels — community-controlled metadata tools that enable Indigenous communities to establish cultural authority and governance over Indigenous data, heritage collections, and cultural and intellectual property. Founded in 2010 by Jane Anderson and Kim Christen, and incorporated as a non-profit under Navajo Nation law in 2022.

title: Leadership Conference Civil Rights for Big Data
abstract: Five civil rights principles for big data systems addressing fairness, accountability, accuracy, transparency, and access.

title: Accenture Universal Data Ethics Principles
abstract: Accenture's universal principles for ethical use of data and AI, covering accountability, transparency, fairness, trust, and societal benefit.

title: CHAI Berkeley Principles for Beneficial AI
abstract: A research mission and set of principles by Stuart Russell and collaborators aiming to reorient AI development toward provably beneficial systems. The approach rests on three core principles: AI systems should achieve what humans want; systems should not assume they know what humans want; and systems should infer human preferences from observed behavior, making AI systems modest and cautious.

title: FAT/ML Principles for Accountable Algorithms
abstract: Five principles for accountable algorithms: responsibility, explainability, accuracy, auditability, and fairness.

title: The Perpetual Line-Up: Unregulated Police Face Recognition
organization: Georgetown Center on Privacy and Technology
abstract: A year-long investigation finding that more than half of American adults are enrolled in a face recognition network searchable by law enforcement, with little to no regulation governing its use. The report documents inconsistent policies across agencies and recommends comprehensive federal and state regulation of police use of facial recognition technology.

title: Leverhulme CFI AI Research Program
organization: Leverhulme Centre for Future of Intelligence
abstract: An interdisciplinary research initiative based at the University of Cambridge (with spokes at Imperial College London and UC Berkeley) exploring the nature, impacts, and ethical implications of AI. The Centre's programme brings together machine learning, philosophy, history, and policy research with the guiding conviction that AI should be used for the common good and must respect values such as fairness, privacy, and autonomy.

title: Satya Nadella 10 AI Rules
abstract: Early AI ethics rules proposed by Microsoft's CEO including: AI must assist humanity, be transparent, maximize efficiency without destroying dignity, protect privacy, be algorithmically accountable, guard against bias, and have safeguards.

title: Partnership on AI Tenets
abstract: Core tenets guiding the Partnership on AI's work, covering safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, and multi-stakeholder collaboration.

title: ACM Statement on Algorithmic Accountability
abstract: Seven principles for algorithmic transparency and accountability in automated decision-making systems, co-signed by major computing professional societies.

title: ACM USACM Algorithmic Transparency 2017
abstract: Seven-principle statement designed to address harmful algorithmic bias: Awareness, Accountability, Explanation, Data Provenance, Auditability, Validation and Testing, and Explainability. Later expanded into a joint USACM-EUACM statement in May 2017. Superseded by the 2022 ACM Principles for Responsible Algorithmic Systems.

title: USACM Algorithmic Transparency Statement
abstract: Seven principles for algorithmic systems issued by the ACM U.S. Public Policy Council, addressing awareness of potential biases, access to redress, institutional accountability, explanation of algorithmic decisions, data provenance, auditability, and ongoing validation and testing. Among the earliest professional computing society statements on algorithmic governance.

title: USACM Statement on AI
abstract: ACM US Technology Policy Committee statement on seven principles for accountable algorithmic systems.

title: China New Gen AI Development Plan 2017
abstract: Established China's three-step roadmap to become a global AI innovation center by 2030, setting targets for 2020 (match global standards), 2025 (major breakthroughs in AI theory), and 2030 (lead globally with AI output exceeding 1 trillion yuan).

title: China New Generation AI Development Plan
abstract: China's foundational national AI strategy setting three-stage development goals through 2030, including targets for AI technology advancement, industry scale, and establishing China as the world's primary AI innovation center. The plan outlines priorities across R&D, industrialization, talent, standards, ethics, and security.

title: Data Ethics Denmark Principles
abstract: Data ethics principles covering transparency, consent, algorithmic accountability, and protection of individual rights in the digital age.

title: DeepMind Ethics & Society Principles
abstract: DeepMind's Ethics & Society research unit, launched in October 2017, operates from the premise that all AI applications should remain under meaningful human control and be used for socially beneficial purposes. The unit's five principles — social benefit, rigorous evidence-based research, transparency, diverse and interdisciplinary approach, and collaborative inclusion — guide research across privacy, fairness, governance, AI risk, and moral values.

title: DeepMind Safety and Ethics
abstract: DeepMind's internal ethics and safety research unit principles focusing on real-world harms, fairness, governance, and human-AI interaction.

title: European Parliament Recommendations on Robotics
abstract: European Parliament resolution calling for civil law rules on robotics and AI, including ethical principles, liability, and a charter of robotics rights.

title: Fo Guang Shan Humanistic Buddhism and AI
abstract: Chief Abbess of Oceania Venerable Manko and Venerable Juefang addressed the relationship between AI and Humanistic Buddhism. Venerable Manko framed AI through Buddhist principles of 'faith, understanding, practice and realization,' proposing that mastering 'precepts, meditative concentration and wisdom' enables responsible technology use. The framework positions AI as a tool to facilitate compassionate action ('3 Good Deeds and 4 Givings') while cultivating a 'pure, compassionate and selfless heart.' Master Hsing Yun's theory of 'the nature of insentient beings' — that insentient beings can possess Buddha-nature — provides a philosophical foundation for Buddhist engagement with AI. Fo Guang Shan's stance: humanistic Buddhism offers moral and spiritual grounding for technological advancement rather than opposing AI development.

title: Science and Technology Committee AI Report
abstract: AI governance framework and principles for responsible AI developed by The Future Society at Harvard Kennedy School.

title: Finland Age of Artificial Intelligence 2017
abstract: Outlined eight priority actions across business competitiveness, data use, skills, public services and governance to accelerate AI adoption in Finland while emphasizing openness, ethics and social wellbeing, positioning Finland as one of the world's top AI-adopting countries.

title: Finland's Age of Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Finland's inaugural national AI strategy outlines eight priority action areas to establish Finland as a global leader in AI applications, covering enterprise competitiveness, data governance via 'MyData', public services, and ethical principles. It introduced the concept of 'areas of free intelligence' for AI experimentation and laid the groundwork for the broader four-year Artificial Intelligence Programme (2017-2019).

title: German Ethics Commission Automated Driving
abstract: 20 ethical rules for automated and connected driving systems, covering liability, data protection, safety trade-offs, and the primacy of human life.

title: ICO Big Data AI ML Data Protection
abstract: ICO guidance on data protection compliance for big data, AI, and machine learning systems, covering fairness, lawfulness, transparency, and data minimization.

title: Ethically Aligned Design (v2)
abstract: A comprehensive framework for ethics of autonomous and intelligent systems developed through a crowd-sourced process involving over a thousand experts. Covers general principles, human rights, wellbeing, accountability, and transparency across diverse AI application domains.

title: IEEE Ethically Aligned Design v2
abstract: Comprehensive 290-page document providing ethically aligned design principles and recommendations for autonomous and intelligent systems.

title: IEEE SA EAD v2 Pre-release 2017
abstract: Second pre-release version (v2) of the Ethically Aligned Design document for public comment, preceding the 2019 First Edition. Contains high-level ethical principles, key issues, and recommendations for autonomous and intelligent systems design, covering human rights, well-being, data agency, effectiveness, transparency, and accountability.

title: Internet Society AI Policy Paper
abstract: Policy recommendations for AI and machine learning covering transparency, multi-stakeholder governance, human rights, and cybersecurity considerations.

title: Japanese Advisory Board on AI Report
abstract: Report by Japan's Cabinet Office advisory board on the social impact of AI and recommended ethical principles for AI development.

title: ITI AI Policy Principles
abstract: Principles for responsible AI development from the major US tech industry trade council, covering transparency, fairness, accountability, and privacy.

title: Asilomar AI Principles
abstract: 23 principles for beneficial AI agreed upon at the Asilomar conference, covering research issues, ethics and values, and longer-term issues.

title: Dutch AI Manifesto
abstract: Dutch AI manifesto calling for responsible AI development emphasizing transparency, fairness, privacy, and democratic governance.

title: Future of Life Institute Asilomar Principles
abstract: 23 AI safety and ethics principles developed at the Asilomar conference, covering research issues, ethics and values, and longer-term safety issues.

title: Intel AI Public Policy White Paper
abstract: Intel's position paper on AI policy principles covering safety, transparency, fairness, and the need for ethical guidelines.

title: Japan AI R&D Principles (MIC)
abstract: Nine principles for AI research and development in Japan covering transparency, user assistance, controllability, safety, security, privacy, ethics, human dignity, and innovation. Developed by MIC's study group on AI networking.

title: JSAI Ethical Guidelines
abstract: Ethical guidelines for AI researchers and developers from Japan's leading AI professional society, covering responsibility, safety, privacy, fairness, transparency, and accountability in AI research and applications.

title: Japanese Society for AI Ethical Guidelines
abstract: Nine ethical guidelines for AI researchers, emphasizing contribution to humanity, education, public disclosure, and accountability.

title: MIT-Harvard Ethics & Governance of AI Fund
organization: MIT Media Lab / Berkman Klein Center
abstract: A $27 million interdisciplinary research initiative co-anchored by MIT Media Lab and Harvard Berkman Klein Center aimed at ensuring social scientists, ethicists, philosophers, and policymakers have meaningful input into AI development. The fund's principles emphasize public-interest AI research, interdisciplinary collaboration, and bridging academia, civil society, industry, and government.

title: Oxford FHI AI Governance Research Agenda
abstract: Oxford FHI technical report outlining a research agenda for AI governance standards, covering safety, alignment, and oversight.

title: Royal Society: Machine Learning — Power and Promise
abstract: Landmark Royal Society report on machine learning technology, its promise, and societal implications. Chaired by Professor Peter Donnelly, the report called for 'careful stewardship' over the next 10 years to ensure that machine learning benefits all of UK society. Included the UK's first in-depth public survey on machine learning (with Ipsos MORI). Key recommendations: build digital skills at all levels including funded Masters and PhD programs; support machine learning research and integrate it into UK industrial strategy; establish fair data governance frameworks for secure sharing of sensitive data; foster public engagement and informed debate. Found that while only 9% of people had heard the term 'machine learning', the vast majority had used its applications.

title: SIIA Ethical Principles for AI
abstract: Industry principles for ethical AI and data analytics covering accountability, transparency, fairness, privacy, and security.

title: UAE National AI Strategy 2031
abstract: Launched in October 2017 as the first national AI strategy in the region, this framework positions the UAE as a global AI leader by 2031, targeting nine priority sectors (transport, healthcare, space, renewable energy, water, technology, education, environment, traffic) and aiming to generate AED 335 billion in economic value. The strategy establishes governance structures, talent development programs, data infrastructure, and an enabling regulatory environment as part of the UAE Centennial 2071 vision.

title: Dutch Data Ethics Decision Aid
abstract: Practical ethical framework tool for municipal governments and public sector to address ethical questions in data-driven projects.

title: UNI Global Union Top 10 Principles for Ethical AI
abstract: Ten principles for ethical AI from a labor perspective, emphasizing worker rights, safety, transparency, and democratic governance of AI systems.

title: COMEST Report on Robot Ethics
abstract: UNESCO ethics commission report on the ethical challenges of robotics, addressing safety, privacy, autonomy, and the human-robot relationship.

title: UNI Top 10 Principles for Ethical AI
abstract: UNI Global Union's foundational AI ethics document providing unions, shop stewards and workers with concrete demands regarding the transparency and application of AI as artificial intelligence, robotics, data and machine learning enter workplaces worldwide. The 10 principles operationalize the key demand that AI must put people and planet first. Key principles include: human-in-control design; worker transparency (workers must know when interacting with AI); protection against surveillance; prohibition of unfair algorithmic bias and discrimination; fair transition support through social dialogue; and mechanisms for workers to participate in AI governance. This was among the first formal trade union AI principles documents globally.

title: Article 19: Privacy & AI 2018
organization_subtype: Free Expression And Privacy Rights NGO Coalition
abstract: Joint scoping paper by ARTICLE 19 and Privacy International examining how AI practices of collecting and sharing data threaten privacy and freedom of expression. Focuses on machine learning and narrow AI applications. Recommends governments review legal frameworks with respect to these rights, conduct empirical research, and engage broad stakeholders in policy development.

title: Article 19 / Privacy Int'l AI and Rights
abstract: A joint report examining how machine learning systems impact the rights to privacy and freedom of expression, analyzing AI-driven surveillance, profiling, and automated decision-making. Recommends that all AI tools be subject to laws and ethical codes meeting international human rights standards, and calls on governments to review legal frameworks for adequacy.

title: ACM Code of Ethics
abstract: Updated code of ethics for computing professionals, explicitly addressing AI and algorithmic systems, privacy, algorithmic bias, and professional responsibility.

title: ADP Ethics in AI Statement
abstract: ADP's ethics statement for AI use in HR and workforce management contexts, covering transparency, fairness, privacy, security, and accountability in algorithmic decision-making affecting workers.

title: AI4People Ethical Framework
abstract: Five principles for AI in society (beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, justice, explicability) with practical recommendations, developed by the AI4People stakeholder forum.

title: AI Now 2018 Report
abstract: The 2018 annual report from the AI Now Institute examining social implications of AI deployment including bias and discrimination, labor impacts, and surveillance. Provides detailed recommendations across healthcare, criminal justice, and public infrastructure contexts.

title: AI Now Algorithmic Impact Assessments 2018
abstract: A practical framework for assessing and overseeing algorithmic decision-making in public agencies, analogous to environmental impact assessments. Authored by Dillon Reisman, Jason Schultz, Kate Crawford, and Meredith Whittaker; provides guidance for affected communities and stakeholders to evaluate AI use and determine where and if such use is acceptable.

title: Algorithmic Justice League Founding Principles
abstract: The AJL, founded by Joy Buolamwini at MIT, advocates for equitable AI through research, artwork, and policy advocacy. Core principles include 'preemptive accountability' (no system should launch without proving it will not harm), universal voice and choice in AI interactions, and a focus on ending harmful AI practices through evidence-based advocacy rather than naming and shaming.

title: Algorithmic Justice League Framework
abstract: Framework from Joy Buolamwini's Algorithmic Justice League combining art and research to illuminate AI harms and biases. Built around three principles: Equitable AI (agency and affirmative consent), Accountable AI (transparency on system design and purpose), and Justice-Centered Approach (preventing AI from automating patterns of injustice). Bridges principles to practice.

title: AMA Augmented Intelligence in Health Care Policy
abstract: AMA policy framework for the evolution of artificial intelligence in health care, using the term 'augmented intelligence' to emphasize human enhancement over replacement. Establishes principles for ethical, equitable, and responsible AI development and deployment, with emphasis on physician and patient transparency.

title: AMA Policy Recommendations on Augmented Intelligence
abstract: AMA policy recommendations for augmented intelligence in medicine, addressing transparency, validation, bias, and patient safety.

title: Accenture UK Responsible AI Framework
abstract: Comprehensive ethical framework for responsible AI and robotics from Accenture UK, covering fairness, transparency, accountability, and well-being.

title: Human Rights in the Age of AI (Access Now)
abstract: A comprehensive scoping study examining how AI-powered technologies implicate nearly every internationally recognized human right, from privacy and free expression to health and education. The report provides recommendations across four categories: data protection, government use safeguards, private sector safeguards, and further research investment.

title: Italian AI White Paper
abstract: Italian government white paper on AI for public administration, including ethical principles and recommendations for responsible AI deployment.

title: Toronto Declaration on ML Rights
abstract: A human-rights-centered declaration calling on governments and companies to protect equality and non-discrimination rights in the context of machine learning. Focuses on algorithmic bias and discrimination risks in policing, welfare systems, healthcare, and online platforms, asserting that human rights law applies to ML system design and deployment.

title: Toronto Declaration
abstract: Declaration applying international human rights law to machine learning systems, focused on preventing discrimination and protecting equality rights.

title: BSA AI Policy Principles (2018)
abstract: BSA's foundational 2018 AI policy framework identified five pillars for responsible AI innovation: fairness, robustness and safety, accountability, transparency, and explainability. This document established BSA's AI governance engagement and was updated and expanded in the 2021 bias framework.

title: Baidu AI Ethics Principles
abstract: Four principles guiding Baidu's AI development: safety, innovation, responsibility, and fairness.

title: CEPEJ Ethical Charter AI in Judicial Systems
abstract: The first European text — and one of the first globally — setting out five ethical principles for AI in judicial systems: respect for fundamental rights, non-discrimination, quality and security, transparency and impartiality, and 'under user control'. Provides a framework for legislators, policymakers, and justice professionals.

title: CIGI G20 Framework for AI in Workplace
abstract: Policy framework from CIGI proposing principles for G20 nations to govern AI in workplace contexts, covering worker rights, transparency, fairness, accountability, and the social implications of automation.

title: CoE Convention 108+ (Modernised)
abstract: Modernisation of the 1981 Convention 108, updating data protection rules to address AI and algorithmic decision-making. Introduces new data subject rights regarding automated processing (including AI), requires notification of security breaches, strengthens proportionality requirements, and proclaims protection of informational autonomy and human dignity.

title: Data & Society Algorithmic Accountability Primer
abstract: Policy primer prepared for the Progressive Congressional Caucus Tech Algorithm Briefing, examining fairness and bias, opacity and transparency, and lack of auditing standards in algorithmic decision-making. Authors Joan Donovan, Robyn Caplan, Jeanna Matthews, and Lauren Hanson call for enforceable standards, independent auditing, and regulatory oversight.

title: Governing Artificial Intelligence: Upholding Human Rights
abstract: A Data & Society report by Mark Latonero assessing how human rights frameworks can guide AI governance. The report offers recommendations for technology companies, governments, intergovernmental organizations, and civil society on incorporating human rights principles into AI development and deployment, reframing AI controversies through a human rights lens.

title: Deutsche Telekom AI Guidelines
abstract: Internal guidelines for AI development at Deutsche Telekom emphasizing human dignity, autonomy, fairness, and responsibility.

title: Norwegian Data Protection Authority AI Report
abstract: Report examining privacy challenges and recommendations for AI systems from Norway's data protection authority.

title: EC Communication: AI for Europe 2018
abstract: The European Commission's first AI strategy, proposing a three-pronged approach: boosting EU technological capacity, preparing for socio-economic changes, and establishing an ethical and legal framework. Launched the European AI Alliance and the High-Level Expert Group on AI.

title: EU Code of Practice on Disinformation
abstract: Self-regulatory code for online platforms and advertisers addressing disinformation, including algorithmic transparency and political advertising disclosure.

title: EFF Santa Clara Principles 2018
abstract: A set of principles for transparency and accountability in social media content moderation, calling for platforms to publicly disclose statistics on removed posts, notify users of removals with clear reasons, and provide meaningful human-reviewed appeals processes. Developed by a coalition led by EFF, CDT, ACLU, and OTI.

title: EGE Statement on AI, Robotics & Autonomous Systems
abstract: The EGE called on the European Commission to launch a process to develop an internationally recognised ethical and legal framework for AI, robotics, and autonomous systems. The statement outlines foundational ethical principles including human dignity, autonomy, responsibility, safety, and accountability, and identifies democratic prerequisites for AI governance.

title: EGE Statement on AI, Robotics 2018
abstract: The independent advisory body to the European Commission President called for an internationally recognised ethical and legal framework for AI, robotics, and autonomous systems, proposing ethical principles based on EU Treaty values and the Charter of Fundamental Rights, including human dignity, autonomy, responsibility, justice, and safety.

title: EGE Opinion 30: Future of Work, Future of Society
organization: European Group on Ethics in Science and New Technologies
abstract: EGE Opinion 30 examines transformations in work and society driven by globalization, demographic shifts, and technological advancement — including the impact of artificial intelligence on jobs, the gig economy, and the use of smart tools and data to recruit and track workers. The opinion warns that while digital technologies create value, they also contribute to wealth concentration and growing inequality, and advocates for reimagining the social contract to protect human dignity, solidarity, and justice.

title: EGE Statement: AI, Robotics and Autonomous Systems
organization: European Group on Ethics in Science and New Technologies
abstract: The EGE calls for the launch of a process to develop a common, internationally recognised ethical and legal framework for the design, production, use, and governance of artificial intelligence, robotics, and autonomous systems, proposing a set of fundamental ethical principles based on the values laid down in the EU Treaties and the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. The statement addresses safety, human moral responsibility, regulation, data privacy, and the explainability and transparency of AI.

title: France AI for Humanity Strategy 2018
abstract: Based on Cédric Villani's commissioned report calling for €1.5 billion investment by 2022, interdisciplinary AI institutes, shared data ecosystems in health, environment, mobility and defense, and an ethical framework for AI development.

title: Charlevoix Common Vision for AI
abstract: G7 leaders' vision statement for AI emphasizing human-centred values, inclusive economic growth, and responsible development and deployment.

title: Mauritius AI Strategy 2018
abstract: Mauritius's inaugural national Artificial Intelligence Strategy, produced by a government Working Group under the chairmanship of the Secretary to the Cabinet with the Economic Development Board as secretariat. The strategy targets six priority sectors—agriculture, health, FinTech, transport, manufacturing, and ocean economy—and provides sector-specific SWOT analysis to outline AI opportunities, risks, and readiness.

title: Germany Federal AI Strategy 2018
abstract: Committed €3 billion in AI investment through 2025, focusing on research, transfer, public dialogue, impact assessment, skills, and data availability to safeguard Germany's position as a research center and build industry competitiveness.

title: Google AI Principles
abstract: Seven objectives Google commits to in AI development, including social benefit, safety, accountability, and privacy preservation, plus four applications they will not pursue.

title: Harmonious Artificial Intelligence Principles
abstract: Principles for harmonious AI development aimed at aligning diverse global stakeholder perspectives.

title: IBE AI Framework for Business
abstract: Interactive framework from the UK Institute of Business Ethics covering fundamental values and principles for ethical AI use in business contexts, addressing accountability, transparency, fairness, and stakeholder rights.

title: Institute for Business Ethics AI Guidelines
abstract: Guidance for business leaders on ethical considerations for AI deployment, covering governance, transparency, and human oversight.

title: IBM Everyday Ethics for AI
abstract: Practical guidance document for AI practitioners on applying ethical principles in everyday design and development work.

title: IBM Principles for Trust and Transparency
abstract: Three principles covering the purpose, transparency, and skills dimensions of trustworthy AI, plus commitment to explainability and bias auditing.

title: ICDPPC Declaration on AI Ethics
abstract: Declaration by data protection and privacy commissioners worldwide addressing ethical challenges of AI, covering fairness, transparency, accountability, and data protection rights.

title: IG Metall: Employee Participation Demands on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: IG Metall-Pressemitteilung (18.9.2018) fordert frühzeitige Beschäftigtenbeteiligung bei KI. 'Computer sollen nicht über Menschen entscheiden, nur Entscheidungen vorbereiten.' Fordert Betriebsrats-Mitbestimmung, Betriebsatlas zur KI-Kartierung und Qualifizierung.

title: AI4People Ethical Framework
abstract: A multidisciplinary ethical framework presenting five core principles for AI — beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, justice, and explicability — with 20 concrete recommendations organized under assess, develop, incentivise, and support. Published in Minds and Machines and highly influential in EU AI ethics policy including the European Commission's Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI.

title: India NITI Aayog National AI Strategy
abstract: Proposed a roadmap for leveraging AI to advance inclusive economic growth in five priority sectors (healthcare, agriculture, education, smart cities, and mobility), recommending Centres of Research Excellence (COREs), International Centres for Transformational AI (ICTAIs), and a national data ecosystem.

title: India National Strategy for AI (#AIforAll)
abstract: India's first national AI strategy establishes a government-led framework to position the country as a global leader in artificial intelligence for inclusive growth, identifying five priority sectors (healthcare, agriculture, education, smart cities, transportation) and recommending institutional structures including Centres of Research Excellence and International Centres for Transformational AI. The strategy addresses critical barriers of data availability, workforce skills, research infrastructure, and ethical AI governance under the #AIforAll philosophy.

title: Bologna Charter of Digital Labour Rights
organization: Riders Union Bologna / Municipality of Bologna
abstract: The first agreement of its kind between a gig worker union, traditional trade unions, a municipality, and platform companies. Signed in Bologna on May 31, 2018, following negotiations between Riders Union Bologna, Italian trade unions CGIL/CISL/UIL, the Municipality of Bologna, and platforms Sgnam and Mymenu. The Charter covers the right to be informed, the right to a fair wage, health and safety, protection of personal data, and the right to disconnect. It includes a right for trade unions to be consulted on the design of managerial algorithms and prohibits rating mechanisms based on platform worker performance. The provisions apply only to the territory of Bologna.

title: Singapore FEAT Principles
abstract: FEAT principles for AI and data analytics in Singapore's financial sector, covering fairness, ethics, accountability, and transparency.

title: Maiam nayri Wingara Data Sovereignty Communique
abstract: The Maiam nayri Wingara Indigenous Data Sovereignty Principles communique asserts the right of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples to govern the creation, collection, ownership, and application of their data, derived from UNDRIP rights. The collective was established in early 2017 to develop data sovereignty principles and identify strategic data assets for Indigenous communities.

title: French Strategy for AI
abstract: France's national AI strategy report (Villani Report) with recommendations for ethical and responsible AI, including data governance, explainability, and inclusion.

title: Mexico National AI Strategy 2018
abstract: Mexico's national AI strategy covering six thematic areas — governance, government and public services, research and development, capacity and education, data infrastructure, and ethics and regulation — making Mexico one of the first ten countries globally and first in Latin America with a national AI strategy.

title: Microsoft Responsible AI Principles
abstract: Six principles for responsible AI: fairness, reliability and safety, privacy and security, inclusiveness, transparency, and accountability.

title: India NITI Aayog National AI Strategy
abstract: India's national AI strategy emphasizing responsible AI for social benefit, focusing on healthcare, agriculture, education, and infrastructure.

title: NITI Aayog National AI Strategy
abstract: India's first national AI strategy establishes the #AIforAll brand reflecting priorities of inclusion, affordability, and replicability for other developing nations. The 115-page discussion paper identifies five focus sectors, proposes an AI oversight body to be hosted by NITI Aayog, and addresses four key barriers: lack of expertise, absence of enabling data ecosystems, high resource costs, and absence of formal privacy regulations. The document recommends a coordinated national approach to AI research, adoption, and international collaboration.

title: Nuffield Council: AI in Healthcare and Research
abstract: This bioethics briefing note examines current and potential applications of AI in healthcare and research, addressing ethical questions including responsibility for AI decisions, human contact in care, privacy and data use, algorithmic bias, and cybersecurity risks. It is the third in a series of bioethics briefing notes from the Nuffield Council on Bioethics.

title: OHCHR SR Report: AI and Freedom of Expression
abstract: Report by Special Rapporteur David Kaye examining AI's impact on the information environment and proposing a human rights framework for states and private actors using AI technologies. Recommends human rights impact assessments prior to AI deployment, particular attention to impacts on minorities and activists, and independent external audits.

title: OpenAI Charter
abstract: OpenAI's charter outlining their mission to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity, commitments to safety, cooperation, and long-term benefit.

title: Privacy International Article 19 AI Privacy
abstract: Joint report applying human rights law to AI, focusing on privacy, freedom of expression, and the right to non-discrimination.

title: Public Voice Universal Guidelines for AI
abstract: 12 universal guidelines for AI endorsed by over 200 organizations, addressing accountability, justice, transparency, and human oversight.

title: Universal Guidelines for AI (Public Voice)
abstract: Twelve universal guidelines for AI systems emphasizing the right to transparency, the right to human determination, the identification requirement, fairness, assessment and accountability, accuracy, data minimization, security, and the right to access.

title: SAP Guiding Principles for AI
abstract: Six guiding principles for AI at SAP: designed for humans, reliability and safety, privacy and security, fairness and inclusion, transparency and explainability, and accountability.

title: Santa Clara Principles (Content Moderation)
abstract: Three foundational principles for platform content moderation calling for transparency (disclosure of takedown data), notice (timely explanations to users), and appeals (meaningful processes for contesting removals). Developed by a civil society and academic coalition and subsequently endorsed by Apple, Meta, Google, Reddit, Twitter, and GitHub, among others. Updated as version 2.0 in December 2021.

title: Sony Group AI Ethics Guidelines
abstract: Sony's AI ethics guidelines covering beneficial AI development, safety, privacy, fairness, transparency, accountability, and human dignity in Sony products and research.

title: Sony Group AI Ethics Guidelines
abstract: Sony Group's AI Ethics Guidelines, established in 2018 and formalized with an AI Ethics Committee in 2019 and an AI Ethics Office in 2021, emphasize fairness and transparency in AI development. Since July 2021, mandatory AI ethics assessments are integrated into quality management for all Sony electronics products.

title: Sweden National AI Strategy 2018
abstract: Presented Sweden's strategy to become a leader in harnessing AI for welfare and competitiveness, covering education and training, innovation and use, and framework and infrastructure, with Vinnova funding AI projects of SEK 675 million in 2020.

title: Sweden: National Approach to AI
abstract: Sweden's national AI strategy sets a vision for Sweden to become the world leader in harnessing AI for welfare and competitiveness, structured around four pillars: regulatory framework and infrastructure, education and training, research, and innovation and use. The document addresses risk management including cybersecurity and ethical concerns through transparent algorithmic principles.

title: Telia Guiding Principles on Trusted AI Ethics
abstract: Telia's guiding principles for trusted AI ethics covering accountability, fairness, transparency, data protection, safety, and security in the development and deployment of AI systems.

title: Principles of Maori Data Sovereignty
abstract: Te Mana Raraunga's Principles of Māori Data Sovereignty articulate that data generated by or about Māori should be subject to Māori governance, aligned with Treaty of Waitangi rights and UNDRIP. The network, founded in 2015 and chartered in 2016, asserts data as a living taonga (treasure) requiring Indigenous stewardship.

title: Tencent ARCC AI Framework
abstract: Tencent's ARCC framework for ethical AI covering Availability (making AI available and beneficial), Reliability (ensuring AI safety and reliability), Comprehensibility (making AI transparent and explainable), and Controllability (ensuring human control over AI).

title: Taiwan AI Action Plan 2018-2021
abstract: Taiwan's first national four-year AI action plan (NT$40 billion funding), guiding Taiwan's AI development under principles of deregulation, open access, and technology investment with goals of training 33,000+ AI professionals, leveraging semiconductor leadership, and attracting international tech company AI R&D centers. Superseded by AI Taiwan Action Plan 2.0.

title: Telefonica AI Principles
abstract: Five principles for responsible AI covering fairness, transparency, human oversight, privacy, and benefiting society.

title: Telefónica AI Principles
abstract: Seven AI principles from Telefónica covering transparency, human-centred design, privacy and security, fairness, continuous learning, environmental responsibility, and education for responsible AI use.

title: Tencent ARCC AI Principles
abstract: Tencent's ARCC principles: Available (accessible to all), Reliable (safe and stable), Comprehensible (transparent), and Controllable (human oversight).

title: Tieto AI Ethics Guidelines
abstract: Tieto's ethical AI guidelines covering human-centered design, transparency, accountability, and privacy.

title: UK Government Data Ethics Framework
abstract: Seven principles for ethical data use by public sector organizations: start with clear user need, use data and tools that have a minimum footprint, work within the law, be transparent and accountable.

title: Montreal Declaration for Responsible AI
abstract: Declaration containing 10 ethical principles for responsible AI development: Well-being, Autonomy, Privacy, Solidarity, Democratic Participation, Equity, Diversity & Inclusion, Caution, Responsibility, and Sustainable Development. Developed after one year of consultations with 100 ethicists and technology professionals; launched December 4, 2018.

title: UK House of Lords AI Report
abstract: Parliamentary committee report setting out five principles for ethical AI development and deployment in the UK.

title: Help Wanted: Hiring Algorithms, Equity, and Bias
abstract: An Upturn analysis of predictive hiring tools examining how algorithmic systems affect equity throughout the hiring process. Documents popular hiring tools used by employers and their potential for bias and discrimination, with recommendations for further scrutiny and legal reform.

title: Public Scrutiny of Automated Decisions
abstract: A report by Aaron Rieke, David Robinson, and Miranda Bogen (published with Omidyar Network) examining how the public can effectively scrutinize automated decision-making systems. Finds that automated decisions combine human judgment, software, and statistical models, and that effective oversight does not require sophisticated technical expertise; highlights promising emerging accountability methods and their limited real-world adoption.

title: Ver.di Position on Artificial Intelligence and Good Work
abstract: Ver.dis erste Positionierung zu KI (November 2018), weiterentwickelt auf dem Digitalisierungskongress 2019. Fordert gesellschaftliche Nützlichkeitsvision, Gute Arbeit by Design, frühzeitige Beschäftigtenbeteiligung. Warnt vor Chatbot-Ersatz in Callcentern und unkontrolliertem KI-Einsatz.

title: W20 AI Gender Inclusion Questions
abstract: G20 women's engagement group statement raising open questions on gender inclusion and bias in AI systems.

title: ABA Resolution 112 — AI Ethics in Law
abstract: ABA urges courts and lawyers to address emerging ethical and legal issues of AI in legal practice, including bias, explainability, transparency, ethical usage, and oversight of AI vendors. Proposed by the ABA Section of Science and Technology Law.

title: Safety First for Automated Driving
abstract: Joint white paper from major automotive companies establishing 12 safety principles for automated driving systems.

title: American College Radiology AI Ethics
abstract: Joint radiology society statement on ethical principles for AI in radiology, addressing bias, transparency, accountability, and patient safety.

title: China Joint Pledge AI Industry Self-Discipline
abstract: Industry self-discipline pledge by China's AI industry alliance covering responsible AI development, human oversight, safety, transparency, privacy protection, and ethical business practices for Chinese AI companies.

title: AI Now 2019 Report
abstract: Annual report by the AI Now Institute spotlighting grassroots resistance to harmful AI, offering 12 recommendations for policymakers, advocates, and researchers to address AI-driven inequality. Focus areas include algorithmic management of workers, benefits and social services, and surveillance of underrepresented communities.

title: Academy of Medical Royal Colleges AI in Healthcare
abstract: Report from UK's Academy of Medical Royal Colleges on implementing AI in healthcare safely and ethically, covering governance, training, and patient safety.

title: AlgorithmWatch AI Ethics Inventory 2019
abstract: Searchable database cataloguing 160+ AI ethics guidelines, principles, and frameworks from governmental, supra-national, private sector, and civil society actors worldwide. Key finding: all guidelines share principles on transparency, non-discrimination, accountability, and safety, but fewer than 10 of 160+ documents have practical enforcement mechanisms. Searchable version at inventory.algorithmwatch.org.

title: AlgorithmWatch Automating Society 2019
abstract: First edition of a landmark study investigating the deployment of automated decision-making systems across 12 European countries and at the EU level, documenting applications in welfare, justice, finance, and public administration. Recommends public registers for ADM systems used in the public sector and legally-binding data access frameworks for public interest research.

title: Argentina National AI Plan 2019
abstract: Argentina's National AI Plan, approved December 9, 2019 on the last day of the Macri administration, framed within the Argentina Digital Agenda 2030 and Argentina Innovadora 2030 Plan. The plan seeks to maximize AI opportunities for national development linked to the UN Sustainable Development Goals, though it was approved without dedicated administrative structures or budget allocations.

title: Australia AI Ethics Principles 2019
abstract: Established eight voluntary principles for responsible AI development and deployment — human, societal and environmental wellbeing; human-centred values; fairness; privacy protection and security; reliability and safety; transparency and explainability; contestability; and accountability — aligned with OECD AI Principles.

title: Australia AI Ethics Principles
organization: Australia Dept. of Industry, Science and Resources
abstract: Australia's voluntary framework sets out eight AI ethics principles—human and societal wellbeing, human-centred values, fairness, privacy and security, reliability and safety, transparency and explainability, contestability, and accountability—intended to guide organisations and government in the design, development, deployment, and operation of AI systems. The principles were developed following public consultation on a CSIRO Data61 discussion paper.

title: Australia's AI Ethics Principles
abstract: Australia's AI Ethics Framework sets out eight voluntary principles — covering human wellbeing, fairness, privacy, reliability, transparency, contestability, and accountability — intended to guide organisations and government in the design, development, and deployment of AI systems. Developed by CSIRO Data61 with the Department of Industry, Science and Resources following a public consultation process, the principles are non-binding and apply across both public and private sectors.

title: Australian AI Ethics Framework
abstract: Eight core principles for ethical AI in Australia: human, social and environmental wellbeing; human-centred values; fairness; privacy protection and security; reliability and safety; transparency and explainability; contestability; and accountability.

title: Beijing AI Principles
abstract: 15 principles for responsible AI research, development and governance endorsed by major Chinese universities, companies and government institutes.

title: Beijing AI Principles
abstract: Fifteen principles for responsible AI development covering research and development, use, and governance of AI, emphasizing harmony between AI and humanity, privacy protection, diversity, and international cooperation.

title: Algo.Rules
abstract: Nine rules for the design of just algorithmic systems, focusing on transparency, accountability, fairness, and societal impact.

title: Belgium: AI 4 Belgium
abstract: Belgium's 2019 AI 4 Belgium report, prepared by 40 multidisciplinary experts, provides the first policy framework for a Belgian national AI strategy, covering ethics and governance, AI sector mapping, community engagement, European funding, education, industrial adoption, and development of AI-driven products and services. It served as a precursor to Belgium's formal 2022 National Convergence Plan for AI Development.

title: China New Generation AI Governance Principles
abstract: Eight principles for new-generation AI governance in China: harmony and friendliness, fairness and justice, inclusiveness and sharing, respect for privacy, safety and controllability, shared responsibility, open collaboration, and agile governance.

title: Chinese AI Alliance Joint Pledge
abstract: Self-discipline pledge by Chinese AI industry members covering ethical principles, privacy protection, safety, and responsible innovation.

title: CEC Response to Draft Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI
abstract: The Conference of European Churches submitted a formal response to the European Commission's High-level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence draft ethics guidelines for trustworthy AI. The response represents a Protestant/Orthodox ecumenical perspective on EU AI governance from an organization representing 114 churches across Europe.

title: CMU K&L Gates Ethics and AI Initiative
abstract: An interdisciplinary initiative at Carnegie Mellon University to elucidate ethical and societal issues in computational technologies, organized around seven core principles: autonomy, explainability, benefit, responsibility and control, non-discrimination, fairness, and dignity. The initiative conducts research, education, and policy work spanning CMU's School of Computer Science, philosophy, public policy, and business.

title: COMECE Robotisation of Life Reflection
abstract: Ethical reflection produced by a COMECE ad-hoc working group on robotisation, led by Prof. Antonio Autiero with experts in theology, philosophy, law, and engineering. Reaffirms the primacy of the human person and promotes a rights-based, person-centred approach to AI and robotics. States that 'no unconditional or emphatic acceptance of Artificial Intelligence is possible.' Published during intense EU-level discussions on AI ethics.

title: Czech Republic: National AI Strategy
abstract: Adopted by the Czech Government on 6 May 2019 as part of the Innovation Strategy of the Czech Republic 2019-2030, this seven-chapter strategy aims to make the Czech Republic a European 'superhub for artificial intelligence.' It covers science and R&D promotion, investment support, AI in industry and public administration, human capital, labour market impacts, legal and ethical aspects, and consumer protection. Implementation proceeds in three stages through 2035.

title: CJLS Teshuvah: AI Moral Machines and Halakhah
abstract: Official halakhic responsum approved by the Conservative movement's Committee on Jewish Law and Standards (CJLS reference HM 182.1.2019). Authored by Rabbi Daniel Nevins, the teshuvah surveys the field of machine ethics and brings it into conversation with halakhic discourses of agency, damages, limited liability, and ethics. Concludes that while AI and autonomous machines may assist humans in important decisions, a human must remain responsible, especially for matters of life and death, and the boundary between human and artificial life is significant and must be maintained.

title: Canada Directive on Automated Decision-Making
abstract: Required federal departments to complete Algorithmic Impact Assessments (AIA), implement scaled mitigation measures by impact level (I–IV), and ensure human oversight, transparency, and recourse for automated decision systems used in administrative decision-making.

title: Beijing AI Principles
abstract: Fifteen guiding principles for responsible AI research, development, use, governance, and long-term planning released by the Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence — a body backed by China's Ministry of Science and Technology and the Beijing municipal government — and endorsed by leading Chinese universities and tech companies including Baidu, Alibaba, and Tencent.

title: China AI Governance Principles 2019
organization: China Ministry of Science and Technology
abstract: Articulated eight responsible AI governance principles — harmony and human-friendliness, fairness and justice, inclusion and sharing, privacy respect, safety and controllability, shared responsibility, openness and collaboration, and agile governance — for China's AI R&D and deployment.

title: China Governance Principles New Gen AI
abstract: Eight principles for responsible AI development issued by China's national AI governance expert committee under MOST, covering harmony and friendliness, fairness and justice, inclusivity, privacy, security, shared responsibility, open cooperation, and agile governance.

title: Chinese Governance Principles for AI
abstract: Eight principles for responsible AI governance from China's national AI governance expert committee, emphasizing harmony, cooperation, and human benefit.

title: Colombia CONPES 3975 AI Policy 2019
abstract: Colombia's landmark national AI policy, adopted in November 2019, establishing a strategic framework to reduce barriers to digital technology adoption, foster innovation, and strengthen human capital for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. With an investment of approximately COP 121.6 billion over five years, CONPES 3975 positioned Colombia as a pioneer in Latin American AI governance.

title: Colombia CONPES 3975 Digital AI Policy
abstract: Colombia's first public policy recognizing AI as an accelerator of digital transformation, with 21 specific AI actions leading to decrees, statutes, programs, and laws; evaluated at 99% compliance by its 2022 expiry.

title: German Data Ethics Commission Report
abstract: Comprehensive recommendations for data governance and AI ethics from Germany's federal data ethics commission, covering algorithmic systems, data economy, and digital infrastructure.

title: Danish National Strategy for AI
abstract: Denmark's national AI strategy aims to establish Denmark as a frontrunner in responsible AI development and use, built on four pillars: an ethical and human-centered foundation for AI, stronger research and competencies, enabling business growth through AI, and improving public sector services. The strategy outlines 24 specific initiatives supported by government funding through 2027.

title: Denmark Data Ethics Council Establishment
abstract: Denmark's independent Data Ethics Council, established in 2019 in line with OECD AI Principles, monitors technological developments and advises on ethical dilemmas arising from data and AI use. The Council provides guidance to businesses and public institutions, and Denmark subsequently enacted legislation (2021) mandating companies disclose data ethics policies in annual reports.

title: EC Communication: Building Trust in AI 2019
abstract: European Commission communication responding to the AI HLEG's Ethics Guidelines, launching a piloting phase for the Assessment List for Trustworthy AI (ALTAI) and reaffirming human-centric AI as the EU's guiding principle. Formed the bridge between the 2018 AI strategy and the 2020 White Paper.

title: EU HLEG Ethics Guidelines for AI
abstract: Presented seven key requirements for trustworthy AI (human agency and oversight, technical robustness and safety, privacy and data governance, transparency, diversity and fairness, societal and environmental wellbeing, and accountability), supported by an assessment checklist (ALTAI).

title: EEA Comments on EU AI Ethics Guidelines
abstract: Official submission by the European Evangelical Alliance to the EU High-Level Expert Group on AI, commenting on the Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. Raises three primary concerns: (1) need for broad societal engagement before AI deployment, (2) protecting humanity from AI applications that risk moral autonomy and diminish decision-making skills, (3) need for internationally enforceable oversight and legislation. Authored by Arie de Pater. Original PDF: eea_contribution_to_eu_hleg_on_ai_-_november_2019.pdf.

title: EU HLEG Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI
abstract: Foundational EU AI ethics document establishing three components of trustworthy AI (lawful, ethical, robust) and seven key requirements: human agency and oversight, technical robustness and safety, privacy and data governance, transparency, diversity and non-discrimination, societal and environmental wellbeing, and accountability. Accompanied by the Assessment List for Trustworthy AI (ALTAI, 2020).

title: EU Ethics Guidelines Trustworthy AI
organization: EU High-Level Expert Group on AI
abstract: A 41-page framework establishing that trustworthy AI must be lawful, ethical, and robust, grounded in four ethical principles (human autonomy, prevention of harm, fairness, explicability) and seven key requirements including human agency and oversight, technical robustness and safety, privacy and data governance, transparency, diversity and non-discrimination, societal and environmental well-being, and accountability.

title: Evangelical Statement of Principles on AI
abstract: Developed over nine months by the Ethics and Religious Liberty Commission (ERLC) and signed by over 70 evangelical leaders (including SBC president J.D. Greear and prominent theologians), this 12-article statement establishes a theological framework for AI grounded in human dignity as image-bearers of God. It addresses bias, lethal autonomous weapons, human dignity, privacy, and the church's unique role in proclaiming human dignity in the age of AI.

title: Estonia: Kratt AI Strategy 2019-2021
abstract: Estonia's 'Kratt' AI strategy (named after a creature from Estonian folklore) was adopted by the Cabinet in July 2019, committing €10 million over 2019-2021 to deploy 50 AI use cases in the public sector by 2020. The strategy rests on four pillars—public sector AI adoption, private sector support, research and skills, and legal environment adaptation—and included the development of the Bürokratt national AI assistant.

title: Fujitsu Group AI Commitment
abstract: Fujitsu's AI Commitment establishes five principles for human-centric, sustainable AI that respects people's decision-making, with emphasis on corporate transparency and accountability. The commitment was backed by an External Advisory Committee on AI Ethics established in September 2019 and a dedicated AI Ethics and Governance Office established in January 2022.

title: G20 AI Principles
abstract: G20 human-centred AI principles adopted in Osaka 2019, building on OECD AI Principles, covering inclusive growth, human rights, transparency, robustness, and accountability.

title: Canada Responsible Use of AI Principles
abstract: Canada's government principles for responsible AI use including understanding and measurement of impacts, transparency, protection of privacy and security, promoting diversity and inclusion, clear accountability, and considering social impacts.

title: America Under Watch: Face Surveillance in the United States
organization: Georgetown Center on Privacy and Technology
abstract: By Clare Garvie and Laura M. Moy. Documents how Detroit and Chicago have deployed real-time citywide face surveillance networks integrated with existing camera systems, and how other major cities run pilots. Identifies privacy, First Amendment, and racial bias risks, and calls for a moratorium on police use of face recognition pending comprehensive regulatory frameworks.

title: Data Ethics Commission Germany Principles
abstract: Comprehensive opinion from Germany's data ethics advisory body on ethical principles for data and AI use, covering human dignity, self-determination, privacy, data security, non-discrimination, and democratic accountability.

title: Hambach Declaration on AI
abstract: Declaration by German data protection authorities on key requirements for AI systems, focusing on data protection, transparency, and accountability.

title: Indigenous Data Governance & Universities Communique
abstract: A formal communique addressed to universities and funding organizations, asserting that institutions creating, using, and holding Indigenous data — including historical records, contemporary datasets, and AI technologies — have obligations to Indigenous data governance. The document covers data about Indigenous individuals, families, communities, environments, knowledges, cultures, languages, and specimens.

title: Huawei AI Security & Privacy Whitepaper
abstract: Huawei's AI Security and Privacy Protection white paper defines seven governance objectives (system security, transparency, privacy, fairness, data management, competence, and deployment assurance) and proposes a shared responsibility model across five stakeholder roles. Released at HUAWEI CONNECT 2019, the paper argues that no single party can address complex AI security threats alone.

title: IACAD Dubai Virtual Ifta AI Fatwa Service
abstract: Dubai's Islamic Affairs and Charitable Activities Department (IACAD) launched 'Virtual Ifta' at The Boulevard, Emirates Towers, on October 29, 2019. Described as the world's first AI-powered fatwa service, the system takes live questions on internet chat and replies with appropriate Islamic rulings from a pre-verified database. Initial pilot phase covers approximately 205 questions related to salah (prayer) in Arabic and English. If the AI is not 85% confident in its understanding of the question, it will not respond, instead offering similar questions or requesting rephrasing. Planned expansion to WhatsApp and additional topics including fasting, purity, ablution, and financial matters. Handles approximately 130,000 fatwa queries per year. Launched under Dubai 10X programme.

title: IEEE Ethically Aligned Design (EAD1e)
abstract: Comprehensive first edition of IEEE's flagship document on ethical AI, covering general principles, embedding values, methodologies, and application areas.

title: IEEE Ethically Aligned Design v1
organization: IEEE Global Initiative on Ethics of A/IS
abstract: A comprehensive consensus-driven framework distilling high-level ethical principles, key issues, and practical recommendations for the design and deployment of autonomous and intelligent systems. Covers human rights, well-being, data agency, effectiveness, transparency, accountability, and misuse prevention, and accompanies the IEEE P7000 standards series.

title: IEEE Ethically Aligned Design v1e (Final)
abstract: The final first edition of IEEE's comprehensive framework for ethically aligned design of autonomous and intelligent systems, covering general principles, human rights, wellbeing, data agency, effectiveness, transparency, and a range of domain-specific applications.

title: IEEE Ethics in Action
abstract: IEEE's ongoing initiative translating ethical principles into actionable specifications and standards for autonomous and intelligent systems.

title: IA Latam Declaration for AI Ethics
abstract: Declaration from Latin American AI community on ethical principles for AI development and deployment in the region.

title: ITU-T Y.3172 ML Framework 2019
abstract: First ITU-T standard addressing machine learning in telecommunications networks, specifying architectural requirements, ML pipeline components, and management and orchestration functionalities for integrating AI/ML into 5G and future networks.

title: Institute for Ethical ML Responsible ML Principles
abstract: Eight principles for responsible machine learning practitioners covering human augmentation, bias evaluation, explainability, reproducibility, displacement strategy, practical accuracy, trust by action, and data risk awareness.

title: Open Data Institute Data Ethics Canvas
abstract: Practical tool to help organizations identify and manage ethical issues in data projects, covering rights, power, and benefits.

title: ITechLaw Responsible AI Global Policy Framework
abstract: Global policy framework for responsible AI from an international technology law perspective, covering governance, liability, and ethical principles.

title: Japan AI Strategy 2019
abstract: Japan's first national AI strategy packaging human resource development, R&D, and social implementation across key sectors into an integrated policy to realize an 'AI-ready society.' Sets goals for AI education reform, industry competitiveness, and international cooperation, with annual review and update cycle.

title: Japan Social Principles Human-Centric AI
abstract: Japan's foundational AI ethics principles grounded in three core philosophies — dignity, diversity and inclusion, and sustainability — and seven social principles including human-centricity, education/literacy, privacy, security, fair competition, fairness/accountability/transparency, and innovation. Forms the ethical backbone of Japan's 'AI-ready society' vision under Society 5.0.

title: Japan Social Principles of Human-Centric AI
abstract: Established three core values (human dignity, diversity and inclusion, sustainability) and seven operational principles for AI in Japanese society as part of the Society 5.0 agenda, designed as soft law to foster multi-stakeholder iterative governance.

title: Keidanren AI Utilization Strategy 2019
abstract: The AI Utilization Strategy (AI活用戦略) by Keidanren sets out business community positions on AI policy for Japan. It advocates for promoting AI adoption across all sectors to realize Society 5.0 (the concept of a human-centric, technology-integrated society), calls for appropriate data infrastructure and interoperability, and addresses workforce transformation through education and reskilling. The document emphasizes the need for flexible, innovation-enabling governance rather than restrictive regulation, and advocates international harmonization of AI governance standards. It also articulates principles for data sharing, privacy, and fairness in AI deployment.

title: Lithuania: AI Strategy — Vision of the Future
abstract: Lithuania's national AI strategy, published April 2019, aims to modernize and expand the AI ecosystem with a goal of becoming a regional leader. It covers AI skills and education for all citizens, strengthening the research and innovation ecosystem, increasing AI deployment across economic sectors, promoting international collaboration, developing ethical and legal AI frameworks, and establishing a responsible data ecosystem. Priority sectors identified include manufacturing, agriculture, healthcare, and transportation.

title: MIC AI Utilization Guidelines 2019
abstract: The AI Utilization Guidelines (AI利活用ガイドライン) were developed by the Conference toward AI Network Society under MIC. They define ten utilization principles that AI users and operators should observe to mitigate risks and increase the benefits of AI: (1) Appropriate use, (2) Appropriate data handling, (3) Safety, (4) Security, (5) Privacy, (6) Transparency, (7) User assistance, (8) Accountability, (9) Education/Literacy, (10) Innovation. The guidelines align with the Cabinet Office's Human-Centric AI Social Principles (March 2019) and the OECD Recommendation on AI (May 2019). They are addressed to a wide range of AI users and reference hypothetical usage scenarios to facilitate practical implementation.

title: Five Principles for AI in Society
abstract: A framework by Luciano Floridi and Josh Cowls proposing five unified principles for ethical AI — beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, justice, and explicability — derived from bioethics and demonstrated to subsume the 47 individual principles found across six major AI ethics documents. Published in Harvard Data Science Review.

title: MIT Task Force Social Implications Computing
abstract: MIT task force report on the future of work in the age of AI, with policy recommendations for inclusive economic growth and worker protection.

title: Megvii AI Application Criteria
abstract: Application criteria from Chinese AI company Megvii (known for facial recognition) for responsible use of AI technologies, covering privacy protection, security, anti-discrimination, and responsible deployment.

title: NEC AI and Human Rights Principles
abstract: NEC's AI and Human Rights Principles address seven focus areas — fairness, privacy, transparency, responsibility to explain, proper utilization, AI and talent development, and dialogue with multiple stakeholders — developed in response to ethical concerns about biometric technologies including face recognition. The principles were developed collaboratively with internal divisions and external stakeholders including nonprofits.

title: Netherlands Dutch AI Coalition Framework
abstract: The Dutch AI Coalition is a voluntary public-private coalition launched in the context of the Dutch Government's Strategic Action Plan for AI (October 2019). It coordinates and accelerates development of trustworthy, human-centred AI in the Netherlands, with objectives including practical ethical frameworks and guidelines for AI, hosting a European Knowledge and Innovation Center, and ensuring inclusive and human-centric AI development with strong data-sharing commitments.

title: Netherlands: Strategic Action Plan for AI
abstract: Launched 8 October 2019 by the Secretary of State for Economic Affairs, the Netherlands' Strategic Action Plan for AI outlines over 100 concrete actions across three pillars: capitalising on AI opportunities, creating enabling conditions (education, data, infrastructure), and strengthening ethical foundations. The plan was co-developed with the public-private AI Task Force and is implemented through the Netherlands AI Coalition (65+ partners) and the AiNed investment programme.

title: OECD AI Recommendation
abstract: First intergovernmental standard on AI, adopted by OECD member countries, setting out five principles for trustworthy AI and five recommendations for AI policy.

title: OECD Recommendation on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: The foundational OECD Recommendation on AI, adopted at Ministerial level on 22 May 2019, providing five principles for trustworthy AI (inclusive growth, human-centred values, transparency, robustness, accountability) and five recommendations to governments on national AI policies and international co-operation. First major intergovernmental AI policy instrument. Revised in November 2023 to update the AI System definition for generative AI, and again in May 2024 (document C/MIN(2024)16/FINAL) to address disinformation, information integrity, and misuse risks.

title: Oxford Munich Code of Conduct for AI
abstract: An academic code of conduct for AI researchers and practitioners, covering transparency, accountability, fairness, and societal responsibility.

title: Singapore Model AI Governance Framework
abstract: Practical framework for private sector organizations to address key ethical and governance issues in AI deployment, with detailed implementation guidance.

title: Portugal AI Portugal 2030 Strategy
abstract: Concentrated on inclusion, education, qualification, specialization and research as drivers of AI deployment, with Portugal positioned as a living laboratory for AI innovation in urban transformation, energy, biodiversity, autonomous driving, and cybersecurity.

title: Portugal: AI Portugal 2030
abstract: Launched in February 2019 following six public consultation meetings, Portugal's AI Portugal 2030 strategy sets seven foundational pillars including societal well-being, AI education and 'digital minds', job creation, and positioning Portugal as a 'living laboratory' for AI innovation in urban transformation, sustainable energy, biodiversity, autonomous driving, and cybersecurity. It is coordinated under the INCoDe.2030 digital skills initiative.

title: Qatar National AI Strategy
abstract: Announced on October 29, 2019 at QITCOM by the Minister of Transport and Communications, this strategy blueprinted by QCRI/QCAI outlines Qatar's six-pillar approach to AI: talent development, data access, employment, wealth creation, embracing an AI+X future, and thought leadership in AI ethics. It aligns with Qatar National Vision 2030 and has since been integrated into the National Development Strategy and Digital Agenda 2030.

title: RANZCR Ethical Principles for AI in Medicine
abstract: Nine ethical principles for AI and machine learning in medicine and radiology, covering privacy and data protection, data retention, transparency, explainability, and the avoidance of bias. Among the first formal AI ethics publications by a healthcare body globally.

title: Russia National AI Strategy 2030
abstract: Presidential Decree No. 490 establishing Russia's national AI strategy through 2030, setting near-term goals (through 2024) and medium-term objectives to position Russia as a leading AI power, covering computing resources, workforce development, and AI application across key sectors. Significantly amended by Presidential Decree No. 124 on February 15, 2024, adding ~40 pages of updates targeting 95% AI readiness in critical industries by 2030.

title: Swiss Data Ethics Alliance Codex
abstract: Ethical codex for Swiss data-intensive service organizations covering transparency, privacy, fairness, and accountability in data processing.

title: Shanghai AI Safety Development Initiative
abstract: Shanghai's initiative for safe AI development emphasizing security testing, oversight mechanisms, transparency, privacy protection, and collaborative governance of AI systems within the Shanghai AI ecosystem.

title: Dubai AI Ethics Principles Toolkit
abstract: Dubai's ethical AI toolkit providing guidance for government and private sector AI implementations, covering transparency, accountability, privacy, and inclusion.

title: Salesforce Trusted AI Principles
abstract: Salesforce's five Trusted AI Principles — Responsible, Accountable, Transparent, Empowering, and Inclusive — were developed through a year-long internal feedback process beginning in 2018 and formalized in 2019 under the Office of Ethical and Humane Use. The principles specifically target Salesforce's Einstein AI products and commit to safeguarding human rights, augmenting human capability, and ensuring inclusivity.

title: Samsung Principles for AI Ethics
abstract: Samsung's principles for ethical AI research and development covering transparency, human dignity, user privacy, fairness, and responsible development of AI technologies.

title: Serbia AI Strategy 2020-2025
abstract: First national AI strategy in the Western Balkans, adopted December 26, 2019 and effective from January 1, 2020, establishing Serbia's five-year vision as a regional AI leader through three phases: infrastructure establishment (2020–2021), human capital development (2021–2023), and application scaling (2023–2025). Aligns with the European AI Initiative and covers economic growth, public services, scientific advancement, and ethical AI governance. Superseded by a successor strategy for 2025–2030.

title: Singapore National AI Strategy 2019
abstract: Singapore's first national AI strategy establishing five national AI projects (transport/logistics, smart cities, healthcare, education, safety/security) and five ecosystem enablers (partnerships, talent, data, R&D/compute, trust/governance). Created the National AI Office and set Singapore's ambition to be a leading AI nation for both economic value and public good.

title: Sony AI Ethics Guidelines
abstract: Sony's internal guidelines for responsible AI covering safety, respect for human rights, fairness, privacy, and transparency.

title: South Korea National AI Strategy 2019
abstract: South Korea's comprehensive national AI strategy announced at the 53rd cabinet meeting, comprising nine strategies and 100 initiatives across three areas: establishing an AI ecosystem, utilizing AI across industries, and creating human-centered AI. Aims to position Korea among the global top AI nations by 2030.

title: South Korean AI National Strategy
abstract: South Korea's comprehensive national strategy for AI development and governance, emphasizing innovation, ethics, and international cooperation.

title: Telia Guiding Principles on Trusted AI
abstract: Telia's ethical principles for trusted AI covering human oversight, fairness, transparency, privacy, accountability, and sustainability.

title: China AI Standardization White Paper 2019
abstract: Published by TC260's Big Data Security Standards Working Group, the 2019 white paper analyzes AI security risks and the then-current standardization landscape in China and internationally (ISO/IEC JTC1/SC42, IEEE, NIST). It proposes a standards roadmap addressing AI-specific security threats and lays groundwork for subsequent TC260 AI standards including the 2024 TC260-003 and the AI Safety Governance Framework.

title: Vienna Manifesto on Digital Humanism
abstract: Manifesto calling for human-centered design of digital technologies including AI, emphasizing democratic values, human rights, and societal benefit.

title: Te Hiku Media Kaitiakitanga License
abstract: The Kaitiakitanga License (Guardianship License) is a data governance mechanism developed by Te Hiku Media to protect the sovereignty of te reo Māori language data collected for AI and speech recognition projects. All content on tehiku.nz is protected under this license, which applies principles of kaitiakitanga (guardianship/stewardship) to prevent commercial exploitation of Māori linguistic data by external parties.

title: UK Alan Turing Institute AI Ethics Guide
abstract: Practical guide for public sector managers on responsible AI development and deployment, covering ethics and safety considerations.

title: Understanding AI Ethics and Safety
abstract: A comprehensive guide by David Leslie providing the UK Government's official public sector guidance on AI ethics and safety, introducing the Process-Based Governance (PBG) Framework, SUM Values (Respect, Connect, Care, Protect), and FAST Track Principles (Fairness, Accountability, Sustainability, Transparency). Developed in partnership with the UK Office for Artificial Intelligence and the Government Digital Service.

title: UAE National AI Strategy 2031
abstract: Set the vision for the UAE to become a global AI hub by 2031, organized around four pillars (industry assets, smart government, data governance, next-generation talent), targeting an AED 335 billion boost to the economy and AI contributing 20% of non-oil GDP by 2031.

title: Defense Innovation Board AI Principles
abstract: Recommendations from the Defense Innovation Board for ethical AI use in the DoD, covering responsible, equitable, traceable, reliable, and governable AI principles for defense applications.

title: UK Code of Conduct Health Data-Driven Tech
abstract: Ten principles for data-driven health technology companies seeking to work with NHS, covering transparency, accountability, safety, and data governance.

title: UK AI in the Public Sector Guide
abstract: Guidance for UK public sector organizations on developing and deploying AI ethically and safely.

title: UN HLP Digital Cooperation: Age of Digital Interdependence
