abstract: Report of the 20-member panel co-chaired by Melinda Gates and Jack Ma, with five recommendations including enhanced global AI cooperation that is trustworthy, human rights-based, safe, and sustainable. Calls for autonomous intelligent systems to be explainable with humans accountable for their use.

title: COMEST Preliminary Study on Ethics of AI
abstract: COMEST preliminary study scoping the ethical implications of AI systems across UNESCO's central mandate domains (education, science, culture, communications), identifying key ethical challenges and proposing generic principles for responsible AI development. Formed the foundational groundwork for the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence.

title: COMEST/UNESCO Suggested AI Principles
abstract: Preliminary study by UNESCO's ethics commission proposing generic principles for ethical AI including respect for human dignity and autonomy, prohibition of harm, fairness, non-discrimination, responsibility, transparency, and privacy.

title: UNICRI-INTERPOL Report: AI for Law Enforcement
abstract: Joint UNICRI-INTERPOL report summarizing findings from the first Global Meeting on Opportunities and Risks of AI and Robotics for Law Enforcement (Singapore, July 2018), providing key findings, challenges, and recommendations for responsible AI use by law enforcement agencies.

title: UNI 10 Principles for Workers' Data Rights
abstract: UNI Global Union's formal document establishing 10 operational principles for workers' data rights and protection, intended to be incorporated into collective bargaining, global agreements, and multinational alliances. The document addresses the growing use of data by management to hire, fire, promote and discipline workers. Workers are granted the right of explanation regarding what data is collected, how it is stored, its provenance, and intended use. The 10 principles are designed to address AI and algorithmic management as data-driven decision-making systems increasingly affect workers globally.

title: UNSCEB UN System-Wide Strategic Approach on AI
abstract: System-wide roadmap endorsed by CEB at its first regular session of 2019, coordinated by ITU, outlining UN commitments to support AI capacity development especially for developing countries and the most vulnerable populations in the context of achieving the SDGs.

title: EO 13859: US AI Leadership Initiative
abstract: Established the American Artificial Intelligence Initiative, a whole-of-government approach to maintaining US leadership in AI, with five key lines of effort: increasing AI R&D investment, unleashing federal AI computing and data resources, setting AI technical standards, building the AI workforce, and engaging international allies.

title: Vodafone AI Framework
abstract: Vodafone's framework for responsible AI addressing fairness, transparency, privacy, and human dignity.

title: Women Leading in AI Principles
abstract: Nine principles for responsible AI with an emphasis on gender inclusion, diversity, and addressing bias in AI development.

title: WMA Statement on Augmented Intelligence (2019)
abstract: Adopted by the 70th WMA General Assembly in Tbilisi, Georgia, October 2019. The WMA defined 'augmented intelligence' as AI systems designed to coexist with and augment physician decision-making rather than replace it, calling for direct physician involvement in AI development, transparent and reproducible systems, and preservation of the patient-physician relationship. Rescinded and archived by the 76th WMA General Assembly in Porto, Portugal, October 2025, superseded by the updated 2025 statement.

title: AIATSIS Code of Ethics for Indigenous Research
abstract: The AIATSIS Code of Ethics establishes principles for ethical research with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, replacing the 2012 GERAIS guidelines. Grounded in the Wiradjuri concept of yindyamarra (acting with honour, respect, and wisdom), the code provides a framework for Indigenous data governance in research contexts, emphasizing Indigenous knowledge systems and their sovereignty.

title: African Union Digital Transformation Strategy
abstract: A 10-year continental strategy addressing digital infrastructure, data governance, cybersecurity, and emerging technologies including AI for Africa's economic transformation, serving as the foundational policy framework for the 2024 Continental AI Strategy.

title: China AI for Children Beijing Principles
abstract: Principles for AI development and deployment specifically for children's contexts, covering safety, education, privacy, digital literacy, non-exploitation, and child wellbeing in AI systems that interact with or affect children.

title: Principled AI: Mapping Consensus
abstract: White paper by Jessica Fjeld, Nele Achten, Hannah Hilligoss, Adam Nagy, and Madhulika Srikumar analyzing 36 prominent AI principles documents to identify consensus. Identifies eight key thematic trends: privacy, accountability, safety and security, transparency and explainability, fairness and non-discrimination, human control of technology, professional responsibility, and promotion of human values.

title: Principled AI: Mapping Consensus
abstract: A white paper by Jessica Fjeld, Nele Achten, Hannah Hilligoss, Adam Nagy, and Madhulika Srikumar that systematically maps 36 prominent AI ethics principle documents across eight thematic categories: privacy, accountability, safety and security, transparency and explainability, fairness and non-discrimination, human control of technology, professional responsibility, and promotion of human values.

title: Bangladesh National Strategy for AI 2020
abstract: Bangladesh's 2020 National AI Strategy identifies seven priority sectors including education, health, and transport, and outlines a five-year roadmap for research and infrastructure development. The ICT Division's framework aims to build a sustainable AI ecosystem through institutional capacity development, research and innovation, human capital development, and ethical governance, positioning AI as a driver of the country's entry into the fourth industrial revolution.

title: Bulgaria AI Concept 2020-2030
abstract: Bulgaria's national strategic framework positioning AI as a catalyst for economic competitiveness through 2030, built on six pillars: digital infrastructure, AI education, research and innovation capacity, data as strategic resource, AI integration across priority sectors (smart agriculture, healthcare, e-governance), and trustworthy human-centric regulation aligned with EU standards. Developed by the Bulgarian Academy of Sciences and finalized after public consultation by the Ministry of Transport.

title: CDT Civil Rights Principles on Technology
abstract: CDT joined The Leadership Conference and dozens of civil rights organizations in endorsing the 2020 Civil Rights Principles for the Era of Big Data, which call for decision-making technologies to ensure just outcomes, preserve constitutional rights, serve historically marginalized communities, collect and use personal data responsibly, enhance individual rights, and be transparent and accountable.

title: CEC Comment on EU White Paper on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: CEC's Thematic Group on Science, New Technologies and Christian Ethics responded to the European Commission's White Paper on AI. The submission addresses anthropological concerns and human dignity in AI development, religious discrimination and freedom of belief in data usage, and calls for a holistic approach addressing anthropological, social, educational and environmental dimensions of AI.

title: CNJ Resolução 332/2020 — AI in Judiciary
abstract: Resolução que dispõe sobre ética, transparência e governança na produção e uso de Inteligência Artificial no Poder Judiciário brasileiro. Estabelece requisitos de auditabilidade, compatibilidade com direitos fundamentais, diversidade nas equipes de desenvolvimento, segurança de dados e restrições ao uso de IA em matéria penal. Revogada pela Resolução CNJ nº 615/2025.

title: CoE CM/Rec(2020)1 Algorithmic Systems
abstract: Council of Europe non-binding recommendation calling on member states to ensure algorithmic systems comply with human rights obligations, including rights to fair trial, privacy, freedom of expression, and equal treatment. Requires that states not deploy systems so opaque that human oversight becomes impractical.

title: Council of Europe PACE Res. 2342 (2020)
abstract: Council of Europe Parliamentary Assembly resolution and accompanying recommendation on the use of AI in policing and criminal justice, identifying risks of historical bias in training data and human rights impacts from facial recognition, predictive policing, and risk assessment tools, calling for proper regulation.

title: DGB Concept: AI for Good Work
abstract: DGB-Konzeptpapier, das die 'Gute Arbeit by design'-Leitlinie für KI-Einsatz in der Arbeitswelt konkretisiert. Fordert verbindliche Mitbestimmungsprozesse, frühzeitige Einbindung der Beschäftigten und betriebliche Gestaltungsrechte. Erweiterung des Impulspapiers von 2018.

title: EC White Paper on AI 2020
abstract: The European Commission's foundational policy consultation document outlining regulatory options for a trustworthy AI framework, proposing risk-based requirements for high-risk AI (health, policing, justice, transport) including transparency, traceability, and human oversight. Preceded the 2021 AI Act legislative proposal.

title: EEA Response to EU White Paper on AI
abstract: Official submission by the European Evangelical Alliance (representing ~23 million evangelical Christians across Europe) to the EU White Paper on AI consultation. Authored by Arie de Pater (Brussels Representative) with an interdisciplinary expert group. Asserts any use of AI should honour that humans are created in God's image. Raises concerns about AI's impact on moral autonomy, relationships, and freedom of choice. Calls for legally enforceable international oversight, comprehensive impact assessments, and inclusive societal debate including faith perspectives.

title: EP Resolution: AI Ethics Framework 2020
abstract: One of three October 2020 EP resolutions on AI, calling on the European Commission to establish a comprehensive legal framework of ethical principles for the development, deployment, and use of AI, robotics, and related technologies.

title: ETUC Resolution on AI and Data Strategies
abstract: Adopted by the ETUC Executive Committee on 2 July 2020, this resolution responds to the European Commission's White Paper on AI and Data Strategy. The ETUC demands a legal and empowering European framework based on human rights for AI governance. Key provisions include: worker protections against workplace surveillance; classification of AI applications affecting workers' rights as high-risk systems subject to stringent regulation; trade union involvement in AI deployment decisions; and data governance frameworks that protect workers' collective interests. The resolution positioned European trade unions at the forefront of shaping the forthcoming EU AI Act.

title: Fraunhofer IAIS Trustworthy AI
abstract: Practical framework for organizations to implement trustworthy AI covering transparency, explainability, fairness, robustness, and privacy.

title: CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance
abstract: The CARE Principles (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility, and Ethics) address limitations of open data movements by centering Indigenous rights and interests in data governance. Drafted at International Data Week in 2018 and formally published in Data Science Journal (2020), these people- and purpose-oriented principles complement FAIR data principles by emphasizing Indigenous control over data concerning their communities and territories.

title: GPAI Founding Statement 2020
abstract: Founding declaration of GPAI by 15 member states (Australia, Canada, France, Germany, India, Italy, Japan, Mexico, New Zealand, Republic of Korea, Singapore, Slovenia, UK, USA, and EU), committing to human-centric AI development aligned with human rights and democratic values. Establishes four working groups: Responsible AI, Data Governance, Future of Work, and Innovation & Commercialization.

title: Germany National AI Strategy Update 2020
abstract: Updated the 2018 AI strategy to address new challenges from the COVID-19 pandemic and increased emphasis on environmental sustainability, climate protection, and European AI competitiveness.

title: Hungary: National AI Strategy 2020-2030
abstract: Published September 2020 and co-developed with the 320-member Artificial Intelligence Coalition (which released an AI Action Plan in October 2019), Hungary's AI strategy covers the full AI value chain from data generation through research to deployment, with priority sectors in manufacturing, healthcare, and agriculture. Goals include training 8,000 citizens in AI, creating AI PhD programs, establishing a National Laboratory for Autonomous Vehicles, and an Innovation Centre for AI by 2025.

title: IAATW Charter and Manifesto
abstract: Launched in January 2020 when app-based drivers from 16 countries and six continents convened in Oxford, England. The IAATW Manifesto demands the right to individual and collective control of all personal data at work, the right to transparency of algorithmic decision-making, transparency on algorithms that control earning and work including bonuses, and the right to protection of privacy at work including strict and transparent limitations on workplace surveillance. It also demands universal labor guarantees, safety protections, and the right to work for multiple companies.

title: Indigenous Protocol and AI Position Paper
abstract: A foundational position paper for designing AI systems through Indigenous epistemologies, developed over 20 months by the Indigenous Protocol and AI Working Group with contributors from Indigenous communities in Aotearoa, Australia, North America, and the Pacific. The paper presents heterogeneous contributions — from design guidelines and scholarly essays to creative works and prototypes — centering Indigenous ethical concerns and rejecting homogenizing approaches that flatten diverse Indigenous knowledge systems.

title: IFLA Statement on Libraries and Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Issued by IFLA's Freedom of Access to Information and Freedom of Expression (FAIFE) committee in October 2020. The statement outlines key considerations for the use of AI and machine learning technologies in the library sector and suggests the roles libraries should strive to take on in a society with growing AI integration. Addresses critical questions about intellectual freedom, equity and privacy, automation, the evolution of necessary digital literacy skills, and relevant Intellectual Property policy frameworks. Key principles include: AI use in libraries must be subject to clear ethical standards (per the IFLA Code of Ethics for Librarians); AI applications relying on extensive data collection must not override patron privacy choices or equity considerations; and libraries should help educate the public about AI. The statement is accompanied by three annexes covering intellectual freedom, AI literacy, and libraries at the forefront of AI.

title: Indonesia National AI Strategy 2020-2045
abstract: Indonesia's comprehensive AI strategy through 2045, launched August 10, 2020, with four focus areas (ethics and policy, talent development, infrastructure and data, and industrial R&D) and five priority sectors (health, government services, education, food security, and smart cities/mobility). Proposes a National AI Ethics and Data Council; being formalized as a Presidential Regulation as of early 2026.

title: South Korea National AI Ethics Guidelines
abstract: South Korea's national AI ethics guidelines establishing three core values (human dignity, public benefit, technology appropriateness) and ten key principles for ethical AI development and use.

title: Civil Rights Principles Era of Big Data 2020
abstract: A coalition statement signed by 25 leading civil rights and technology advocacy organizations (including ACLU, NAACP, EFF, CDT, Color Of Change, and Brennan Center for Justice) updating the 2014 Civil Rights Principles for the Era of Big Data to address algorithmic discrimination. Six core principles cover ending high-tech profiling, ensuring just automated decisions, preserving constitutional principles, serving marginalized communities, responsible data use, and transparency.

title: Latvia: Developing AI Solutions
abstract: Latvia's national AI strategy, released in February 2020, outlines six pillars for AI adoption: raising public competencies through education, promoting AI in public and private sectors, fostering international cooperation, developing a legal and ethical AI framework, building a robust data ecosystem, and investing in digital and telecommunications infrastructure. The Ministry of Interior allocated approximately €1.5 million for initial digitalization and AI deployment efforts.

title: Korea National Guidelines for AI Ethics
abstract: These interagency guidelines establish foundational standards for human-centered AI development and deployment. Built on three basic principles (respect for human dignity, common good of society, proper use of technology), they specify ten key requirements covering human rights, privacy, non-discrimination, harm prevention, public good, solidarity, data stewardship, accountability, safety, and transparency—applicable throughout the full AI lifecycle.

title: Lawyers' Committee Facial Recognition Comments to PCLOB
organization: Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
abstract: Formal regulatory submission to the Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board (PCLOB) opposing expansive facial recognition deployment, particularly in law enforcement and government surveillance contexts. The submission emphasizes documented accuracy and bias issues with particular disparities affecting Black Americans and communities of color, surveillance concerns regarding discriminatory targeting, and calls for robust legal constraints including requirements for transparency, accuracy testing, and clear limitations on deployment scope. Calls for moratoriums on certain applications and legislative action rather than reliance on voluntary industry standards.

title: Mexico Agenda Nacional IA 2030
abstract: A multisectoral national AI agenda for Mexico covering the period to 2030, developed through broad civil society, academic, and industry collaboration. The agenda addresses five thematic axes aimed at promoting AI development with ethical, inclusive, and sustainable principles, and was submitted to the Mexican Senate as a policy proposal.

title: Mozilla Trustworthy AI White Paper
abstract: A white paper defining trustworthy AI as tech that is demonstrably worthy of trust and that considers accountability, agency, and individual and collective well-being. Identifies four strategic levers: changing AI development norms, building new tech and products, raising consumer awareness, and strengthening AI regulation. Emphasizes privacy, fairness, safety, and human agency.

title: Norway National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Norway's national AI strategy positions the country to develop AI grounded in democratic values, adopting the European Commission HLEG's seven ethical principles: human autonomy, safety, privacy, transparency, inclusion, societal benefit, and accountability. The strategy emphasizes data governance, research infrastructure, skills development, and the establishment of an AI regulatory sandbox by the Norwegian Data Protection Authority.

title: NZ Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa NZ
abstract: The Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand commits signatory public sector agencies to six principles for transparent, accountable, and Treaty-consistent use of algorithms in government decision-making, embedding a Te Ao Māori perspective and requiring meaningful human oversight. Launched in July 2020 as a key deliverable of the 2018–2020 Open Government Partnership National Action Plan, it was signed by 21 public sector agencies and described as a world-first framework for public algorithmic governance.

title: New Zealand Algorithm Charter 2020
abstract: A voluntary charter for public sector agencies committing to six obligations including transparency about algorithmic decision-making, embedding a Te Ao Māori (Māori World) perspective, and retaining human oversight — cited as a world-first national government algorithm charter.

title: New Zealand Algorithm Charter
abstract: Charter committing New Zealand government agencies to meaningful transparency and human rights considerations when using algorithms in decision-making.

title: New Zealand Trustworthy AI Principles
abstract: New Zealand's AI principles addressing inclusive growth; sustainable and human-centred development; transparency and accountability; privacy and security; reliability and safety; fairness and impartiality; and incorporating Māori values and Treaty of Waitangi principles.

title: Norway: National Strategy for AI
abstract: Norway's national AI strategy, launched in January 2020, outlines policy actions to maximise opportunities from AI for individuals, businesses, and the public sector, emphasising ethical principles rooted in human rights and democracy. It identifies seven core AI principles including human autonomy, safety, privacy, transparency, non-discrimination, societal benefit, and institutional accountability, with sector focus on health, energy, and maritime industries.

title: OxCAIGG Four Principles Framework
organization: Oxford Commission on AI & Good Governance
abstract: A working paper by Lisa-Maria Neudert and Philip N. Howard establishing four principles for integrating AI with good governance, intended to serve as a starting point for evidence-based policy recommendations. Published as the first deliverable of the Oxford Commission on AI & Good Governance, launched July 2020.

title: Rome Call for AI Ethics
organization: Pontifical Academy for Life et al.
abstract: A joint call for ethical AI development signed by the Vatican's Pontifical Academy for Life, Microsoft, IBM, and Italian government, promoting six principles: transparency, inclusion, responsibility, impartiality, reliability, and security and privacy.

title: Poland AI Development Policy 2020
abstract: Set activities in six key areas (AI and society, innovative companies, science, education, international cooperation, and public sector) under the mission of protecting human dignity and supporting fair competition, with a Virtual Research Institute for AI and multi-agency governance.

title: Singapore Model AI Governance Framework
abstract: Singapore's governance framework for responsible AI adoption, providing guidance on internal governance structures, human oversight, operations management, and stakeholder interaction for businesses using AI.

title: Plum Village Mindful Technology Ethics
organization: Plum Village Community of Engaged Buddhism
abstract: Plum Village's Centre for Applied Ethics articulates ethical commitments for technology development rooted in the Five Mindfulness Trainings. Key principles: (1) develop technology in line with the Five Mindfulness Trainings, emphasizing healthy consumption and mindful technology use; (2) open-source code 'in the spirit of generosity and sharing'; (3) prioritize accessibility for those facing barriers due to disability, age, location, language, or financial hardship; (4) complete transparency in donations and expenses via public dashboard; (5) design for conscious consumption rather than exploitative engagement. Thich Nhat Hanh envisioned an 'Online Monastery' where people could practice mindfulness via computers, grounding digital tools in contemplative ethics.

title: Philips Responsible AI in Healthcare
abstract: Philips' principles for responsible AI in healthcare covering human oversight, transparency, safety, privacy, and inclusive benefit.

title: Pontifical Academy for Life AI Ethics Note (2020)
abstract: Following the February 2020 Rome Call signing, the Pontifical Academy for Life published this scholarly reflection on Catholic contributions to AI ethics in the digital era, contributing to the journal Nature Machine Intelligence. The document extends the Rome Call's principles of 'algorethics' and reflects on how Catholic social thought and anthropology inform ethical AI development.

title: Rome Call for AI Ethics
abstract: Joint declaration by the Vatican, Microsoft, and IBM calling for ethical AI centered on transparency, inclusion, impartiality, reliability, security and privacy, and protection of people.

title: Royal College Canada AI Task Force Report
abstract: Comprehensive task force report on integrating AI and emerging digital technologies into specialist physician training and practice in Canada, based on expert interviews and survey of over 4,000 physicians. Includes recommendations on ethics, privacy, social justice, and curriculum integration.

title: EKD SI Theses for an AI-Competent Society
abstract: Position paper from the EKD's Social Science Institute presenting theses for an AI-competent society from a Protestant perspective. Emerged from the conference 'Autonom? Kompetentes Leben und Arbeiten mit KI-Systemen aus protestantischer Perspektive' (November 2020). Covers three sectors: AI competency in education and society, AI competency in economics and labor, and AI competency in church and diaconal work. Authored by Prof. Dr. Jorg Kopecz (AEU), Prof. Dr. Georg Lammlin (SI-EKD), and Prof. Dr. Tobias Popovic (HFT Stuttgart).

title: Saudi Arabia NSDAI 2020
abstract: Announced at the first Global AI Summit in Riyadh, the strategy aims to position Saudi Arabia as a global AI leader by 2030 in alignment with Vision 2030, focusing on five sectors (education, healthcare, energy, mobility, government) through six first-phase initiatives.

title: Saudi Arabia National Strategy for Data and AI
abstract: Announced at the first Global AI Summit in Riyadh in October 2020, this strategy maps Saudi Arabia's path to become a global AI leader aligned with Vision 2030. The six-pillar framework covers positioning the Kingdom as a global data and AI hub, workforce development, a legislative framework, investment, research institutions, and digital infrastructure, with priority sectors including education, healthcare, energy, mobility, and government.

title: Singapore Model AI Governance Framework
abstract: Singapore's practical governance framework for private sector organizations deploying AI, grounded in two principles: AI decisions should be explainable, transparent and fair; AI should be human-centric. The second edition expands coverage to robustness/reproducibility and broader stakeholder communications, supported by an Implementation and Self-Assessment Guide (ISAGO) and Compendium of Use Cases.

title: South Korea National AI Ethics Standards
abstract: South Korea's voluntary national AI ethics guidelines — also known as the 'Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence Ethics Standards' — establishing three core values (humanity, the public interest, and technology purposiveness) and ten core requirements guiding the full AI lifecycle for public and private sector actors. Designed as a living document subject to ongoing revision.

title: Spain ENIA National AI Strategy
abstract: Spain's national AI strategy defines six strategic axes including research and innovation, talent, data and infrastructure, AI in value chains, public sector adoption, and an ethical and normative framework. The ethical axis aims to protect rights, reduce algorithmic bias, and build public trust, including a plan for protection of vulnerable groups and an AI trustmark (Sello IA).

title: Switzerland: Federal AI Guidelines
abstract: The Swiss Federal Council adopted seven guidelines for AI use within the federal administration on 25 November 2020, led by the principle 'People at the Heart,' emphasizing that human dignity, fundamental rights protection, and the common good must be paramount. The guidelines serve as a binding reference for federal agencies and partners entrusted with governmental tasks, with Chapter 3 outlining ongoing monitoring provisions. Switzerland has opted for a framework approach rather than a standalone national AI strategy.

title: syndicom AI Guiding Principles and Position
abstract: syndicom hat an ihrer nationalen Konferenz im November 2019 eine Resolution zur Künstlichen Intelligenz verabschiedet und anschliessend eine Broschüre mit 9 KI-Leitprinzipien veröffentlicht. Forderungen umfassen: gesetzliche Regulierung ähnlich EU-Standards, Schutz vor KI-gestützter Arbeitsplatzüberwachung, menschliche Aufsicht, Urheberrechtsschutz für Kreative, Transparenzanforderungen und stärkere Mitbestimmungsrechte.

title: Tzu Chi Medical Journal AI Bioethics Principles
abstract: Published in the Tzu Chi Medical Journal (Vol. 32, Issue 4), this article by Prof. Michael Cheng-Tek Tai proposes four bioethics principles for AI: (1) Beneficence — AI should benefit humanity and the universe, development must be 'altruistic, not egocentric'; (2) Value-Upholding — AI must align with universal social and moral values while remaining unbiased; (3) Lucidity — AI must be transparent and comprehensible, with 'explainability and interpretability absolutely required' in high-stakes applications; (4) Accountability — developers bear responsibility for AI's societal impacts. The framework reflects Buddhist values of compassion and non-harm applied to AI governance.

title: US DoD AI Ethics Principles
abstract: Five ethical principles for AI in the US military: responsible, equitable, traceable, reliable, and governable, applicable to both combat and non-combat AI applications.

title: AI Ethics Principles for US Intelligence Community
abstract: Six principles for ethical AI use in the US Intelligence Community: respect for law, accuracy, security, bias mitigation, transparency, and accountability. Applies across all 18 agencies in the intelligence community.

title: UNICEF Policy Guidance on AI for Children
abstract: Nine requirements for child-centered AI systems, covering privacy, safety, transparency, accountability, and inclusion of children's voices.

title: UN SG Roadmap for Digital Cooperation
abstract: Secretary-General's action-oriented roadmap for global digital cooperation, including support for AI that is trustworthy, human rights-based, safe, sustainable, and promotes peace. Addresses universal connectivity, digital public goods, AI governance, digital trust and security, and effective architecture for digital cooperation.

title: OMB M-21-06: AI Regulation Guidance
abstract: Issued ten principles to guide federal agencies in determining whether and how to regulate AI applications developed and deployed by the private sector, and required agencies to submit plans identifying AI-related regulations and non-regulatory actions.

title: US National AI Initiative Act 2020
abstract: Codified a national strategy and funding approach to accelerate AI research, development and innovation, train an AI-skilled workforce, and position the US as a global leader in trustworthy AI, authorizing nearly $6.3 billion across NSF, DOE, and NIST over five years.

title: Ukraine Concept of AI Development 2020
abstract: Cabinet Decree No. 1556 establishing Ukraine's 2020–2030 vision for AI development, focused on socio-economic modernization, public administration, cybersecurity, and legislative harmonization with international standards. The document emphasizes compliance with rule of law, fundamental rights, and democratic values, and served as the cornerstone for subsequent AI roadmaps and white papers.

title: Ver.di Ethical Guidelines for AI Development and Use
abstract: Ver.di-Bundesvorstand beschließt Ethische Leitlinien für KI (3.3.2020) anlässlich Eröffnung des KI-Observatoriums BMAS. Auf Deutsch und Englisch verfügbar. Richtet sich an Beschäftigte in Entwicklung, Einkauf und Implementierung von KI-Systemen. Ergänzt die Position von 2018.

title: WEF Framework for Facial Recognition
abstract: Framework for responsible deployment of facial recognition technology, addressing bias, transparency, data governance, and accountability.

title: WFEO Big Data and AI Principles in Engineering
abstract: WFEO's Committee on Engineering for Innovative Technologies (CEIT) formulated seven principles to promote responsible conduct of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence application and innovation in engineering. Released on the first World Engineering Day (March 4, 2020; announced March 16, 2020), the principles were developed through three rounds of survey consultation with more than 40 organizations and experts worldwide. The seven principles are: (1) Good for Humanity and Its Environment; (2) Inclusiveness, Fairness, Public Awareness and Empowerment; (3) Opening and Sharing while Respecting Privacy and Data Integrity; (4) Transparency; (5) Accountability; (6) Peace, Safety and Security; (7) Collaboration.

title: White House AI Regulation Guidance
abstract: Ten principles guiding US federal agencies in regulating AI applications, prioritizing innovation while protecting safety, rights, and democratic values.

title: Alibaba AI Ethics Principles
abstract: Alibaba Group's technology ethics committee established six guiding principles for AI: human-centric, inclusiveness and integrity, safety and reliability, privacy protection, trustable and accountable, and openness and collaboration. The framework involves members from DAMO Academy, legal and compliance teams, and seven independent external experts from technology, law, and philosophy.

title: AK Wien Policy Paper AI in the Workplace 2021
abstract: Positionspapier der Arbeiterkammer Wien zu Künstlicher Intelligenz in der Arbeitswelt. Der KI-Einsatz im Betrieb wird oft als unterstützend und entlastend für Beschäftigte dargestellt, birgt aber erhebliche Risiken für Arbeitnehmerrechte. Die AK fordert: umfassende Mitbestimmungsrechte bei KI-Einführung, Vetorechte, einen nachvollziehbaren Bottom-up-Ansatz und das Verbot bestimmter Anwendungen.

title: EU AI Act Civil Society Statement
abstract: A collective statement signed by 120 civil society organizations calling for the EU AI Act to prioritize fundamental rights, with eight key recommendations including comprehensive prohibitions on social scoring, remote biometric identification, emotion recognition, and physiognomy systems; mandatory fundamental rights impact assessments; enforceable rights; and environmental transparency.

title: Adobe AI Ethics Principles
abstract: Adobe's principles for responsible AI covering accountability, responsibility, and transparency in AI-driven creative tools.

title: AlgorithmWatch Civil Society AI Act Fundamental Rights
abstract: AlgorithmWatch co-authored this joint civil society statement with EDRi and 119 organizations calling on EU institutions to prioritize fundamental rights in the proposed AI Act. The statement calls for prohibitions on high-risk AI systems, mandatory impact assessments, public transparency, and meaningful redress for those affected by AI.

title: Amnesty International Ban the Scan 2021
abstract: Open letter and global 'Ban the Scan' campaign calling for a total ban on the use, development, production, and sale of facial recognition technology for mass surveillance by police and government agencies. Argues facial recognition violates rights to privacy, peaceful assembly, and expression; disproportionately harms Black and marginalized communities. Endorsed by 180+ rights groups.

title: Australia's AI Action Plan 2021
abstract: Australia's AI Action Plan set out a vision for Australia to become a global leader in developing and adopting trusted, secure, and responsible AI, backed by AUD $124.1 million in government investment. Key initiatives included the National AI Centre, four AI and Digital Capability Centres, business-government AI pilots, and a Next Generation AI Graduates Program.

title: Austria: AI Mission Austria 2030
abstract: Austria's AIM AT 2030 strategy commits to promoting responsible, broad AI use in the public interest based on European fundamental values, reducing dependence on global technology monopolies, and strengthening fundamental AI research. Implemented jointly by three agencies (aws, FFG, FWF) with an annual budget of approximately €22 million, the strategy targets applications in healthcare, education, public services, and the economy.

title: Bits of Freedom AI Act Fundamental Rights Co-Statement
abstract: Bits of Freedom co-authored and signed the joint civil society statement with EDRi calling for fundamental rights-centered AI regulation. In parallel Dutch-language publications, the organization argued the EU AI law proposal provides insufficient human rights protections, called for a complete ban on biometric surveillance in public spaces, and demanded prohibition of manipulation and 'social scoring' of citizens' behavior.

title: Reflections of Our Values: Digital Technologies and a Just Transition
abstract: Baha'i International Community statement presented to the UN Commission for Social Development on digital technologies, equity, and just transition.

title: BSA Framework to Confront AI Bias
abstract: BSA's 32-page framework provides over 50 diagnostic statements for organizations to perform AI impact assessments to identify and mitigate bias across an AI system's lifecycle, promoting fairness, transparency, and accountability. BSA simultaneously called on governments to legislate requirements for impact assessments on high-risk AI uses, building on its 2018 five-pillar responsible AI innovation framework.

title: BSI: AI Cloud Service Compliance Criteria Catalogue (AIC4)
abstract: The BSI AIC4 provides AI-specific criteria enabling evaluation of cloud-based AI services across their full lifecycle. The catalogue addresses robustness, security, reliability, integrity, transparency, explainability/interpretability, fairness, non-discrimination, and data quality. It is periodically revised to reflect advances in AI technology, and supports independent auditor assessment.

title: Brazil EBIA 2021
abstract: Brazil's first national AI strategy, established by MCTI Ordinance No. 4,617 of April 6, 2021, organizing 74 strategic actions across nine thematic pillars including ethics, research, innovation, and talent development. The strategy promotes ethical and conscious use of AI while establishing eight AI centers focused on agriculture, healthcare, industry, and smart cities.

title: Brazil EBIA National AI Strategy 2021
abstract: Brazil's first dedicated AI public policy, setting principles, nine thematic axes and 73 strategic actions to guide public policy, research, capacity-building, ethics and international alignment on AI, based on OECD guidelines and developed through public consultation with ~1,000 contributions.

title: China National New Gen AI Ethical Norms
abstract: China's ethical norms for AI development articulating six basic ethical requirements: enhancing well-being, promoting fairness and justice, protecting privacy and security, ensuring controllability and trustworthiness, strengthening responsibility, and improving ethical literacy.

title: CLC Convention Policy: A Good Jobs Agenda
abstract: CLC convention policy paper 'A Good Jobs Agenda' adopted at the 2021 convention. Examines threats to workers through technological change and proposes 45 recommendations, a substantial number addressing the need for workers and unions to have a voice on AI in the workplace. Advocates for worker participation in AI deployment decisions, collective bargaining rights, and protection against algorithmic and automated decision-making.

title: Church of Scotland AI Report
abstract: Report by the Church of Scotland's Society, Religion and Technology (SRT) project examining how increasing reliance on technology and AI implementations affect relational human beings made in the image of God. Explores AI applications including search engines, voice assistants, facial recognition, business, government, and healthcare uses. Presented to the General Assembly. Accompanied by a 4-session congregational resource series (2022) covering 'What is AI?', 'Using Your Personal Data', 'Robotics', and 'Theology and AI'.

title: Chile National AI Policy 2021
abstract: Chile's ten-year national AI policy, presented October 28, 2021, developed with input from more than 7,500 participants through a broad consultative process. The policy establishes 185 initiatives and 70 priority actions across three axes — enabling factors, technology development and use, and ethics and security — with CLP 26 billion in public investment. Updated in 2024 extending applicability to 2031.

title: China AI Ethical Norms 2021
organization: China Ministry of Science and Technology
abstract: Established ethical norms for AI R&D and application emphasizing augmenting human well-being, fairness, transparency, security, privacy protection, accountability, and ethical self-discipline for AI developers, deployers, and users.

title: China Ethical Norms New Gen AI
abstract: Ethical guidelines for integrating ethics into the full AI lifecycle, covering management, R&D, supply, and use activities. Establishes six core ethical requirements — human welfare, fairness, privacy/security, controllability, accountability, and ethical cultivation — and 18 specific requirements. Non-binding; no enforcement penalties specified.

title: Coworker.org — Little Tech Is Coming for Workers
abstract: Coworker.org's flagship report analyzing the proliferation of employment technologies harming workers. Based on a database of over 550 labor-focused technology products, the report documents how AI, location tracking, and biometrics are used to manage employees and collect their data, often without their knowledge. Over 30% of the tracked products emerged between 2020-2021. The report and accompanying Bossware and Employment Tech Database serve as a comprehensive resource for worker advocates. A follow-up report, 'Little Tech Goes Global,' was published in June 2025 expanding analysis to Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, Nigeria, Kenya, and India.

title: EC Communication: Fostering EU Approach to AI 2021
abstract: Commission communication accompanying the 2021 AI Act proposal and Coordinated Plan update, articulating the EU's human-centric, trustworthy AI vision and setting out a comprehensive policy package for innovation while protecting fundamental rights.

title: EC Proposal for AI Act 2021
abstract: The original European Commission proposal for the AI Act, introducing a risk-based regulatory framework with four tiers and establishing the European AI Board. Launched the trilogues that resulted in the final Regulation (EU) 2024/1689.

title: EU Coordinated Plan on AI 2021
abstract: Updated EU-wide coordinated plan aligning Member State AI strategies with EU policy, targeting at least EUR 20 billion in annual AI investment by 2030. Emphasises application in climate, healthcare, robotics, public sector, and mobility. As of 2024, 24 of 27 member states have adopted national AI strategies.

title: EDRi Beyond Debiasing Report
abstract: Commissioned by EDRi and authored by researchers from Delft University of Technology, this 155-page report argues that policymakers must address root causes of power imbalances caused by AI systems rather than relying on technical debiasing. It criticizes the EU's technocratic approach for narrowly focusing on algorithmic debiasing while underestimating broader social, economic, and political harms, and calls for structural regulatory reforms.

title: EDRi Civil Society AI Act Fundamental Rights Statement
abstract: EDRi and 119 civil society organizations urge EU institutions to prioritize fundamental rights in the proposed Artificial Intelligence Act, calling for prohibitions on high-risk AI systems threatening human rights, mandatory impact assessments, public transparency and accountability, and meaningful redress for those affected by AI. Signed by 123 organizations including Bits of Freedom, Panoptykon Foundation, epicenter.works, AlgorithmWatch, and Fair Trials.

title: EKD Freiheit digital Denkschrift
abstract: Official EKD memorandum (Denkschrift) examining digital transformation through the lens of the Ten Commandments from a Protestant ethical perspective. Addresses responsible use of new digital freedoms including data protection, disinformation, power abuse, interpersonal relationships, digitalization of military technology, and the power of individual companies in digital spaces. Published by Evangelische Verlagsanstalt (ISBN 978-3-374-06858-6). While covering digitalization broadly rather than AI exclusively, it provides the EKD's foundational ethical framework applicable to AI technologies.

title: EP Resolution: AI in Criminal Law 2021
abstract: European Parliament resolution establishing red lines for AI in law enforcement and judicial settings, addressing algorithmic bias and discrimination in AI tools used for policing, risk assessment, and criminal justice. Adopted 377 to 248 with 62 abstentions.

title: epicenter.works AI Act Fundamental Rights Statement
abstract: epicenter.works co-authored this joint civil society statement with EDRi, Access Now, Panoptykon Foundation, AlgorithmWatch, Bits of Freedom, Fair Trials, EDF, PICUM, and ANEC, signed by over 100 organizations. The statement outlines recommendations for the European Parliament and Council to amend the AI Act proposal to foreground fundamental rights and prohibit high-risk AI systems that threaten human rights.

title: Egypt National AI Strategy (First Edition)
abstract: Launched July 2021 by MCIT, Egypt's first national AI strategy positions the country as a regional AI leader in Africa and the Arab world, aligned with Egypt Vision 2030. Built on four pillars—AI for Government, AI for Development, AI for Human Capacity Building, and AI for External Relations—it established the National Council for Artificial Intelligence and made Egypt the first Arab and African country to adhere to the OECD Recommendation on Artificial Intelligence.

title: Egypt National AI Strategy 2021
abstract: Launched to build capacity for AI integration in the public sector, foster international collaboration, and support the 'Digital Egypt' initiative, establishing a National AI Council under MCIT to oversee implementation.

title: Egypt National AI Strategy
abstract: Egypt's first National AI Strategy, launched by President El-Sisi in July 2021 under the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, established four strategic pillars — AI for Government, AI for Development, AI for Human Capacity Building, and AI for External Relations — positioning Egypt as a regional AI leader in Africa and the Arab world. Superseded by the 2025-2030 second edition.

title: Estonia: Kratt Strategy 2022-2023
abstract: The second iteration of Estonia's Kratt Strategy, published February 2021, builds on the 2019 strategy with a goal to deploy 130 AI solutions across 60 public institutions by 2023, allocating a minimum of €20 million for 2022-2023 plus €100 million in broader digitization. Key deliverables include launching the Bürokratt AI government assistant, expanding open datasets to 1,800 entries, and training 450 AI professionals.

title: CNPEN Opinion on Autonomous Vehicles
abstract: The CNPEN's second opinion examines ethical dilemmas raised by automated driving vehicles across five dimensions: terminology, road safety, human control, personal freedoms, and social/environmental impact. The opinion makes 19 recommendations, including a preference for the term 'automated driving vehicle' over 'autonomous vehicle' and options for programming behavior in unavoidable harm scenarios.

title: CNPEN Opinion on Conversational Agents
abstract: The CNPEN unanimously adopted this opinion identifying eight dimensions of ethical reflection on chatbots and AI conversational agents. It formulates 13 recommendations on use, 10 principles for design, and 11 research questions, with key concerns about the status of conversational agents and their capacity to imitate emotions and manipulate users.

title: G20 Rome Declaration AI Reaffirmation (2021)
abstract: The 2021 Rome G20 Leaders' Declaration reaffirmed willingness to implement trustworthy AI and committed to further advancing the implementation of the G20 AI Principles adopted at Osaka 2019, while welcoming the transformation of the Digital Economy Task Force into a Working Group.

title: G20 Interfaith Forum AI Policy Brief
abstract: Policy brief submitted to the G20 Summit in Italy, produced by the Technology and Innovation Working Group chaired by Marco Ventura. Addresses how emerging digital technologies and AI raise normative, legal, and ethical challenges requiring interfaith engagement. Argues AI should supplement, not replace, human judgment and control. Recommends human-centered AI design, protection of the most vulnerable in algorithmic decision-making, and an international dialogue framework fostering human rights. Calls for religious and belief organizations to play a constructive role in AI governance.

title: G20 Interfaith Forum Religion and AI in the EU Report
abstract: Report examining how religion or belief actors in the European Union engage with artificial intelligence policy, produced in response to the European Commission's White Paper on AI. Explores the role of religious organizations in AI governance within the EU regulatory framework, the ethical perspectives various faith traditions bring to AI debates, and recommendations for including religious voices in European AI policy. Part of a series of publications from the Research and Innovation Working Group that also includes a 2019 Position Paper on Religion and Innovation and a 2020 Document on Religion and AI in the EU.

title: GPAI Responsible AI Working Group
abstract: Working group recommendations from GPAI's Responsible AI stream, covering topics including AI and pandemic response, data governance principles, future of work guidance, and innovation frameworks. Produced by multidisciplinary expert panels from 25 member countries, grounded in the OECD AI Principles.

title: ICRC Position on Autonomous Weapon Systems
abstract: The ICRC formally recommends that states adopt legally binding international rules to regulate autonomous weapon systems (AWS) — machines that select and apply force to targets without human intervention. The position identifies three primary risks: civilian harm, IHL compliance challenges, and ethical concerns about delegating lethal decisions to machines. The ICRC proposes: (1) prohibition of unpredictable autonomous systems whose effects cannot be sufficiently understood or predicted; (2) prohibition on autonomous systems designed or used to apply force against human persons; and (3) strict regulation of all remaining AWS, constraining target types, operational duration/scope, deployment circumstances, and mandatory human-machine interaction. The ICRC calls for timely adoption of internationally agreed limits given rapid technological development, and submitted the position to the UN GGE on Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems.

title: IEEE 7000-2021 Ethical Concerns Standard
abstract: IEEE standard providing a model process to identify and analyze potential ethical issues in systems and software during the design phase. Part of the broader IEEE P7000 series translating the Ethically Aligned Design principles into technical standards.

title: IFIP Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
abstract: A formal Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct adopted by the IFIP General Assembly (September 2020, published 2021), adapted from the ACM Code of Ethics. The Code comprises three sections: seven common ethical principles shared with other professions; nine professional responsibilities specific to IT professions; and seven additional responsibilities applicable to IT professionals in leadership positions. Designed to inspire and guide the ethical conduct of all computing professionals including practitioners, instructors, and students. Also endorsed through IP3 (International Professional Practice Partnership). While not AI-specific, the Code governs conduct of computing professionals working on AI and related technologies.

title: Canada Advisory Council AI Annual Report 2020-21
organization: Canada Advisory Council on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Canada's Advisory Council on Artificial Intelligence, established in 2019, advises the federal government on building AI strengths, identifying economic opportunities, and ensuring AI reflects Canadian values. The 2020-2021 annual report documents the launch of a Public Awareness Working Group that engaged 437 Canadians across virtual workshops plus dedicated youth sessions, focusing on AI literacy and ethical AI development. The Council advises that AI governance should emphasize 'made-in-Canada' values and sustained public engagement.

title: Data & Society: Assembling Accountability
abstract: Maps the challenges of constructing algorithmic impact assessments (AIAs) and provides a framework for evaluating the effectiveness of current and proposed AIA regimes. Analyzes use of impact assessment in other domains (finance, environment, human rights, privacy) and identifies common components of existing impact assessment practices. Foundational framework report for AI accountability policy. Authors: Emanuel Moss, Elizabeth Anne Watkins, Ranjit Singh, Madeleine Clare Elish, Jacob Metcalf.

title: India NITI Aayog Responsible AI Principles (Part 1)
abstract: India's first approach document on responsible AI articulates seven broad principles: safety and reliability, inclusivity and non-discrimination, equality, privacy and security, transparency, accountability, and protection of positive human values. The document positions India's approach within constitutional principles and advocates for sector-specific implementation aligned with the 'AI for All' vision.

title: India Responsible AI Part 1: Principles
abstract: The first part of NITI Aayog's two-part responsible AI approach document establishes guiding principles for the ethical development and deployment of AI in India, covering inclusive growth, safety and reliability, privacy and data protection, transparency, accountability, human oversight, and environmental well-being. The principles were developed through inter-ministerial consultations, global multi-stakeholder consultations, and public feedback over 15 months.

title: Israel National AI Program 2021
abstract: A five-year, NIS 5.26 billion (~$1.48 billion) national AI program with five bold objectives and three 'moonshots,' pursuant to Government Resolution 212 (August 2021), to position Israel at the cutting edge of AI through research, infrastructure, talent, and international collaboration.

title: Israel National AI Program
abstract: A five-year, NIS 5.26 billion (~$1.48 billion USD) national investment program approved under Government Resolution 212 (August 1, 2021), establishing a cross-ministerial National AI Program with an AI Directorate in the Prime Minister's Office. Core components include human capital development, a public-sector supercomputing centre, promotion of NLP capabilities in Hebrew and Arabic, and an enabling regulatory environment, positioning Israel as a global AI leader.

title: KCC Basic Principles for AI-Based Media Recommendation Service User Protection
abstract: 방송통신위원회가 AI 기반 미디어 추천 서비스의 투명성과 다양성 확보를 위해 서비스 사업자에게 권고하는 자율규제 기준. 투명성·공정성·책임성을 3대 핵심 원칙으로 설정하고, 딥페이크 성범죄물 차단, 이용자 인격 보호 모니터링 체계 구축, AI 산출물 의사결정 과정 정보 제공 등을 권고한다. 2022년 4월 28일 해설서 발간.

title: La Quadrature du Net AI Regulation Surveillance Lobbies
abstract: La Quadrature du Net argues that the European Commission's proposed AI regulation, despite claiming to protect freedoms, facilitates surveillance expansion. The statement critiques the regulation's weak prohibitions on biometric identification, loopholes that merely classify predictive policing and lie detection as 'highly risky' rather than prohibited, and industry capture through mandatory regulatory sandboxes that exempt companies from rights protections.

title: Malaysia National AI Roadmap 2021-2025
abstract: Defined how Malaysia's AI capabilities will be harnessed over five years across 11 national AI use cases in five priority sectors (agriculture, healthcare, smart cities, education, public services), with the vision of positioning Malaysia as a regional AI hub and in the top 20 of the Government AI Readiness Index by 2025.

title: Korea Trustworthy AI Implementation Strategy
abstract: 2021년 5월 13일 제4차산업혁명위원회 제22차 전체회의에서 발표된 '신뢰할 수 있는 인공지능 실현전략'. 민간 인공지능 서비스의 신뢰성 체계적 지원, 이용자가 안전하게 활용할 수 있는 제도 보완, 사회 전반 건전한 AI 윤리 확산을 목표로 3대 전략 10개 실천과제를 2025년까지 단계적으로 추진하는 내용을 담고 있다.

title: Malaysia National AI Roadmap 2021-2025
abstract: Malaysia's five-year national AI roadmap (AI-RMAP) defining six strategic objectives: AI governance, R&D, digital infrastructure, talent development, AI acculturation, and innovation ecosystem. Aims to position Malaysia as a nation where AI augments employment, drives competitiveness, and enables inclusive economic prosperity.

title: Colombia Ethical Framework for AI 2021
abstract: Marco de principios éticos no vinculantes para el diseño, desarrollo e implementación de proyectos de inteligencia artificial en entidades públicas colombianas. Establece diez principios: transparencia, explicabilidad, privacidad, control humano, seguridad, responsabilidad, no discriminación, inclusión, prevalencia de derechos de niños y adolescentes, y beneficio social.

title: NATO AI Strategy (2021)
abstract: NATO's first AI strategy, adopted October 2021, establishing six Principles of Responsible Use of AI in Defence (Lawfulness, Responsibility and Accountability, Explainability and Traceability, Reliability, Governability, Bias Mitigation) and outlining strategic outcomes for responsible AI adoption across the Alliance.

title: NATO Principles of Responsible Use of AI
abstract: Six principles for responsible use of AI in defense adopted by NATO: lawfulness, responsibility, explicability, reliability, governability, and bias mitigation.

title: NATO Principles on Responsible AI in Defence
abstract: Six principles for responsible AI use in NATO defence contexts: lawful, responsible and accountable, explicit and traceable, reliable, governable, and bias mitigation. Establishes shared framework for member nations.

title: NITI Aayog Responsible AI India
abstract: India's framework for responsible AI covering safety, equality, inclusivity, non-discrimination, privacy, transparency, and accountability.

title: NITI Aayog Responsible AI Part 1
abstract: Part 1 of NITI Aayog's two-part Responsible AI series anchors seven AI governance principles in India's constitutional values and recommends a multi-stakeholder governance model with NITI Aayog as coordinating policy body, working with MeitY, sectoral regulators, standards bodies, academia, and civil society. The document proposes risk-based governance, capacity-building measures, and a framework for operationalizing ethics-by-design across public, private, and academic sectors. Developed with contributions from IIT Madras's RBCDSAI research center.

title: NITI Aayog Responsible AI Part 2
abstract: Part 2 moves from principle articulation to implementation, prescribing concrete multi-disciplinary actions and behavioral changes in organizational processes for government ministries, private industry, and academic institutions. The document covers regulatory interventions (model cards, audit mechanisms), capacity building programs, mechanisms to incentivize ethics-by-design, and guidance for compliance with AI standards. It builds on the National AI Strategy and Part 1 principles.

title: OHCHR Report: AI and Right to Privacy
abstract: OHCHR report examining multifaceted impacts of AI on the right to privacy and associated human rights, stressing the urgent need for a moratorium on sale and use of AI systems posing serious risks to human rights and calling for banning AI applications that cannot comply with international human rights law.

title: OIF Digital Francophonie Strategy 2022-2026
abstract: Adoptée lors de la 39e session de la Conférence ministérielle de la Francophonie (10 décembre 2021), cette stratégie vise à accélérer la transformation numérique de l'espace francophone au profit des populations (en particulier les jeunes et les femmes), de la diversité culturelle et linguistique, et de la démocratie. Elle inclut des objectifs explicites sur le développement responsable de l'IA respectueux des droits humains et sur l'adaptation des cadres juridiques et réglementaires pour un numérique inclusif.

title: OxCAIGG: AI in Public Service 2021
abstract: Report from Oxford Commission on AI & Good Governance setting out three clear recommendations and three short-term actions for how governments can harness AI for good governance. Guided by four principles: inclusive design, informed procurement, purposeful implementation, and persistent accountability. Commissioners include Rumman Chowdhury, Wendy Hall, and Safiya Noble.

title: PAI ABOUT ML Reference Document
abstract: Multi-year, multi-stakeholder initiative to develop, test, and implement ML system documentation practices at scale. Establishes transparency standards for ML systems via documentation best practices across datasets, models, and deployed systems. Structured as both artifact workstream (documentation templates) and process workstream (implementation guidance).

title: Peru National AI Strategy 2021-2026
abstract: Guides Peru's AI policy for 2021–2026, addressing governance, talent, data, public sector use, ethics and inclusion, with the primary objective of creating AI-based solutions to national problems and developing priority productive sectors and public services.

title: PIPC AI Personal Information Protection Self-Inspection Checklist
abstract: 개인정보보호위원회가 AI 서비스 개발·운영 시 발생할 수 있는 개인정보 침해를 예방하기 위해 마련한 자율점검표. 개발자·운영자용으로 6개 원칙, 16개 점검 항목, 54개 확인사항으로 구성되어 있으며, 기존에 추상적 원칙 수준에 머물렀던 AI 개인정보보호 기준을 구체화하였다. 2024년 12월 'AI 프라이버시 리스크 관리 모델'로 대체.

title: Peru ENIA 2021-2026
abstract: Peru's National AI Strategy for 2021-2026, developed through coordinated multi-stakeholder engagement by the PCM's Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation. The strategy focuses on human talent development, digital infrastructure, data openness, ethical frameworks, and responsible AI use, and is updated biannually as part of the National Digital Transformation Policy.

title: Philippines National AI Roadmap 2021
abstract: The Philippines' first national AI strategy roadmap — a whole-of-nation collaborative framework led by DTI with nine government agencies — structured around two pillars, four strategic dimensions, and seven strategic imperatives. Targets establishing the Philippines as an ASEAN AI Centre of Excellence, projecting a 12% GDP boost (~USD 92B) from AI by 2030. Superseded by NAISR 2.0 in 2024.

title: Poland: Policy for AI Development
abstract: Released January 2021 and building on the EU Coordinated Plan on AI, Poland's national AI policy organises activities across six areas: AI and society, innovative companies, science, education, international cooperation, and the public sector, with short (2023), medium (2027), and long-term (post-2027) objectives. Governance is led from the Prime Minister's Chancellery and includes a Virtual Research Institute for AI (VIR), a Scientific Council for AI, and AI observatories for labour markets and international policy.

title: Russia AI Ethics Code 2021
abstract: Voluntary national ethical framework adopted at the First International Forum 'Ethics of Artificial Intelligence: The Beginning of Trust' on October 26, 2021, authored jointly by the AI Alliance, the Analytical Center under the Russian Government, and the Ministry of Economic Development. Establishes guiding principles for trusted AI development; a Commission for AI Ethics Code was established in May 2022 to oversee implementation; over 423 organizations have signed.

title: SAE AIR6988 — AI in Aeronautical Systems: Statement of Concerns
abstract: Published April 30, 2021 (announced June 29, 2021) by SAE International's G-34 Artificial Intelligence in Aviation committee (jointly with EUROCAE WG-114). This Aerospace Information Report (AIR) reviews current aerospace software, hardware, and system development standards used in the certification and approval process of safety-critical airborne and ground-based systems, and assesses whether these standards are compatible with typical AI and machine learning development approaches. It identifies concerns and gaps in existing certification frameworks for AI/ML-based aeronautical systems, serving as a foundational reference for AI governance in aviation.

title: Santa Clara Principles 2.0
abstract: Version 2.0 of the Santa Clara Principles expands on the original 2018 framework with foundational and operational standards for platform content moderation. It addresses human rights integration, rule clarity, cultural competence, state involvement disclosure, and system integrity across moderation processes. The updated principles are designed to help technology companies comply with human rights responsibilities and to assist advocates in holding platforms accountable for transparent and fair content moderation practices.

title: Sberbank AI Ethics Principles 2021
abstract: Пять принципов, обязательных для всей Группы Сбер: безопасность ИИ (управляемость и контролируемость), прозрачность и ответственность, надёжность, ответственное отношение к интересам клиентов и сотрудников, справедливость (недискриминация). Принципы разработаны с учётом требований Национальной стратегии развития ИИ до 2030 года. Утверждены Наблюдательным советом 2 марта 2021 г.

title: Slovenia: National Programme for AI by 2025
abstract: Adopted May 2021, Slovenia's national AI programme defines 10 strategic objectives across six implementation areas (health, Industry 4.0, digital public services, language technologies, sustainable food production, and spatial planning), with total planned investment of €112 million including approximately €65 million from EU Recovery and Resilience Facility funds. The programme builds on Slovenia's four-decade history in AI research and addresses talent emigration and research funding gaps.

title: Smart Africa AI for Africa Blueprint
abstract: Published in 2021 by the Smart Africa Alliance (a coalition of 40+ African nations), this blueprint provides recommendations for member states to develop national AI strategies and legal frameworks, emphasizing avoidance of dependency on international AI platform monopolies and alignment with Fourth Industrial Revolution goals.

title: TUC Dignity at Work and the AI Revolution
abstract: TUC's landmark AI manifesto, adopted in March 2021 following four years of research on AI in the employment relationship. The manifesto sets out fundamental values and proposals to ensure technology at work benefits everyone while preserving human agency. Key proposals include: a statutory duty to consult trade unions before deploying high-risk AI and automated decision-making (ADM) systems; right to human review and human contact when employment decisions are made by predictive AI; collective agreements reflecting manifesto values; targeting high-risk AI/ADM for enhanced regulation; and protecting equality, transparency, explainability, and work/home boundaries. The manifesto covers topics including algorithmic management, workplace surveillance, and automated disciplinary processes.

title: Thailand AI Ethics Guideline 2021
abstract: Established six core AI ethics principles (competitiveness and sustainability, law and ethics, transparency and accountability, security and privacy, fairness and inclusiveness, reliability) with a multi-layered governance approach covering internal organizational, technical, and national coordination levels.

title: Tunisia National AI Strategy 2021-2025
abstract: Tunisia's five-year AI roadmap (2021-2025) developed through a multi-ministerial MOU signed February 19, 2022, aims to secure Tunisia's place in the AI race by developing AI skills, establishing necessary infrastructure, adopting data policies, and promoting networking. Formalized through collaboration with ANPR and aligned with UNESCO AI ethics principles, it targets integration of AI in health, education, environment, and transport sectors, and laid the groundwork for the country's 2026-2030 National Development Plan which used AI tools in its drafting.

title: Turkey National AI Strategy 2021-2025
abstract: Enacted August 20, 2021 via Presidential Circular No. 2021/18, Turkey's national AI framework defines six strategic priorities with 24 objectives and 119 measures for the period 2021-2025. The vision is to 'create value on a global scale with an agile and sustainable AI ecosystem for a prosperous Turkey,' with targets including increasing AI's contribution to GDP to 5%, reaching 50,000 AI sector employees, and entering the top 20 countries in international AI indices.

title: UK AI Council AI Roadmap
abstract: The UK AI Council's roadmap makes 16 recommendations to the UK government structured around four pillars: Research, Development and Innovation; Skills and Diversity; Data, Infrastructure and Public Trust; and National Cross-sector Adoption. The document calls for sustained public sector investment, diversity benchmarking, data governance leadership, and an independent entity to oversee AI governance and ethics.

title: UK National AI Strategy 2021
abstract: Set out the UK government's plan to maintain its position as a global AI superpower through three pillars: investing in the long-term needs of the AI ecosystem, supporting transition to an AI-enabled economy, and getting national and international AI governance right.

title: UNCTAD Technology and Innovation Report 2021
abstract: UNCTAD annual flagship report examining how frontier technologies including AI, robotics, and biotechnology may widen inequalities and create new ones, analyzing the $350B frontier technology market projected to reach $3.2T by 2025. Recommends national and international policy reforms to create equitable opportunity and address digital divides.

title: UNESCO Recommendation on AI Ethics
abstract: First global normative framework on AI ethics adopted by all 193 UNESCO member states, addressing values, principles, and policy actions across education, science, culture, and communication.

title: UNESCO Steering AI for Knowledge Societies (ROAM)
abstract: UNESCO framework applying the ROAM-X principles (Rights, Openness, Accessibility, Multi-stakeholder, Cross-cutting) to assess the impact of AI and advanced ICTs on knowledge societies, providing a structured approach for national-level evaluation of AI governance.

title: UNI Europa ICTS AI Position Paper 2021
abstract: UNI Europa ICTS position paper on how trade unions should engage with artificial intelligence in the world of work. Addresses three key topics: data collection and management, skills and training, and Just Transition. Emphasizes that AI must enhance the quality of services from an employee-centered perspective and advocates for workers' rights, human rights, and decent working standards. Available in English, French, German, Spanish and Swedish. Developed with input from Unionen's ombudsman for digital labor markets.

title: UNI Europa Insurance Sector Joint Declaration on AI 2021
organization: UNI Europa Finance / Insurance Europe / BIPAR / AMICE
abstract: Joint declaration between UNI Europa Finance and European insurance sector employer organizations (Insurance Europe, BIPAR, AMICE) on responsible and ethical deployment of AI. Signed after 18 months of negotiations in the presence of the European Commission. Establishes that AI should enhance rather than replace human abilities, with deployment following the 'human in control' principle. First sectoral social partner declaration to include a 'People Plan' for responsible AI throughout the value chain. Commits to transparency in AI data processing and protection against unfair bias and discrimination.

title: UN SG Our Common Agenda 2021
abstract: Secretary-General Guterres's vision for the future of multilateral cooperation addressing long-term challenges including managing AI and gene editing. Proposed the Global Digital Compact for shared principles on an open, free, and secure digital future for all, to be agreed at the Summit of the Future in 2024.

title: UNISON: Dignity at Work and the AI Revolution (TUC manifesto endorsement)
abstract: UNISON endorsed the TUC's 'Dignity at Work and the AI Revolution' manifesto (March 25, 2021), warning employment law is failing to keep pace with AI expansion. Key demands: legal union consultation rights before high-risk AI deployment; human review mechanisms for AI decisions; anti-discrimination safeguards in UK GDPR and Equality Act; right to disconnect. UNISON president stated reforms are urgent given increased COVID-era remote surveillance.

title: Vietnam National AI Strategy 2021-2030
abstract: Vietnam's national AI strategy through 2030, issued as Prime Ministerial Decision 127/QD-TTg, aiming to position Vietnam among the top four ASEAN countries and top 50 globally in AI R&D. Sets targets of 10 regional AI brands, three national innovation centers, and at least one top-20 ASEAN AI research institution.

title: WHO Ethics and Governance of AI 2021
abstract: First global report establishing a comprehensive ethical framework for AI in healthcare, identifying ethical challenges and risks, six consensus principles for public benefit, and governance recommendations for equitable, responsible, and safe clinical and public health AI, targeting developers, health ministers, and healthcare workers.

title: WHO Guidance on AI in Health
abstract: WHO guidance on AI ethics in health with six principles: protecting autonomy, promoting well-being, ensuring transparency, fostering responsibility, ensuring inclusiveness, and promoting responsive AI.

title: WRR: Mission AI — The New System Technology
abstract: WRR characterises artificial intelligence as a system technology with societal implications comparable to the steam engine or electricity. The 512-page report identifies five core tasks for embedding AI into Dutch society: demystification, contextualization, engagement of civil society, strategic regulation, and international positioning through AI diplomacy. It urges the government to create an inter-ministerial AI Coordination Centre.

title: ACM Principles for Responsible Algorithmic Systems
abstract: Joint statement by ACM's US and Europe Technology Policy Committees establishing nine principles for responsible algorithmic systems: legitimacy and competency, minimizing harm, security and privacy, transparency, interpretability, maintainability, contestability, accountability, and limiting environmental impacts.

title: ACM Responsible Algorithmic Systems 2022
abstract: Nine-principle statement by ACM's Global Technology Policy Council (joint US and Europe committees) for fair, accurate, and beneficial algorithmic decision-making: Legitimacy and Competency, Minimizing Harm, Security and Privacy, Transparency, Interpretability and Explainability, Maintainability, Contestability and Auditability, Accountability and Responsibility, and Limiting Environmental Impacts. Lead authors Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Jeanna Matthews.

title: Bug Bounties for Algorithmic Harms
abstract: By Josh Kenway, Camille François, Sasha Costanza-Chock, Inioluwa Deborah Raji, and Joy Buolamwini. Explores whether bug bounty programs from cybersecurity can be adapted to identify and redress algorithmic harms. Analyzes Twitter's bias bounty pilot and provides 25 design recommendations for vulnerability disclosure mechanisms for AI systems.

title: Who Audits the Auditors?
abstract: By Sasha Costanza-Chock, Inioluwa Deborah Raji, and Joy Buolamwini. The first comprehensive field scan of the AI audit ecosystem, cataloging 438 individuals and 189 organizations engaged in algorithmic auditing. Based on 152 survey responses and 10 expert interviews, identifies best practices, common methods, and barriers, and presents six policy recommendations targeting lawmakers, regulators, and auditors.

title: AMJA 18th Conference AI Resolutions
abstract: AMJA's 18th Annual Conference addressed contemporary fiqh issues related to technology across five major areas: general technology principles (including AI and virtual reality), worship-related technology (digital Quran, apps, holograms), financial transactions (cryptocurrency, fintech), dawah and education (online fatwas, AI-based learning), and social communication. Addressed whether AI can serve as a source for learning Quran and fiqh, and the Shariah ruling on AI, robots, and virtual worlds.

title: ANA Ethical Use of AI in Nursing Practice
abstract: ANA position statement providing ethical guidance for nurses on AI use in health care, covering methodological elements, justice, fairness, and equity, data and informatics, and regulatory principles. Affirms that AI augments but does not replace nursing judgment, critical thinking, or assessment skills.

title: Belgium: National Convergence Plan for AI
abstract: Approved by Belgium's Council of Ministers on 28 October 2022, this national plan contains 70 lines of action organised around nine strategic areas: trustworthy AI, cybersecurity, competitiveness, data economy, health, sustainable mobility, environment, lifelong learning, and citizen services. It integrates and aligns the AI strategies of Belgium's three federated regions (Flanders, Wallonia, and Brussels).

title: CCBE Guide on AI-Based Tools for Lawyers (AI4Lawyers)
abstract: The final deliverable of the EU-funded AI4Lawyers project (a collaboration between CCBE and the European Lawyers Foundation), this guide provides practical information for lawyers and small-sized law practices on integrating AI applications into everyday legal work. Developed following two earlier project reports on IT capabilities and natural language processing tools, the guide covers how to evaluate, adopt, and use AI-based tools while respecting professional obligations and the rights of clients within the European legal framework.

title: CFPB Circular 2022-03: Adverse Action Notifications and Complex Algorithms
abstract: CFPB interpretive circular clarifying that ECOA adverse action notification requirements fully apply to credit decisions made by complex AI/ML algorithms. Creditors cannot use algorithmic opacity as a reason for failing to provide specific denial reasons. Establishes CFPB enforcement position on explainability obligations for AI-based credit decisions.

title: China Science and Technology Ethics Governance Opinion 2022
abstract: The Opinions establish a comprehensive governance framework for science and technology ethics in China. They designate the National Science and Technology Ethics Committee to oversee ethics governance nationally, require universities, research institutions, hospitals, and enterprises working in sensitive fields (life science, medicine, AI) to set up ethics review committees, and call for ethics legislation during the 14th Five-Year Plan period.
