AI is explicitly listed as one of the priority sectors requiring ethical scrutiny.

title: China Algorithm Recommendation Rules 2022
abstract: Imposed transparency, user-rights, and content-moderation obligations on platforms using recommendation algorithms, including rights to opt out of personalized recommendations, requirements to disclose algorithmic logic, and a filing regime for large platforms — the world's most extensive set of rules for recommendation algorithms at the time.

title: China Algorithmic Recommendations Provisions
abstract: China's first legally binding regulation governing algorithmic recommendation systems used in internet information services, effective March 1, 2022. Requires service providers to register algorithms with public opinion or social mobilization attributes, prohibits manipulation of content rankings, and protects user rights to understand and opt out of personalized recommendations.

title: China Deep Synthesis Provisions
abstract: China's regulatory framework for deep synthesis (deepfake) technology used in internet-based information services, effective January 10, 2023. Requires prominent labeling of AI-generated content, user consent for biometric data use, and establishes compliance obligations for service providers across content review, data security, and personal information protection.

title: China Deep Synthesis Regulations 2022
abstract: Regulated deepfake and generative AI technologies ('deep synthesis'), requiring identity verification, visible labeling of synthetic content, training-data safeguards, security assessments, and recordkeeping — covering text, image, audio, video, and virtual scene generation.

title: EC AI Liability Directive Proposal 2022
abstract: European Commission proposal to adapt civil liability rules to AI, introducing a disclosure obligation for 'black box' AI evidence and a presumption of causation between fault and damage. Complemented the AI Act but was withdrawn from the 2025 Commission work programme.

title: EDRi Civil Society Ban Predictive Policing AI
abstract: Over 40 civil society organizations, coordinated by Fair Trials and EDRi, call on the EU to ban predictive and profiling AI systems in law enforcement and criminal justice within the Artificial Intelligence Act. The statement argues these technologies disproportionately harm marginalized communities, violate fundamental rights including the presumption of innocence, and perpetuate structural discrimination.

title: EDRi Civil Society React EP AI Act Draft
abstract: A coalition of 123 civil society organizations evaluates the IMCO-LIBE draft Report on the EU Artificial Intelligence Act and calls on Members of the European Parliament to support amendments that center people affected by AI systems, prevent harm, and ensure fundamental rights protection. The statement urges legislators to prioritize fundamental rights and public concerns in ongoing AI Act negotiations.

title: EP AIDA Resolution: AI in Digital Age 2022
abstract: Final recommendations of the AIDA Special Committee adopted 495 to 34, urging the EU to become a global standard-setter on AI. Covers AI in health, climate, pandemic response, and calls for human-centric, rights-compatible digital transition. Also calls for binding international rules on autonomous weapons.

title: EPSU-TUNED Agreement on Digitalisation for Central Government 2022
abstract: Sectoral social partner agreement for central government administrations on a human-centered approach to digitalisation and AI. Negotiated from January 2021 to January 2022 and signed October 6, 2022 by Ministers Petra de Sutter (Belgium) and Stanislas Guerini (France) for EUPAE, and Marco Ouwehand (FNV, Netherlands) for TUNED. Covers approximately 8 million workers and civil servants. Provides protections on telework, right to disconnect, training, health and safety, personal data protection, outsourcing, and mandates a human-in-command approach to artificial intelligence. EPSU requested the European Commission implement it through a Council decision.

title: ETUC Resolution on Algorithmic Systems at Work
abstract: Adopted by the ETUC Executive Committee on 6 December 2022, this resolution calls for dedicated EU legislation on algorithmic systems in the workplace. The ETUC argues that the EU AI Act is insufficient for protecting workers from the realities of algorithmic management and that a dedicated Directive is needed. Key demands include: upholding the 'human in control' principle; empowering trade unions and workers' representatives to influence AI implementation decisions; mandatory information and consultation rights before deployment of algorithmic systems; prohibition of automated dismissals; and transparency requirements for algorithmic decision-making affecting employment. The resolution was a direct precursor to ETUC's campaign for a Quality Jobs Directive.

title: Fairwork AI Principles
abstract: Developed through tripartite global stakeholder consultation with GPAI, the Fairwork AI Principles re-assess OECD AI principles with a focus on worker welfare. Organized around five core areas — Fair Pay, Fair Conditions, Fair Contracts, Fair Management, and Fair Representation — they establish minimum fairness standards for AI in the workplace. The principles specifically address algorithmic explainability, worker appeal mechanisms, freedom of association, and democratic governance through union recognition. They were applied to rate companies like Sama and Amazon in the 2023 and 2024/25 Fairwork AI Ratings reports.

title: Finland: AI Ethics Guidance for Public Administration
abstract: The Finnish Ministry of Finance sets out ethical guidelines for AI use across public administration. The guidance affirms five core principles: impartiality and independence, transparency and legitimacy, rights protection, democratic values advancement, and awareness of AI's societal impacts. It emphasises that public authorities bear full responsibility for AI systems they deploy and must not treat AI as an autonomous decision-maker.

title: A Forensic Without the Science: Face Recognition in Criminal Investigations
organization: Georgetown Center on Privacy and Technology
abstract: Examines the application of facial recognition technology in U.S. criminal investigations and exposes weaknesses in policies and procedures governing law enforcement use of the technology, raising concerns about the scientific validity and procedural safeguards around facial recognition as a forensic method.

title: GPAI Tokyo Declaration 2022
abstract: GPAI Ministerial Council Declaration from Tokyo reaffirming commitment to the OECD AI Principles based on human-centered values, opposing illegal and irresponsible AI use, welcoming India as incoming Chair for 2023, and advancing responsible AI governance among 24 member states and EU.

title: Hong Kong Ethical AI Framework
abstract: Hong Kong government's ethical framework for AI and big data analytics adoption, providing guiding principles, leading practices, and an AI Assessment template primarily for government bureaux and departments, with the guidance adapted for broader organizational use. Updated in August 2023 to incorporate generative AI considerations.

title: HRW An Agenda for Action: Killer Robots Treaty
abstract: Joint report by Human Rights Watch and the Harvard Law School International Human Rights Clinic. Examines why the Convention on Conventional Weapons (CCW) has failed to produce binding regulations on autonomous weapons systems despite nearly a decade of discussions, concluding that 'progress on a legally binding instrument on autonomous weapons systems is unlikely to occur under the CCW's auspices' due to consensus-based decision-making, lack of shared purpose, and absence of deadlines. Proposes that proponents of regulation pursue alternative treaty-making processes modeled on the Ottawa Process (Mine Ban Treaty, 1997), Oslo Process (Convention on Cluster Munitions, 2008), and UN General Assembly approach (Treaty on Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons, 2017). Recommends an independent or UNGA-initiated negotiating process featuring majority-vote decisions, ambitious timelines, and broad civil society participation. HRW co-founded the Stop Killer Robots campaign comprising 270+ NGOs across 70 countries.

title: Japan AI Strategy 2022
abstract: Updated national AI strategy shifting focus from capability-building to the deployment and social implementation of AI systems. Emphasizes practical application of AI in diverse sectors and updates Japan's human-centric approach in line with generative AI developments and international AI governance discussions at the G7 and OECD.

title: Jordan AI Strategy and Implementation Plan
abstract: Launched in November 2022 at the 9th MENA ICT Forum, this five-year strategy and implementation roadmap contains 68 targeted projects across five strategic pillars: capacity building and national AI skills, scientific research and development, investment and entrepreneurship, legislative and regulatory environment, and AI in government services. Specific targets include a 30% increase in AI researchers, 50 new startups, and training 15,000 individuals by 2027.

title: State of AI Ethics Report Vol. 6
abstract: The sixth volume of MAIEI's flagship annual series examines contemporary AI ethics issues including algorithmic bias, privacy, EU/US/UNESCO regulatory developments, gender representation in generative AI, and how organizations can build AI ethics teams. Introduces new sections on industry ecosystem trends and AI design governance across multiple languages.

title: METI AI Governance Guideline Ver.1.1 2022
abstract: The Governance Guidelines Ver.1.1 (AI原則実践のためのガバナンス・ガイドライン) were prepared by METI's Expert Group on How AI Principles Should be Implemented. They define action targets spanning strategy, design, development, deployment, and monitoring phases of AI systems, aligned with the Cabinet Office's Social Principles of Human-Centric AI (2019). The guidelines include a self-assessment checklist and hypothetical enterprise scenarios for gap analysis. Ver.1.1 (January 2022) updated Ver.1.0 (July 2021) based on public comments and international developments. The document was later integrated into the joint MIC+METI AI Guidelines for Business Ver.1.0 (April 2024).

title: Lawyers' Committee Biometric Technology Comments to OSTP
organization: Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
abstract: Response to OSTP's Request for Information on biometric technologies, framed through Title VI civil rights analysis. The Lawyers' Committee argues facial recognition is 'racially discriminatory' and disproportionately harms people of color, that it violates federal nondiscrimination obligations, and that the technologies 'exacerbate injustices in policing.' Urges Congress to enact a nationwide ban on law enforcement use of facial recognition technology. Part of the Digital Justice Initiative's biometric advocacy work.

title: Shanghai AI Industry Promotion Regulations 2022
abstract: The Regulations govern AI scientific and technological innovation, business development, application promotion, and sector governance within Shanghai. They introduce a graded management system distinguishing risk levels of AI applications, allow sandbox piloting of technologies meeting international standards before national regulation, and include provisions ensuring AI ethics compliance and protection of individual rights. Key sectors targeted include autonomous vehicles, urban governance, and healthcare.

title: Shenzhen SEZ AI Industry Promotion Regulations 2022
abstract: The Regulations are the first dedicated AI industry legislation in China, predating national-level AI law. Seven chapters cover: basic research and technology development, industrial infrastructure, application expansion, promotion and assurance, governance principles and measures. A unique feature is the statutory requirement for a government-level AI Ethics Committee, a requirement internationally uncommon outside enterprise self-governance. Low-risk AI products may proceed under international standards before domestic regulation is finalized.

title: NASEM: Fostering Responsible Computing Research
abstract: NASEM Consensus Study Report presenting best practices for funding agencies, academic organizations, and individual researchers to formulate and conduct computing research — including AI — in a responsible manner. Examines ethical issues in computing research and ways to promote responsible practices through education and training. Outlines recommendations for the computing research community to ensure ethical and societal impacts are considered from the start of research projects. Draws on expertise across computer science, information science, social sciences, philosophy, and law.

title: NASSCOM Responsible AI Resource Kit
abstract: A joint industry self-regulation initiative comprising openly accessible tools and guidance for AI practitioners, covering ethical principles, assessment frameworks, and practical implementation guidance. The kit is designed as an evolving reference that progressively builds on emerging research and best practices. It is hosted through the Responsible AI Hub launched at NASSCOM's October 2022 event and is also accessible via indiaai.gov.in.

title: OHCHR SR Report: AI and Disability Rights
abstract: Thematic study by Special Rapporteur Gerard Quinn examining how AI systems interact with the rights of persons with disabilities, covering both risks and opportunities. Presented to the Human Rights Council at its 49th session in March 2022.

title: ORG Analysis UK Data Protection Bill AI
abstract: Open Rights Group analyses the UK Data Protection and Digital Information Bill, specifically criticizing provisions that would weaken Article 22 GDPR protections against solely automated decision-making with significant effects. The analysis argues the Bill exposes individuals to AI-driven automated decisions against their will and shifts the burden of scrutiny from developers to affected individuals, with particularly serious implications for algorithmic governance.

title: ORG Response to UK AI Regulation Paper
abstract: Open Rights Group critiques the UK government's light-touch AI regulation approach, arguing it weakens existing GDPR protections on automated decision-making, warns against sector-specific regulation citing US privacy law failures, and contends robust cross-sectoral frameworks actually support innovation. The response argues the UK's 'pro-innovation' framing effectively removes meaningful safeguards against algorithmic harm.

title: Samsung AI Ethics Principles
abstract: Samsung Electronics' AI Ethics Principles establish three core pillars — Fairness, Transparency, and Accountability — backed by an AI Ethics Council and mandatory inspection processes. Implementation guidelines cover data documentation, model evaluation procedures, and employee training programs to operationalize the principles across all AI products.

title: Saudi Arabia AI Ethics Principles v1.0
abstract: SDAIA's first operational ethical framework for AI, structured around seven principles: fairness, privacy and security, humanity, social and environmental benefits, reliability and safety, transparency and explainability, and accountability and responsibility. Non-binding guidelines designed to help public and private sector entities incorporate ethics throughout the AI system development lifecycle; a 2025 update (Version 2.0) has since been published.

title: NHRC Korea AI Human Rights Guidelines
abstract: 국가인권위원회가 인공지능 개발·활용 과정의 인권침해·차별 방지를 위해 마련한 가이드라인으로, 국무총리와 관련 부처 장관에게 권고. 인간의 존엄성·자율성·다양성 보장, 투명성과 설명 의무, 자기결정권, 차별금지, 인공지능 인권영향평가 실시, 위험도 등급별 법·제도 마련 등을 주요 내용으로 한다.

title: Tunisia National AI Strategy 2021-2025
abstract: A national AI roadmap (2021-2025) focusing on AI skills development, infrastructure (cloud and high-performance computing), data policies, and networking activities, developed through a memorandum of understanding signed February 19, 2022 by four ministries.

title: Transparency International: The Corruption Risks of Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Transparency International working paper introducing the concept of 'corrupt AI' — the intentional abuse of AI systems by those in positions of public or private power for personal gain. The paper argues that while AI is increasingly promoted as an anti-corruption tool, its potential as a new enabler of corruption has received insufficient attention. Power in the digital age concentrates with those controlling algorithms, typically tech companies and governments, creating corruption risks when these actors abuse this power for private gain through designing, manipulating, or deploying AI systems. Recommends: developing regulatory frameworks mandating ethical AI design and model audits; ensuring transparent code, data, and interoperability; and training AI developers in ethics and anti-corruption through codes of conduct.

title: Tunisia National AI Strategy
abstract: Tunisia's National AI Strategy, formally agreed through a ministerial memorandum of understanding signed February 19, 2022 and delivered in June 2022, was developed by The Future Society in partnership with four ministries and GIZ Digital Transformation Center Tunisia, establishing priority sectors (health, education, environment, transport), a legislative framework for personal data, and digital infrastructure goals including cloud and high-performance computing.

title: Thailand National AI Strategy 2022-2027
abstract: Thailand's six-year national AI strategy and action plan approved by the Prime Minister's Cabinet in July 2022, comprising five core strategies and 15 work plans to develop an AI-driven economy. Targets development of 30,000+ AI talents within six years, with emphasis on ethics/law, infrastructure, human capability, and innovation across economic and social domains.

title: Drivers, Doctors, Friends
abstract: Briefing paper by Hannah Waite and Nick Spencer examining whether robots and AI will or should replace humans in various roles. Based on a YouGov survey of 5,000+ UK adults and 100+ in-depth interviews. Finds that almost a third of under-30s would consider an AI companion (vs 10% of over-60s). Joint research between Theos and the Faraday Institute, funded by the Templeton Religion Trust.

title: Spiritual Silicon
abstract: Briefing paper by Hannah Waite and Nick Spencer exploring whether robots could one day have souls or rights. Based on a YouGov survey of 5,000+ UK adults and over 100 in-depth interviews with leading scientific and religious thinkers. Finds that 75% of people are not convinced robots are 'just like us' and do not believe they have souls, though 27% of under-30s think robots will one day have human rights. Joint research between Theos and the Faraday Institute, funded by the Templeton Religion Trust.

title: UN System Chief Executives AI Principles
abstract: Principles for ethical AI use adopted across all UN agencies, covering human rights protection, impartiality, accountability, transparency, safety, privacy, and the need to uphold UN values in AI applications.

title: UCU Congress Motion on AI and Datafication
abstract: Congress motion noting increased technology use in post-16 education during the pandemic and expanded employer data collection on members. Notes that Data Protection Impact Assessments should be carried out when implementing new systems using workers' personal data but unions are not consulted. Believes AI can be useful but must not infringe on workers' rights or discriminate against individuals and must be fully accessible. Resolves to: (a) set up an expert group on AI and datafication to identify threats and opportunities and make recommendations on UCU AI policy; (b) include in national claims an element on agreeing an ethical AI policy with employers establishing a consistent approach across sectors; (c) establish the role of Data Representative in each branch.

title: UNESCO Caribbean AI Policy Roadmap 2022
abstract: A four-pillar AI policy roadmap tailored to the Caribbean's unique opportunities and risks, developed by UNESCO and the Caribbean Telecommunications Union. Based on the global UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (adopted by 193 member states), the roadmap offers frameworks for policy development and regional collaboration to enable ethical, inclusive AI governance across Caribbean states.

title: UNSCEB Principles for Ethical Use of AI in UN System
abstract: Ten principles endorsed by the UN Chief Executives Board (CEB) governing the ethical use of AI across all UN system entities, grounded in the UNESCO 2021 Recommendation. Covers harm prevention, fairness, privacy, transparency, and accountability throughout the AI system lifecycle.

title: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights
abstract: A non-binding framework articulating five principles and protections — safe and effective systems, algorithmic discrimination protections, data privacy, notice and explanation, and human alternatives — to guide the design, development, and deployment of automated systems.

title: Worker Info Exchange — Managed by Bots Report
abstract: Published by Worker Info Exchange, this report documents harms caused by gig platforms' algorithmic workforce management systems. Based on over 500 subject access requests on behalf of workers at Amazon Flex, Bolt, Deliveroo, Free Now, Just Eat, Ola, and Uber, it finds woefully inadequate transparency of algorithmic management systems, questionable predictive tools used to predict 'fraudulent behaviour' of workers, and disproportionate surveillance resulting in unaccountable expansion of law enforcement access to personal data. The report was co-authored by researcher Cansu Safak and former Uber driver James Farrer.

title: AAAI Code of Ethics
abstract: AAAI Code of Ethics adapted from the ACM Code of Ethics, designed to inspire and guide ethical conduct of all AI professionals including practitioners, instructors, students, and AI users. Emphasizes public good as primary consideration, privacy responsibilities, and heightened leadership accountability.

title: AAFP Ethical Application of AI in Family Medicine
abstract: AAFP policy stating that the family medicine experience is rooted in a deeply personal physician-patient interaction supported by technology. As AI expands the care triad, the patient-physician relationship must be preserved and ideally enhanced. AI/ML solutions must adhere to principles including transparency around training data and model decisions, processes to identify and mitigate algorithmic bias, training data diversity, data privacy with explicit consent, user-centered design, rigorous evaluation comparable to other clinical tools, and trustworthy systems design. AAFP supports risk-based liability frameworks and requires companies providing AI solutions to disclose how their models make predictions.

title: ABA Resolution 604 — AI Accountability Guidelines
abstract: ABA guidelines urging organizations that design, develop, deploy, and use AI to ensure human authority and oversight, accountability for consequences, and transparency and traceability. Calls on Congress, federal agencies, and state legislatures to incorporate these guidelines into AI laws and standards.

title: ABC Recomendações sobre IA no Brasil 2023
abstract: Documento de recomendações da Academia Brasileira de Ciências elaborado por grupo de trabalho de 16 pesquisadores (GT-IA), coordenado pelo Acadêmico Virgilio Almeida (UFMG/Harvard). Mapeia os desafios para o desenvolvimento de IAs nacionais e aponta caminhos para seu uso responsável em todos os setores da sociedade, abrangendo infraestrutura, formação profissional, retenção de talentos e soberania tecnológica.

title: ACLU Letter on Centering Equity in AI Executive Order
abstract: Formal letter from the ACLU addressed to Vice President Harris advocating for specific principles — equity, safety, and non-discrimination — to be incorporated into an executive branch AI policy order. Available as PDF download, representing the ACLU's official position communication to government leadership ahead of the Biden administration's AI executive order.

title: ACLU Statement on Biden's AI Executive Order
abstract: The ACLU's formal institutional response to President Biden's October 2023 Executive Order on AI. The statement welcomes requirements for agencies to protect civil rights in governmental AI use, but criticizes the order for exempting national security AI applications and providing insufficient protection from law enforcement uses of facial recognition and surveillance AI. Senior staff Cody Venzke and ReNika Moore warn the administration 'essentially kicks the can down the road' on law enforcement AI accountability.

title: ACM Principles for Generative AI Technologies
abstract: Eight principles for generative AI technologies addressing intellectual property, future of work, human safety, transparency, auditability, personal data control, and risk management. Four principles are specific to generative AI; four are adapted from the 2022 Responsible Algorithmic Systems statement.

title: ACM TechBrief: Generative Artificial Intelligence
abstract: A technical policy brief examining the societal risks of rapidly commercialized generative AI technologies, including misinformation, cyberattacks, and environmental harms. Recommends end-to-end governance addressing risks by design across the full development-to-deployment lifecycle, comprehensive regulation of foundation models and their supply chains, and legal liability for irresponsible practices. Co-authored by David Leslie (Alan Turing Institute) and Francesca Rossi (IBM AI Ethics). NOTE: This is distinct from the 2023 ACM Principles for Generative AI (STMT-0329), which is already in vault.

title: ACTRA Submission on AI and Canadian Performers
abstract: Formal 11-page submission dated September 8, 2023 to Canada's Standing Committee on Industry and Technology's Artificial Intelligence and Data Act consultation. Core principles: consent, compensation, and control for performers. Key demands: new AI legislation must allow performers to consent to and control the use of their voice, image, or likeness by AI; compensation must be provided for such use; AI-generated material should never be considered Canadian content. 98% of surveyed members concerned about misuse of their name/image/likeness by AI; 93% concerned AI will eventually replace human actors.

title: ADL Comments to PCAST on Generative AI
abstract: Joint formal submission from the ADL and the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights to the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST). Recommends that legislators update laws concerning defamation, consumer protection, election interference, and digital abuse. Calls on industry actors to implement voluntary safeguards before harms occur. Emphasizes that the emergence of generative AI 'lends unprecedented urgency to online safety concerns' as the technology outpaces existing regulations.

title: ADL Submission on NTIA AI Accountability Policy
abstract: ADL's formal comment to the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA) on their AI accountability policy rulemaking. Part of ADL's structured engagement with federal AI governance, focusing on hate speech, antisemitism, and extremism risks of AI systems. The submission addresses accountability frameworks for AI developers and platforms in relation to harmful content generation and moderation.

title: ADL Submission on OMB Federal AI Governance
abstract: ADL's formal comment to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) on their Federal AI Governance and Innovation request for comment. Addresses how federal agencies should govern AI procurement and deployment with attention to antisemitism, extremism, and hate speech risks in AI systems used by government.

title: AI Faith & Civil Society Commission Open Letter to UK Government
abstract: Open letter signed by 20 faith and civil society leaders addressed to Secretary of State Michelle Donelan ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Highlights six priorities: accountability, accessibility of progress, ethical guidelines, short-term harm prevention, diverse voices, and closing the AI literacy gap. Chaired by Rt Hon Sajid Javid MP at inaugural high-level meeting. Commissioners include Dr Omar Al Derei, Shaykh Abdullah Bin Bayyah, Father Paolo Benanti (Pontifical Academy for Life), Rev Dr Kathryn Pritchard (Lambeth Palace), and Rabbi Dr Harris Bor. Advocates five core values: Truth, Transparency, Justice, Trust, and Accessibility.

title: AFN Brief on Bill C-27 and First Nations Rights
abstract: Formal brief submitted by the Assembly of First Nations to the Canadian Parliamentary Standing Committee on Industry and Technology regarding Bill C-27 (Digital Charter Implementation Act, 2022). AFN argues that the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) and the Voluntary Code of Conduct Relating to Advanced Generative AI Systems do little to assure First Nations that their individual and collective rights will be respected. The brief asserts First Nations hold an inherent right to data sovereignty essential to self-determination and self-government, and criticizes the lack of Nation-to-Nation consultation in developing the legislation. AFN warns that Bill C-27's Consumer Privacy Protection Act does not account for collective rights harm from de-identification and aggregation of First Nations data, and that the legislation infringes First Nations rights to self-government. Calls for amendments including non-derogation clauses, duty to consult, and constraints on government authority to develop and use AI affecting First Nations.

title: AFT Resolution on Social Media, AI and Generative AI
abstract: Adopted by the AFT Executive Council on June 1, 2023 in Washington, D.C. The resolution calls for social media, AI, and machine learning models to be developed and employed ethically, with developers held accountable for real-world harms. It urges governments to implement strict regulations protecting privacy and security, demands equity and social accountability from AI and social media platforms, and calls for worker protections against AI-driven job displacement across education, healthcare, and public services. The AFT specifically opposes using AI to undermine collective bargaining and calls for transparency in technology deployment. The resolution acknowledges both the transformative potential and significant risks of rapid AI advances including ChatGPT, highlighting concerns about misinformation, bias, and privacy violations.

title: Authors Guild Policy Proposals on Generative AI
abstract: Published May 2023, setting out the Authors Guild's formal policy proposals on generative AI. Key demands: Consent (require permission for use of writers' works in AI training), Compensation (ensure authors are paid when works are used), Transparency (require AI developers to disclose training data), Use in outputs (require permission when works/names/identities appear in outputs), and Labeling (require AI-generated works to be labeled). Advocates for collective licensing as the mechanism for AI training data. The Guild represents over 13,000 professional authors and has since pursued class-action litigation against OpenAI, Microsoft, and Anthropic.

title: Amazon Responsible AI Principles
abstract: Amazon's responsible AI framework grounds eight priorities — fairness, explainability, privacy and security, safety, controllability, veracity and robustness, governance, and transparency — in the leadership principle that 'success and scale bring broad responsibility.' The AWS Responsible AI Policy (published January 2025) provides binding terms for use of AWS AI/ML services.

title: AI Now 2023 Landscape Report: Confronting Tech Power
abstract: The 2023 AI Now Landscape Report frames the central challenge of AI as one of power concentration. Authored by Amba Kak and Sarah Myers West, the report provides strategic analysis of AI governance challenges and presents structural policy recommendations for limiting tech company power, identifies ineffective popular policy approaches, and recognizes nonregulatory interventions including worker organizing and collective action. The objective is ensuring technology serves public rather than corporate interests.

title: ANPD NT 16/2023 — Analysis of PL 2338/2023
abstract: Segunda análise técnica da ANPD sobre o PL 2338/2023, propondo modelo institucional em quatro instâncias complementares com a ANPD como autoridade central de regulação de IA, criação de um Fórum de Órgãos Reguladores Setoriais, sete interseções entre o PL e a LGPD, e proposta de alterações legislativas aos artigos 32-35 sobre supervisão e fiscalização.

title: APEC Golden Gate Declaration (2023)
abstract: Adopted at the San Francisco APEC Summit on 17 November 2023 under US presidency, the Golden Gate Declaration addresses digital economy governance including emerging technologies, encouraging implementation of the APEC Internet and Digital Economy Roadmap and calling for continued international discussion on digital technology governance including AI.

title: ASA Statement on Role of Statistics in Data Science and AI
abstract: An ASA board statement adopted August 4, 2023, revising and expanding the 2015 ASA Statement on the Role of Statistics in Data Science to explicitly incorporate artificial intelligence. The statement articulates the essential contributions of statisticians to AI and data science endeavors, emphasizing that successful AI requires statistical expertise in uncertainty quantification, sampling design, and causal inference, and that data science and AI require multifaceted collaboration. Notes that the 'big tent of statistics has grown massively bigger' and that statisticians must adapt their roles to this evolving landscape.

title: AlgorithmWatch GPAI Policy Brief
abstract: AlgorithmWatch and the AI, Media, and Democracy Lab of the University of Amsterdam publish key policy proposals for governing General Purpose AI (GPAI) and Generative AI (GenAI) for final EU AI Act negotiations. The brief identifies five critical governance areas: clarifying definitions, addressing complexity and power imbalances, preventing accountability gaps along the value chain, ensuring democratic oversight, and complementing other regulatory frameworks.

title: AlgorithmWatch Police Migration AI Fundamental Rights
abstract: AlgorithmWatch calls for EU regulation of AI systems deployed by law enforcement, migration control, and national security authorities, arguing such systems disproportionately target marginalized communities and enable mass surveillance. The statement demands legal prohibitions on biometric surveillance and predictive policing, public transparency requirements, and comprehensive regulation without national security exemptions.

title: Amnesty International AI and Human Rights
abstract: Amnesty International's detailed recommendations to the Council of Europe on a proposed binding framework convention on AI, calling for strong human rights protections, mandatory prohibitions on surveillance AI, and effective remedies. Critiques weak provisions in drafts and advocates for a precautionary, rights-first approach to AI governance.

title: Anthropic Responsible Scaling Policy
abstract: Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy establishes a tiered AI Safety Level (ASL) system (ASL-1 through ASL-4+) modeled on biosafety standards, defining technical and organizational safety requirements that must be met before scaling to more capable models. The policy aims to create a 'race to the top' where safety innovations are prerequisites for capability scaling.

title: Australia Safe and Responsible AI Discussion Paper
abstract: This discussion paper sought public views on whether Australia had the right governance arrangements in place to support safe and responsible development and use of AI, consulting on potential regulatory and non-regulatory measures. The consultation received over 500 submissions and informed an interim government response published in January 2024, which proposed mandatory guardrails for high-risk AI applications.

title: Beijing General AI Innovation Development Measures 2023
abstract: The 21 measures aim to make Beijing a leading hub for AGI innovation through five pillars: computing power partnerships, data infrastructure and training datasets, large model research and key technology breakthroughs, application scenario demonstrations (autonomous driving, urban governance), and risk prevention and regulatory frameworks. Includes provisions for ethical review and safety governance of large models.

title: BRICS Johannesburg II Declaration AI (2023)
abstract: At the 15th BRICS Summit, leaders for the first time formally committed to launching a BRICS AI Study Group to monitor and foster responsible AI development, emphasising AI's role in economic growth and the need for inclusive multilateral governance through UN mechanisms.

title: Brazil AI Framework Bill PL 2338/2023
abstract: Comprehensive risk-based legislation governing the development, promotion, and ethical use of artificial intelligence in Brazil, centering on human dignity and fundamental rights. Approved by the Federal Senate in December 2024 with 244 amendments, the bill establishes the National AI Regulation and Governance System (SIA) coordinated by the ANPD and sets out obligations for developers, operators, and deployers of AI systems.

title: Brazil ANPD Technical Note on AI Bill
abstract: Brazil's data protection authority published Technical Note 16/2023 analyzing the national AI regulation bill (PL 2338/2023), proposing a centralized institutional model with ANPD as the central regulatory body, and drawing intersections between the bill and the LGPD (data protection law). The ANPD also published a preliminary study on Generative AI concluding it must be developed from an 'ethical, legal, and socio-technical' perspective.

title: Brazilian Union Centrals Joint Note on AI Regulation (PL 2338/2023)
abstract: Nota pública conjunta de oito centrais sindicais brasileiras em apoio ao PL 2338/2023 sobre regulamentação da inteligência artificial. Defendem que os sistemas de IA devem estar subordinados a princípios de segurança, transparência, rastreabilidade, não discriminação e supervisão humana. Exigem participação humana nas decisões de IA, avaliações de impacto algorítmico, proibição de sistemas que explorem populações vulneráveis e negociação coletiva sobre implementação de IA no trabalho.

title: Brennan Center: AI and Election Security
abstract: Examines how generative AI affects election infrastructure security and public confidence in elections. Identifies four primary threat vectors: enhanced phishing attacks, advanced malware, deepfakes and website impersonation, and weaponized FOIA requests. Recommends strengthening cybersecurity infrastructure, providing technical support to under-resourced local jurisdictions, authenticating election communications, training election workers, and countering false narratives. Authored by Lawrence Norden and Gowri Ramachandran.

title: Brennan Center: Safeguards for AI in Election Administration
abstract: Examines how AI is currently deployed in election administration and recommends safeguards for its expanded use. Presents the 'AI CPR' framework: Choose systems carefully (prioritizing simplicity and human oversight), Plan thoughtfully (including staff training and contingency procedures), and Review thoroughly (assessing performance and ensuring human involvement in consequential decisions). Addresses risks including algorithmic bias, hallucinations in generative systems, and potential voter disenfranchisement. Authors: Edgardo Cortés, Lawrence Norden, Heather Frase, Mia Hoffmann.

title: Brennan Center: Senate AI Forum Statement
abstract: Formal institutional statement submitted to the U.S. Senate AI Insight Forum. Addresses AI's potential impact on elections and democracy. Submitted by Lawrence Norden, Senior Director of the Elections & Government Program, on behalf of the Brennan Center for Justice.

title: China CAC Facial Recognition Draft Measures 2023
abstract: The draft measures require that facial recognition only be used where other non-biometric identification methods are insufficient, mandate consent from individuals, prohibit coercive use, require public signage, forbid installation in hotel rooms, bathrooms, and changing rooms, and require CAC registration for large-scale face databases. The 2023 draft was superseded by a finalized version approved September 30, 2024 (effective June 1, 2025).

title: CAIS Statement on AI Risk 2023
abstract: One-sentence open statement — 'Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority alongside other societal-scale risks such as pandemics and nuclear war' — signed by prominent AI scientists and public figures including Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, Demis Hassabis, Sam Altman, and Dario Amodei. Represents historic expert consensus on existential AI risk.

title: CAIS Statement on AI Risk
abstract: A one-sentence statement — 'Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority alongside other societal-scale risks such as pandemics and nuclear war' — signed by a historic coalition including Turing Award winners Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio, and AI lab CEOs Sam Altman (OpenAI), Demis Hassabis (Google DeepMind), and Dario Amodei (Anthropic).

title: CBA Framework for AI: Submission on Council of Europe AI Treaty
abstract: The Canadian Bar Association's submission on international negotiations for a Council of Europe treaty on artificial intelligence, human rights, and the rule of law. Developed jointly by CBA sections on Privacy and Access Law, Immigration Law, and Ethics and Professional Responsibility, the submission supports the treaty approach and addresses application to public and private sectors, protection of vulnerable populations including migrants, algorithmic transparency, facial recognition and biometric surveillance risks, deep fake technology, civil and criminal liability frameworks, and digital profiling. The CBA advocates for regulatory frameworks that harness AI's potential while minimizing risks.

title: CCBE Statement on AI in the Justice System and Law Enforcement
abstract: The CCBE's formal statement on the use of AI in the justice system and law enforcement acknowledges the potential benefits of AI tools while highlighting the need for careful consideration of risks to human rights and the rule of law. The statement calls for AI use to fully respect fundamental rights including privacy, freedom of expression, non-discrimination, and the right to a fair trial. It calls for a ban on predictive and profiling AI tools in law enforcement and criminal justice, asserts that judicial decisions must remain human-driven, and demands that AI systems not undermine lawyer-client confidentiality. The CCBE calls for defined requirements including transparency, non-discrimination, and protection of the presumption of innocence before any AI tools are deployed in justice systems.

title: CCNE-CNPEN Joint Opinion on Medical AI Diagnosis
abstract: A joint opinion by France's national bioethics committee (CCNE) and its digital ethics committee (CNPEN) examining ethical issues in AI-assisted medical diagnosis, addressing the Prime Minister's 2019 letter of mission. The opinion covers accountability for AI diagnostic decisions, the role of human clinicians, patient privacy, algorithmic transparency, and governance of AI tools in health systems.

title: Church of England Synod Motion on AI and Work
abstract: The Church of England General Synod approved a motion affirming the 'dignity and value of purposeful work' in the face of AI-driven labor market transformation, emphasizing work as central to human flourishing. The Archbishop of Canterbury included an amendment endorsing the Rome Call for AI Ethics, marking the Church of England's formal commitment to an ethical AI framework.

title: CDBIO Report on AI and Doctor-Patient Relationship
abstract: The CDBIO (formerly DH-BIO/Committee on Bioethics) formal report examining the application of AI in healthcare and its impact on the patient-doctor relationship, with reference to the European Convention on Human Rights and Biomedicine ('Oviedo Convention'). Prepared by Brent Mittelstadt (Oxford Internet Institute). Investigates six themes: inequality in access to high quality healthcare, transparency, social bias in AI systems, dilution of the patient's account of well-being, automation bias and displaced liability, and the right to privacy. Document CDBIO(2023)7REV3.

title: Consumer Federation of America: Next Consumer Protection Front — AI
abstract: Policy analysis by CFA Senior Fellow and Privacy Advocate Susan Grant examining emerging consumer protection challenges posed by AI and generative AI. Identifies risks including voice impersonation, political manipulation, and imposter scams. Argues current regulatory frameworks are insufficient and calls for collaborative exploration of legislative approaches involving the FTC, consumer organizations, and relevant agencies. Urges consumer advocates, Congress, regulatory agencies, and businesses to prepare for issues arising from AI's expanding applications.

title: CFDT AI Positions — National Bureau Validation and Action Toolkit
abstract: Bureau national CFDT valide positions IA (décembre 2023). Crée groupe de travail confédéral et boîte à outils militante. Revendique accord-cadre négocié IA dans secteur public, registre outils IA dans instances paritaires (CSA), assurance transition emploi pour travailleurs exposés.

title: CISAC Seven Principles on AI
abstract: Open letter issued July 20, 2023 by CISAC and 12 other international creator/performer organizations representing over 6 million artists globally. Seven principles for policymakers: (1) creators' and performers' rights must be upheld when exploited by AI; (2) licensing solutions must be available for AI exploitation of copyright works; (3) text/data mining exceptions must not permit unauthorized exploitation; (4) credit attribution for creators whose works are exploited by AI; (5) transparency obligations for disclosure of creative works used and identification of AI-generated content; (6) legal responsibility and accountability for AI companies; (7) fully autonomous AI-generated works cannot benefit from same protection as human-created works. Co-signed by AEPO-ARTIS, ALCAM, AMA, APMA, BIEM, CIAGP, CIAM, ECSA, EVA, IMPF, MCNA, SCAPR.

title: Chief Rabbinate of Israel: Abrahamic Commitment to Rome Call for AI Ethics
abstract: Chief Rabbi Eliezer Simha Weisz, member of the Council of the Chief Rabbinate of Israel, signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at a Vatican ceremony on January 10, 2023, alongside representatives of Christianity and Islam. The Rome Call, originally established in February 2020, urges governments, technology companies, and organizations to advance an ethical approach to AI that honors human dignity. The Chief Rabbinate's Commission for Interfaith Relations co-promoted the event. Rabbi Weisz stated that 'Judaism extols the wisdom of humanity, created in the Divine Image of God manifest generally in human innovation and in Artificial Intelligence in particular.' This represents the first formal Jewish institutional commitment to a major international AI ethics framework.

title: Commonwealth AI Consortium Launch (2023)
abstract: Launched in June 2023 following the 2022 Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM) in Rwanda, the CAIC brings together global tech firms (NVIDIA, Microsoft, DeepMind, HP), universities (UC Berkeley), and non-profits to bridge AI capacity gaps in the 56 Commonwealth member states, with a focus on small states and youth empowerment.

title: CWA AI Principles for Bargaining and Policy
abstract: Developed by the CWA Committee on Artificial Intelligence and presented to the CWA Executive Board, this report establishes three core principles—Accountability, Proactive Bargaining, and Early and Meaningful Worker Voice—to guide union bargaining and public policy engagement on AI in the workplace. It directs CWA bargaining committees to negotiate contract language protecting members against invasive surveillance, automated employment decisions, work intensification, and job reduction, while emphasizing that the goal is not to stop technology but to ensure workers win their fair share of economic gains.

title: China Generative AI Interim Measures 2023
abstract: The first regulation on generative AI in China, applying to services generating text, images, audio, or video for the public in mainland China, requiring content compliance, training data quality, security assessments, and prohibiting content that endangers national security or social order.

title: China Generative AI Interim Measures
abstract: China's first regulation specifically governing generative AI services, effective August 15, 2023. Establishes content standards, data handling requirements, user protections, and training data obligations for providers of generative AI products to Chinese users, requiring pre-launch security assessments for services with public opinion or social mobilization attributes.

title: Common Cause FEC Comment on Deceptive AI in Campaign Ads
abstract: Formal comment co-filed by Common Cause and the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights with the Federal Election Commission, supporting Public Citizen's petition for rulemaking to amend the 'fraudulent misrepresentation' regulation to include deliberately false AI-generated content in campaign communications. Argues AI deepfakes pose an 'exponential threat to democracy,' disproportionately harm Black and brown communities already targeted by voter suppression, and require federal FEC action beyond state-level regulation. Cites examples of AI misuse in the Chicago mayoral race and Republican primary.

title: Cru Digital Academy AI Guidance
abstract: Organizational guidance on AI published through Cru's Digital Academy as part of 'The Digital Download' series. Features perspectives from four Cru leaders including Cheryl Boyd (Global VP of Digital Strategies) and Andre Echevarria (US Digital Strategies leader). Outlines three core pillars: pray for wisdom, seek understanding, look at the fruit. Includes two formal guidance documents: one for appropriate AI uses in ministry and an ethical framework with six principles. States AI is 'neither good nor bad' but use must promote deeper relationships rather than isolation. Policy mandates that fully automated virtual content is never used. Positions AI as an 'advanced intern' requiring human oversight.

title: Deutscher Ethikrat: Humans and Machines (AI) 2023
abstract: The German Ethics Council (Deutscher Ethikrat) examines philosophical concepts including intelligence, reason, action, and responsibility in relation to AI. Its central question asks whether AI expands or diminishes human authorship and conditions for responsible conduct, analysed across four societal domains: medicine, school education, public communication and opinion formation, and public administration.

title: Church of Norway AI Guidance
abstract: Advisory guidance published December 2023 (last updated January 2026) through the Church of Norway's Ressursbanken. Provides practical advice for church employees on AI use, recommending internal tools like Microsoft Copilot Chat (via @kirken.no accounts) for document summarization, email drafting, and meeting notes. Warns against external chatbots like ChatGPT due to data security risks. Requires transparency about AI involvement and proper attribution of AI-generated images. Includes a winter 2025/2026 pilot project for Copilot 365.

title: DSCI AI Privacy Handbook
abstract: Targeted at early-stage AI developers and startups in India, this handbook guides practitioners in applying ethical and privacy-by-design principles from the conception and design stage through deployment. It encourages interdisciplinary thinking beyond algorithmic accuracy to include social impact assessment, accountability structures, and transparency mechanisms. The handbook aligns with India's emerging data protection regulatory landscape (DPDP Act) and globally recognized ethical frameworks. Produced through a collaboration between DSCI (NASSCOM-promoted industry body), Germany's GIZ (development agency), Digital India Foundation, and Koan Advisory Group.

title: Democratizing AI: Principles for Meaningful Public Participation
abstract: A Data & Society policy brief arguing that public participation is uniquely necessary for AI governance because AI systems can produce inaccurate, biased, and discriminatory outcomes rooted in historical and social inequities. Sets out principles for meaningful public participation in AI decision-making processes.

title: EDRi Trilogue AI Act Rights Statement
abstract: A coalition of 150 civil society organizations, co-authored by EDRi, Access Now, AlgorithmWatch, Amnesty International, Bits of Freedom, Panoptykon Foundation, and others, urges EU institutions to prioritize fundamental rights in final AI Act negotiations. The statement warns against tech industry lobbying weakening regulations and calls for restrictions on surveillance by authorities, particularly biometric surveillance and predictive policing.

title: EFF Framework for AI Legislation 2023
abstract: Two-page framework guide from EFF outlining principles for legislators addressing AI harms, emphasizing civil liberties protections, privacy, free expression, and innovation. Advocates for open-source AI and against surveillance applications; grounds AI policy in existing digital rights frameworks while addressing new AI-specific risks.

title: EGE Opinion 33: Democracy in the Digital Age
organization: European Group on Ethics in Science and New Technologies
abstract: EGE Opinion 33 examines how digital technology configurations — including algorithmic surveillance, manipulation and discrimination, AI-driven misinformation, foreign interference, and the expansion of Big Tech into public sectors — can contribute to the weakening of democratic institutions. The opinion argues that strengthening democracy is an ethical necessity and proposes measures to address these threats to fundamental rights and democratic values.

title: ENISA AI Cybersecurity Framework 2023
abstract: ENISA's three-layer framework (cybersecurity foundations, AI-specific cybersecurity, sector-specific cybersecurity for AI) providing step-by-step guidance for national competent authorities, individual stakeholders, and researchers on building trustworthy AI systems. Addresses AI's specific threat landscape and gaps in standardisation.

title: EPIC Comments to OMB: AI Guidance (December 2023)
abstract: EPIC's formal comments to the Office of Management and Budget responding to proposed AI guidance for federal agencies. Recommends a rights- and risk-based framework for governing AI in federal government, emphasizing transparency, accountability, privacy protection, and civil rights safeguards. Urges OMB to require that federal AI systems meet the principles in the White House's Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights.

title: EPIC Generating Harms: Generative AI's Impact & Paths Forward
abstract: EPIC's white paper examining the harms posed by generative AI tools including ChatGPT, Midjourney, and DALL-E. Identifies harms including information manipulation, impersonation, data breaches, intellectual property theft, labor manipulation, and discrimination. Argues that rapid integration of generative AI into consumer products has undermined transparency and accountability, leaving consumers facing new and heightened risks. Proposes regulatory and policy paths forward grounded in a human rights framework.

title: EPIC Senate Judiciary Testimony: Online Data & AI (2023)
abstract: Congressional testimony by EPIC Executive Director Alan Butler before the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Privacy, Technology, and the Law. Addresses the need for strong AI transparency legislation, comprehensive privacy law, and accountability mechanisms for automated decision-making systems. Part of EPIC's advocacy for common-sense protective AI regulation at the federal level.

title: epicenter.works AI Act Final Negotiations Open Letter
abstract: epicenter.works and 69 civil society organizations urge EU lawmakers in final AI Act negotiations to unequivocally reject attempts to legalize dangerous and discriminatory police AI systems. The letter opposes proposals by governments and security industry lobbyists seeking to weaken protections against discriminatory and dangerous AI technologies in law enforcement contexts.

title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI
abstract: Adopted April 20, 2023 by the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, this charter is Egypt's ethics framework adapted from OECD AI Principles to the local context. Structured around five core principles (Human-Centeredness, Transparency and Explainability, Fairness, Accountability, and Security and Safety), it provides 13 general guidelines and 16 implementation guidelines applicable to government AI projects, with mandatory MCIT supervision and annual review.

title: Engineers Australia Responsible AI Submission 2023
abstract: Engineers Australia's formal submission to the Australian Government's 'Safe and Responsible AI in Australia' discussion paper (July 2023). The document articulates Engineers Australia's position that engineers have a professional responsibility to navigate the ethical and technical considerations of AI to harness its potential while ensuring the safety and wellbeing of society. It characterizes AI from an engineering perspective as an engineered system with defined inputs, outputs, and operations, emphasizing that AI is fundamentally deterministic in behavior and does not have intent independent of programming. Advocates for robust governance frameworks, engineering oversight, and ethical guardrails.

title: Equity UK AI Toolkit and Vision Statement
abstract: Launched mid-2023, Equity's comprehensive AI toolkit was developed in partnership with IP expert Mathilde Pavis to protect performers from unregulated AI. Includes: a vision statement on ethical AI applications, template AI contract for performance cloning work, model AI clauses to prevent unauthorized cloning, and template take-down notices. Core principles: explicit consent, transparency of terms, and fair remuneration for usage. Equity argues the UK legal framework is inadequate to protect performers from unauthorized AI imitations. In 2025, 99% of 7,000 members voting supported refusing digital scans to secure AI protections, and Equity escalated to industrial action threat.

title: FBE Guidelines on AI for Lawyers (2023)
abstract: The FBE New Technologies Commission's guidelines on the responsible use of generative AI by European lawyers, providing seven core recommendations: (1) understand the technology, (2) acknowledge limitations and context, (3) adhere to existing rules on AI use, (4) complement legal expertise, (5) maintain attorney-client privilege, (6) ensure data protection and privacy, and (7) inform clients and assume responsibility. The guidelines affirm that legal decisions must remain under human control and that AI serves as a tool supporting — but never replacing — professional experience and critical judgment. Translated into Italian, Spanish, Polish, German, and Turkish. Superseded by the 2024 Guidelines 2.0.

title: FEZANA Journal — STEAM Issue (Summer 2023)
organization: FEZANA (Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America)
abstract: FEZANA Journal special issue on STEAM topics including perspectives on technology and ethics from the Zoroastrian tradition.

title: FIA Guide to Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Published 17 November 2023 (updated March 2025), this guide helps FIA affiliates understand AI's implications for performers. It outlines FIA's core principles for responsible AI deployment: informed consent for digital replicas of performers' voices and likenesses, fair compensation models, transparency of training datasets, and protection of performers' biometric data. The guide provides practical bargaining strategies for trade unions to enhance protections through collective agreements and advocates for regulatory frameworks centered on consent and compensation.

title: Joint Statement on AI and the EU AI Act (Authors and Performers)
abstract: Issued 9 February 2023 by six European authors' and performers' organisations (ECSA, EWC, FERA, FIA, FIM, FSE), this joint statement addresses the EU AI Act. It advocates for a human-centric approach based on fundamental rights including artistic freedom and property rights. The statement demands mandatory consent before works are used to train AI, full transparency and disclosure requirements covering all deepfakes without creative-work exceptions, and protection of professional creators from unauthorized AI misappropriation of their economic interests and attribution rights.

title: Future of Life Traditional Religions on Positive AI Futures
abstract: Research project examining how the world's major traditional religions view the development and governance of artificial intelligence, and what positive AI futures look like through the lens of diverse faith traditions. Notes that most of the global population participates in a traditional religion, yet religious perspectives are largely absent from strategic AI discussions. Explores how Buddhism's mindfulness and compassion, Islam's justice and ethical values via Sharia, Christianity's emphasis on human dignity and stewardship, and other traditions approach AI technology.

title: Frontier Model Forum Founding Commitments
abstract: The Frontier Model Forum, launched July 2023 by Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI (later joined by Amazon and Meta), is an industry body focused on ensuring safe and responsible development of frontier AI models through best practices, safety research, and cross-sector information sharing. The Forum established a $10M+ AI Safety Fund in October 2023 and facilitated the White House voluntary AI commitments.

title: Finland: Generative AI Guidelines for Public Administration
abstract: The Finnish Ministry of Finance, through an inter-ministerial working group, provides guidance for public sector employees on using generative AI as a work support tool. It encourages adoption while emphasising safety, responsibility, and alignment with public administration values. Organisations may supplement with sector-specific policies. Available in Finnish, Swedish, and English.

title: Free Press Big Tech Backslide: Social-Media Rollbacks Endanger Democracy
abstract: Free Press report by Nora Benavidez examining how Meta, Twitter, and YouTube are failing to protect democracy ahead of the 2024 U.S. elections. Documents elimination of 17 critical platform policies between November 2022 and November 2023, including rollbacks of election-misinformation policies and weakening of protections around political ads. Addresses AI-generated deepfakes as a specific emerging threat to electoral integrity. Notes collective layoffs of more than 40,000 employees in content-moderation and trust-and-safety roles. Calls for reinstatement of platform safeguards and regulatory action on AI-generated disinformation.

title: G20 New Delhi Declaration 2023
abstract: G20 Leaders' Declaration from India's presidency committing to unlock AI's potential, share its benefits, and mitigate risks through international cooperation and further discussions on global governance for AI, with emphasis on responsible, fair, transparent, and accountable AI.

title: G7 Hiroshima Comprehensive Policy Framework
abstract: G7 Digital & Tech Ministers' Statement formalizing the Hiroshima AI Process Comprehensive Policy Framework, the first successful international framework combining guiding principles and a code of conduct for advanced AI systems, and introducing the Hiroshima Process International Guiding Principles for All AI Actors.

title: G7 Hiroshima Leaders Communique 2023
abstract: G7 Leaders' Communiqué from Hiroshima launched the Hiroshima AI Process for international discussion on generative AI opportunities and risks, committing to advance trustworthy AI governance in line with democratic values and tasking ministers with follow-up work.

title: Hiroshima AI Code of Conduct Oct 2023
abstract: Voluntary code of conduct for AI developers, complementing the Hiroshima Guiding Principles, providing actionable measures for organizations to promote safe, secure, and trustworthy AI worldwide, particularly for advanced foundation models and generative AI systems.

title: Hiroshima AI Guiding Principles Oct 2023
abstract: Eleven international guiding principles from the Hiroshima AI Process providing voluntary guidance to organizations developing advanced AI systems—including foundation models and generative AI—covering risk assessment, transparency, safety testing, cybersecurity, and reporting of AI capabilities and limitations.

title: Hiroshima Process All AI Actors Principles
abstract: Extension of the Hiroshima AI Process Guiding Principles to all AI actors (not just developers of advanced systems), introduced at the December 2023 G7 Digital & Tech Ministers' meeting, broadening the governance framework to encompass the full AI ecosystem.

title: Graphic Artists Guild Position on AI Image Generative Technologies
abstract: Published September 2023 (also issued as statement of concern to IFRRO General Assembly). Position grounded on three fundamental rights of artists: credit, consent, and compensation. Key positions: works created solely via AI without human-created input do not constitute works of human authorship; any use of copyrighted works for AI/ML training without permission is copyright infringement; the primacy of human endeavor must be recognized; creative professions and livable income must be protected. Submitted formal comments to U.S. Copyright Office in October 2023 arguing AI image generation is not fair use. Also joined the Human Artistry Campaign. Advocates for collective licensing as remuneration mechanism.

title: GCC Guiding Manual on AI Ethics
abstract: Reviewed and approved by the GCC Ministerial Committee for eGovernment at its 7th meeting in Muscat on November 9, 2023. The manual reflects shared Gulf values ensuring AI respects human dignity, Islamic principles, and GCC national identity. Core principles include preserving human autonomy, safety and reliability, justice and non-discrimination, and privacy and data security. Bahrain formally adopted the manual in July 2025.

title: Humans and Machines: Challenges of AI
abstract: The German Ethics Council explores how AI reshapes human identity and relationships, examining philosophical foundations and evaluating whether AI expands or diminishes human authorship and responsible action. The opinion covers four domains—medicine, education, public communication, and public administration—and concludes that AI must foster human flourishing rather than replace human judgment or responsibility.

title: George Fox AI Language Models Statement
abstract: George Fox University's formal institutional statement on AI language models establishes that AI use for graded work requires explicit instructor permission, and unauthorized use constitutes academic dishonesty. The policy explicitly names ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and GrammarlyGO. It emphasizes Christian faith integration, academic integrity, and wise discernment, with specific guidelines for both students and faculty including FERPA compliance and transparent disclosure requirements.

title: Ghana National AI Strategy 2023-2033
abstract: A ten-year comprehensive plan to harness AI for inclusive and sustainable development across eight strategic pillars including AI education, workforce development, digital infrastructure, data governance, ecosystem coordination, sector adoption, applied research, and public sector AI, with the vision of transforming Ghana into an AI-powered society by 2033.

title: Indigenous Peoples' Rights in Data
abstract: GIDA's comprehensive framework establishing rights for Indigenous peoples concerning data, contributing to the development of Indigenous research sovereignty. Published in the peer-reviewed journal Frontiers in Research Metrics and Analytics (2023) and also available via Figshare.

title: Ghana National AI Strategy 2023-2033
abstract: Launched on October 1, 2023 following 40+ stakeholder consultations, Ghana's 10-year AI strategy outlines eight strategic pillars including AI education, digital infrastructure, data governance, and sectoral adoption, with a dedicated Responsible AI Office overseeing implementation; developed with support from Smart Africa, GIZ FAIR Forward, and The Future Society.

title: GPAI Document GPAI(2023)2
abstract: GPAI governance and work programme document adopted at the 2023 New Delhi Summit, setting out GPAI's working group structure, research priorities, and cooperative framework for responsible AI including areas of employment, data governance, and innovation.

title: GPAI New Delhi Declaration 2023
abstract: GPAI Ministerial Declaration unanimously adopted by 29 member countries, reiterating commitment to responsible, trustworthy AI rooted in democratic values and human rights, addressing misinformation, transparency, fairness, intellectual property, and threats to democratic values from AI deployment.

title: Georgetown AI Guidelines
abstract: Official institutional AI guidelines developed by Georgetown University Information Services in consultation with the Office of General Counsel. Establishes six foundational principles: accountability (members responsible for AI-assisted work products), transparency (disclosure of AI use), fairness (recognition of AI bias), privacy (protection of PII), security (integrity of university resources), and intellectual property/confidentiality. Updated through February 2026. Aligns AI use with existing Georgetown policies and Jesuit institutional values.

title: Human Artistry Campaign Core Principles
abstract: Launched March 16, 2023 by a coalition of 40+ organizations spanning music, film, TV, publishing, and visual arts. Sets out seven core principles for AI that emphasize: technology should empower human expression; human-created works remain essential; use of copyrighted works requires authorization and licensing; AI must not receive copyright exemptions; copyright protects human creativity not machine output; transparency and accountability in AI development; and creators must maintain control. Co-signatories include RIAA, Recording Academy, SAG-AFTRA, AFM, NMPA, Nashville Songwriters Association, Graphic Artists Guild, and many others. One of the most broadly endorsed multi-union creative AI statements globally.

title: Hastings Center Editors' Statement: Generative AI in Scholarly Publishing
abstract: Lead-authored by Gregory E. Kaebnick (editor of Hastings Center Report) and co-signed by editors of multiple bioethics journals, this statement issues five recommendations governing the use of generative AI in scholarly publishing: AI may not be listed as an author; authors must be transparent about AI use; editors and reviewers must not rely solely on AI for review; and human accountability must be maintained throughout the editorial process.

title: Haredi Rabbinic Proclamation Against AI Chatbots
abstract: Twenty-five prominent Haredi rabbis signed a formal proclamation publicly banning the use of AI chatbots within their communities, declaring that the information generated by such chatbots contains 'heresy and abomination' and that exposure to them creates serious religious and spiritual danger. This represents a formal Orthodox Jewish halakhic position on AI chatbots.

title: HRW EU: AI Regulation Should Protect People's Rights
abstract: Human Rights Watch joined 149 civil society organizations urging EU institutions to prioritize fundamental rights during AI Act negotiations. Calls for: mandatory human rights impact assessments during AI development and deployment; public transparency requirements; prohibitions on harmful surveillance technologies including real-time biometric identification, predictive policing systems, and emotion recognition tools in public spaces; bans on discriminatory AI applications; and effective remedies for people harmed by AI systems. States that 'companies and governments will continue to use AI systems that exacerbate mass surveillance' without strong regulation.

title: IATSE Core Principles for Applications of AI and ML Technology
abstract: Eight core principles adopted by IATSE addressing AI and machine learning in the entertainment industry, developed by the union's Commission on Artificial Intelligence established in May 2023. The principles cover: (1) Comprehensive Approach spanning research through collective bargaining; (2) Research into AI/ML capabilities and impacts; (3) Collaboration with partners and stakeholders including AFL-CIO and international labor groups; (4) Education and upskilling rights for members; (5) Organizing entertainment workers in new AI-related job classifications; (6) Maintaining workers' rights, job security, and union jurisdiction, with AI serving as a complementary tool rather than replacement; (7) Political and legislative advocacy for strong copyright and IP protections; and (8) Collective bargaining, declaring AI a topic for future contract negotiations with demands for employer transparency.

title: ICMR AI Ethics Guidelines Healthcare
abstract: Released by the Department of Health Research and ICMR's Artificial Intelligence Cell, these guidelines provide India's first comprehensive ethics framework for AI in biomedical research and healthcare. The document includes an ethics review process for AI-based medical interventions, governance structures for AI use, informed consent requirements for AI-involved care, and special protections for sensitive health data. Target audience includes AI creators, researchers, clinicians, ethics committees, sponsors, and funders. Guidance addresses fairness in training datasets, privacy-by-design, and psychological/social risk communication.

title: ICN Digital Health Transformation and Nursing Practice
abstract: ICN position statement affirming that digital technologies including AI can support equitable and universal access to health services. Calls for nurses to participate in designing and implementing digital health systems, for technology to align with patient safety standards, and for the nursing workforce to develop digital competencies. Warns about the digital divide preventing equitable benefits across countries and communities, and addresses ethical implementation, environmental impacts, and interdisciplinary collaboration.

title: IESBA Technology-Related Revisions to the Code of Ethics
abstract: Final Pronouncement issued April 11, 2023 by the International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA), an independent standard-setting board under the auspices of IFAC. Introduces technology-related revisions to the International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants, addressing the ethical responsibilities of accountants and auditors when using or advising on technology including AI. Available in multiple languages. Related companion documents include the IESBA Technology Working Group Phase 2 Report (November 2022) and guidance on Applying the Code's Conceptual Framework to Independence in technology-related situations (July 2023). These represent the most formally binding AI-related guidance in the IFAC ecosystem, as IESBA standards are adopted by member bodies worldwide.

title: ITEC Ethics in Disruptive Technologies Handbook
abstract: 140-page operational handbook developed by the Institute for Technology, Ethics, and Culture (ITEC) at Santa Clara University's Markkula Center for Applied Ethics, in collaboration with the Vatican's Dicastery for Culture and Education. Authors: José Roger Flahaux, Brian Patrick Green, and Ann Skeet. Offers organizations a strategic plan to enhance ethical management practices while navigating disruptive technologies including AI, machine learning, encryption, and tracking. Provides an operational roadmap for understanding ethical risks and implementing mitigation infrastructure throughout the product and service life cycle. Available as free PDF download.

title: IPI General Assembly Resolution on AI and Media Freedom 2023
abstract: Unanimously adopted at the IPI 72nd Annual General Assembly during the IPI World Congress in Vienna, Austria, May 2023. The resolution calls on governments to ensure AI development includes 'strong human rights safeguards that protect media freedom and enable independent, pluralistic information ecosystems.' Main concerns addressed: AI's potential to spread disinformation and harmful content; risk of AI-enabled surveillance and tracking of journalists; need for human rights-based governance of AI systems. The resolution acknowledges AI can help media organizations become more resilient and sustainable through improved newsgathering and storytelling, but warns of risks including AI-generated disinformation and deepfakes. It calls on states to consult 'a diverse spectrum of civil society stakeholders' including journalists and media professionals. The document references the EU AI Act, OSCE Policy Manual on Artificial Intelligence and Freedom of Expression, and Council of Europe recommendations as relevant frameworks.

title: ISACA: The Promise and Peril of the AI Revolution
abstract: ISACA white paper examining how organizations should navigate AI adoption while managing associated risks. Provides a framework for enterprise AI governance covering benefits analysis, risk identification (IP leakage, data integrity, cybersecurity threats, liability), continuous risk management via a three-step approach (identify risk, define appetite, monitor/manage), and eight security protocols. Targeted at CISOs, IT risk managers, and senior executives. Includes guidance on establishing acceptable use policies, designating an AI governance leader, and building AI ethics programs. Formally issued by ISACA as an institutional guidance document.

title: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AI Management System
abstract: World's first AI management system standard, published December 2023 by ISO and IEC, specifying requirements and guidance for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an AI management system, addressing transparency, fairness, bias mitigation, accountability, and data governance for all organization types.

title: UN Advisory Body on AI Interim Report 2023
abstract: Interim report from the UN Secretary-General's High-Level Advisory Body on AI proposing early analysis and preliminary recommendations for international governance of AI, preceding the final report and feeding into the Global Digital Compact process.

title: Ada Lovelace Institute: Regulating AI in the UK
abstract: This report evaluates the UK government's contextual, sector-based approach to AI regulation and identifies significant gaps in legal protection.
