It applies three tests—coverage, capability, and urgency—and makes 18 recommendations including strengthened data protection, new ombudsman mechanisms, increased regulatory funding, and mandatory transparency requirements for foundation model developers.

title: Brookings: Responsible Use of Generative AI — Institutional Principles
abstract: Formal institutional policy governing Brookings staff use of generative AI in research and operations. Developed through a deliberate process launched by Brookings Interim President Amy Liu and executive leadership via the Emerging Technologies Advisory Group (ETAG). Establishes four core institutional principles: complying with existing policies, validating AI outputs, protecting sensitive data, and disclosing AI usage appropriately. Described as 'the first step' in a comprehensive institutional AI strategy. Note: This is a genuine institutional position adopted by Brookings executive leadership — distinct from the many individual researcher publications on AI policy.

title: Buddhist Perspective on AI: Future of Life
abstract: A formal Buddhist scholarly perspective on AI ethics, emphasizing principles of non-harm (ahimsa), compassion (karuna), and freedom of attention as foundational criteria for ethical AI. The document advocates for AI systems designed to decrease suffering and preserve the diversity of human attention and wisdom traditions.

title: DAIR Stochastic Parrots Authors on AI Pause
abstract: A counter-statement by Timnit Gebru, Emily M. Bender, and Angelina McMillan-Major critiquing the FLI AI pause letter for focusing on hypothetical extinction risks while ignoring real and present harms from deployed AI including worker exploitation, synthetic media proliferation, and power concentration. Advocates for transparency, accountability, labor protections, and inclusive AI governance.

title: Pause Giant AI Experiments Letter
abstract: Open letter calling for all AI labs to immediately pause for at least six months the training of AI systems more powerful than GPT-4, citing risks to society and humanity. Signed by over 31,800 people including Yoshua Bengio, Stuart Russell, Elon Musk, Steve Wozniak, and Yuval Noah Harari.

title: ITUC AlgorithmWatch Report 2023
abstract: A research report commissioned by the ITUC and produced by AlgorithmWatch, presenting findings of a global mapping exercise on trade union responses to algorithmic management across 26 countries. The report finds that workers worldwide are confronting algorithmically-driven automation through automated workforce management procedures, leading to new forms of workplace surveillance that potentially undermine workers' rights. Key findings include: transparency and accountability are critical tools against automation risks; unions have primarily focused on establishing ethical frameworks rather than practical implementation; and inter-union knowledge exchange is underdeveloped. The report calls for unions to transition from principles to practical implementation through concrete tools at company level.

title: Abrahamic Commitment to Rome Call
organization: Pontifical Academy for Life / Abu Dhabi Forum / Chief Rabbinate of Israel
abstract: Representatives of the three Abrahamic religions — Catholic Christianity (Mons. Vincenzo Paglia, President of the Pontifical Academy for Life), Islam (Sheikh Abdallah bin Bayyah, UAE Fatwa Council), and Judaism (Chief Rabbi Eliezer Simha Weisz, Council of the Chief Rabbinate of Israel) — formally signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at the Vatican. This was the first interfaith religious endorsement of the Rome Call's framework for ethical AI development.

title: Israel AI Regulation and Ethics Policy
abstract: Israel's inaugural AI policy framework, published December 2023, recommends a risk-based, sector-specific regulatory approach that empowers existing regulators rather than creating horizontal AI legislation. Guided by six regulatory principles including international interoperability, incremental development, and multistakeholder cooperation, it addresses seven AI challenges including algorithmic bias, lack of transparency, safety risks, and accountability gaps, and proposes an AI Policy Coordination Center for inter-agency coordination.

title: Jordan AI Strategy 2023-2027
abstract: Approved by the Jordanian Cabinet and launched with UNIDO, ESCWA, and EU partnership at the 9th MENA ICT Forum. Five main objectives: (1) capacity building for AI systems, (2) developing Jordanian skills and expertise, (3) encouraging scientific research and development, (4) enhancing investment and entrepreneurship environment, (5) ensuring legislative and regulatory environment for safe AI deployment. 68 targeted projects across a five-year horizon. Cabinet approved in 2022/2023; brochure version also available.

title: Indonesia Kominfo AI Ethics Circular 2023
abstract: Surat Edaran Menkominfo Nomor 9 Tahun 2023 menetapkan pedoman etika kecerdasan artifisial bagi pelaku usaha berbasis AI (KBLI 62015) dan penyelenggara sistem elektronik publik maupun swasta. Dokumen ini memuat tiga area kebijakan utama: nilai-nilai etika AI (inklusivitas, kemanusiaan, keamanan, aksesibilitas, transparansi, kredibilitas dan akuntabilitas, perlindungan data pribadi, pembangunan berkelanjutan, hak kekayaan intelektual), implementasi nilai-nilai etika, serta akuntabilitas dalam pemanfaatan dan pengembangan AI. Meski bersifat non-binding, pelanggaran dapat diproses berdasarkan UU ITE dan UU PDP.

title: Keidanren AI Utilization Strategy II 2023
abstract: The AI Utilization Strategy II (AI活用戦略II) is Keidanren's updated policy proposal addressing the rapidly evolving AI landscape four years after the original 2019 strategy. The document responds specifically to the rise of generative AI (e.g., large language models) and sets out guidance across three domains: (1) preparation by companies to deploy AI responsibly, (2) preparation by individuals to work with AI, and (3) creation of social systems and governance frameworks enabling safe AI diffusion. It endorses the Hiroshima AI Process principles and calls for international coordination on AI standards, urging the Japanese government to adopt risk-based, innovation-friendly regulation.

title: Lakewood Rabbanim Kol Koreh: Ban on AI Chatbots
abstract: A kol koreh signed by 25 leading rabbanim and roshei yeshiva from Lakewood, NJ, banning the use of AI chatbots including ChatGPT. The proclamation states it is 'assur to connect with and use artificial intelligence chats, whether via a phone, text, or computer, even for a necessary use, and kal vachomer for divrei torah.' Applies to 'all men, women, and children.' Notes that business-use exceptions remain under discussion. Distinct from the earlier Skver ban (April 2023) and the Haredi Rabbinical Council proclamation already in corpus.

title: League of Women Voters Comment to FEC on AI in Campaign Ads
abstract: Formal LWVUS comment supporting a petition to the Federal Election Commission arguing the FEC should regulate 'Deceptive AI Campaign Communications' under its existing 'fraudulent misrepresentation' rules. The League argues voters have a right to 'true, genuine, and complete information' about elections and that AI-generated deepfakes in campaign communications directly threaten democratic participation. Applies the League's existing positions on citizen's right to know and campaign finance transparency to AI-generated disinformation.

title: Law Council of Australia: Safe and Responsible AI Submission
abstract: The Law Council of Australia's submission to the Department of Industry, Sciences and Resources in response to the government's discussion paper on Safe and Responsible AI in Australia. The Law Council argues that the significant risks posed by AI use justify a strengthened, precautionary approach to regulation where existing laws prove insufficient. It advocates for multifaceted regulation including targeted legislation beyond voluntary codes, while opposing a comprehensive 'AI Act' as the primary mechanism. Key recommendations include product stewardship across the supply chain, transparency and disclosure obligations, and expanded regulation in high-risk sectors.

title: China Science and Technology Ethics Review Trial Measures 2023
abstract: Joint issuance by 10 ministries/bodies mandating institutional-level ethics review committees for research in sensitive fields. The measures specify that activities involving invasive brain-computer interfaces, AI models capable of manipulating public opinion, and highly autonomous decision-making systems endangering human life require advance ethics review. Takes effect December 1, 2023.

title: MEAA Draft AI Position Statement
abstract: Draft position statement on AI for media, creative, and arts workers. States media companies should not use AI to compromise ethical standards or reduce employment, and must be transparent about AI use. Part of broader campaign calling for economy-wide AI Act.

title: MEXT Generative AI Guidelines for Schools 2023
abstract: The Provisional Guidelines on Generative AI in Primary and Secondary Education (初等中等教育段階における生成AIの利用に関する暫定的なガイドライン) were issued by MEXT's Elementary and Secondary Education Bureau in July 2023 as an urgent response to the rapid spread of tools such as ChatGPT among students. The guidelines define (1) general principles for school-level AI use, (2) specific situations where use is appropriate vs. inappropriate (e.g., inappropriate for assessments measuring individual ability), (3) countermeasures for risks including privacy, copyright, misinformation, and over-reliance, and (4) guidance on fostering digital and AI literacy. They target school boards, school principals, and teachers. A revised Version 2.0 was issued December 26, 2024, with an updated English translation released April 22, 2025.

title: Bletchley Declaration 2023
abstract: First-ever global declaration on AI safety signed by 29 countries (including the US, China, and EU), identifying shared frontier AI safety risks of concern and committing to risk-based national policies, international scientific cooperation, and transparency requirements for advanced AI system developers.

title: China Global AI Governance Initiative 2023
abstract: The Initiative sets out China's core positions on international AI governance cooperation, covering AI's role in sustainable development, respect for national sovereignty in AI governance, ensuring equal development and use rights for all countries, strengthening privacy protections, and preventing bias and discrimination. It advocates for a UN-led open and inclusive global AI governance system.

title: MSIT AI Ethics and Reliability Promotion Plan
abstract: 과학기술정보통신부가 제4차 인공지능 최고위 전략대화에서 발표한 추진계획. 비전은 '누구나 신뢰할 수 있는 인공지능, 안전한 인공지능 개발·활용 확산'이며, 채용·생성형 AI 분야별 가이드라인 마련, 민간 자율 신뢰성 검·인증 추진, 초거대 AI 신뢰성 기술개발 지원(220억), 워터마크 도입 제도화 검토 등을 포함한다.

title: Meta Responsible AI Framework
abstract: Meta's Responsible AI framework covers safety evaluations, red-teaming, Llama Guard safety tools, watermarking of AI-generated content, and participation in the C2PA steering committee. The framework emphasizes open-source deployment alongside collaborative development with researchers and civil society.

title: NASSCOM GenAI Responsible Guidelines
abstract: Described as a first-of-its-kind industry-led self-governance framework globally, these guidelines divide obligations across three actor categories — researchers, developers, and users — and establish joint obligations including AI literacy programs, technical safety research, and regulatory reform projects. Developers must employ ethics councils and risk officers, publicly disclose data and algorithm sources, and ensure responsible use terms of service. The guidelines target risks specific to generative AI including deepfakes, hallucinations, and job displacement.

title: NCCS Paper on Big Data and AI
abstract: A 33-page formal position paper submitted in response to Singapore's Bioethics Advisory Committee (BAC) consultation on 'Ethical, Legal and Social Issues Arising from Big Data and Artificial Intelligence Use in Human Biomedical Research' (May-Jul 2023). The paper is structured around two core theological-ethical principles: (1) human dignity, grounded in the Christian doctrine of Imago Dei, and (2) the common good, drawn from Scripture and the Christian social ethics tradition. It addresses: the nature of big data as a 'hyperobject'; the risk of 'Dataism' as a techno-religion; the relationship between Christian faith, science, and technology; trust in AI systems; data privacy and security (endorsing 9 data protection principles including purpose limitation, data minimization, privacy by design, right to erasure, and data breach notification); accountability and responsibility for AI errors; the adverse consequences of algorithmic bias and the need for solidarity with marginalized populations; the epistemological challenge of big data (correlation vs. causation); governance and the role of public authority; data stewardship and informed consent challenges; and the Church's role in promoting the common good. The paper argues that ethics, not technology, must be at the centre of discussions about big data and AI, and that the human person must always be front and centre in considerations about AI development and deployment. It warns against 'enthrone[ing] technology as salvific' and calls for technology to serve human dignity and the common good rather than the reverse.

title: NIST AI RMF 1.0 (2023)
abstract: Voluntary, sector-agnostic AI risk management framework developed through consensus process, structured around four core functions (GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE), helping organizations identify, assess, and manage risks across the AI lifecycle in all domains.

title: Global Principles on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: The Global Principles on Artificial Intelligence (August 2023) were co-signed by 26 international publisher organizations representing thousands of creative professionals across news, magazine, and book publishing. The document sets out 12 numbered principles governing AI development, deployment, and regulation: (1-4) Intellectual Property — AI developers must respect IP rights; publishers are entitled to negotiate remuneration for use of their IP; copyright applies to AI training use; existing licensing frameworks should be used; (5) Transparency — AI systems must keep granular records of publisher works used in training, with full provenance chains; (6) Accountability — providers must cooperate to ensure accountability for outputs and must not be shielded by safe harbor provisions; (7) Quality and Integrity — quality publisher content upstream ensures quality AI outputs downstream; (8) Fairness — AI systems must not create anti-competitive market outcomes; (9-10) Safety — AI must promote trusted information sources and must protect user privacy; (11) By Design — these principles must be incorporated at design stage, not as afterthought; (12) Sustainable Development — AI systems must align with human values and support long-term information ecosystems. Signatories include WAN-IFRA, News/Media Alliance, European Newspaper Publishers' Association, News Media Association (UK), Digital Content Next, European Publishers Council, FIPP, Korean Association of Newspapers, and 18 other national and regional publisher bodies.

title: NMA AI Principles
abstract: The NMA AI Principles (April 2023) articulate the organization's foundational position that unlicensed use of journalistic content by generative AI systems constitutes intellectual property infringement. The document covers six categories: (1) Intellectual Property — GAI developers must respect copyright and seek explicit permission for training, surfacing, and synthesizing publisher content; publishers have the right to negotiate fair compensation; fair use doctrine does not justify unauthorized AI use of publisher archives; (2) Transparency — publishers have the right to know what content is used in AI systems; surfaced and synthesized outputs must attribute original sources clearly; (3) Accountability — deployers of GAI systems must not be shielded from liability for outputs; (4) Fairness — GAI systems must comply with competition laws; unauthorized use of publisher content by firms with market power should be considered evidence of competition law violation; (5) Safety — GAI systems must protect user privacy; collection of personal data should be minimal and disclosed; (6) Design — all principles should be incorporated by design into GAI systems. The document explicitly states these principles 'apply equally to all publisher content, whether in text, image, audiovisual or any other format.'

title: NSPE AI Position Statement
abstract: NSPE position statement asserting that individuals designing, developing, or overseeing safety-critical AI systems should meet the same professional licensure standards as traditional engineers. Covers seven focus areas: ethical responsibility, safety, licensure, transparency, privacy, continuous education, and interdisciplinary collaboration.

title: NTEU AI Discussion Paper
abstract: Member-led AI policy paper endorsed by NTEU National Council in October 2023. Developed through a working group of NTEU members formed in 2023 with widespread consultation and focus groups. Recognises AI's potential to enhance and enrich work in the tertiary sector provided ethical implementation. Key positions: staff are best positioned to make decisions about AI integration in their discipline's learning context; access to and training on AI should be provided at institutional level to ensure equity; protection of intellectual property and author moral rights; need for strong guardrails beyond Fair Work Act provisions; collaborative design processes rather than post-decision consultation. Also informed NTEU's submission to government inquiries on AI regulation.

title: NU Munas Decision on AI and Fatwas
abstract: NU's National Assembly of Religious Scholars (Musyawarah Nasional Alim Ulama) established that it is haram (forbidden) to use AI-generated answers as authoritative religious guidance. AI lacks the qualifications required of a mufti including piety, scholarly depth, and contextual understanding. AI may inform by providing information about what scholars have said, but must not rule by issuing its own religious judgments. This decision forms the basis for the subsequent SOUL.md AI ethics framework.

title: NWU Platform and Principles for Generative AI Policy
abstract: Formally adopted and ratified October 30, 2023 after drafting over summer/fall 2023. Establishes six core principles: Solidarity, Humanity, Control and Compensation, Transparency, Accountability, and Integrity. Outlines five key policy areas: Control, Compensation, Credit/Labeling/Transparency, Fair Contracts, and Compliance with the Berne Convention and other copyright treaties. Emphasizes that creators should never be replaced by generative AI and that if AI is used, it must serve as a tool to assist human workers. Also submitted response to U.S. Copyright Office NOI on same date. The NWU is UAW Local 1981, representing freelance and contract writers.

title: NZ Interim GenAI Guidance for Public Service
abstract: This joint interim guidance from New Zealand's public service System Leaders sets out responsible and trustworthy use of Generative AI (GenAI) across the New Zealand Public Service, encouraging agencies to use GenAI cautiously and to understand necessity before deployment. Published in July 2023, it was subsequently updated and expanded into the Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service (GenAI), which superseded it.

title: G20 AI Governance OECD Report 2023
abstract: OECD stocktaking report on generative AI for the G7 Hiroshima Process, providing analysis of opportunities and challenges, policy landscape, and governance options for advanced AI systems including large language models and foundation models.

title: OJK Fintech AI Code of Ethics Indonesia 2023
abstract: Panduan ini diterbitkan bersama oleh OJK dan empat asosiasi fintech (AFTECH, AFSI, AFPI, ALUDI) pada 24 November 2023 dalam pembukaan Indonesia Fintech Summit & Expo (IFSE) 2023. Dokumen ini menetapkan standar etika AI yang bertanggung jawab dan terpercaya di industri teknologi finansial Indonesia, mencakup prinsip-prinsip etika, manajemen risiko, transparansi, dan akuntabilitas. Panduan ini bersifat sukarela namun menjadi acuan industri fintech nasional.

title: OTI Endorses Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023
abstract: OTI formal endorsement of the Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023, introduced by Senator Ron Wyden, Senator Cory Booker, and Representative Yvette Clarke. OTI Policy Director Prem Trivedi stated the bill would require companies to conduct impact assessments for bias and effectiveness and provide meaningful transparency. OTI framed the legislation as an important step in holding companies accountable for automated decision-making in critical areas like housing, employment, and education, while balancing innovation protections.

title: Guidance for Safe Foundation Model Deployment
abstract: A PAI framework developed with a working group of 40+ global institutions, providing 22 possible guidelines for foundation and frontier model providers to anticipate and address risks — including bias, misuse, and worker harm — across varying capability levels and release methods. Complements rather than replaces regulatory efforts and emphasizes collective action among industry, civil society, and academia.

title: Guidelines for AI and Shared Prosperity
abstract: A PAI resource providing tools for designing and deploying AI systems in ways that advance workers' rights and economic well-being. Includes a job impact assessment methodology and stakeholder-specific recommendations for AI developers, deploying organizations, policymakers, and labor groups on minimizing AI-driven economic disruption.

title: PAI Responsible Practices for Synthetic Media
abstract: A Partnership on AI framework providing recommendations for the responsible development and deployment of synthetic media (AI-generated audiovisual content). Addresses builders, creators, and distributors through practices centered on consent, disclosure, and transparency; framed as a living document with institutional support from 18 organizations.

title: Panoptykon ChatGPT AI Regulation Position
abstract: Panoptykon Foundation examines the rapid rise of generative AI tools like ChatGPT and their societal implications, identifying concerns including legal liability for AI-generated content, algorithmic bias, privacy erosion, and disinformation. The paper argues that existing regulations (GDPR, DSA/DMA) have gaps in AI governance, and advocates for EU AI Act amendments requiring fundamental rights impact assessments and mandatory labeling of AI-based systems.

title: Pax Christi International Inter-Faith Statement on Killer Robots
abstract: Joint interfaith statement issued in the context of the UN CCW expert meeting (12–19 May 2023, Geneva) that failed to achieve legally binding controls on lethal autonomous weapons systems. Pax Christi International joined 14 other Christian, Buddhist, and Muslim organizations calling for a ban on autonomous weapons that target people or cannot be used with meaningful human control. The statement condemns 'digital dehumanization' — the reduction of humans to data points for automated attacks — as 'fundamentally repulsive to all people who share a belief in the inalienable dignity of the human person and the inestimable worth of human life.' Affirms that machines will never have human consciousness or awareness. All 15 signatories: Aotearoa New Zealand Stop Killer Robots Campaign, Christian World Service (NZ), Fellowship of Reconciliation (England and Scotland), Hungary Stop Killer Robots Campaign, Institute of Church and Society (Ibadan, Nigeria), Kairos Foundation of Nigeria, New Zealand Anglican Pacifist Fellowship, Pax Christi Aotearoa New Zealand, Pax Christi International, Peace Movement Aotearoa NZ, Soka Gakkai International, St Andrew's on the Terrace Presbyterian Church (Wellington NZ), Sustainable Peace and Development Organization (SPADO), The InterAgency Institute, World Council of Churches.

title: Peru AI Promotion Law 31814
abstract: Peru's AI promotion law establishing conditions guaranteeing responsible development and use of artificial intelligence for the country's economic and social development. The law provides a legal foundation for responsible AI use and positions the PCM's Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation as the national technical-normative authority for AI oversight.

title: Pontifical Academy Life AI Ethics (2023 Update)
abstract: The Pontifical Academy for Life published a document on artificial intelligence and human communication, exploring how AI intersects with authentic human connection, wisdom, and the heart. The document contributes Catholic reflection on generative AI's implications for truth, relationship, and integral human communication.

title: Public Knowledge Senate AI Testimony: Privacy and Liability (2023)
abstract: Formal testimony by Public Knowledge President and CEO Chris Lewis before the U.S. Senate's bipartisan AI Insights Forum on privacy and liability. Advocates for Congress to establish a regulatory framework including a comprehensive federal privacy law to ensure AI transforms society for the better. Calls for passing the American Data Privacy and Protection Act as a national standard to protect consumer data privacy in the AI era.

title: Public Citizen Model AI Legislation for State Lawmakers
abstract: Public Citizen model state legislation package providing template laws addressing harmful AI practices. Covers five issue areas: non-consensual intimate deepfakes, election deepfakes, chatbot disclosure requirements, AI in healthcare coverage decisions, and chatbot safety for minors. Offers expert support for state lawmakers to tailor models to specific state needs. Part of Public Citizen's broader effort to develop common-sense legislative solutions to mitigate the most dangerous harms of unregulated AI in the absence of federal action.

title: QIF Clerk Post on AI and Collaborative Wisdom
abstract: Clerk's reflection by Gray Cox calling Quakers to engage with AI ethics. Identifies concerns about opacity of black-box AI systems, speed outpacing oversight, training data absorbing unethical content, and documented harms (racial discrimination in lending, facial recognition bias, democratic interference). Calls for slowing AI development, directing technology toward ecologically balanced 'Collaborative Wisdom,' engaging Quaker communities in spiritual discernment, and transforming systems through spirit-led approaches. Proposes workshops examining AI from Quaker perspectives.

title: Qom Seminary AI for Islamic Scholarship
abstract: Ayatollah Alireza Arafi, head of Qom's seminaries, stated: 'The seminary must get involved in using modern, progressive technology and artificial intelligence.' Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei urged clergy to explore AI. Qom seminaries aim to use AI to parse Islamic texts faster and help fatwas keep pace with evolving society. Mohammad Ghotbi noted: 'Robots can't replace senior clerics, but they can be a trusted assistant that can help them issue a fatwa in five hours instead of 50 days.' AI positioned as assistant to human jurists, not replacement.

title: RTDNA Use of AI in Journalism Guidelines
abstract: RTDNA recommends that news organizations establish clear policies for AI use in newsgathering, editing, and content distribution. The guidelines address three primary areas: (1) Accuracy, Context and Clarity — newsrooms should verify AI capabilities before implementation, establish safeguards against unintended plagiarism, and mandate human review before publication; (2) Transparency and Disclosure — 'disclosing how you use artificial intelligence is preferable to non-disclosure,' with organizations required to define what constitutes AI use and where disclosure occurs; (3) Privacy — AI should operate within ethical and legal boundaries, as it cannot properly weigh public interest against individual privacy rights in the way human journalists do. The overarching principle is that 'AI should not be used to replace human judgment and critical thinking — essential elements of trusted reporting.' The document acknowledges this is 'an emerging and fast-changing area' requiring periodic review.

title: Paris Charter on AI and Journalism
abstract: The first global ethical benchmark for AI and journalism, defining ten principles that journalists, newsrooms, and media outlets can apply when working with AI. Covers human editorial control, transparency, content traceability, authentic vs. synthetic distinction, responsible personalization, and fair compensation to rights holders. Chaired by journalist and Nobel laureate Maria Ressa; co-signed by 16 partner organizations.

title: Bank of Russia AI in Financial Market Consultation Paper 2023
abstract: Доклад для общественных консультаций Банка России, посвящённый перспективам и рискам применения технологий искусственного интеллекта участниками финансового рынка. Обсуждаются текущий статус внедрения ИИ, подходы к управлению рисками, принципы регулирования и инициативы регулятора. Принят риск-ориентированный и технологически нейтральный подход; ставка делается на мягкое регулирование — рекомендации, руководства и стандарты.

title: Rwanda National AI Policy 2023
abstract: Established a roadmap for Rwanda to harness AI benefits and mitigate risks, building on Vision 2050 and the Smart Rwanda Master Plan, with the goal of positioning Rwanda as Africa's Centre of Excellence in Artificial Intelligence.

title: Rwanda National AI Policy
abstract: Rwanda's formal National AI Policy, approved by Cabinet and published in December 2023, positions Rwanda as Africa's AI hub and center of excellence, establishing 14 key policy recommendations across skills development, data governance, public sector transformation, and ethical AI guidelines, with an estimated $589 million AI ecosystem target over five years.

title: SAG-AFTRA Statement on AI and Digital Doubles
abstract: SAG-AFTRA's formal position statement asserts that terms for digitally simulating performers' voices and likenesses are mandatory subjects of bargaining under the National Labor Relations Act, requiring companies to bargain with the union before acquiring these rights. The statement reaffirms Global Rule One, opposes circumvention of collective bargaining via individual contracts, supports the Human Artistry Campaign's AI principles, and advocates against government creation of IP exemptions that would allow AI developers to exploit creative works without permission or compensation.

title: SBC Resolution on AI and Emerging Technologies
abstract: The Southern Baptist Convention became the first major national denomination to pass a formal resolution on AI ethics, adopted at its Annual Meeting in New Orleans. The resolution affirms human dignity as image-bearers of God over productivity, calls for proactive engagement with AI rather than fear or uncritical embrace, and directs Christ-centered, transparent use of AI that prioritizes human uniqueness.

title: Skver Beis Din: Ban on Artificial Intelligence Tools
abstract: The New Square Beis Din, signed by 16 rabbinic members, issued a proclamation banning the use of 'Open AI and the like' in all forms — via computer, text, or phone. The decree characterizes AI as 'an open internet without any filter, open to all abominations, heresy, and infidelity without limits.' Circulated in Hebrew and Yiddish. This is distinct from the separately issued Haredi Rabbinical Council kol koreh (already in corpus) and the later Lakewood kol koreh.

title: SDA South American Division AI Ethical Principles
organization: Seventh-day Adventist Church - South American Division
abstract: Voted November 5, 2023 at the regional Annual Council in Brasilia, Brazil. Drafted by experts in communication, technology, and compliance. Establishes principles of transparency, fairness, trustworthiness, accountability, security and privacy, and sustainability for AI use within the SDA South American Division. Emphasizes AI as complementary tool, not substitute for human wisdom and empathy. Commits to anti-discrimination, human oversight in decision-making, and compatibility with biblical-Adventist worldview. Directs denominational entities (education, health, publishing, etc.) to create sector-specific guidelines.

title: Brazil PL 2338 AI Framework Bill
abstract: Dispõe sobre o uso da Inteligência Artificial, estabelecendo princípios, direitos e deveres para o desenvolvimento, implementação e monitoramento de sistemas de IA no Brasil, com abordagem baseada em riscos (risco inaceitável, alto risco, risco limitado e risco mínimo). Aprovado pelo Senado Federal em 10 de dezembro de 2024 e remetido à Câmara dos Deputados em março de 2025.

title: SHRM AI in the Workplace Playbook
abstract: SHRM's guide to ethical, efficient, and employee-centric AI integration published in late 2023. The playbook emphasizes a human-centered approach: 'To flourish in the AI era, we must rethink work, worker, and workplace — reimagining a world of work where human intelligence and ingenuity are upskilled, not replaced.' Addresses ethical considerations, transparency, training for responsible AI integration, and organizational synergy between AI and human intelligence (HI). Calls for human oversight in critical employment decisions including hiring and firing, and establishes the framework for SHRM's broader 'AI+HI' initiative. Includes guidance on governance frameworks for algorithmic fairness, data privacy, and ethical use.

title: Senegal National AI Strategy (SNDIA)
abstract: Launched in September 2023 by Senegal's Ministry of Communication, Telecommunications and Digital Economy, the SNDIA positions AI as a catalyst for socio-economic development under the Plan Sénégal Émergent, focusing on job creation for youth, productivity enhancement, and ethical AI governance, developed through stakeholder consultations with UNESCO Readiness Assessment Methodology support.

title: Samford AI Faculty Guideline
abstract: Samford University's Provost's Task Force on Artificial Intelligence produced a comprehensive 7-page faculty guideline affirmed by the University Faculty Senate on November 27, 2023. The document is grounded in Samford's University Core Values including spiritual growth, integrity, and learning. It frames AI engagement through the lens of a 'Christ-centered learning community' and addresses benefits/limitations of AI, assignment design, student engagement strategies, detecting misuse, and available resources.

title: Singapore National AI Strategy 2.0
abstract: Singapore's updated national AI strategy, launched December 4, 2023, with goals of Excellence and Empowerment over the next 3-5 years. Sets out 3 systems, 10 enablers, and 15 actions to accelerate AI R&D and governance; commits over SGD 1 billion in investment and targets tripling AI practitioners to 15,000. Shifts from flagship projects to a whole-of-society systems approach.

title: TRAI AI and Big Data Recommendations
abstract: TRAI's sector-specific AI governance recommendations address the application of AI and big data across telecom networks and services, recommending establishment of the AIDAI as a cross-sectoral AI governance authority with legally binding powers for high-risk use cases. The document covers AI adoption for network security and optimization, customer grievance redressal, skill development partnerships with AICTE, and data access mechanisms for training datasets. TRAI recommends collaboration with telecom service providers to enhance capabilities through responsible AI adoption.

title: Office of National Buddhism Complaint on AI Monk Images
abstract: สำนักงานพระพุทธศาสนาแห่งชาติ (พศ.) ทำหนังสือถึงตำรวจไซเบอร์เพื่อขอให้ตรวจสอบและดำเนินการกับเพจเฟซบุ๊กที่ใช้ปัญญาประดิษฐ์ (AI) สร้างภาพพระภิกษุในลักษณะที่ไม่เหมาะสม จำนวน 9 ภาพ แสดงพระภิกษุกำลังเล่นดนตรี เล่นกีตาร์ และแข่งจักรยานยนต์ นายสุภัทรณ์ เมืองแมด ผู้อำนวยการสำนักเลขาธิการมหาเถรสมาคมและโฆษก พศ. แถลงว่าการใช้ AI สร้างภาพดังกล่าว 'ไม่เหมาะสม' และอาจทำให้เกิดความเข้าใจผิด ส่งผลเสียต่อภาพลักษณ์ของคณะสงฆ์

title: Turkopticon-DAIR AI Worker Letter to Congress
abstract: A coalition letter from 26 organizations to Senate Majority Leader Schumer demanding Congress prioritize AI worker protections. Signed by Turkopticon, DAIR, Alphabet Workers Union-CWA, Amazon Employees for Climate Justice, AI Now Institute, and others. Key demands include: prioritizing health, safety, and fair wages for data workers developing AI; strengthening workers' organizing rights without employer surveillance; ending predatory workplace monitoring and automated management systems; addressing discriminatory 'black box' hiring, firing, and wage processes; and establishing transparency, due process, and job security protections.

title: Bin Bayyah Rome Speech on AI Ethics in Islam
organization: UAE Fatwa Council / Forum for Promoting Peace
abstract: Formal speech by Sheikh Abdullah bin Bayyah, Chairman of the UAE Council for Fatwa, delivered at the AI Ethics Forum in Rome on January 10, 2023. Emphasizes that Islamic teachings encourage knowledge acquisition while maintaining ethical frameworks. Argues AI innovations must be guided by moral principles to protect human dignity. Advocates an ethical framework based on universal values: virtue, respect for humanity, solidarity, cooperation, honesty, justice, integrity, accountability, and transparency. Invokes the Islamic legal maxim 'la darar wa la dirar' (there is no harm nor reciprocal harm) as a foundational principle for AI ethics. References UAE government vision and founding principles of Sheikh Zayed.

title: US Chamber AI Commission Report
abstract: Independent, bipartisan report by the US Chamber's Commission on AI Competitiveness, Inclusion, and Innovation. Developed through 87 expert witness hearings across five field sessions. Establishes five foundational pillars for AI regulation: Efficiency (leverage existing laws, fill gaps), Collegiality (federal interagency coordination), Neutrality (technology-neutral, outcomes-focused rules), Flexibility (support industry self-regulation and soft-law approaches), and Proportionality (risk-based regulatory frameworks). Addresses workforce preparation, global competitiveness, and national security dimensions of AI policy.

title: UK AI Regulation Pro-Innovation Approach
abstract: UK government white paper on AI regulation proposing a principles-based, context-specific regulatory framework for responsible AI innovation.

title: UK AI Regulation White Paper 2023
abstract: Set out a pro-innovation, context-based regulatory framework for AI built on five cross-sectoral principles (safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, contestability), empowering existing regulators rather than creating new AI-specific legislation.

title: UK AI Safety Institute 2023
abstract: Established the world's first state-backed AI Safety Institute to evaluate risks of frontier AI models before and after release, evolving from the Frontier AI Taskforce (established April 2023) into a permanent government body.

title: UN HLAB-AI Interim Report: Governing AI for Humanity
abstract: Interim report identifying seven critical governance functions needed for inclusive international AI governance, with guiding principles that AI should be governed for the benefit of all, in the public interest, and in step with data governance. Invited global public comments through March 2024.

title: UK Music Five Key Principles on AI
abstract: Published September 18, 2023, with accompanying formal Policy Position Paper on AI. Five principles: (1) Creators' choice — creators should decide if and how they want their talent used; (2) Record keeping — tech providers must maintain auditable records of music ingested; (3) Human creativity — without human creativity there should be no copyright; (4) Labelling — AI-generated music must be labelled as such; (5) Protection of personality rights — a new personality right should protect the personality/image of songwriters and artists. Members include BPI, Musicians' Union, Music Publishers Association, PRS for Music, and others. Represents the collective position of the UK music industry including the Musicians' Union.

title: UN HRC Resolution: New Technologies and Human Rights
abstract: Human Rights Council resolution unanimously adopted at its 53rd session, requiring respect, protection, and promotion of human rights throughout the lifecycle of AI systems, including human rights impact assessments, due diligence, effective remedies, human oversight, transparency, and protection from AI-based discrimination.

title: UNICRI-INTERPOL Toolkit: Responsible AI in Law Enforcement
abstract: Joint UNICRI-INTERPOL toolkit comprising seven standalone resources providing law enforcement agencies with theoretical foundations on responsible AI innovation based on human rights law, ethics, and policing principles, and practical tools covering the full AI journey from technical foundations to organizational readiness and risk assessment.

title: EO 14110: Safe, Secure, Trustworthy AI
abstract: Established comprehensive new standards for AI safety and security, protecting privacy, advancing equity, and promoting innovation; required developers of powerful AI systems to share safety test results with the federal government before public release.

title: UnidosUS Comments on AI in Campaign Ads (FEC)
abstract: FEC regulatory comments responding to Public Citizen's petition for rulemaking on AI in campaign advertising. UnidosUS urges the FEC to clarify that existing prohibitions on fraudulent misrepresentation apply to deliberate AI deepfake use in campaign communications, with particular attention to protecting Hispanic voters from AI-generated disinformation. The organization supports targeted, constitutionally grounded regulation focused on deliberate misuse rather than broad AI restrictions.

title: UnidosUS Comments on OMB AI Memorandum
abstract: Regulatory comments submitted to the White House Office of Management and Budget on draft AI governance guidance. UnidosUS articulates three interconnected pillars for AI governance: Values (strong ethical guardrails against algorithmic bias), Voice (formal community engagement roles for impacted groups, especially Latino communities), and Investment (public capacity building for inclusive participation in technology governance). The organization warns that AI technologies carry inherent risks of scaling existing inequalities and that safeguards for civil liberties must accompany broader inclusive participation measures.

title: Unite the Union: AI Guide and Model Agreement for Officers and Representatives
abstract: Unite the Union's guide and model workplace agreement on AI provides a framework for union officers and representatives. Key provisions: nothing introduced without union agreement; full training for union representatives before any AI/AMS consideration; human contact mandatory for workplace decisions (no automated detriment without human review); transparency requirements; union access to relevant data. Unite holds that AI benefits society only if linked to strong worker/citizen protections and effective collective bargaining.

title: Less Discriminatory Algorithms
abstract: By Emily Black, Logan Koepke, Pauline Kim, Solon Barocas, and Mingwei Hsu. Argues that for any given algorithmic prediction task there exist multiple models of equivalent predictive performance but divergent demographic impact, and that disparate impact law should require organizations deploying AI in civil rights domains (housing, employment, credit) to actively search for and implement less discriminatory alternatives before deployment.

title: WCC Statement on Unregulated Development of AI
abstract: The WCC Central Committee, meeting in Geneva, adopted a formal statement expressing serious concern at the accelerating commercial development of generative AI without adequate regulatory oversight. The statement warns against AI as 'a modern form of idolatry,' identifies risks including algorithmic bias, disinformation, autonomous weapons, and threats to indigenous knowledge, and appeals to governments to establish binding regulatory regimes based on the precautionary principle.

title: WEF AI Governance Alliance 2023
abstract: WEF initiative launched June 15, 2023, uniting 200+ organizations to champion responsible global design and release of transparent and inclusive AI systems. Employs a 360-degree governance framework structured around three pillars: Harness past, Build present, and Plan future, with four-pronged approach (risk-based, rules-based, principles-based, outcomes-based).

title: WHO AI for Health LMMs Guidance 2023
abstract: WHO guidance specifically addressing large multi-modal AI models in healthcare, providing updated ethics and governance recommendations on risks and safeguards for generative AI tools used in clinical and public health settings.

title: WHO Regulatory Considerations on AI for Health
abstract: A 61-page WHO guidance document providing 18 key regulatory considerations and six areas for regulation of AI for health, developed in consultation with WHO's Working Group on Regulatory Considerations on AI for Health, with members drawn from regulatory authorities, policy makers, academics, and industry representatives. Intended as a resource for governments and regulatory authorities to develop or adapt national/regional guidance on AI-embedded medical devices.

title: WPA Position Statement on Global Digitalization in Mental Health Care
abstract: WPA position statement approved at the 23rd WPA General Assembly in Vienna (September 30, 2023), advocating for digital transformation of mental health and care systems. Reports on emerging evidence across Internet-based interventions, digital therapeutics, digital phenotyping, extended reality, and artificial intelligence in mental health contexts. Includes high-priority recommendations for global implementation of digital mental health solutions, with attention to equitable access across WPA member countries.

title: JW.org AI Blessing or Curse
abstract: Official JW.org educational article in the 'More Topics' series addressing AI from a biblical perspective. Acknowledges AI's dual nature: potential healthcare benefits vs. threats to safety, civil rights, privacy, and democratic institutions. Cites Proverbs 14:12 (human limitation in foreseeing consequences), Ecclesiastes 2:18-19 (loss of control over creations), and divine promises of protection. Takes balanced approach noting both risks and potential benefits while directing readers to biblical guidance rather than specific AI policy recommendations.

title: ACFTU AI Application Action Plan
abstract: 全国总工会广泛应用人工智能行动方案，2024年试点推出'职工之家'智能APP，2025年全面运行，涵盖智能法律援助、维权服务、创业支持等。

title: ACLU Comment on Senate AI Working Group Roadmap
abstract: Formal comment submitted by ACLU Senior Policy Counsel Cody Venzke on the Senate AI Working Group's policy roadmap. The ACLU criticizes the roadmap for doubling down on defense and intelligence AI applications 'with virtually nothing mandating robust safeguards' for surveillance, border screening, or lethal force decisions. Calls for mandatory civil rights standards, algorithmic audits, and human alternatives. Concludes the roadmap 'sets us on the wrong path.'

title: ACM TechBrief: Trusted AI
abstract: A technical policy brief arguing that trustworthiness mechanisms and measures advanced in AI regulations and standards may not actually increase public trust in AI. Contends that AI must not only be engineered to be trustworthy but must actually be trusted, and that fostering trust requires policymakers to demonstrate accountability to public concerns. Co-authored by John T. Richards (IBM) and Bran Knowles (Lancaster University). Published as ACM TechBrief Issue 9.

title: ACS Publications AI Best Practices and Policies
abstract: Formal institutional policy by ACS Publications governing the responsible use of AI tools in scientific publishing, last updated December 13, 2024. Establishes binding expectations for authors, reviewers, and editors across ACS journals. Key elements: authors must disclose all AI tool usage in the Acknowledgment section; AI tools cannot qualify for authorship; AI-generated content must be disclosed with description of when and how tools were used; reviewers are prohibited from disclosing manuscripts to third-party AI services (breach of confidentiality); editors may reject submissions with 'too extensive' AI use for substantive commentary. Grounded in existing ACS Ethical Guidelines to Publication of Chemical Research.

title: ACTU Congress 2024: Artificial Intelligence Policy
abstract: Policy resolution adopted at ACTU Congress (Adelaide, June 4-6, 2024). Asserts AI should mean more jobs, higher pay, and improved productivity with benefits shared with workers. Demands legally binding mechanisms for AI regulation, mandatory employer consultation before AI introduction, transparency, privacy and anti-discrimination protections, and enforcement of consultation obligations. 'Nothing about us, without us.' Workers must be involved in every debate about AI.

title: AFGE Report of the Ad Hoc Committee on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Formal report of the AFGE Ad Hoc Committee on Artificial Intelligence, established by AFGE President Everett Kelley to study the impact of AI on federal agencies' missions and workforces. The report provides analysis and recommendations on contract bargaining language for AI, legislative and policy positions the union should support, and training that AFGE members should receive on new technologies. It includes research into member and leader attitudes about AI, addresses concerns about job displacement and technology failures, and advocates for responsible AI integration that prioritizes employee welfare, transparency, accountability, and fairness in the deployment of AI technologies across the federal government.

title: AFSCME Resolution on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Passed at AFSCME's 46th International Convention, this resolution commits the union to advocating for worker involvement in AI development and deployment through collective bargaining, supporting robust policies protecting workers' civil liberties, demanding meaningful decision-making roles for workers across all stages of AI implementation, ensuring equitable benefits sharing and retraining for displaced workers, and collaborating with other unions on AI-related labor challenges in public sector workplaces.

title: AFT Commonsense Guardrails for Advanced Technology in Schools
abstract: AFT policy document issued June 2024 establishing commonsense guardrails for the use of advanced technology, including AI, in schools. Emphasizes empowering students to be responsible and innovative in their technology use, fostering collaborative responsibility among stakeholders (educators, administrators, parents, and technology developers), and ensuring environmental sustainability in technology adoption. Announces launch of the National Academy for AI Instruction in partnership with Microsoft, OpenAI, Anthropic, and the United Federation of Teachers.

title: AFT Resolution on Artificial Intelligence (2024)
abstract: Comprehensive 2024 resolution addressing AI integration across education, healthcare, and public services. The AFT affirms that 'critical decision-making must remain with teachers, paraprofessionals, higher education faculty, healthcare professionals, and public service workers.' The federation pledges to ensure AI development prioritizes transparency, worker rights protection, and equitable access. The resolution establishes that 'the impact of AI in the workplace is a mandatory subject of bargaining,' enabling unions to negotiate protections against worker displacement. It commits to member empowerment through training and research initiatives, and calls for robust regulations addressing privacy, bias, misinformation, and deepfakes. Reaffirms that technological advancement must 'serve to enrich and enhance the professional endeavors of educators and staff, healthcare workers and public employees.'

title: AHA: AI and Cardiovascular Imaging
abstract: This review examines where AI adds value to cardiovascular imaging at every step of the patient journey, from selecting the appropriate test to optimizing image acquisition, interpreting results for classification and diagnosis, and predicting risk for major adverse cardiac events. Covers AI tools applied to cardiac computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and positron emission tomography.

title: AHA: Use of AI in Heart Disease
abstract: This statement outlines the current state of the art on the use of AI algorithms and data science in the diagnosis, classification, and treatment of cardiovascular disease. It identifies best practices, gaps, and challenges for stakeholders and sets out how digital tools may provide clinical insights, address bias in clinical studies, and facilitate education and implementation science to improve cardiovascular and stroke outcomes.

title: AHAD-AI-Hinduphobia-Detection
abstract: Official press release announcing AHAD's partnership with Tattwa.ai to launch AI-driven tools for detecting anti-Hindu bias. Tools include HinduHate Detector (multidimensional bias analysis using deep learning and 2,000+ rules), a profiling database of commentators on Hindu issues, a 360-degree political view tool, and a forthcoming X/Twitter feed analyzer. This is a product/initiative announcement rather than an ethics position statement, but represents a Hindu organization's institutional engagement with AI technology.

title: Sweden AI Commission: Roadmap for Sweden (SOU 2025:12)
abstract: The Swedish AI Commission's final report (SOU 2025:12), presented November 2024 to the Ministry of Finance, proposes 75 measures grouped under five focus areas: political leadership and strategic coordination; AI education and competence building; public sector transformation through a shared AI workshop; world-class research infrastructure including SEK 1.8 billion in HPC investment; and innovation ecosystems. Diagnoses Sweden's drop to 25th in the Global AI Index as evidence of insufficient national coordination.

title: ANMF Submission on Adopting AI
abstract: Formal submission to the Australian Senate Select Committee on Adopting AI. Federal Secretary Annie Butler and Associate Professor Micah Peters appeared at hearing on 17 July 2024. Generally positive on AI's potential in healthcare but cautions that adoption is extremely complex and must be carefully overseen. AI should never substitute for human-provided care but be a tool for delivering person-centred care. Identifies potential applications: streamlining automatable tasks, enhancing diagnostics, remote health monitoring, health chatbots, drug discovery, treatment planning, risk stratification, and education. Chief concerns: workforce redundancies and dehumanisation of healthcare. Calls for safeguarding measures developed in consultation with consumers and key stakeholders to ensure safe, controlled, and effective integration.

title: ANPD NT 27/2024 — Personal Data in Generative AI Training
abstract: Nota técnica da ANPD sobre o tratamento de dados pessoais de terceiros com a finalidade de desenvolver modelos de IA generativa, analisando as bases legais para esse tratamento nos termos da LGPD, princípios de minimização de dados e obrigações de conformidade para desenvolvedores de IA.

title: ANPD Radar Tecnológico 3 — Generative AI
abstract: Terceiro volume da série Radar Tecnológico da ANPD, dedicado à Inteligência Artificial Generativa. Produzido pela Coordenação-Geral de Tecnologia e Pesquisa (CGTP), identifica riscos à privacidade e proteção de dados, analisa casos reais de uso no setor público, saúde e financeiro, e oferece orientações preliminares sobre a aplicação da LGPD a sistemas de IA generativa.

title: APA Statement on AI and Psychology
abstract: APA policy statement recognizing AI's transformative impacts across robotics, chatbots, machine learning, and automated decision-making. Identifies three domains for psychology's role: shaping AI's societal impact, using AI to promote health and well-being, and addressing ethics and privacy. Passed by APA Council of Representatives 156-2.

title: APEC AI Governance Cooperation (2024)
abstract: APEC's 2024 policy feature articulating the rationale for regional AI governance cooperation among the 21 member economies, addressing regulatory interoperability, standards alignment, and the Sub-Committee on Standards and Conformance's role in implementing international AI standards for safe adoption.

title: APTA Digital Health Technologies, Digital Therapeutics, and AI in Physical Therapist Practice
abstract: APTA policy (HOD P07-24-12-11) adopted September 23, 2024 asserting that digital health technologies, digital therapeutics, and AI have significant potential to strengthen physical therapist practice by expanding access, enhancing care delivery models, promoting safety, reducing administrative burden, and improving patient outcomes.

title: APTA Ethical and Effective Integration of AI Across Physical Therapist Practice
abstract: APTA policy (HOD P07-24-10-09) adopted September 23, 2024 by the House of Delegates, affirming support for ethical development and integration of AI that reduces administrative burden and enhances physical therapist practice, education, and research for the best interest of patients, clients, the profession, and society. Emphasizes responsible AI implementation that addresses practical operational challenges while maintaining core professional values.

title: APTA Inappropriate Use of Artificial Intelligence by Payers
abstract: APTA policy (HOD P07-24-11-10) adopted September 23, 2024 opposing the inappropriate use of artificial intelligence by payers to deny or restrict access to, or reduce payment for, physical therapist services or other medically necessary services.

title: ARS Code of Conduct for AI Developers
abstract: Published May 3, 2024 (updated June 2, 2024). ARS issued a Code of Conduct establishing ground rules for AI developers to mitigate harm to visual artists' livelihoods. Key positions: mandatory disclosure requiring AI developers to register copyrighted works used in training with the U.S. Copyright Office; voluntary collective licensing requiring active consent; support for Fairly Trained certification of AI systems. Also co-drafted comments with Copyright Alliance to the Copyright Office, co-signed letters to U.S. senators supporting extended collective licensing, and co-signed the CISAC global creators' letter representing millions of creators. ARS represents over 120,000 visual artists.

title: ASA Statement on Ethical AI Principles for Statistical Practitioners
abstract: Formal ASA board statement adopted October 8, 2024, providing guidance for statistical practitioners on the ethical use of artificial intelligence. Organized around three main themes — Accountability, Transparency, and Fairness — the statement draws on the ASA Ethical Guidelines for Statistical Practice (2022) and references NIST's AI Risk Management Framework, the US DOJ's Guidance on AI and Civil Rights, and the EEOC's Algorithmic Fairness Initiative. Aims to address challenges specific to statisticians implementing AI in ways that are safe and responsible.

title: ASCE Policy Statement 573 — AI and Engineering Responsibility
abstract: Adopted by ASCE's Board of Direction on July 18, 2024 (following task committee approval February 15, 2024 and public policy committee approval May 1, 2024), this policy statement asserts that professional engineers must retain full accountability for all phases of civil engineering projects—planning, design, construction, operations, and maintenance. ASCE recognizes AI's potential to enhance efficiency, innovation, and sustainability in civil engineering, but maintains that AI cannot replace the professional judgment, training, experience, or accountability of a licensed Professional Engineer in protecting public health, safety, and welfare. The statement notes that rapid AI advancement is outpacing regulatory frameworks.

title: ASEAN Guide on AI Governance 2024
abstract: Non-binding ASEAN guide endorsed by ASEAN Digital Ministers in February 2024, establishing common principles (transparency, fairness, security, reliability, privacy, accountability, human-centricity) and best practices for designing, developing, and deploying traditional AI in commercial applications across the region.

title: ASME AI and DEI Statement
abstract: Published November 1, 2024 by ASME Executive Director/CEO Thomas Costabile, P.E., FASME, this official statement addresses the ethical implications of AI for diversity, equity, and inclusion in engineering. It identifies key risks including bias in AI systems trained on publicly available data, perpetuation of workplace discrimination through biased hiring algorithms, and data privacy threats. It also identifies AI benefits for DEI including bias detection in workplace materials and enhanced accessibility. ASME commits to incorporating AI responsibly while upholding ethical standards and societal values including DEI. References Harvard Business Review's 13 principles for responsible AI use.

title: AU Continental AI Strategy
abstract: Endorsed by the AU Executive Council during its 45th session in Accra, Ghana in July 2024, this strategy promotes an Africa-centric, development-focused approach to AI across all 55 member states, targeting $1.5 trillion in economic contribution by 2030 while emphasizing ethical, responsible, and equitable AI practices aligned with Agenda 2063.

title: African Union Continental AI Strategy (2024)
abstract: Endorsed by the African Union Executive Council at its 45th Ordinary Session (Accra, Ghana, July 2024), this Africa-centric strategy promotes ethical, responsible, and equitable AI development through five focus areas: harnessing AI benefits, building capabilities, minimising risks, stimulating investment, and fostering cooperation, with implementation from 2025 to 2030.

title: African Union Continental AI Strategy 2024
abstract: Pan-African AI strategy endorsed by the African Union Executive Council in July 2024, providing a development-focused, Africa-centric approach across five pillars: harnessing AI benefits, building capabilities, minimizing risks, stimulating investment, and fostering cooperation, with implementation from 2025 to 2030.

title: African Union Digital Compact (2024)
abstract: Adopted at the 2nd Extraordinary Session of the Specialized Technical Committee on Communication and ICT (June 2024), this companion document to the Continental AI Strategy outlines Africa's unified approach to digital transformation including AI, data governance, cybersecurity, and digital infrastructure to support Africa's contribution to the Global Digital Compact.

title: AlgorithmWatch EU AI Act Human Rights Assessment
abstract: AlgorithmWatch presents a critical assessment of the EU's final AI Act after three years of advocacy, concluding it falls short of meaningful human rights protections due to transparency loopholes, surveillance exceptions for law enforcement, industry self-regulation in risk classification, and inadequate environmental measures. The organization identifies implementation through member states as the key arena for maximizing rights protections.

title: Apple Responsible AI Principles
abstract: Apple's four Responsible AI principles — Empower users with intelligent tools, Represent users authentically, Design with care, and Protect privacy — guide development of Apple Intelligence and all AI features. Apple emphasizes on-device processing, Private Cloud Compute, and a commitment not to use personal user data for training foundation models.

title: Australia ADM Consultation Paper
abstract: This consultation paper sought views on transparency measures and safeguards needed for a new whole-of-government legislative framework governing automated decision-making (ADM) by Australian Government agencies, following Recommendation 17.1 of the Royal Commission into the Robodebt Scheme. It proposed improved governance, legal frameworks, and privacy protections to ensure accountable and rights-respecting use of ADM in public services.

title: Australia National Framework Assurance of AI in Government
abstract: This framework establishes cornerstones and practices for AI assurance across Australian Government agencies, providing a consistent approach to assessing and mitigating AI risks grounded in Australia's AI Ethics Principles. Agreed by the Data and Digital Ministers Meeting on 21 June 2024, it is designed to complement the DTA's Policy for the Responsible Use of AI in Government and the AI Ethics Framework.

title: Australia Policy for Responsible Use of AI in Government
abstract: This policy sets binding requirements for Australian Government agencies on the responsible use of AI, emphasising risk assessment, transparency, human oversight, and accountability in AI-enabled decision-making. Version 1.1 took effect 1 September 2024; Version 2.0 (effective December 2025) expands requirements in line with the Voluntary AI Safety Standard and the National Framework for AI Assurance.

title: Australia Voluntary AI Safety Standard
abstract: Australia's Voluntary AI Safety Standard establishes 10 guardrails covering governance, risk management, data protection, testing, human oversight, transparency, contestability, supply-chain accountability, recordkeeping, and stakeholder engagement, applicable to all organisations in the AI supply chain. Released alongside a proposals paper on mandatory guardrails for high-risk AI settings, it provides a practical baseline for safe and responsible AI development and deployment across both public and private sectors.

title: Bits of Freedom AI Regulation in Force Statement
abstract: Bits of Freedom acknowledges the EU AI Act entering into force on August 1, 2024 with qualified optimism, noting that while the final law contains opportunities for positive change, civil society organizations are disappointed that human rights are not sufficiently protected. The organization outlines five key recommendations for implementation: robust human rights-respecting enforcement, proactive measures addressing regulatory gaps, additional national restrictions on harmful AI, swift action against non-compliant systems, and transparent inclusive policy processes.

title: BYU Graduate Studies AI Statement
abstract: Graduate-level AI use policy at BYU (LDS Church-affiliated university). Requires AI use to be consistent with Honor Code and Academic Honesty Policy, pre-approved by graduate committee or faculty advisor, and disclosed in final products. Covers coursework, research, theses, dissertations, presentations, and publications. Violations may result in disciplinary action including dismissal. Directs to genai.byu.edu for broader guidance. Emphasizes data security (no institutional data in GenAI services), proper attribution of AI-generated content, and fact-checking requirements.

title: BYU Honors Program AI Policy
abstract: Approved June 11, 2024. BYU Honors Program specific AI policy aligned with the university-wide Academic Use of AI guidance and Honor Code. Establishes principles for responsible AI use in honors coursework.

title: BMA Principles for AI in Healthcare
abstract: BMA framework supporting safe, effective, ethical, and equitable adoption of AI in UK healthcare. Calls for robust assessment for safety and efficacy, stronger governance and regulation, staff and patient involvement in development, and clarification of legal liability. Emphasizes that AI is a tool only as good as its implementation.

title: Business Roundtable AI Policy Recommendations
abstract: Business Roundtable's formal AI policy recommendations comprising three white papers: (1) Defining and Addressing AI Risks — advocates context-specific, risk-based approaches focusing on high-risk deployments rather than broad sector classifications; (2) Promoting American Leadership in AI Innovation — supports public-private partnerships, expanded government dataset access, and voluntary industry standards through multistakeholder collaboration; (3) Safely Harnessing Synthetic Content's Potential — promotes content authentication initiatives, technical and human-centered risk management, and remediation opportunities before penalties. Led by Technology Committee Chair Adena Friedman (Nasdaq CEO).

title: Bangladesh National AI Policy 2024 (Draft)
abstract: Bangladesh's draft National AI Policy 2024, published by the ICT Division in April 2024, establishes a national vision to integrate AI into the development agenda around six core principles: social equity, transparency and accountability, safety and robustness, sustainability, partnership and collaboration, and human-centered AI. The policy proposes a National Artificial Intelligence Centre of Excellence (NAICE) under a National AI Advisory Council and was subject to stakeholder consultations with UNESCO and UNDP.

title: Bar Council Guidance on ChatGPT and Generative AI (January 2024)
organization: Bar Council of England and Wales
abstract: The Bar Council's Information Technology Panel issued guidance for barristers on the use of ChatGPT and similar large language model systems. The guidance identifies key risks including hallucinations, information disorder, bias in training data, and confidentiality breaches from inputting privileged information into AI systems. It concludes that there is nothing inherently improper about using reliable AI tools when deployed responsibly and with proper understanding. Barristers remain fully responsible for all work product and must verify AI outputs. Updated in November 2025 to reflect High Court rulings on AI misuse.

title: Biola Biblical Principles for AI
abstract: Biola University's AI Lab published 'Biblical Principles for Understanding and Using Artificial Intelligence,' a foundational ethical framework offering direction on how Christians can engage with AI aligned with scripture. Created with input from multiple Biola schools, this document serves as the university's formal ethical framework for addressing AI from a Christian perspective, anchoring AI ethics in biblical rather than purely secular frameworks.

title: Biola Christian AI Manifesto
abstract: Biola University's AI Lab published a 'Christian AI Manifesto' as a collaborative position statement on AI and Christian values, alongside 'Biblical Principles for Understanding and Using Artificial Intelligence.' The AI Lab's mission is to serve as a leading resource center for understanding and responsibly utilizing AI 'in a moral and ethical manner, rooted in Christian values.' Led by Dr. Bourgeois, the Lab integrates scriptural principles into AI development and application frameworks.

title: Brennan Center: AI Counterterrorism PCLOB Comment
abstract: Joint comment from the Brennan Center for Justice and the ACLU urging the Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board (PCLOB) to undertake a deep-dive examination of the use of AI in counterterrorism and related national security programs. Raises concerns about civil liberties, civil rights, and privacy impacts of AI in national security contexts.

title: Brennan Center: Fighting AI-Backed Voter Suppression
abstract: Examines how AI tools could amplify voter suppression efforts. Documents AI-related threats including voice-cloning technology for misleading robocalls, AI-powered systems for filing mass voter challenges, and generative AI's capacity to spread election misinformation at scale. Proposes seven policy solutions including amending deceptive practices laws, regulating AI-generated content, strengthening robocall oversight, and protecting election infrastructure. Author: Mekela Panditharatne.

title: Brennan Center: OMB Draft AI Guidance Comments
abstract: Formal institutional comments submitted to OMB regarding draft AI governance guidance for federal agencies (submitted December 5, 2023; published January 8, 2024). Recommends strengthening safeguards for AI systems affecting safety and rights, removing intelligence community exemptions, clarifying public disclosure requirements, establishing data evaluation standards, and tightening oversight mechanisms.

title: Brennan Center: OMB Federal AI Procurement Comment
abstract: Joint comment from Brennan Center, EPIC, EFF, Fight for the Future, TechTonic Justice, and UnidosUS responding to OMB's request for input on 'Responsible Procurement of Artificial Intelligence in Government.' Advocates for procurement guidelines that enhance agency awareness and public visibility of AI systems purchased from private vendors, and strengthen how agencies manage rights and safety concerns throughout the procurement lifecycle.

title: CGI.br RES/2024/049 — AI as Priority Theme 2024–2027
abstract: Resolução do CGI.br que designa a Inteligência Artificial como um dos sete temas prioritários para as ações do comitê no período 2024-2027. Define IA como sistemas que produzem resultados a partir de grandes volumes de dados, permitindo aprendizagem para predições, classificações, recomendações e decisões com impactos sociais variados. Compromete o CGI.br a estudos, debates multissetoriais e ações colaborativas de governança de IA.

title: CBA Ethics of AI for the Legal Practitioner Toolkit
abstract: The Canadian Bar Association's AI ethics toolkit, led by Professor Karen Eltis of the University of Ottawa Faculty of Law, addresses the ethical, professional, and practical implications of using generative AI in legal practice. The toolkit urges lawyers to use AI 'as a tool, not a crutch,' to consider disclosing AI use to clients or courts, and to remain clear about technology's limitations. It covers confidentiality, intellectual property, data protection, and protocols for reviewing AI-generated work. The guidance emphasizes that lawyers bear proactive responsibility for managing AI-related risks and cannot rely solely on client consent to satisfy their professional obligations.

title: Color of Change Black Tech Agenda 2024
abstract: Color of Change's comprehensive policy agenda for ensuring technology and AI systems help rather than harm Black communities. The 2024 update builds on the 2022 original by addressing emerging challenges including protection of Black workers from unfair AI systems, ensuring Black artists receive fair compensation when AI uses their work, and preventing Big Tech from building polluting infrastructure in Black neighborhoods. Six policy pillars address digital access, individual and community control, economic fairness, worker protections, and creator rights. Reflects the organization's sustained 'Create Digital Safety' initiative and campaign for robust AI regulations that promote equity and prioritize safety, privacy, and civil rights.

title: CEC Colombia AI Reflection Document 2024
abstract: Formal document issued by the Conferencia Episcopal de Colombia (CEC) through its Commission on Doctrine and Theological Committee. Originally prepared as a reflection guide for bishops at the CXVII Plenary Assembly (July 1-5, 2024), then publicly released due to its relevance for evangelization. Addresses both positive aspects of AI adoptable at social and ecclesial levels, and principal risks regarding ethical, formative, and regulatory dimensions. Draws heavily on Pope Francis's teachings on artificial intelligence. Includes two annexes: a chronology of AI development and a glossary of key terms with ethical analysis. The document was developed under the responsibility of Father Raúl Ortiz Toro during the period 2021-2024, when Monsignor Óscar José Vélez Isaza (Bishop of Valledupar) served as president of the Episcopal Commission on Doctrine.

title: Canadian Council of Churches Signs Rome Call
abstract: On October 15, 2024, the Faith and Life Sciences Reference Group of the Canadian Council of Churches was accepted as a signatory to the Rome Call for AI Ethics, with endorsement from the Governing Board. The group committed to ongoing theological and ethical reflection on AI, educating Canadian Christians on AI ethics challenges, and engaging the World Council of Churches' Faith and Science Working Group to expand Rome Call signatories.

title: CFA: CFPB Should Advance Ethical AI in Financial Services (2024)
abstract: CFA statement by Brad Blower and Adam Rust urging the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to issue concrete guidance on preventing discriminatory AI use in lending. Argues the CFPB should move beyond general statements to provide clear guidance on testing methodologies for model fairness. Proposes publishing anonymized case studies in supervisory reports demonstrating fair-lending compliant practices as a middle path. Contends ambiguity currently disadvantages smaller institutions and that better guidance could democratize ethical AI adoption and advance financial inclusion.

title: CFPB Circular 2024-06: Algorithmic Scores in Employment Decisions
abstract: CFPB circular establishing that algorithmic worker scoring systems and background dossiers used in employment decisions typically qualify as consumer reports under FCRA, subjecting providers to consumer reporting agency obligations. Extends CFPB's regulatory reach over AI-based employment assessment tools.

title: CHT Framework for Incentivizing Responsible AI
abstract: The Center for Humane Technology's liability-based framework for regulating the riskiest AI systems, combining products liability with consumer safety regulation for 'inherently dangerous AI.' Proposes reporting requirements including an 'AI Data Sheet,' limited liability protections for compliant parties, and enforcement through both private right of action and government oversight, aimed at incentivizing safe development while preserving innovation.

title: NRC-AI-Abuja-Communique
abstract: Communique from a meeting of Nigerian Christian and Muslim leaders in Abuja (November 19-21, 2024), co-organized by Future of Life Institute. Christian Council of Nigeria (CCN) and Jama'atu Nasril Islam (JNI) leaders agreed on the need for religious leaders to take the lead in shaping Nigeria's AI direction, including through education and governance. The communique was featured in the Nigerian Tribune and Vanguard. This was the foundational meeting that led to the formation of the Nigeria Religious Coalition on Artificial Intelligence (NRC-AI) and the subsequent 'God in the Engine' report.

title: Côte d'Ivoire National AI and Data Governance Strategy 2030
abstract: La SNIA 2030 de la Côte d'Ivoire, officiellement validée le 13 décembre 2024 à Abidjan-Plateau, couvre la période 2024-2030. Élaborée par 60 experts sur huit mois, elle repose sur trois piliers (investissement, inclusion, gouvernance), six axes stratégiques, quinze objectifs spécifiques et 109 projets et réformes. Dotée d'un budget de 912 milliards FCFA sur cinq ans, elle prévoit la création d'un label 'Safe IA' pour encadrer légalement et éthiquement le développement des solutions IA, et la création d'une Agence de l'Intelligence Artificielle.
