title: CNBB 61st Assembly AI Discussion 2024
abstract: The 61st General Assembly of the CNBB (April 15-19, 2024) dedicated sessions to 'Artificial Intelligence and Impacts on Pastoral Action.' A support text was presented addressing AI's impact on pastoral areas, based on philosopher Byung-Chul Han's concept of the information regime. Experts highlighted sociocultural changes, ethical dilemmas, new doctrinal reflections, new missionary options, and changes in Catholic religious ethos. Warnings about information bubbles, radicalization, polarization, and misinformation. Called for orientation regulations establishing ethical limits across all AI development stages, transparency in data collection, and concerns about dehumanization risks.

title: CNIL First AI Recommendations
abstract: Following a public consultation that received 43 contributions, the CNIL published seven practical recommendation sheets to help professionals reconcile AI innovation with GDPR obligations. The guidance covers legal basis for data processing, impact assessments, privacy-by-design, and evaluation of whether AI models fall under data protection requirements.

title: Chiefs of Ontario First Nations and AI Research Paper
abstract: Comprehensive 57-page research paper by Dr. Benjamin Wald, published by the Chiefs of Ontario Research and Data Management Sector. Covers: (1) Overview of AI technology including history, symbolic AI, and machine learning; (2) AI legislation and regulation including Canada's AIDA, UK, US, and EU approaches; (3) Discrimination by AI including algorithmic bias, training data bias, and risk factors; (4) AI and data sovereignty including First Nations data sovereignty, training data, generative AI outputs, and AI identifying First Nations; (5) Opportunities for First Nations in AI including language revitalization, support for governance, and environmental preservation. Funded by the Ministry of Health. Includes glossary and extensive references.

title: COMECE Statement on EU AI Act
abstract: Official COMECE statement welcoming the EU AI Act as the first robust EU regulatory framework for AI. Drafted by the COMECE Commission on Ethics under Bishop Anton Jamnik. Emphasizes fundamental rights protection, human-centric AI design, opposes assigning legal personality to robots/AI, endorses liability measures, and aligns with Pope Francis' call for an international legally binding treaty on AI governance.

title: Costa Rica ENIA 2024-2027
abstract: Costa Rica's National AI Strategy for 2024-2027, launched October 24, 2024, making it the first country in Central America to have a public AI policy. The strategy promotes responsible and ethical AI use centered on human dignity, transparency, and equity, and establishes plans for a National Center of Excellence in AI and 5G network expansion. Costa Rica also assumed a co-facilitator role with Spain in the UN inter-governmental AI governance process.

title: Sant'Egidio Paris AI Ethics Forum 2024
abstract: Forum titled 'Giving Ethics to Artificial Intelligence' held September 24, 2024, at the International Meeting for Peace in Paris. Speakers included Michel de Virville (honorary director, College des Bernardins), Archbishop Andrew John (Wales), Xing Qu (Deputy Director General, UNESCO), Chief Rabbi Jaron Engelmayer (Vienna), and Milena Santerini (Council of Europe rapporteur on racism). Demonstrated that an ecumenical and interreligious approach to ethical issues regarding AI is possible. Referenced the Rome Call for AI Ethics (2020) and Pope Francis's concept of 'algor-ethics' — the need for an ethical approach to algorithms and AI. Engelmayer noted AI lacks 'empathy, compassion, love, caring for others' which constitute emotional intelligence. UNESCO's 2021 Recommendation on Ethics of AI was cited as a framework. No separate formal declaration was issued; the forum served as a dialogue platform within Sant'Egidio's broader peace meeting.

title: CTF-FCE AI in Public Education Policy Brief
abstract: Policy brief calling for urgent regulatory action on AI in Canadian K-12 public education. Identifies a critical gap in Canadian AI legislation — federal Bill C-27 lacks education-specific protections for minors. Calls on federal government and Council of Ministers of Education to enact enforceable policies protecting student privacy and data security, regulate classroom AI use, and promote responsible and ethical AI systems. Emphasizes that the role of teachers in creating caring, human-centred classrooms must be prioritized in all AI policy development.

title: CWU AI Awareness Campaign — Making AI Work For Workers
abstract: The Communication Workers Union launched its 'AI Awareness: Making AI Work For Workers' campaign addressing AI deployment in telecoms and financial services sectors, where over 50 systems have been identified that could affect job security and working conditions. The campaign rests on three pillars: building representative competency to identify and negotiate AI impacts, establishing bargaining positions including a model collective agreement, and mobilizing members through awareness campaigns. Key demands include worker voice in all AI rollouts, fair distribution of AI-generated benefits through wages and training, and robust safeguards against bias and discrimination. A 2024 CWU survey found 79.3% of members unaware of new workplace technologies and 80% distrusting technological advances.

title: Chile National AI Policy 2024 Update
abstract: The 2024 revision of Chile's National AI Policy, which reaffirms the original ten-year timeframe and extends its applicability through 2031. The update aligns the policy with developments in international AI governance and UNESCO recommendations following Chile's completion of the Readiness Assessment Methodology.

title: CoE CM/Rec(2024)5 AI in Prisons
abstract: Council of Europe recommendation establishing nine basic principles for AI use by prison and probation services, including legality, good governance, transparency, traceability, and right to human review of decisions. Covers safety/security use cases, offender management, risk assessment, and staff management.

title: CoE Framework Convention on AI (CETS 225)
abstract: The world's first internationally legally binding treaty on AI, adopted 17 May 2024 and opened for signature 5 September 2024. Requires that AI lifecycle activities comply with human rights, democracy, and the rule of law; covers eight chapters and 26 articles; is technology-neutral. Signatories include the EU, USA, UK, Canada, Japan, and others.

title: Council of Europe AI Convention CETS 225
abstract: World's first internationally legally binding AI treaty, adopted 17 May 2024 and opened for signature 5 September 2024, aimed at ensuring AI systems are fully consistent with human rights, democracy, and the rule of law; entered into force 1 November 2025 after ratification by the UK, France, Norway, and others.

title: Consumer Reports AI Policy Recommendations
abstract: Formal policy guide published by Consumer Reports outlining institutional positions for legislators and regulators. Six core recommendations: (1) require disclosure when algorithmic tools are used in consequential decisions (loans, housing, employment, insurance); (2) require companies to explain adverse algorithmic decisions to consumers; (3) mandate access for public interest researchers; (4) prohibit algorithmic discrimination; (5) require pre-deployment and post-deployment third-party bias and accuracy testing; (6) require risk and impact assessments from AI developers. Framed around consumer protection in high-stakes algorithmic decision-making.

title: Dar al-Ifta AI Strategy for Moderation
abstract: Position paper by Dr. Ibrahim Negm, Secretary-General of the General Secretariat for Fatwa Authorities Worldwide, presented at the annual meeting of Arab think tanks at the Arab League. Asserts AI is a strategic opportunity to foster moderation and disseminate balanced thought while combating extremist ideologies. Proposes: developing AI-driven platforms for reliable religious guidance; integrating AI into religious education and fatwa issuance; promoting interfaith dialogue through technological innovation; positioning AI as an instrument for peace and coexistence.

title: DGB Discussion Paper: Regulatory Framework for AI in the Workplace
abstract: DGB-Diskussionspapier des Bundesvorstands (30.9.2024) fordert nationalen Rechtsrahmen für KI in der Arbeitswelt, verpflichtende betriebliche KI-Folgenabschätzungen, Stärkung der Mitbestimmung und tarifvertraglicher Autonomie. Begrüßt EU-KI-Verordnung, kritisiert unzureichende Hochrisikoeinstufungen für Arbeitskontext.

title: DGB Short Assessment of the EU AI Act
abstract: DGB-Kurzbewertung der EU-KI-Verordnung (2.5.2024). Begrüßt risikobasierten Ansatz und Transparenzregeln. Positiv bewertet: Öffnungsklausel für nationale arbeitsrechtliche Regelungen. Fordert ergänzenden nationalen Regulierungsrahmen.

title: Church of Norway Safe AI Use Guidance
abstract: Advisory guidance published December 2024 (updated May 2025) on safe AI use in the Church of Norway. Emphasizes using internal AI services (Microsoft Copilot in M365) rather than external free services like ChatGPT. Requires privacy impact assessments (DPIA) before AI implementation, prohibits fully automated decisions under data protection regulations, and mandates human participation in all AI-assisted decision-making. Warns that information shared with external AI services is used for model training.

title: Diyanet 7th Din Surasi AI Communique
abstract: Final communique of the 7th Din Surasi (Religious Council) held by Turkey's Presidency of Religious Affairs (Diyanet) on November 26-28, 2024 in Ankara. Theme: 'Dijitalesen Dunyada Diyanet Hizmetleri: Etkililik-Verimlilik-Surdurulebilirlik' (Religious Services in a Digitalized World: Effectiveness-Efficiency-Sustainability). 328 participants across 5 commissions adopted 30 decisions. AI-relevant decisions include: (Decision 4) establish an AI-based integrated platform enabling call centers, content tracking, publication analysis, and AI assistants; (Decision 6) develop AI chatbots containing authentic religious information and respecting cultural sensitivities; (Decision 7) use NLP and modern technologies to structure religious content into machine-readable knowledge networks; additional decisions on AI-assisted religious publications and multilingual service delivery. Diyanet President Erbas announced the communique publicly.

title: Diyanet 7th Din Surasi Final Communique
abstract: Final communique of Turkey's 7th Religious Council (Din Şurası) themed 'Religious Services in a Digitalized World: Effectiveness-Efficiency-Sustainability.' 328 participants across 5 commissions. AI recommendations include: establish an AI-based integrated platform for religious services; develop AI chatbots with accurate religious information respecting cultural sensitivities; restructure religious content as machine-readable data ontology; enhance fatwa databases with semantic search; integrate digital literacy into Diyanet Academy curricula.

title: Diyanet Critique of KuranGPT
abstract: Turkey's Directorate of Religious Affairs (Diyanet) criticized KuranGPT, an AI application that provides Quran-based answers, stating that Islam is not comprised solely of the Quran and that it draws from multiple sciences including hadith and jurisprudence (fiqh). Warned that KuranGPT could be 'misleading' because it excludes the broader corpus of Islamic legal and scholarly tradition. Islam without fiqh and hadith remains 'incomplete.'

title: EU AI Pact 2024
abstract: Voluntary industry commitment mechanism launched by the European Commission to encourage early adoption of AI Act obligations before the regulation's full application. Organisations pledge to implement AI governance practices in advance, particularly for general-purpose AI and high-risk systems.

title: Episcopal Church 2024 AI Resolutions
abstract: The Episcopal Church's 81st General Convention passed two resolutions on AI: one reaffirming digital age principles and calling on AI developers to implement safety guardrails, transparent ethics policies, and independent reviews; and a second creating a 12-member Task Force on AI and Intellectual Property to report findings to the next General Convention in 2027.

title: Disability-Inclusive AI Act: Implementation Guide (v1)
abstract: EDF's comprehensive implementation guide for disability organizations to understand and monitor the EU AI Act. Covers provisions most relevant to persons with disabilities, including monitoring compliance with accessibility requirements, advocating for bias testing and transparency. Includes accessible formats (Easy Read). Described as a living document open to updates.

title: EDPB Opinion 28/2024 on AI Models
abstract: EDPB opinion addressing when AI models trained on personal data can be considered anonymous, conditions under which legitimate interest can serve as a legal basis for AI development, and implications of unlawfully processed training data — providing key GDPR guidance for AI developers and deployers.

title: EI Resolution on Technology, AI and Teaching Profession
abstract: Resolution adopted at Education International's 10th World Congress in Buenos Aires, establishing that education is a human endeavour where nothing can replace the teacher-student relationship. Mandates member organizations to demand equitable technology access, advocate for enhanced data privacy protections, insist on educator agency in technology development and evaluation, push for transparent algorithms in education, and secure professional development opportunities. Warns that AI can undermine education as a human right, erode working conditions and professional autonomy, and perpetuate harmful social and environmental consequences if left unregulated.

title: ELCA Church Council AI Issue Paper
abstract: The ELCA Church Council approved an issue paper on artificial intelligence at its fall 2024 meeting, providing theological and ethical guidance on AI drawing on ELCA social statements including those on genetics. The paper addresses concerns about rapidly developing technology, environmental impacts, human rights, and the application of AI in ways consistent with human dignity.

title: Finnish Church AI Governance Models (Digi@Kirkko)
abstract: On November 7, 2024, the Church Assembly (kirkolliskokous) approved the Digi@Kirkko flagship project (2025-2026), and the Church Board (kirkkohallitus) subsequently approved AI governance models developed within this project. The aim is to ensure AI is used across the organization in accordance with church values, strategy, and legislation. The governance models cover data protection, information security, cost sharing, ethical principles, and training. The church aims to use AI 'boldly but in a controlled manner, recognizing risks and considering ethical perspectives.'

title: Ethiopia National AI Policy
abstract: Adopted by the Ethiopian Council of Ministers on June 27, 2024, this policy aims to position Ethiopia as a continental AI center of excellence by 2035, with strategic pillars covering data governance, ethical oversight (including bias audits and algorithmic explainability), digital infrastructure, and nationwide capacity building, coordinated by the Ethiopian Artificial Intelligence Institute.

title: EP Bioethics Conference AI Conclusions (Rethymno 2024)
abstract: Formal conclusions of the 2nd International Scientific Bioethics Conference of the Ecumenical Patriarchate, held at the THEOMITOR Conference Centre in Rethymno, Crete (Nov 29-Dec 1, 2024). Presided by Archbishop Makarios of Australia as Chair of the Synodal Committee on Bioethics. Key conclusions: (1) AI is a significant technological achievement but constitutes a God-given gift requiring responsible stewardship; (2) dangers include technology dependence, social isolation, weakened faith, and arrogant self-perception; (3) Orthodox theology distinguishes the human nous from computational intelligence, asserting human perfection requires theosis; (4) AI in sensitive domains must remain auxiliary, acknowledging spiritual and cultural dimensions; (5) AI cannot address fundamental existential questions. Enthusiastically adopted by conference participants at closing session.

title: Patriarch Bartholomew - AI Technoethics and Youth Address
abstract: Patriarch Bartholomew inaugurated the 4th International Ecological Well-Being Conference in Athens, affirming that scientific achievements 'when used responsibly and ethically, can contribute to genuine progress without compromising fundamental moral and spiritual principles.' He emphasized that Orthodox tradition provides 'vital principles and truths' for addressing AI challenges and encouraged young people to cultivate 'spiritual intelligence' alongside technological advancement.

title: Patriarch Bartholomew - Christmas Encyclical 2024 (AI and Metahuman)
abstract: The Christmas 2024 encyclical directly addresses AI and transhumanism, stating: 'There is much talk about the metahuman and praise of artificial intelligence.' The Church is described as 'not technophobic' but approaching science as 'a divinely granted gift' while warning against scientism. The encyclical sets against 'the man-god of the contemporary world' the 'God-man as the ultimate measure of all things,' positioning the Incarnation as the Orthodox answer to technological utopianism.

title: EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689)
abstract: The world's first comprehensive risk-based legal framework for artificial intelligence, establishing four risk tiers (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal risk), conformity assessment requirements, and a governance structure including the European AI Board and AI Office. Published in the Official Journal on 12 July 2024.

title: EPIC Comments to NIST: AI Foundation Model Risks
abstract: EPIC's comments to the National Institute of Standards and Technology on managing risks of misuse with AI foundation models, particularly generative AI. Addresses risks associated with large foundation models, including privacy violations, bias, and systemic safety failures. Recommends that NIST's guidance prioritize human rights protections and establish strong accountability mechanisms for developers and deployers of foundation models.

title: EPIC Comments to NTIA: Dual-Use Foundation AI Models (2024)
abstract: EPIC's formal regulatory comment to NTIA on dual-use foundation AI models with widely available weights. Addresses the governance and oversight challenges presented by open-weight AI models, emphasizing privacy protections, human rights safeguards, and accountability requirements. Part of EPIC's broader advocacy for a human-rights-based approach to AI policy.

title: EPIC Comments to PCLOB: AI in Counterterrorism (July 2024)
abstract: EPIC's comments submitted to the Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board in advance of the PCLOB's public forum on AI in counterterrorism. Argues that AI systems used in national security contexts have structural flaws (data security vulnerabilities, lack of explainability) and that intelligence community databases used as training data disproportionately target marginalized communities. Calls for facial recognition prohibition and robust safeguard frameworks before any AI deployment in counterterrorism. Urges PCLOB to conduct a thorough investigation of how the intelligence community is designing, procuring, and deploying AI.

title: EPIC FTC Complaint: In re OpenAI (GPTs and Third-Party APIs)
abstract: EPIC formal complaint to the Federal Trade Commission calling on the FTC to investigate OpenAI for failing to meet established public policy standards for responsible AI use, offering products with unsafe security/privacy/business practices, and causing significant consumer harm. Focuses on OpenAI's third-party API integrations and GPT Store, which have expanded AI deployment into high-risk industries including financial services and real estate without adequate consumer safeguards.

title: EPIC Generating Harms II: Generative AI's New & Continued Impacts
abstract: Follow-up to EPIC's 2023 generative AI report, led by Calli Schroeder of EPIC's AI and Human Rights project. Examines additional harm categories and expands proposed remedies. Argues that potential benefits of generative AI are overshadowed by actual harms, and calls for stronger regulatory frameworks to protect consumers from AI-enabled privacy violations, discrimination, and deception.

title: EU AI Act Regulation 2024/1689
abstract: World's first comprehensive AI regulation, published in the EU Official Journal on 12 July 2024, entering into force 1 August 2024, applying a risk-based approach across the full AI lifecycle across all sectors; most provisions applicable from 2 August 2026.

title: EU AI Act
abstract: First comprehensive AI regulation globally, establishing risk-based framework with prohibitions, requirements for high-risk AI, and governance structures.

title: FBE Guidelines 2.0 on AI for Lawyers (2024)
abstract: An updated and expanded version of the FBE's 2023 AI guidelines for European lawyers, authored by Maria Dymitruk for the FBE New Technologies Commission. The Guidelines 2.0 cover eight main recommendations for legal professionals using generative AI tools, including acknowledging limitations of GenAI in legal practice, adhering to existing professional rules, considering AI use by opposing parties and courts, complementing rather than replacing legal expertise, maintaining attorney-client privilege, ensuring data protection, guaranteeing transparency toward clients, and considering the broader impacts on legal practice. The document reflects developments in both AI technology and the emerging EU regulatory landscape including the EU AI Act.

title: FCNL Nuclear Weapons AI Statement
abstract: FCNL policy brief supporting H.R. 8070, Section 1627 of the FY2025 National Defense Authorization Act, a bipartisan provision prohibiting federal funds for launching nuclear weapons with autonomous systems lacking meaningful human control. Argues AI could misread signals and make dangerous miscalculations during crises. Upholds 'positive human actions in execution' principle for nuclear decisions. References Biden-Xi agreement that humans, not AI, should control nuclear weapons. By Shawn Adams, 2024 Program Assistant for Nuclear Disarmament.

title: FCNL World We Seek: AI Legislative Policy
abstract: FCNL's foundational legislative policy document includes a dedicated Artificial Intelligence section (III.2.12) calling for federal legislation ensuring AI applications are ethical and protective, particularly for children and vulnerable populations. Key positions include: preventing harmful bias in AI training systems; protecting privacy and intellectual property; ensuring universal affordability; protecting democracy from AI manipulation; requiring AI products be clearly identified with full liability; mitigating negative employment and economic effects; opposing autonomous AI weapons; and opposing excessive AI demands on energy, water, and land. The section emphasizes that safeguarding truth, knowledge, and discernment guided by spiritual principles is essential in AI policymaking.

title: FDI Policy Statement: Artificial Intelligence in Dentistry
abstract: FDI policy statement adopted at the FDI World Dental Congress General Assembly in Istanbul, Turkiye (September 2024). Calls on dental professionals to develop AI literacy and employ AI applications as assistance systems while maintaining human autonomy and responsibility for diagnoses. Urges dental educators, researchers, developers, policymakers, and payers to incorporate AI curriculum in training programs, support standardization and regulatory oversight, and ensure AI performance assessment across diverse populations. Warns against bias arising from poor-quality training data.

title: EU Creative Workers Joint Statement on GenAI and AI Act
abstract: Joint statement dated April 25, 2024 from 13 international creative workers' organizations representing hundreds of thousands of writers, performers, composers, screenwriters, visual artists, journalists, and translators. Welcomes EU AI Act adoption as a 'first step' but calls for stronger implementation. Demands: comprehensive legal clarification on text-and-data-mining exceptions for generative AI; mandatory informed consent and fair remuneration for creators whose works train AI systems; strengthened transparency requirements; technical tools to differentiate authentic content from AI-generated material. Signatories: CEATL, ECSA, EFJ, EGAIR, EWC, FERA, FIA, FIM, FSE, IAO, IFJ, UNI MEI, UVA. Note: FIA and FIM overlap with L4-GUF search.

title: FIM Statement on AI in Music
abstract: Published 27 June 2024, FIM's statement addresses the AI ecosystem's lack of adequate normative frameworks protecting musicians whose work and talent are exploited by generative AI. It supports Principle 11 of the G7's International Guiding Principles on respecting intellectual property rights and ensuring dataset transparency. FIM argues that existing copyright and neighbouring rights frameworks were not designed to address generative AI's use of recorded performances, and calls for explicit language mandating licensing, consent, and compensation for musicians' works used in AI training.

title: Focolari-NetOne AI Pathway to Global Peace UN Event
abstract: Event held February 21, 2024, at United Nations Headquarters, New York (Conference Room 6, 1:15-2:45 p.m.), co-organized by NetOne and New Humanity (organizations founded on the spirit of the Focolare Movement) with the Mission of the Holy See to the UN. Opening remarks by Archbishop Gabriele Caccia (Permanent Observer, Holy See to UN): 'We stand at the precipice of a technological revolution unlike any other in human history.' Speakers included Father Philip Larrey (Boston College, president of Humanity 2.0) who warned 'Humans understand meanings. Machines don't'; Laura Gherlone (semiotics researcher) on digital decolonization and post-colonial AI costs; Marianne Najm (communications engineer, Beirut) on a digital oath concept inspired by the Hippocratic Oath; and Marcelle Momha (Cameroonian AI/cybersecurity policy analyst). Moderated by Maddalena Maltese (New Humanity). Addressed AI implications for peace, sustainable development, and human dignity. Referenced Pope Francis's January 1, 2024, message on AI consequences.

title: IAWG-FSE Five Principles on AI in Screenwriting
abstract: Joint resolution passed April 11, 2024 by IAWG (14 member guilds from 12 countries) and FSE (32 organizations from 26 European countries), binding on member guilds, representing approximately 67,000 professional screenwriters globally. Five principles: (1) only writers create literary material — AI cannot replace writers; (2) obligatory transparency and accountability mechanisms to inform writers if AI-generated material is used; (3) robust licensing mechanisms requiring explicit consent for use of writers' IP in AI training data; (4) only human beings are entitled to authors' rights/copyright for machine-generated material; (5) fair remuneration for use of writers' IP in AI systems. Member guilds span North America (WGA East/West, WGC, SARTEC), Europe (via FSE), India (SWA), Israel, Korea, New Zealand, South Africa.

title: AI, Ethics & Faith Handbook
abstract: Joint handbook published by the Faraday Institute for Science and Religion and Forum for Peace examining the relationship between AI, ethics, and faith. Emphasises the importance of diverse faith perspectives in shaping the future of AI. Addresses how AI as the transformational technology of the era raises questions about human identity and purpose that religion has long explored. Argues that people of faith need to be actively engaged in the debate about AI's future.

title: Free Press Statement: Big Tech Voluntary AI Accord — Empty Promises
abstract: Free Press press statement by Senior Counsel Nora Benavidez criticizing the voluntary AI election integrity accord signed by 20 technology companies (including Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, TikTok). Argues that voluntary promises are insufficient to address global challenges facing democracy and that tech cannot be engineered around this problem. Calls for robust human-based content moderation, stronger political advertising policies, in-language moderation teams, and binding regulatory requirements rather than voluntary corporate commitments.

title: G20 Rio de Janeiro Declaration 2024
abstract: G20 Leaders' Declaration from Brazil's presidency acknowledging AI threats in the information domain, calling for AI regulation, and promoting transparency, accountability, human oversight, and copyright protection, with emphasis on inclusive and sustainable development.

title: G7 Apulia Leaders Communique 2024
abstract: G7 Leaders' Communiqué from the 50th G7 Summit endorsed the G7 Toolkit for AI in the Public Sector, committed to enhance interoperability between AI governance approaches, and launched an action plan on AI in the world of work under Italy's presidency.

title: G7 Italy Digital Ministerial Statement 2024
abstract: G7 Tech and Digital Ministers' Joint Statement advancing the Hiroshima AI Process reporting framework pilot, endorsing a Toolkit for AI in the Public Sector, and committing to continued dialogue on safe, secure, and trustworthy AI governance.

title: G7 Trento Ministerial Declaration 2024
abstract: G7 Ministerial Declaration on Industry, Technology, and Digital under Italy's 2024 G7 presidency, advancing AI governance commitments including interoperability between national approaches, public sector AI deployment, and the Hiroshima AI Process reporting framework.

title: CSET Enabling Principles for AI Governance
abstract: A policy brief by Owen Daniels and Dewey Murdick proposing three enabling principles for regulators: Risk Intelligence (track AI incidents and harms), Literacy Development (build AI understanding in policymakers and the public), and Adaptive Policy Design (develop flexible, iterative governance that evolves with technology).

title: GI FB KI GenAI and AI Act Position Paper
abstract: The GI Artificial Intelligence Department examines social contexts of AI system deployment and argues that regulation should focus on social mechanisms of human-AI interaction rather than limiting technical capabilities or basing rules solely on system architecture. The paper contends that 'the social context of how people use AI systems must be analyzed and evaluated' when determining regulatory requirements.

title: GI FG Informatik und Ethik AI Act Position Paper
abstract: The GI Ethics Working Group (Fachgruppe Informatik und Ethik) analyses the EU AI Act's moral dimensions, noting it aims to embed EU values and fundamental rights into AI systems through a human-centered, risk-based approach. The paper argues AI systems present society with qualitatively new challenges requiring discussion not only technically but also through legal, ethical, and social perspectives, particularly regarding technology impact assessment and human dignity.

title: Kyrgyzstan Digital Transformation Concept 2024-2028
abstract: Стратегический документ, определяющий направления цифровой трансформации государственного управления, экономики и социальной сферы Кыргызстана до 2028 года. Раздел об ИИ предусматривает создание Национальной платформы ИИ, центров компетенций по ИИ, высокопроизводительной вычислительной инфраструктуры, а также внедрение ИИ-инструментов в здравоохранение, образование, сельское хозяйство и правоохранительную деятельность. Особое место занимает поддержка ИИ, понимающего кыргызский язык.

title: GOARCH AI-Theology Working Group Reports
abstract: The Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America established an AI and Theology Working Group that has produced multiple reports: (1) 'Imagine an AI Indistinguishable from Us' examining bioethics principles of respect, beneficence, equity, and justice through Orthodox lens; (2) 'Are AIs the New Intermediary Beings?' exploring AI through the theological lens of angels, demons, and intermediary beings in early Christian thought; (3) 'Awe or Analysis?' on the potential emergence of superintelligence as a metaphysical event. The group draws on St. Maximus the Confessor and patristic thought to frame AI ethics.

title: GOARCH Clergy-Laity Panel on AI, Personhood, and Theosis
abstract: The 2024 Clergy-Laity Congress of the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America included a dedicated ministry panel on 'Artificial Intelligence, Personhood, and Theosis,' featuring Orthodox Christian hierarchs and members of the Assembly discussing AI's implications for persons of faith and for the Orthodox Church. The panel addressed how digital technology through AI algorithms, social media, and content creation culture is transforming Orthodox sociality, authority, education, and theological values.

title: GPAI Belgrade Declaration 2024
abstract: GPAI Ministerial Declaration adopted by 44 member states and the EU in Belgrade, committing to safe, secure, and trustworthy human-centric AI development building on OECD AI Principles, advancing best practices on employment, data governance, intellectual property, and environmental impacts.

title: GPAI New Delhi Declaration 2024 (July)
abstract: GPAI 6th Ministerial Council Declaration from New Delhi advancing evidence-based analysis on key AI topics including employment, data governance, intellectual property, risk mitigation, environmental impacts, and biotechnology, under India's continued leadership.

title: HIMSS AI Global Policy Principles
abstract: HIMSS global AI policy principles developed in response to member requests, presented at the 2024 HIMSS AI in Healthcare Forum (September 2024, Boston). Provides guidance covering safety, accountability, transparency, privacy, interoperability, innovation, and workforce development. Designed to ensure governments of all structures and geographies have tools to improve policy, support rapid innovation, and address growing complexity of health delivery systems.

title: HIMSS Policy Principles: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
abstract: HIMSS public policy principles for AI and machine learning in healthcare, revised with new guidance to promote and accelerate responsible governance and deployment. Covers safety and trust; transparency and privacy; and workforce and sustainability. Calls for risk-based regulatory approach, policy frameworks addressing the full AI lifecycle from development through deployment, standards harmonization for data exchange, and workforce education. Principles are formally revisited biannually.

title: HRW Gaza: Israeli Military's Digital Tools Risk Civilian Harm
abstract: Human Rights Watch institutional news release and accompanying Q&A examining the Israeli military's use of four AI-enabled digital targeting tools in Gaza operations since October 7, 2023: (1) cell tower-based mobile phone tracking for evacuation monitoring; (2) 'Lavender' — a machine learning system that assigns suspicion scores to residents to classify them as potential combatants; (3) 'The Gospel' — an algorithm generating lists of structural targets; and (4) 'Where's Daddy?' — location-tracking tool used to time attacks when targets are at home. HRW concludes these tools rely on 'incomplete data, flawed calculations, and tools not fit for purpose,' potentially violating international humanitarian law principles on distinction between combatants and civilians, and obligations to take feasible precautions minimizing civilian casualties.

title: HRW Submission: Australia Senate Committee on AI
abstract: Formal institutional submission by Human Rights Watch to Australia's Senate Select Committee on AI adoption. Documents HRW's research findings on the unauthorized scraping of Australian children's personal photographs into LAION-5B, a major AI training dataset, with 190 identifiable child photos found across all Australian states and territories. Warns these images enable deepfake creation and pose severe privacy risks, particularly for Indigenous children. Recommends regulations protecting children's data from AI system misuse. Represents HRW's institutional position on children's digital rights and AI training data practices.

title: IAASB Technology Position Statement
abstract: Formal Technology Position Statement adopted by the IAASB in October 2024, outlining eight guiding actions for facilitating technology adoption by audit and assurance practitioners. The Position commits to: (1) adapting existing standards to be technology-agnostic or technology-enabling; (2) conducting ongoing gap analyses to ensure standards align with technological advancements including AI; (3) developing non-authoritative guidance materials on automated tools and techniques; (4) monitoring technological trends including AI and emerging tech. Accompanied by a 'Catalog of Issues and Possible Actions' (updated semi-annually) covering nine key themes including AI. Also includes an Artificial Intelligence Primer (March 2022) as a Digital Technology Market Scan. The position is tagged as addressing 'AI and Emerging Tech' though content is technology-broad rather than AI-specific.

title: IBA Report: AI and the Legal Profession
abstract: Comprehensive IBA/CAIDP report exploring AI's transformative impact on the legal profession, including governance, ethical deployment of AI in legal practice, regulation, and best practices for the global legal industry. Produced by three IBA working groups.

title: ICC Statement on AI in Advertising
abstract: ICC formal statement on responsible AI use in marketing, grounded in the ICC's self-regulatory framework established in 1937. Key principles: marketer responsibility is unchanged whether AI assists partially or fully in content creation; all marketing communications — human-created or AI-assisted — must meet identical standards (legal, decent, honest, truthful) and comply with data privacy requirements; the ICC Code applies to those responsible for algorithms and AI used for marketing communications; ethical safeguards must prevent discrimination, avoid stereotypes, and promote diversity. Announced forthcoming revised ICC Code (late 2024) to explicitly address AI responsibilities.

title: ICCR Digital Rights and AI Accountability Shareholder Resolutions
abstract: Ongoing shareholder engagement campaign by ICCR pressing technology companies to adopt human rights due diligence policies regarding AI. Filed AI Transparency Report resolutions targeting Apple (2024, lead filer AFL-CIO) and other tech companies, demanding disclosure of AI use in business operations and adopted ethical guidelines aligned with the White House AI Bill of Rights principles: safe systems, anti-discrimination, data privacy, notice/explanation, and human alternatives. Addresses algorithmic discrimination, worker surveillance, deepfake risks, and AI's impact on low-wage workers. ICCR pioneered shareholder engagement 50+ years ago.

title: ICCR Shareholder Resolution: AI Transparency Report (Comcast)
abstract: ICCR-coordinated shareholder resolution requesting Comcast prepare a transparency report explaining the company's use of AI in business operations, the Board's role in overseeing AI, and any ethical guidelines adopted regarding AI. Concerns addressed include potential discrimination or bias in employment decisions, mass layoffs due to job automation, misuse and disclosure of private data, and creation of deepfake media. References the White House AI Bill of Rights five principles. Lead filer: AFL-CIO (Brandon Rees).

title: ICCR Shareholder Resolution: GenAI Misinformation Risks (Alphabet)
abstract: ICCR-coordinated shareholder resolution filed with Alphabet, Inc. requesting the Board issue a report assessing risks from the company's role in facilitating AI-generated misinformation and disinformation, particularly ahead of major global elections in 2024. The resolution cites Google's Palm technology generating false information at a 27% rate and highlights legal, financial, and reputational risks of rapidly deploying AI products without adequate harm assessment. Lead filer: Arjuna Capital; co-filers: Ekō and Open MIC.

title: ICCR Shareholder Resolution: Human Rights Impact on AI Deployment (Amazon)
abstract: ICCR-coordinated shareholder resolution requesting Amazon's Board charter a new committee of independent directors on Artificial Intelligence to address human rights risks associated with the development and deployment of AI systems. Cites Amazon's scrapped hiring algorithm that discriminated against women and Amazon contracts for military drone analysis. Lead filer: AFL-CIO (Brandon Rees); co-filers include Adrian Dominican Sisters and Mercy Investment Services.

title: ICESCO Riyadh Charter on AI for Islamic World
abstract: Launched at the third Global AI Summit (GAIN) in Riyadh by ICESCO and the Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA), this charter provides a comprehensive ethical framework for AI development aligned with Islamic values. Subsequently unanimously approved by 53 Islamic member states, the charter establishes principles of truth, integrity, fairness, privacy, reliability, transparency, accountability, and humanitarian values.

title: ICRC Building a Responsible Humanitarian Approach: AI Policy
abstract: The ICRC's first comprehensive institutional AI policy establishes a humanitarian-focused framework developed through collaborative, multidisciplinary internal effort. The policy aims to help ICRC staff understand AI and responsibly explore its humanitarian applications while maintaining alignment with the organization's mandate and Fundamental Principles of humanity, impartiality, neutrality, and independence. Anchored in the 2024–2027 Institutional Strategy, the policy provides foundational guidance for implementing AI to support the ICRC's humanitarian mission. The ICRC explicitly applies its core principles as limiting conditions on any AI use — the organization does not seek problems for AI to solve but turns to these tools only when they serve affected populations without creating new risks.

title: IET Leading with Intelligence — AI Report 2024
abstract: Published by the IET as part of its Digital Futures policy initiative, this report summarizes insights from a 2024 global webinar series (May–July 2024) with participants from 18 countries across nine webinars, guided by the IET's immediate past President Dr. Gopichand Katragadda. The report covers emerging AI technologies, societal challenges around data and AI, and the vital role of robust engineering governance and ethics in protecting privacy, safety, and truth. Key recommendations include establishment of a regulatory oversight body with experts from industry, academia, and social science; development of agile AI training programs for engineers comparable to GDPR compliance training; and attention to AI sustainability and energy consumption. Note: exact publication date within 2024 not confirmed from available sources.

title: Law Society AI Strategy: The Three Is
organization: Law Society of England and Wales
abstract: The Law Society of England and Wales published its AI strategy built on three pillars: innovation, impact, and integrity. The strategy aims to ensure AI is used in a responsible and ethical way, helping solicitors and law firms benefit from AI tools while protecting the public and supporting access to justice. The Law Society commits to being an authoritative voice on AI, supporting both the profession and the UK economy, while balancing opportunities with appropriate ethical safeguards.

title: IFJ Recommendations on the Use of Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Formal recommendations issued jointly by the IFJ and journalists' unions worldwide calling for action on artificial intelligence in journalism. The document addresses three core areas: (1) Journalists as defenders of journalism — affirming AI cannot replace human journalists and that all published journalism must be under appropriate human oversight, noting that generative AI models cannot fact-check or assess source credibility; (2) Information custodianship — calling for clear labeling of AI-generated content and requiring that licensing agreements between news organizations and AI companies include journalist consent and compensation; (3) Workers' rights — mandating transparency in AI deployment affecting employees and freelancers, human review of AI-based assessments, and mandatory worker consultation before AI implementation. The document also calls for international regulation of AI with journalist representation.

title: ILO Mind the AI Divide 2024
abstract: Joint report by the United Nations and the International Labour Organization, published 26 July 2024. Examines how unequal AI adoption is widening global inequities, finding that disparities in digital infrastructure, advanced technology, quality education, and training are deepening existing inequalities. Identifies risks that less-developed nations face of falling further behind economically and socially as economies shift toward AI-driven models. Advocates for bridging the digital divide through infrastructure investment, skills development, and social dialogue in workplaces. Recommends international collaboration to build AI capacity in disadvantaged regions, ensure quality employment opportunities throughout the AI value chain, and establish equitable global AI governance that upholds human dignity and labour standards.

title: Iran National AI Document
abstract: Iran's National Artificial Intelligence Document, approved by the Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution (SCCR) in its 901st session on June 18, 2024 (Khordad 29, 1403 in Persian calendar). Published in Iran's Official Gazette on August 20, 2024. Establishes the National AI Organization as an independent body under the President's supervision. Creates a National AI Steering Council responsible for planning, guidance, coordination, and supervision of implementation. Mandates development of sectoral roadmaps and ethical guidelines. Emphasizes 'data sovereignty' (data generated by Iranian citizens stored and processed within national borders), indigenous hardware, localized data processing, and Persian-language software ecosystems. Framework ensures AI development aligns with 'national security interests and Islamic cultural values.' A comprehensive AI Ethics Charter providing guidance on 'religious and cultural boundaries of AI development and deployment' is mandated as a future release from the National AI Organization. Supplemented by the 2025 Law on Combating Untrue News Content.

title: ISACA: Artificial Intelligence Governance Brief
abstract: ISACA institutional brief addressing three critical organizational questions about AI deployment: preparedness to use AI, capacity to govern and manage its use, and whether AI governance supports strategic objectives. Includes a practical self-assessment questionnaire for organizations to evaluate their AI governance systems. Addresses how AI governance must be evidenced throughout the AI lifecycle to realize benefits in achieving enterprise strategy while minimizing inherent risk. Formally issued by ISACA as institutional guidance.

title: ISACA: Keeping Pace With AI
abstract: ISACA formal guidance eBook addressing the gap between AI adoption and organizational readiness: 85% of digital trust professionals expect they will need AI training within two years, yet most enterprises lack formal policies for responsible AI deployment. Covers AI policy frameworks, ethical considerations in AI implementation, risk management approaches, workforce training requirements, and organizational readiness. Positioned as authoritative professional guidance from ISACA on helping organizations navigate AI responsibly.

title: IOP 38th Conference: AI and Faith, Religion, Thought
abstract: The Institute of Oriental Philosophy (IOP), Soka Gakkai's research arm, held its 38th Academic Conference at Soka University (May 25-26, 2024) themed 'AI and Faith, Religion, and Thought.' Key positions: (1) AI relies on quantitative computation while humans possess qualitative, non-algorithmic dimensions; (2) Buddhist perspective — AI knowledge persists across generations unlike human knowledge, requiring careful discourse on proper use; (3) AI's 'black box' nature means ultimate solutions to human suffering require religious meaning, not algorithmic answers; (4) large language models process symbols devoid of semantic meaning, presenting a crisis when machines appear to replicate human capabilities. Followed by a 4-part public lecture series (Nov-Dec 2024) including Osaka University's Hiroshi Ishiguro on 'AI and Humans' and Hanazono University's Shigeki Mori on 'AI Recreation of the Dead/Saints.' Published in 東洋学術研究 (Oriental Academic Research).

title: UN Advisory Body on AI Final Report 2024
abstract: Final report of the UN Secretary-General's High-Level Advisory Body proposing establishment of an International Scientific Panel on AI, a policy dialogue forum, a global AI data framework, and a UN AI office to coordinate governance efforts globally.

title: ITF Technology and Decent Work Charter 2024
abstract: ITF's formal charter establishing worker rights in the context of technology deployment in the transport sector, published February 2024. Designed to inform both unions and employers of rights workers should maintain during technology introduction. Key provisions address AI and algorithmic management specifically: mandatory negotiation of criteria used by algorithms in AI and decision-making systems; individual and collective worker access to datasets and algorithms; explanation requirements for algorithmic systems (who wrote them, how they work, what criteria apply); commitment not to use emotional recognition technologies; pre-deployment impact assessments for discriminatory bias; and data rights establishing that workers' data should be subject to shared control through negotiation. The charter positions AI governance as requiring mandatory worker participation in design and decision-making, not merely post-implementation notification.

title: Data & Society: A Sociotechnical Approach to AI Policy
abstract: Explainer brief on what a sociotechnical perspective is and why it matters in AI policy. Drawing on established literature and historical and present-day examples, argues that a technology's real-world safety and performance is always a product of technical design and broader societal forces, including organizational bureaucracy, human labor, social conventions, and power. Authors: Brian J. Chen and Jacob Metcalf.

title: Data & Society: AI Governance Needs Sociotechnical Expertise
abstract: Policy brief arguing that effective AI governance requires integrating humanities and social science expertise. Outlines ways that sociotechnical expertise can help assess AI system performance and mitigate harms. Authors: Serena Oduro and Tamara Kneese.

title: ITU United Nations Activities on AI 2024
abstract: Annual ITU compilation documenting AI-related activities across the UN system, mapping how various UN entities are engaging with artificial intelligence for development, governance, and operational purposes.

title: PHAICON 2024 Posthumanism and AI Findings
abstract: Conference findings from PHAICON 2024, a 4-day international scientific conference on 'Post-Humanism and Artificial Intelligence' held November 18-21, 2024 in Athens, Greece. Organized by the Saint Maximus the Greek Institute in cooperation with the Office of the Orthodox Church in the European Union. Featured 115 speakers from 18 countries across disciplines including computer science, robotics, genetics, astronomy, medicine, philosophy, theology, sociology, law, and psychology. Key outcomes: presentation of Orthodox Christian anthropology principles defining humans as created in God's image and likeness; debate on compatibility of transhumanism with Christian values; recognition of AI achievements in medicine, genetics, and everyday life with emphasis on need for regulations; establishment of PHAICON.NET as a permanent international online observatory on posthumanism and AI.

title: Iceland: AI Action Plan 2024-2026
abstract: Iceland's first national AI Action Plan, released in draft November 2024 and finalised July 2025, outlines 25 targeted measures across five areas: AI for society, competitiveness, education, public administration, and healthcare. The plan promotes ethical, human-centred AI development aligned with European regulatory standards, and supports digital infrastructure investment including a national AI and supercomputing centre receiving ISK 1.4 billion with EU funding.

title: IndiaAI Mission 2024
abstract: The Cabinet of India approved the IndiaAI Mission on 7 March 2024 with a budget of Rs. 10,371.92 crore, establishing a seven-pillar national initiative covering compute capacity (10,000+ GPUs), an AI innovation centre for indigenous large multimodal models, a datasets platform, application development, FutureSkills education programs, startup financing, and Safe & Trusted AI governance frameworks. The mission aims to democratize AI access, build technological sovereignty, and ensure responsible and inclusive growth of India's AI ecosystem.

title: IndustriAll Europe AI Strategy 2024
abstract: Adopted at IndustriAll Europe's Executive Committee meeting on 28-29 November 2024 in Porto, this comprehensive trade union strategy addresses AI's impact on European industrial workers. The strategy identifies three key worker risks: (1) health and safety concerns including cognitive overload and burnout from automation; (2) deskilling and wage polarization particularly affecting intermediate-skilled workers; (3) surveillance and monitoring practices used for union-busting, with 44% of AI systems showing gender bias. Three foundational strategic elements are proposed: worker information and consultation during AI implementation; collective bargaining focused on transparency and human oversight; and strengthened trade union digital capacity. The strategy references the 2020 European Social Partners Framework Agreement on Digitalisation and advocates for EU legislation addressing algorithmic management across all sectors, not just platform economies. Deputy General Secretary Isabelle Barthès stated: 'Both adequate legal framework and constructive social dialogue are needed to ensure AI leads to quality jobs.' Note: IndustriAll Europe is the European regional body; IndustriAll Global Union is a separate (though related) organization.

title: Iran National AI Document 2024
abstract: Approved June 2024 by the Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution, this comprehensive policy blueprint for AI development underpins Iran's ambition to rank among the world's top 10 AI powers. Built on the concept of 'AI Sovereignty,' it prioritizes indigenous hardware, localized data processing, and Persian-language software ecosystems. Article 5 mandates ministries to develop technical, legal, and regulatory frameworks; a National AI Organization was established July 2024 under the President, and parliament approved a National AI Plan in May 2025.

title: Italy AI Strategy 2024-2026
abstract: Italy's national AI strategy, developed by a 14-member expert committee coordinated by Professor Gianluigi Greco, defines strategic actions across four macro areas: Research, Public Administration, Enterprise, and Training. AGID plays a central role in regulating AI, promoting responsible public-sector adoption, ensuring ethics and security, and facilitating public-private collaboration. The strategy includes a monitoring system and regulatory analysis aligned with the EU AI Act.

title: J-AISI Guide to Evaluation Perspectives on AI Safety 2024
abstract: The Guide to Evaluation Perspectives on AI Safety (AIセーフティに関する評価観点ガイド) was published by J-AISI to support voluntary AI safety evaluation by industry. It presents evaluation perspectives organized by risk category, example risk scenarios, example evaluation items, and guidance on who should conduct evaluations and at what stage of the AI lifecycle. The guide was developed in coordination with similar institutes in the US (NIST) and UK (DSIT) and references the J-AISI/NIST crosswalk on AI risk management interoperability. J-AISI was jointly established by ten ministries and five government agencies in February 2024. Version 1.01 was the first public release (September 2024); Version 1.10 was released March 28, 2025.

title: Jordan Dar al-Ifta Article on AI and Fatwa Drafting
abstract: Published on the Jordanian General Iftaa' Department website on September 1, 2024, by Dr. Mousa Za'atreh. Analyzes the impact of artificial intelligence on drafting religious edicts (fatwas). Identifies advantages: AI enables categorization and rapid access to religious rulings across languages, facilitates verification of information authenticity, and makes fatwas more widely accessible. Critical limitations: AI lacks contextual comprehension necessary for nuanced religious questions (e.g., divorce cases requiring dialogue), cannot replicate the interpretive dimension of qualified scholars, may propagate biased or non-mainstream opinions without discernment, and cannot perform independent reasoning (ijtihad). Central conclusion: AI is merely a dissemination tool and should never replace scholarly consultation; all AI outputs require verification by qualified scholars.

title: Hiroshima Appeal — AI Ethics for Peace
abstract: The Hiroshima Appeal is a joint declaration adopted on July 10, 2024, at the conclusion of the 'AI Ethics for Peace' interfaith gathering in Hiroshima, Japan. Co-issued by Religions for Peace Japan, the Pontifical Academy for Life, the Abu Dhabi Forum for Peace, and the Commission for Interreligious Relations of the Chief Rabbinate of Israel, the Appeal calls on the international community to ensure AI is used only for the good of humanity and never for destructive purposes, drawing on lessons from Hiroshima's atomic bomb history. It specifically urges that AI not be used to develop or control autonomous weapons or nuclear weapons systems, and calls for peaceful resolution of all armed conflicts. The appeal is grounded in the Golden Rule shared across faiths and was presented before 150+ participants representing 11 world religions and 13 nations. It is distinct from (though issued alongside) the Rome Call for AI Ethics signing ceremony held at the same event.

title: Rissho Kosei-kai Co-Hosting of Hiroshima AI Ethics for Peace
abstract: Rissho Kosei-kai co-hosted the 'AI Ethics for Peace' multi-faith gathering in Hiroshima (July 9-10, 2024) as a co-organizing partner through the WCRP/RfP Japan Committee. Niwano Kousho (next-generation president), General Affairs Director Wada Shigekumi, and Executive Director Nemoto Masahiro represented the organization. The event brought 150 religious leaders from 13 countries to sign the Rome Call for AI Ethics, with eleven world religions and sixteen new signatories committing to ethical AI development. The Hiroshima Appeal on generative AI governance was also introduced.

title: Rome Call Hiroshima Multi-Faith Signing
organization: Pontifical Academy for Life / Religions for Peace Japan / Abu Dhabi Forum
abstract: A historic two-day interfaith forum in Hiroshima brought together eleven world religions — including Buddhism, Shinto, Confucianism, Hinduism, Sikhism, Bahá'í, Christianity, Islam, and Judaism — with sixteen new religious signatories formally committing to the Rome Call for AI Ethics. The event also introduced the Hiroshima Addendum on generative AI, emphasizing the need for ethical governance of generative AI systems.

title: Kazakhstan AI Development Concept 2024-2029
abstract: Kazakhstan's Concept for AI Development 2024-2029, adopted by the Government on 16 July 2024 (Resolution No. 592), establishes a strategic framework to integrate AI across the economy with a target of fivefold growth in AI-enabled products by 2029, focusing on state administration, energy, mining, agriculture, and transportation. Key initiatives include development of KazLLM (a sovereign large language model for Kazakh and Russian), a national AI computing infrastructure with a supercomputer, expanded university AI programs, and a strengthened legislative framework for AI governance.

title: Church of Jesus Christ AI Guiding Principles
abstract: Introduced to Church employees worldwide by Elder Gerrit W. Gong and Elder John C. Pingree, this statement establishes four foundational principles for responsible AI use: supporting spiritual connection rather than supplanting it, maintaining transparency, safeguarding privacy and security, and ensuring accountability. The principles emphasize that AI cannot replace divine inspiration, the gift of the Holy Spirit, or meaningful human relationships with God.

title: LCMS CTCR Online Technology in the Church
abstract: CTCR study document requested by the 2023 LCMS convention to aid congregations in using technology. Addresses livestreamed worship, virtual multi-parish arrangements, online church discipline, online-only membership, and AI-generated sermons. Argues that a Lutheran sermon is not replaceable with doctrinally correct data from a language model, and that pastors must deliver theological truth personalized to the people God has given them to serve. Approved December 2024.

title: Lausanne Seoul Statement
abstract: 97-article, 13,000-word theological statement from the Fourth Lausanne Congress. Section VII ('Technology: The Accelerating Innovation We Discern and Steward') contains 7 articles (88-94) addressing AI, genetic engineering, biotechnology, virtual reality, and digital media through a biblical lens. Affirms technological ability reflects the image of God, warns sin adversely influences technology, and calls Christians to prophetically critique technology and discern technologies reshaping human nature. Specifically addresses AI within broader technology stewardship framework.

title: La Quadrature du Net AI Act Biometric Surveillance Failure
abstract: La Quadrature du Net, as part of a civil society coalition, argues the EU AI Act will not effectively ban biometric mass surveillance despite EU lawmakers' claims. The statement contends the deal permits police use of facial recognition, leaves emotion recognition and biometric categorization with huge loopholes, and sets a 'chilling global precedent' by legitimizing discriminatory surveillance practices.

title: La Quadrature du Net AI State-of-the-Art Exploitation
abstract: La Quadrature du Net contends that AI represents an intensification of exploitative digital capitalism rather than a revolutionary technology, built on 'triple accumulation' of data, capital, and natural resources. The statement argues AI amplifies inequality across economic, public, and military sectors, exploits hundreds of thousands of 'data workers', and that European policy prioritizes tech industry interests over rights protection and ecological sustainability.

title: Morocco CESE AI Opinion 2024
abstract: Adopté à l'unanimité, cet avis du CESE analyse les usages et les perspectives de développement de l'IA au Maroc. Il constate l'absence d'un cadre réglementaire spécifique à l'IA et formule des recommandations : élaborer une stratégie nationale d'IA, réviser la loi 09-08 pour intégrer les spécificités des données générées par l'IA, créer un fonds public-privé pour l'innovation, accélérer la libération des données publiques, et renforcer les programmes éducatifs. Une consultation citoyenne via la plateforme 'Ouchariko' a impliqué plus de 800 participants.

title: China National AI Industry Standardization Guide 2024
abstract: The 2024 Guidelines outline a seven-area national AI standardization system: basic common standards, infrastructure, key algorithm technologies, intelligent products and services, industry applications, safety/security and ethics standards, and standard testing/evaluation. They provide the planning framework for China's AI standards pipeline through 2026 and serve as the successor to earlier 2018 standardization guides.

title: MCTI Plano Brasileiro de Inteligência Artificial 2024–2028
abstract: Plano estratégico e de investimentos em IA do governo brasileiro para 2024-2028, denominado 'IA para o Bem de Todos', com R$ 23 bilhões projetados em quatro anos. Organizado em cinco eixos: Infraestrutura e Desenvolvimento de IA, Disseminação e Capacitação, IA para Melhoria dos Serviços Públicos, IA para Inovação Empresarial, e Apoio ao Processo Regulatório. Lançado na 5ª Conferência Nacional de Ciência, Tecnologia e Inovação em Brasília. Inclui 31 ações de curto prazo em saúde, educação, ambiente e agricultura. Versão final publicada em junho de 2025.

title: MEAA Stop AI Theft Campaign & AI Act Call
abstract: MEAA advocates for economy-wide AI Act with independent regulator, transparency requirements, creator compensation, and misinformation safeguards. 93% of surveyed members support greater government AI intervention.

title: Frontier AI Safety Commitments 2024
abstract: Voluntary safety commitments by 20 frontier AI companies (including Amazon, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI) to conduct risk assessments, maintain accountability frameworks, ensure transparency, perform red-teaming, and publish safety frameworks before the France AI Summit.

title: Seoul Declaration for Safe AI 2024
abstract: Declaration by 10 nations and the EU committing to interoperable AI governance frameworks, expanded AI safety institutes and research programs, human-centric AI development with private sector and civil society collaboration, and continued implementation of the Hiroshima Process Code of Conduct.

title: Seoul Ministerial Statement 2024
abstract: Ministerial statement by 28 countries and the EU affirming the need for collaborative international approaches to rapid AI advancements, setting ambitions for shared frontier AI risk thresholds, transparency, and robust risk management while promoting AI-driven ecosystems and equitable benefits.

title: Seoul Statement on AI Safety Science 2024
abstract: Annex to the Seoul Declaration establishing a statement of intent for an international network of AI Safety Institutes to accelerate AI safety science, promote complementarity and interoperability between institutes, and foster a common international understanding of AI safety approaches.

title: Korea Multi-Faith AI Symposium
abstract: A historic ecumenical symposium on AI held at Myeongdong Cathedral, Seoul, on September 27, 2024, co-organized by the Orthodox Metropolis of Korea, the Catholic Bishops' Conference of Korea (CBCK), and the National Council of Churches of Korea (NCCK). The symposium examined AI from a Christian theological perspective, debating whether it functions as 'servant or overlord.' This marked a rare collaboration between Orthodox, Catholic, and Protestant bodies in Korea on AI ethics, coinciding with the 20th anniversary of the Metropolis of Korea.

title: MIC+METI AI Guidelines for Business Ver.1.0 2024
abstract: The AI Guidelines for Business (AI事業者ガイドライン) represent Japan's comprehensive voluntary AI governance framework, consolidating existing guidelines and responding to international developments including the 2023 G7 Hiroshima AI Process. The document is structured in three tiers: (1) foundational values (human dignity, diversity, sustainability), (2) ten common principles applicable across the AI lifecycle, and (3) specific guidance tailored to roles (AI developers, AI providers, AI users/businesses). Appendix materials include sector-specific reference cases, implementation checklists, and international standards crosswalk. Version 1.01 was issued November 2024, and Ver.1.1 in March 2025. Published simultaneously in Japanese and English (provisional translation).

title: Malaysia National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) 2024
abstract: Garis Panduan Nasional Tadbir Urus dan Etika Kecerdasan Buatan (AIGE) dilancarkan oleh MOSTI pada 20 September 2024 dengan tema 'AI untuk Malaysia, AI untuk Semua'. Panduan sukarela ini menetapkan tujuh prinsip AI utama: keadilan, kebolehpercayaan/keselamatan/kawalan, privasi/keselamatan data, inklusiviti, ketelusan, akauntabiliti, dan manfaat manusia. AIGE bertujuan untuk memperkukuh kepercayaan awam, mengurangkan risiko etika, dan meningkatkan penggunaan AI merentas pelbagai sektor secara bertanggungjawab.

title: Morocco Digital 2030 (AI Component)
abstract: Launched on September 25, 2024 in Rabat by Morocco's Ministry of Digital Transition, the Morocco Digital 2030 strategy positions AI as a core economic driver under the 'Maroc IA 2030' component, targeting 200,000 AI-trained graduates, 50,000 AI-linked jobs, $10 billion in GDP contribution, and a national AI governance agency; Morocco lacks a standalone AI strategy but integrates AI centrally into its digital transformation framework.

title: MUIS Generative AI Guidance for Muslims
abstract: MUIS adopts generative AI judiciously with appropriate safeguards. IftaSG, an initiative under MUIS's Fatwa Lab, uses prompt engineering and curated datasets to tailor an LLM for enhanced fatwa research. Warns against using AI as primary source of religious guidance for complex matters requiring scholarly interpretation. Asatizah (Islamic scholars) maintain oversight on final religious rulings. MUIS has stepped up AI and digital literacy training across mosques and madrasahs.

title: MeitY AI Deployment Advisory
abstract: Originally issued March 1, 2024, and revised March 15, 2024 (dropping the government pre-approval requirement after industry pushback), MeitY's advisory is addressed to all intermediaries and platforms deploying AI, LLMs, and generative AI technologies. It requires due diligence to prevent bias, discrimination, and threats to the electoral process, and mandates appropriate labeling of AI-generated content from potentially unreliable systems. Primarily targets large technology platforms; later clarified as not applying to startups.

title: Moldova AI White Paper 2024
abstract: Moldova's inaugural AI policy framework establishing strategic direction for a cohesive national AI and data governance ecosystem, emphasizing ethical, inclusive, and sustainable AI aligned with EU and international standards. Defines nine key requirements for AI systems (interoperability, human agency, transparency, safety, adaptability, accountability, accessibility, sustainability, data privacy) and four central objectives covering skills, infrastructure, societal trust, and governance. Accompanied by Moldova's signature of the Council of Europe AI Convention in September 2024.

title: NAACP Issue Brief: AI in Predictive Policing
abstract: Formal NAACP issue brief calling on state legislators to evaluate and regulate the use of AI in predictive policing. Documents that AI-driven predictive policing perpetuates racial bias, violates privacy rights, and undermines community trust. Five key recommendations: establish independent algorithmic oversight bodies; require disclosure of data sources and impact assessments; ensure community participation in AI deployment decisions; eliminate historically biased crime data from algorithms; create legislation with meaningful enforcement penalties.

title: NAACP Resolution on AI Representation and Bias
abstract: Formal resolution adopted at the NAACP's 2024 National Convention. Addresses AI's impact on healthcare, education, criminal justice, labor, and media. Resolves to advocate for mandating comprehensive inclusion of diverse data sets to mitigate algorithmic bias across all sectors. Commits to opposing AI misuse in criminal justice and lending, protecting Black creatives from unauthorized AI exploitation, and demanding government oversight. Recognizes that African Americans have a 10% greater likelihood of automation-based job displacement.

title: NASSCOM Developer's Playbook Responsible AI
abstract: Launched at the inaugural Confluence for Responsible Intelligence (RICON) conference in New Delhi with government endorsement, the playbook is a voluntary practitioner-focused tool designed to help Indian developers systematically address AI risks across commercial development, deployment, and use contexts. It covers AI model conception, data collection/processing, design and development, testing, and post-deployment monitoring. Reviewed and validated by diverse stakeholders from industry, government, academia, and civil society in India and internationally. Published jointly by NASSCOM Responsible AI Hub and Anand & Anand law firm.

title: NASUWT AI and Digital Technologies Principles
abstract: Six principles for the ethical development and application of AI and digital technologies in education: (1) Education as a public good and human right — AI must support education goals and democratic values; (2) Promoting human expertise, review and interaction — AI should empower teachers, not replace professional judgment or direct human interaction; (3) Safety and privacy — uphold data protection rights through impact assessments and clear governance; (4) Protecting teachers' jobs and securing workers' rights — technologies must not replace or displace teachers; (5) Promoting equality, diversity and inclusion — systems should remove risks of bias, discrimination and exclusion; (6) Strategic vision — schools need clear governance based on inform, consult, collaborate and empower principles.

title: NATO Revised AI Strategy (2024)
abstract: The July 2024 revision of NATO's AI Strategy updates outcomes for member-state AI readiness, addresses generative AI and AI-enabled disinformation/information operations, and adds new priorities for interoperability, DIANA ecosystem engagement, and countering adversarial AI.

title: NCAI Resolution on Tribal Digital Sovereignty
abstract: Resolution adopted at NCAI 2024 Mid Year Convention calling upon Federal, State, and local governments to recognize Tribal Digital Sovereignty and its crucial role in modern Tribal self-governance. Builds upon previous resolutions PDX-11-034 (2011), ANC-22-010 (2022), SAC-22-016 (2022), and SAC-22-026 (2022). Collectively asserts that Tribal Nations possess inherent sovereign rights to enforce digital sovereignty standards on AI data usage; AI technologies must not circumvent Tribal data collection protocols; all data collection regarding Tribal communities must comply with Tribal laws; and Tribal Nations maintain authority to determine parameters of data collection, assert ownership, and require non-Tribal entities to comply with Tribal digital standards. 4-page resolution.

title: Nuffield/Ada Lovelace: Predicting the Future of Health (AI and Genomics)
abstract: This joint report from the Nuffield Council on Bioethics and the Ada Lovelace Institute is the final output of a multi-year project on AI and genomics futures. It assesses the potential benefits and ethical risks of AI-powered genomic health prediction (AIGHP) technology, warns that the NHS should not yet widely roll out such technology, and issues 10 recommendations including on consent models for genomic data sharing and new legislation to address genomic discrimination.

title: NCTM Artificial Intelligence and Mathematics Teaching
abstract: NCTM's position affirms that AI-driven tools can personalize mathematics learning and adapt to student interests, but cannot replace teachers' mathematical, pedagogical, and relational expertise. The statement identifies three core concerns: (1) AI systems may contain biases from training data and produce hallucinated incorrect answers, requiring students to develop strong problem-solving skills to critically evaluate AI outputs; (2) teaching and assessment must evolve — AI creates pressure to move beyond shallow computational assessments and makes experienced math educators more critical, not less; and (3) AI offers personalization opportunities by generating customized problems and multiple explanations, but math educators must participate in tool development to address bias. The statement references ISTE (2023) and emphasizes that AI-driven tools can respond to students' thinking in ways previous tools could not, while teachers remain essential guides.

title: NEA Policy Statement on AI in Education
abstract: Adopted by the NEA's 103rd Representative Assembly, this policy statement establishes five core principles for AI use in education: maintaining the student-educator relationship at the center of teaching and learning, requiring evidence-based AI adoption, protecting educators from surveillance and undermining of their rights, prohibiting AI-only high-stakes decisions, and mandating human oversight with risk mitigation and data protection. The statement calls for collective bargaining protections, equitable access, and federal and state legislative advocacy.

title: NEA Task Force Report on Artificial Intelligence in Education
abstract: The NEA Task Force on Artificial Intelligence in Education — established by NEA President Becky Pringle — investigated the current and future roles of AI in Pre-K–12 and higher education and issued five core principles: (1) students and educators must remain at the center of education; (2) evidence-based AI technology must enhance rather than replace educational experiences; (3) ethical development and use of AI with strong data protection practices; (4) equitable access to AI tools across all student demographics; and (5) ongoing education with and about AI literacy. The report notably addresses disability inclusion and environmental impacts of AI infrastructure. It emphasizes that 'Technology is a transformative force in education. The digital revolution and artificial intelligence must be pedagogically harnessed by educators and integrated through active and human-centered teaching and learning methods.' The report was published June 26, 2024, and updated October 22, 2024.

title: NNU Nurses and Patients Bill of Rights on AI
abstract: National Nurses United's formal guiding principles document establishes patients' and nurses' rights in relation to AI deployment in healthcare, asserting that no AI system will ever replace human clinical expertise and judgment. Key rights include the right to high-quality person-to-person care, access to explainable AI rationale, and the right of nurses to exercise professional judgment and override AI decisions without disciplinary threat. The document calls for an immediate pause on implementing unproven AI in health care settings.

title: NRDC At the Crossroads: Managing Data Center Load Growth and AI
abstract: NRDC report by Jake Schmidt and Amanda Levin addressing the electricity demand surge driven by AI-powered data centers. Argues that the AI boom has brought the energy system to a crossroads, with data centers projected to increase from 4.5% to 10% of U.S. electricity consumption within four years. Provides a suite of policy recommendations to manage data center electricity demand including state incentive criteria requiring consumer protection and environmental impact minimization, grid reliability safeguards, and requirements that data center tax breaks include eligibility criteria protecting against negative environmental and economic impacts.

title: NTEU AI Policy Paper
