abstract: AI policy paper endorsed by NTEU National Council in October 2024. Addresses AI's impact on higher education, intellectual property misappropriation, employee data privacy, and calls for cooperative AI design and stronger worker protections.

title: Netherlands: Government-wide Vision on Generative AI
abstract: Submitted to the Dutch Parliament on 17 January 2024, this 54-page vision document addresses generative AI's opportunities and risks and sets out six concrete action lines for government-wide coordination. It affirms the applicability of existing laws, anticipates forthcoming EU AI Act obligations, and calls for a multi-ministerial approach to maximize benefits and mitigate risks of large-scale generative AI adoption.

title: Nigeria National AI Strategy 2024
abstract: Outlined Nigeria's vision for harnessing AI for economic growth, social development, and technological leadership through five pillars: foundational AI infrastructure, world-class AI ecosystem, AI adoption and sector transformation, responsible and ethical AI, and a robust AI governance framework.

title: Nigeria National AI Strategy
abstract: Nigeria's first National AI Strategy, co-created by the National Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (NCAIR) and NITDA and released in August 2024, aims to establish Nigeria as a global AI leader through 32 targeted outcomes over five years, including ethical governance frameworks, high-performance computing infrastructure, and an AI Ethics Expert Group.

title: OAB Recomendação 001/2024 — AI in Legal Practice
abstract: Recomendação do Conselho Federal da OAB estabelecendo diretrizes éticas para o uso responsável da IA generativa na advocacia brasileira, em quatro eixos: Legislação Aplicável, Confidencialidade e Privacidade, Prática Jurídica Ética e Comunicação sobre Uso de IA Generativa. Elaborada pelo Observatório Nacional de Cibersegurança, IA e Proteção de Dados da OAB Nacional.

title: OAS Declaration on Safe and Trustworthy AI 2024
abstract: A declaration and plan of action adopted at the OAS VII Ministerial Meeting on Science and Technology (December 13, 2024) committing Americas member states to sound governance and institutional frameworks for responsible AI deployment. The declaration emphasizes AI's impact across OAS's four pillars (democracy, human rights, integral development, and security) and launched a U.S.-OAS partnership to develop an AI policy framework for the Americas.

title: OAS MIGDIA Framework 2024
abstract: The OAS Inter-American Framework on Data Governance and AI (MIGDIA), released November 2024, provides 104 guidelines across 12 thematic areas to help member states govern AI in public administration. Developed through collaborative working groups with government, civil society, and private sector participants, it includes a regional assessment study and a model policy framework that countries can adapt to national contexts.

title: OECD AI Recommendation 2024 Update
abstract: Major 2024 revision of the OECD AI Principles adopted at the Ministerial Council Meeting on 3 May 2024, reflecting technological developments including generative AI and adding provisions on information integrity, safeguarding against misuse, transparency clarifications, and intellectual property.

title: OECD MCM 2024 Communique on AI
abstract: OECD Ministerial Council Meeting communiqué adopting the 2024 update to the OECD AI Principles and calling on the OECD to develop an action plan to seize the benefits and address the risks of AI in the labour market.

title: OIC-IPHRC Jeddah Declaration on AI
abstract: Adopted at the 23rd Regular Session of the OIC's Independent Permanent Human Rights Commission in Jeddah, this declaration formulates a comprehensive AI ethical strategy aligning Islamic values — including the concepts of Free Will and human trusteeship ('Viceregent') — with global human rights standards. The declaration addresses AI's impact on human rights, ensures ethical development and deployment, and draws on Islam's historical scientific legacy including the foundations of the algorithmic method.

title: Oman National AI Policy 2024
abstract: Published August 2024 by the ITA under the National Programme for Artificial Intelligence and Advanced Digital Technologies. Sets objectives for contributing to digital economy goals, creating enabling environment (legislative, regulatory, technological), enhancing public awareness, encouraging innovation, and ensuring ethical and responsible AI. Applies to all government units, private sector, startups, and academic/research institutions in Oman.

title: Oman General Policy for Safe and Ethical AI
abstract: Issued by the Ministry of Transport, Communications and Information Technology as a national reference framework. Covers all stages from data collection and storage through design, training, deployment, and evaluation of AI systems. Key pillars: transparency, fairness, accountability, privacy protection, and inclusivity. Applies to all regulatory bodies, which are called to align internal systems with the policy. Explicitly covers generative AI.

title: ORG AI Regulation Labour Government Analysis
abstract: Open Rights Group evaluates Labour's AI regulation commitments following the 2024 election, expressing concern that an overarching focus on economic growth could undermine promised protections including binding regulation on powerful AI models. The analysis critiques the narrow regulatory scope, deregulatory legacy risks, and public service AI inequality threats, advocating for rights-based regulation with independent oversight and effective remedies.

title: OTI Comments to NTIA: Open-Source AI Ecosystem (2024)
abstract: OTI's formal comments to the NTIA responding to the request for public input on dual-use foundation AI models with widely available weights. Proposes four guiding principles: study marginal risk with specificity, establish uniform requirements across all foundation models, consider broader national security objectives before sweeping restrictions, and develop thoughtful cybersecurity liability approaches. Advocates for balanced regulation that avoids vague security concerns while fostering a robust open-source AI ecosystem in the United States.

title: PAI Guidelines for Participatory and Inclusive AI
abstract: PAI guidelines developed by a multi-stakeholder task force to support meaningful stakeholder engagement between AI practitioners and representatives from socially marginalized communities. Provides actionable guidance for inclusive approaches in AI design and development processes.

title: PACE Opinion 303 on CoE AI Convention 2024
abstract: PACE unanimously adopted opinion welcoming the draft Framework Convention on AI but expressing regret that it does not equally cover public and private actors. Recommended stronger provisions for private sector accountability and civil society oversight before the convention was finalised in May 2024.

title: PCK AI Era Pastoral Ethics Declaration
abstract: Adopted at the 109th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church of Korea (Tonghap), this declaration establishes AI ethics principles in two parts: (1) a General AI Ethics Statement for Society asserting that AI is not human, must not diminish human capacity, freedom, autonomy, or democracy, must not harm human life, and requires commercial deployment regulation; (2) Pastoral AI Ethics Guidelines requiring pastors to develop AI competency, affirming that sermons must originate from the Holy Spirit not AI generation, mandating verification of AI-generated information, and declaring pastors as bearers of divinely-designed intelligence distinct from artificial intelligence.

title: PCS AI Model Agreement for Civil Service
abstract: The Public and Commercial Services Union (PCS), representing UK civil servants, launched a campaign and model agreement for AI use in government in July 2024. The model agreement stipulates that new AI systems may only be introduced following union consultation and agreement, all existing AI systems must be disclosed, and AI must not lead to job losses. It prohibits AI use in human-affecting decisions including recruitment, promotion, dismissal, and performance monitoring. The agreement requires productivity gains from AI to be shared with workers through reduced hours and increased pay. PCS's national executive committed to campaigning for legislation protecting unions, jobs, and workers' rights.

title: PCUSA 226th GA AI & Faith Study (DOE-07)
abstract: Approved by the Domestic Engagement Committee of the 226th General Assembly (June 25-July 4, 2024, Salt Lake City) with a 36-5 vote. Directs the Advisory Committee on Social Witness Policy (ACSWP) to form a team to study the ethical implications of artificial intelligence technology for society and the work of the church, and to write a policy in response. The team is to work with Research Services and use existing related policies to guide work on AI in the interim. A comprehensive AI policy is being developed for the 227th General Assembly in 2026.

title: Philippines National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 (NAISR 2.0)
abstract: The National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 was adopted by the Philippine Department of Trade and Industry on July 3, 2024, updating the 2021 roadmap to address generative AI and contemporary developments. It establishes strategic priorities across entrepreneurship and competitiveness, public service effectiveness, responsible AI governance, and R&D investment. The document launched alongside the Center for AI Research (CAIR) and positions the Philippines as a regional AI hub.

title: Panoptykon AI Act Civil Society Statement 2024
abstract: A civil society statement co-published by Panoptykon Foundation addressing the EU Artificial Intelligence Act from a fundamental rights perspective. The statement advocates for human rights-based enforcement and implementation of the AI Act following its adoption.

title: PIPC AI Privacy Risk Management Model
abstract: 개인정보보호위원회가 AI 기술 도입·적용 과정에서 개인정보 관련 내부관리체계를 수립·유지하려는 기업·기관을 위해 AI 프라이버시 리스크 관리의 방향과 원칙을 제시한 모델. 2021년 자율점검표를 대체하며, AI 유형·적용 식별, 리스크 측정, 완화조치 마련의 3단계 리스크 관리 프로세스를 포함한다.

title: PIPC Guidance on AI Use of Publicly Available Personal Data
abstract: 다양한 웹사이트에 공개된 데이터가 AI 모델 학습에 이용되는 상황에서 사업자가 공개된 개인정보를 적법하게 활용할 수 있는 법적 근거와 안전조치를 안내한 지침서. 주소·이메일·고유식별정보 등 공개 데이터의 프라이버시 침해 없는 활용 기준을 제시하여 기업의 법적 불확실성을 해소하고자 마련됨.

title: PIT-CNT Documento 0 on AI and Work
abstract: Documento base del PIT-CNT (Instituto Cuesta Duarte, con apoyo de Friedrich Ebert Stiftung) para debate sindical sobre inteligencia artificial y su impacto en el trabajo. Aborda gestión algorítmica, discriminación, salud mental, privacidad de datos, responsabilidad humana en decisiones automatizadas y distribución de ganancias de productividad. Propone comisión especializada en IA, auditorías de algoritmos y protecciones en la negociación colectiva.

title: Providence Joins Rome Call for AI Ethics
abstract: Providence, the largest not-for-profit health system in the western United States, became the first US health system to sign the Rome Call for AI Ethics. Signed by Providence's president and CEO, board chairperson, and sponsors council chairperson. Providence committed to implementing the Rome Call principles through ethics consultations, discernment practices, an internal AI ethics subcommittee, and participation in the Trustworthy & Responsible AI Network (TRAIN) and Coalition for Health AI (CHAI).

title: Cisco Signs Rome Call for AI Ethics
abstract: On April 24, 2024, Cisco Chair and CEO Chuck Robbins signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at a ceremony in Vatican City. Cisco committed to an ethical approach to AI development in the areas of Ethics, Education, and Rights, aligning its six AI principles (transparency, fairness, accountability, privacy, security, reliability) with the Rome Call's framework. Cisco is the first major networking and infrastructure technology company to sign.

title: PSI Generative AI: A Worker's Perspective
abstract: A PSI formal training document and policy guide published October 2024, produced in connection with a global workshop on Generative AI for trade unions and worker advocates. Part 1 explains what Generative AI is, how it works, and use cases, then covers critical problems including worker displacement (up to 300 million jobs at risk per consultancy estimates), lack of accountability, lack of human oversight, hallucinations, cultural insensitivity, environmental impact, intellectual property infringement, and discrimination. Part 2 provides recommendations for workplace AI policies including: guarantees of non-discriminatory AI usage; public right to know when Generative AI is involved in public service decisions; supply chain due diligence for AI development workers including data annotators and content moderators; and mandatory employer disclosure of AI use. Targeted particularly at public sector workers and unions.

title: PCK AI Era Pastoral Ethics Declaration
abstract: The Presbyterian Church of Korea (PCK/Tonghap) adopted at its 109th General Assembly (September 26, 2024) a formal 'AI Era, Pastoral Ethics Declaration' consisting of two parts: (1) a societal AI ethics declaration stating that AI robots are not human, AI must not degrade human capabilities, must not undermine freedom/autonomy/democracy, must not harm human life, and uncontrolled commercial AI deployment must be regulated; and (2) pastoral AI guidelines stating sermons arise from Holy Spirit inspiration not AI generation, pastors must verify AI-generated information against hallucination risks, and pastors possess 'divinely-designed intelligence' (신공지능) that should not be surrendered to artificial systems.

title: PC(USA) AI Study Mandate
abstract: The 226th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.) directed its Advisory Committee on Social Witness Policy to develop a new social witness policy and conduct a study on the responsible use of AI, applying principles of Christian ethics, justice, access, and equity. The PC(USA) Office of Innovation is leading engagement with AI across the denomination.

title: Pepperdine IT AI Position Statement
abstract: Pepperdine University's IT department and Keck Data Science Institute published a formal position statement on AI. The statement frames GenAI as a tool to 'augment -- not replace -- human judgment' while maintaining alignment with Pepperdine's commitment to academic rigor and Christian values. It identifies four opportunity areas (teaching enhancement, learning support, research acceleration, operational efficiency) and six best practices including ethical use, critical evaluation, data privacy, and prompt engineering.

title: Prospect AI Charter for Companies
abstract: Prospect, a UK union representing over 150,000 scientists, engineers, and technology professionals, pledged an AI campaign including a charter for companies at its June 2024 conference. The charter covers seven commitments: job security, ethical practices, training and upskilling, implementation consultation, responsible transition, data privacy, and accountability. General Secretary Mike Clancy stated that 'AI can be a force for good, but it could also destroy economies if not managed properly.' The union campaigns for a 'Just Digital Transition' ensuring worker involvement in decisions about AI deployment and new employment opportunities when technology eliminates jobs.

title: Public Knowledge: Public AI — Infrastructure for the Common Good
abstract: White paper by Public Knowledge Senior Policy Counsel Nicholas Garcia advocating for the development of public artificial intelligence infrastructure accessible to the common good. Argues that AI infrastructure should be governed to benefit society broadly rather than concentrating power among private entities. Recommends using programs like the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) as a foundation for publicly accountable AI development.

title: Public Knowledge: Putting the Public First — Road to Accountable AI
abstract: Policy report by Public Knowledge Senior Policy Counsel Nicholas Garcia responding to the Senate's AI legislative roadmap. Endorses a broader shadow report from public interest organizations. Argues that AI innovation cannot come at the cost of rights and must broadly benefit all people. Recommends establishing a dedicated digital regulator, protecting privacy and civil rights, preventing AI monopolies, and ensuring democratic participation in technology policy. Identifies open-source research and fair use protections as additional critical issues.

title: Public Citizen FCC Comment: AI-Generated Content in Political Ads
abstract: Public Citizen's formal regulatory comment strongly supporting the FCC's proposed rule requiring disclosure of AI-generated content in political advertisements. Argues deepfakes pose a serious threat to democratic integrity. Recommends extending disclosure requirements to cable, DBS, and satellite providers; refining the definition to cover content primarily generated or significantly edited by AI; requiring disclosures at the beginning of ads; and clarifying First Amendment compliance. Cites international election interference examples and US cases including fake Biden robocalls.

title: Qatar Central Bank AI Guideline
abstract: Issued September 4, 2024 by the Qatar Central Bank. Applies to all QCB-licensed entities (banks, insurance, financial services). Requirements: AI governance strategy, corporate governance structures, risk assessments, human oversight mechanisms, AI lifecycle management, data governance and security. Defines 'high-risk' AI systems requiring prior QCB approval before deployment. Addresses corporate governance, risk management, outsourcing, and audit. First binding sectoral AI regulation in Qatar.

title: RCGP Statement on AI in Postgraduate Training, Examinations and Registration
abstract: RCGP guidance (September 2024, subject to annual review) on AI use in GP postgraduate training, MRCGP examinations, and registration. Rejects blanket prohibitions while emphasizing that critical AI use will be a required GP skill. Warns against generic commercial AI (e.g. ChatGPT) for clinical diagnosis or management due to patient safety risks. Addresses educational authenticity, reflective learning requirements, and differential attainment considerations as AI evolves in medical education.

title: Regent College Generative AI Guidelines
abstract: Regent College's Academic Catalogue includes 'Generative AI Guidelines' as part of its Academic Integrity section. The policy defines plagiarism as presenting work of another person or AI text generator as one's own, and categorizes unauthorized use of AI text generators as cheating. This positions AI-generated content within the same framework as traditional academic dishonesty offenses.

title: Hizmet Respect GS AI Ethics Council Declaration
organization: Respect Graduate School / Friends of Respect
abstract: AI Ethics Council (Yapay Zeka Etigi Divani) held June 22-23, 2024, with approximately 500 participants. Organized by Respect Graduate School, a Hizmet (Gulen movement)-affiliated institution. Discussions focused on how 'people of service' (Hizmet) could develop themselves on AI ethics, measures against misuse of AI, and effective/ethical ways of using AI. Featured academic papers from Prof. Dr. Abdullah Demir, Dr. Zeynep Orhan, and others. A final declaration was prepared in both English and Turkish. Materials accessible through HizmetWiki. This is the first known formal AI ethics engagement from the Hizmet movement, representing a significant development in Islamic civil society engagement with AI governance.

title: Religions for Peace Japan - Rome Call Hiroshima Addendum
abstract: Religions for Peace Japan co-organized the historic Hiroshima interfaith AI ethics forum (July 9-10, 2024) with the Pontifical Academy for Life, Abu Dhabi Forum for Peace, and the Chief Rabbinate of Israel. Rev. Yoshiharu Tomatsu (Chairperson, Religions for Peace Japan) committed the organization to ensuring AI promotes 'inclusivity and mutual respect for everyone.' Eleven world religions — including Buddhism, Shinto, Confucianism, Hinduism, Sikhism, and Baha'i — with 16 new signatories from 13 nations committed to the Rome Call for AI Ethics. The event also introduced the Hiroshima Addendum on generative AI governance.

title: Romania National AI Strategy 2024-2027
abstract: Romania's government-approved national AI strategy for 2024-2027 establishes strategic objectives for AI development across public and private sectors. It covers digital transformation of public administration (including AI-assisted risk analysis by the tax authority ANAF), digital economy growth, education and skills development, cybersecurity integration, and governance roadmap aligned with the EU AI Act. The strategy was developed by the Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitalization (MCID) and the Authority for the Digitalization of Romania, with input from the Romanian Committee for Artificial Intelligence (CRIA, established April 2023) and extensive public consultation from September 2023.

title: Royal Society: Science in the Age of AI
abstract: A major Royal Society report examining how AI technologies — including deep learning and large language models — are transforming the nature and methods of scientific inquiry, and exploring changing notions of research integrity, research skills, and research ethics. Based on research with over 100 scientists, an expert working group, a taxonomy of AI in science, a historical review of disruptive technologies, and a patent landscape review. Key recommendations: governments and research funders should improve access to essential AI infrastructure; funders and developers should prioritize accessibility and usability of AI tools for scientific research; research funders and scientific communities should ensure AI-based research meets open science principles; scientific communities should build capacity to oversee AI systems and ensure their ethical use for the public good. Warns that opaque 'black box' AI systems could undermine reproducibility and public trust in scientific findings.

title: Russia AI Alliance Declaration on Responsible Use of Generative AI 2024
abstract: Декларация закрепляет этические принципы, рекомендации и стандарты для разработчиков и пользователей решений на основе генеративного ИИ. Разработана во исполнение президентских поручений, данных по итогам конференции «Путешествие в мир ИИ» (ноябрь 2023 г.). Подписана в рамках AI Day на выставке «Россия» крупнейшими технологическими компаниями и ведущими университетами страны.

title: Russia AI Alliance White Book on AI Ethics 2024
organization: AI Alliance Russia / AI Ethics Commission
abstract: Двухсотстраничное практическое руководство по этике ИИ, дополняющее Кодекс этики 2021 года. Охватывает 42 ключевых вопроса: конфиденциальность данных, предвзятость алгоритмов, автономное принятие решений, создание цифровых копий человека, ответственность разработчиков. Основные рекомендации: обеспечение прозрачности алгоритмов, защита персональных данных, механизмы обжалования решений ИИ, разработка международных стандартов. Адресовано широкому кругу специалистов.

title: Russia AI Strategy 2030 Amendments 2024
abstract: Presidential Decree No. 124 (February 15, 2024) substantially amending (~40 pages) Russia's 2019 National AI Strategy through 2030, targeting 95% AI readiness in critical industries by 2030 (up from 12% in 2022) and annual AI spending of RUB 850 billion. Addresses computing power shortages, personnel gaps, and information security threats; mandates AI developer access to medical, anonymized personal, industrial, and scientific data; and reaffirms principles of security, human autonomy, and risk-oriented regulation.

title: SACRU Catholic Universities AI Roadmap
abstract: Position paper from the SACRU alliance of eight Catholic research universities (Australian Catholic University, Boston College, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Pontifícia Universidade Católica do Rio de Janeiro, Sophia University, Universidade Católica Portuguesa, Universitat Ramon Llull, Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore). Written following a Scientific Colloquium in Milan (July 13-14, 2023). Organized under three headings reflecting Catholic university priorities: teaching, research, and service to society. Presents a normative vision for Catholic universities in a technological age, grounded in Catholic social teaching and human dignity.

title: SBTS Academic Integrity AI Guidelines
abstract: SBTS issued academic integrity guidelines addressing generative AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini). The default policy prohibits generating or using AI text in coursework. Grammarly's generative features are prohibited while traditional grammar-checking remains permitted. The seminary uses Turnitin AI detection and frames AI use within its commitment to 'formation of minds and souls' and maintaining a 'clear distinction' between work by image-bearing humans and artificial intelligence. Individual professors may establish parameters for acceptable usage.

title: SALDEF Co-Signs Coalition Letter on AI Election Misinformation
abstract: SALDEF joined over 200 civil advocacy groups in a coalition letter to CEOs of Meta, Reddit, Google, X, and 8 other tech companies urging stronger defenses against AI-generated misinformation during the 2024 global election cycle. The letter demanded: prohibition of deepfakes in political advertisements, labeling of all AI-generated content in political ads, stronger watermarking systems for AI detection, and increased transparency regarding data powering AI models. Other signatories included Color of Change, GLAAD, Free Press, and Frances Haugen's Beyond the Screen. While SALDEF is a Sikh civil rights organization, this was a broad multi-faith/multi-community coalition effort, not a Sikh-specific AI statement.

title: SDAIA AI Adoption Framework
abstract: Unveiled by SDAIA at the 3rd Global AI Summit (GAIN) in Riyadh, September 10-12, 2024. Provides a practical, risk-aware roadmap for AI implementation across sectors. Sets staged maturity levels and prescribes templates for readiness assessments, use-case prioritisation, and risk mitigation. Announced activation of AI offices in 23 government entities. Builds on SDAIA's 2023 AI Ethics Principles and January 2024 Generative AI Guidelines. Designed to interoperate with Vision 2030, national security, and economic transformation priorities.

title: SDAIA Generative AI Guidelines for Government
abstract: Published January 2024 by SDAIA. Non-binding but authoritative guidance for Saudi government entities and employees. Sets out principles, risk classifications, data handling rules, role definitions (GenAI Champions, Risk Assessors, Procurement Review Boards, Data Protection Officers), and a compliance checklist. Instructs entities to establish or empower internal Data Management Offices. Prevents harms including misinformation, data leakage, discriminatory outcomes, and threats to critical infrastructure. Companion document to the 2023 AI Ethics Principles. A public version also exists separately.

title: SEBI AI/ML Consultation Paper
abstract: Building on SEBI's AI/ML reporting requirements since 2019 circulars to stockbrokers, MIIs, and mutual funds, this consultation paper proposes binding amendments requiring all regulated entities to implement human oversight, robust governance structures, independent audits, five-year data log retention, and clear investor disclosures for AI-driven decisions. The framework addresses black-box opacity risks, algorithmic bias, and cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Entities must hold full operational liability for AI/ML outputs. Public comment period closed November 28, 2024.

title: SGI Statement on Autonomous Weapons Systems
abstract: Delivered by Hayato Yamashita at the Vienna Conference 'Humanity at the Crossroads: Autonomous Weapons and the Challenge of Regulation,' SGI called for an international treaty to prohibit and regulate autonomy in weapons systems. The statement emphasized that AI technologies must prioritize humanity's overall welfare, that life-and-death decisions must never be delegated to machines, and introduced the concept of 'ethical disconnect' — the danger of physical separation between attacker and target enabling dehumanization. SGI called for safeguarding meaningful human autonomy and human rights in the face of AI-enabled weapons.

title: Sri Lanka National Strategy on AI 2028
abstract: Sri Lanka's first National AI Strategy (AI Sri Lanka 2028), published by the Ministry of Technology in July 2024, aims to position the country as a regional hub for responsible and inclusive AI development through dual tracks: immediate value via pilots in healthcare, education, and public services, and long-term investments in skills, infrastructure, and data governance. The strategy establishes a National Centre for AI and a Responsible AI Advisory Council, guided by seven core principles including inclusivity, trustworthiness, human-centricity, and sustainability.

title: Senegal National AI Strategy (SNIA 2024)
abstract: La SNIA 2024 du Sénégal définit une vision d'une IA éthique, de confiance et souveraine, avec six orientations stratégiques : promouvoir 100 000 talents en IA d'ici 2028, bâtir un écosystème IA dynamique, renforcer les alliances régionales et internationales (Sénégal solidaire), faire de l'IA l'affaire de tous, et mettre en place une gouvernance pour une IA éthique. Élaborée avec l'ANSTS, elle prévoit 7 milliards FCFA sur deux ans.

title: Church of Sweden AI Policy
abstract: In June 2024, the Church Board (Kyrkostyrelsen) adopted a formal policy on AI use, accompanied by guidelines (Riktlinje för nyttjande av artificiell intelligens). The policy is binding at the national level and offered to dioceses, parishes, and congregations. It permits AI for administration and energy management but prohibits its use in pastoral care (själavård). The policy requires feasibility and risk analysis before AI deployment, and emphasizes responsibility, transparency, ethics, and data protection. Available as downloadable PDF on the church website.

title: Sierra Club Demanding Better: AI Data Center Energy Guide
abstract: Formal policy guide published by the Sierra Club addressing the unprecedented electricity demand surge driven by AI data centers, expected to grow from 3% to 8-9% of US power demand by 2030. Directed at data center developers and large tech companies. Recommends: implementing 24/7 carbon-free energy solutions; adopting advanced green tariffs; engaging directly with utilities; advocating for clean energy standards; demanding utility planning transparency to prevent stranded fossil fuel assets; and ensuring residential ratepayers are protected from bearing the financial burden of AI infrastructure buildout. States that purchasing renewable credits 'in a far away place is no longer sufficient.'

title: Society of Authors AI Resolution and Letters to Tech Companies
abstract: Following an Extraordinary General Meeting on May 2, 2024, where members voted in favour of a resolution asserting they do not consent to generative AI developers using their works, the SoA sent formal letters (August 2024) to Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, Apple, and Meta. Letters demand: identification of works used to train AI models, a system for obtaining permission, 'appropriate remuneration', and removal of any work used without permission with evidence of compliance. Seven-day deadline to acknowledge receipt, 21-day deadline for substantive response. Survey of 12,500+ members showed 95% demand consent before use and 94% demand credit and compensation. A quarter of illustrators and a third of translators already losing work to AI.

title: Solidarność Position on AI in the Workplace
abstract: Stanowisko Prezydium Komisji Krajowej NSZZ Solidarność nr 3/24, przyjęte 4 grudnia 2024 r. Dokument przedstawia postulaty związku dotyczące wdrażania sztucznej inteligencji w miejscu pracy, w tym obowiązkowe programy przekwalifikowania, transparentność systemów AI, regularne audyty algorytmów pod kątem niedyskryminacji, ochronę danych osobowych pracowników, dialog ze związkami zawodowymi oraz szczególną ochronę pracowników starszych i zagrożonych wykluczeniem cyfrowym.

title: South Africa National AI Policy Framework
abstract: Released for public comment in October 2024 by South Africa's Department of Communications and Digital Technologies, this framework establishes the country's vision for responsible and trustworthy AI, outlining principles to shape regulatory approaches and cultivate a dynamic AI ecosystem through research, talent development, and infrastructure enhancement.

title: NHRC Korea AI Human Rights Impact Assessment Tool
abstract: 국가인권위원회가 AI 개발·활용 주체가 기술적 위험성뿐 아니라 인권에 미치는 영향과 심각도를 총체적으로 점검할 수 있도록 마련한 총 4단계 72개 문항 평가 도구. 과기정통부장관에게 공공기관 AI 전체 및 민간 고위험 AI에 대한 자율적 인권영향평가 수행과 보급을 권고하는 의견 표명과 함께 발표.

title: South Korea AI Basic Act
abstract: South Korea's comprehensive AI framework law — the second nationally comprehensive AI legislation enacted globally after the EU AI Act — passed by the National Assembly on December 26, 2024, promulgated January 21, 2025, and effective January 22, 2026. Establishes risk-based requirements for high-impact AI and generative AI, promotes innovation, and creates institutional governance structures under MSIT.

title: TC260 AI Safety Governance Framework 1.0 (2024)
abstract: Framework 1.0 provides a comprehensive, non-mandatory governance architecture for AI safety in China, addressing risks along the entire AI value chain. It covers explainability deficiencies, algorithmic bias, data breaches, security vulnerabilities, and misuse scenarios. Officially published in both Chinese and English by TC260, it forms a baseline for industry self-governance and future mandatory standards.

title: TC260 Basic Safety Requirements for Generative AI Services 2024
abstract: TC260-003 specifies safety requirements for providers of generative AI services, including requirements for training data (corpus) safety, model safety, safety measures, and security assessment processes. It is non-mandatory but represents the leading technical standard for the generative AI sector, complementing the legally binding CAC Interim Measures 2023. Later superseded as national standard GB/T 45654-2025.

title: TCU Guia de IA Generativa
abstract: Guia prático do TCU para uso ético e responsável de ferramentas de IA generativa por servidores, estagiários e terceirizados. Abrange segurança de dados, standards de governança para ferramentas internas (ChatTCU, CopilotTCU) e externas, e princípios de privacidade e confiabilidade para adoção de IA. O TCU foi um dos primeiros órgãos públicos do Brasil a oferecer tecnologia de IA generativa conectada a múltiplos sistemas internos.

title: Faithful Futures AI & the Church Summit Journal
abstract: The first-ever multi-denominational AI summit (August 12-15, 2024, Seattle) brought together leaders from three mainline Protestant denominations — the Episcopal Church, ELCA, and PCUSA — along with representatives from Catholic, Orthodox, United Methodist, and Evangelical traditions. Featured a keynote by Father Paolo Benanti (Vatican AI advisor) and futurist Bob Johansen. Produced the 'AI & the Church Summit Journal' with transcripts, videos, and slides. Concluded that addressing AI's ethical, theological, and ecclesiological implications 'requires expertise that no one church body can fully address' and established this as an ongoing ecumenical conversation. No formal joint declaration was issued, but this was the foundational event for the larger 2025 Faithful Futures gathering.

title: TIU/TEDS Generative AI Academic Integrity Policy
abstract: Trinity International University's academic catalog treats the use of generative AI tools without faculty permission and proper citation as a violation of the university's academic integrity policy. The policy permits AI use only when faculty grant explicit permission and students provide proper citation. Additional detailed guidelines are contained in the TEDS Student Handbook. The institution provides faculty resources including presentations on 'Teaching in the Age of AI' and AI detection tool guidance.

title: TUAC Statement on AI and Immersive Technology Risks
abstract: Published 15 May 2024, TUAC's statement calls on OECD governments for urgent policy action on AI and immersive technologies. It argues that principles and voluntary codes of conduct are insufficient and that binding regulation is needed to safeguard human rights, protect democracy, and ensure benefits for all. The statement highlights a 53-fold increase in generative AI incidents since 2022, risks of high-skilled job displacement, perpetuation of discrimination through AI systems, and mass data collection threats from VR (20 minutes of VR generates ~2 million data points). It demands comprehensive regulatory frameworks with human oversight and democratic accountability.

title: TUC AI (Regulation and Employment Rights) Bill 2024
abstract: TUC's draft legislative proposal published 18 April 2024, produced by a Special Advisory Committee and AI Taskforce convened in 2023 (meeting September 2023, November 2023, February 2024; approved by TUC Executive Committee 5 March 2024). The Bill regulates employer use of AI systems in relation to workers, employees and jobseekers. Key provisions concern 'high-risk decision-making' — decisions with potential legal or significant consequences for workers — including: mandatory employer consultation with trade unions before deploying AI in high-risk decisions; transparency and explainability requirements; prohibition of fully automated disciplinary decisions; right to human review; anti-discrimination safeguards; and enforcement mechanisms. While not formally introduced to Parliament (a draft advocacy document), it represents TUC's formal institutional position on AI workplace legislation. Survey cited: 69% of workers think employers should consult staff before introducing AI; 86% oppose AI making firing decisions.

title: Norway Teknologirådet: Generative AI in Norway
abstract: This Teknologirådet report maps the paradigm shift of generative AI and identifies 12 priority recommendations for Norwegian government. Key concerns include concentration of corporate power, job displacement, AI-generated disinformation, and election integrity. Policy proposals span regulation, public AI infrastructure, language preservation, fact-checking capacity, and psychological defence against foreign disinformation campaigns.

title: Thailand ETDA GenAI Governance Guideline 2024
abstract: แนวทางการใช้ปัญญาประดิษฐ์เชิงสร้างสรรค์ด้วยธรรมาภิบาลที่ดีสำหรับองค์กร ออกโดย MDES และ ETDA ผ่านศูนย์ธรรมาภิบาล AI (AIGC) เมื่อวันที่ 30 ตุลาคม 2567 เอกสารประกอบด้วย 5 ส่วน: ความเข้าใจเกี่ยวกับ Generative AI, ประโยชน์และข้อจำกัด, ความเสี่ยง, ข้อพิจารณาในการนำไปใช้งาน, และหลักธรรมาภิบาลที่ดีสำหรับองค์กร มุ่งส่งเสริมการใช้ AI อย่างมีความรับผิดชอบและมีจริยธรรม

title: AI and the Afterlife
abstract: Research report by Nathan Mladin examining how AI is reshaping our relationship with death and bereavement. Covers digital legacy and memorialisation, AI-powered 'griefbots' and digital personas, and the transhumanist concept of mind uploading. Argues that while AI technology may help with grief under certain conditions, hyper-realistic deepfake simulations of the deceased pose serious ethical dangers. Compares transhumanist and Christian beliefs about death, the body, and the afterlife.

title: UGT Guide on Digitalization AI and Occupational Risk Prevention 2024
abstract: Guía de UGT sobre digitalización, inteligencia artificial y prevención de riesgos laborales. Aborda cómo las tecnologías digitales e IA afectan la seguridad y salud en el trabajo, las necesidades de formación de los trabajadores, y la protección de datos. Destaca la formación y la información a los trabajadores como pilares fundamentales para un entorno laboral digital seguro y eficiente.

title: UN HLAB-AI Final Report: Governing AI for Humanity
abstract: Final report of the 39-expert advisory body established by the Secretary-General, proposing seven recommendations for inclusive global AI governance including an International Scientific Panel on AI, a Global Dialogue on AI Governance, a Global Fund on AI for the SDGs, an AI Capacity Development Network, a Standards Exchange, a Global Data Framework, and a small AI Office at the UN.

title: UMC Discipleship Ministries AI Usage Guidelines
abstract: Agency-level AI usage guidelines published January 2024 by UMC Discipleship Ministries, featuring a 'Three Categories of Generated Content' framework. The guidelines govern how AI tools may be used in content development, requiring all final text to be written, reviewed, and approved by human contributors. Recognized by other denominations (notably cited as 'incredibly helpful' by an ELCA Bishop). Note: this is an agency-level document from Discipleship Ministries, not a General Conference resolution; the 2024 General Conference did not pass a formal AI resolution.

title: G7 Toolkit AI in Public Sector 2024
abstract: G7 practical toolkit developed with OECD and UNESCO support providing frameworks and tools for governments to responsibly develop, deploy, and use AI in the public sector while respecting rule of law, democracy, and human rights.

title: UN Global Digital Compact 2024
abstract: First truly worldwide agreement on international AI cooperation, adopted as an annex to the Pact for the Future at the UN Summit of the Future, establishing commitments to govern AI through an Independent International Scientific Panel on AI and a Global Dialogue on AI Governance, with a goal of global AI standards by 2030.

title: UN Pact for the Future 2024
abstract: Landmark UN agreement adopted by all member states at the Summit of the Future, containing 56 commitments including on science, technology, and innovation and digital cooperation, with the Global Digital Compact and Declaration on Future Generations as annexes.

title: UNGA Resolution 78/265: Safe & Trustworthy AI
abstract: The first-ever UNGA resolution on artificial intelligence, adopted without a vote by 193 member states at the 63rd plenary meeting of the 78th session. Led by the United States and co-sponsored by over 120 countries, it calls for safe, secure, and trustworthy AI systems that promote sustainable development and bridge digital divides.

title: UNGA Resolution 78/311: AI Capacity-Building
abstract: UNGA resolution proposed by China and co-sponsored by over 140 countries, adopted unanimously, emphasizing human-centered AI development and encouraging international cooperation to strengthen AI capacity-building especially in developing countries. Advocates for an open, fair, and non-discriminatory business environment and a central UN role in AI cooperation.

title: UNGA Resolution A/RES/78/265 on AI 2024
abstract: First-ever standalone UN General Assembly resolution on AI, adopted by consensus of all 193 member states (US-led, co-sponsored by 123 states), establishing a global consensus approach to AI governance centered on safe, secure, trustworthy AI, human rights, and accelerating the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.

title: UNGA Resolution A/RES/79/239 AI Military
abstract: First UN General Assembly resolution specifically on AI in the military domain, affirming the applicability of international law throughout the AI lifecycle, encouraging states to address AI opportunities and challenges related to military applications, and requesting a Secretary-General report.

title: UNICRI Principles for Responsible AI Innovation
abstract: UNICRI principles for the responsible use of AI by law enforcement agencies, addressing lawfulness, human rights considerations, fairness, accountability, transparency, explainability, safety, and robustness. Part of the broader Toolkit for Responsible AI Innovation in Law Enforcement, revised February 2024.

title: UNI Europa Banking Sector Joint Declaration on AI 2024
organization: UNI Europa Finance / EBF / EACB / ESBG / WSBI
abstract: Joint declaration signed by 90 European social partners in the banking sector on employment aspects of artificial intelligence. Commits to: ensuring AI deployment does not lead to unjustified job losses; continuous training and upskilling of employees; ongoing consultation with employee representatives before implementing significant AI systems; protecting working conditions and fair treatment; and balancing business efficiency gains with social responsibility. First of its kind in the EU banking sector. Signed at a ceremony on May 14, 2024.

title: UNSCEB Framework: Model Policy on Responsible AI
abstract: Non-mandatory guidance framework for UN system organizations developing their own AI policies, structured around AI ethics, governance, risk management, and monitoring and compliance. Rooted in the CEB Principles for the Ethical Use of AI and addresses standards for development, testing, acquisition, and interoperability of foundational AI technologies.

title: UNSCEB Report: Operational Use of AI in UN System
abstract: HLCM report documenting how AI is currently being used operationally across UN system organizations, providing an evidence base for AI governance decisions. Companion document to the Framework for a Model Policy on the Responsible Use of AI in the UN System.

title: UNSCEB White Paper on AI Governance
abstract: Approved by HLCP at its 47th session and endorsed by CEB, this white paper analyzes the UN system's institutional models, functions, and existing international normative frameworks applicable to global AI governance. Developed by the Inter-Agency Working Group on AI (IAWG-AI), co-led by UNESCO and ITU, comprising over 40 UN entities.

title: UODO Poland Position on AI Act Implementation
abstract: The President of Poland's Personal Data Protection Office (UODO), Mirosław Wróblewski, issued formal guidance on considerations for Poland's implementation of the EU AI Act. The position stresses that the AI Act's implementation into Polish law must ensure compliance with constitutional personal data protections (Article 51) and privacy rights (Article 47), as well as full alignment with GDPR and the Police Directive. It notes that AI systems inherently involve large-scale processing of personal data including special categories, and calls for systematic review of national provisions to identify and resolve interactions between the AI Act and existing data protection laws. UODO offered expert support for legislative drafting and emphasized the office's readiness to engage with the emerging AI governance framework.

title: USCCB Labor Day Statement on AI and Dignity
abstract: Issued jointly by Archbishop Borys Gudziak and Bishop Mark J. Seitz, this Labor Day statement addresses the impact of AI on labor and workers, calling for an economy that honors the sacredness of human work and protects the dignity of all laborers. The bishops emphasize that technology, including AI, must enrich rather than diminish human labor and dignity.

title: UNISON: AI and New Technology in the Workplace Guide
abstract: UNISON's September 2024 updated guide on AI and new technology in the workplace. Covers AI basics, AI in recruitment, algorithmic management, biometrics, data privacy risks, and job displacement. Supports TUC's Artificial Intelligence (Regulation and Employment) Bill. Includes model new technology policy. Addresses democratic deficit in public sector AI deployment and calls for worker representative consultation before algorithm deployment.

title: Uruguay National AI Strategy 2024-2030
abstract: Uruguay's comprehensive national AI strategy for 2024-2030, approved by the Strategic Committee of the Public Sector for Artificial Intelligence and Data on November 21, 2024. Developed with technical cooperation from CAF and UNESCO through a broad participatory process, the strategy organizes action lines across three pillars: AI governance, capacity and technology, and sustainable development applications.

title: Uzbekistan AI Strategy until 2030
abstract: Uzbekistan's Strategy for the Development of AI Technologies until 2030, issued by presidential resolution on 14 October 2024, establishes five objectives: developing a legal framework aligned with international standards, deploying AI across banking, healthcare, agriculture, and other priority sectors, building technical infrastructure and datasets, cultivating human capital, and fostering international partnerships. The strategy sets targets of $1.5 billion in AI revenue by 2030 and top-50 ranking on the Government AI Readiness Index, with a dedicated Center for AI and Digital Economy Development overseeing implementation.

title: Pope Francis World Day of Peace 2024: AI and Peace
abstract: Pope Francis addresses the promise and peril of artificial intelligence in an eight-section message, emphasizing that AI must serve humanity's integral development guided by human dignity and fundamental rights. He calls for international cooperation to establish binding treaties regulating AI, warns against autonomous lethal weapons systems, and urges global educational engagement with AI ethics.

title: WEF AIGA Briefing Paper Series 2024
abstract: Series of briefing papers released January 2024 providing strategies for equitable AI advancement, structured around the 360-degree governance framework. Covers: Harness (learning from past AI governance), Build (present policy interventions), and Plan (future-proofing AI regulation). Supports policy-makers and regulators with implementable strategies.

title: Woodenfish Buddhism, Consciousness, and AI Summit
abstract: The Woodenfish Foundation's 5th Forum on Buddhism, Science, and Future (June 21-23, 2024, Grand Hotel, Taipei) convened international scholars, Buddhist practitioners, and AI researchers to explore the intersection of Buddhist philosophy, consciousness studies, and AI ethics. Sessions included 'AI and Buddhist Ethics,' 'Building Bodhisattvas: Towards Powerful, Reliable, and Caring Intelligence,' and discussions on how AI consciousness differs from human consciousness. The conference brought Buddhist ethical frameworks (compassion, mindfulness, non-harm) into direct dialogue with AI development practices.

title: WHO Ethics Guidance on Large Multi-Modal Models
abstract: WHO guidance document addressing ethics and governance of large multi-modal models (LMMs) — generative AI systems accepting multiple data types. Provides over 40 recommendations for governments, technology companies, and healthcare providers across five health applications: diagnosis and clinical care, patient self-care, administrative tasks, medical education, and scientific research/drug development. Updates and extends the 2021 WHO AI guidance. ISBN 978-92-4-008475-9.

title: WTS Honor Code AI Provision
abstract: Westminster Theological Seminary's Honor Code explicitly addresses AI-generated content within its plagiarism framework. The policy states that any assignment including words, ideas, or data generated by artificial intelligence (e.g., ChatGPT) must include complete, accurate, and specific references. Rather than banning AI outright, WTS treats AI-generated content similarly to other sources requiring proper attribution. Failure to cite AI outputs constitutes plagiarism under the Honor Code, which is grounded in the Eighth and Ninth Commandments.

title: YOK Turkey GenAI Ethics Guide Higher Education
abstract: This guide from Turkey's Council of Higher Education (YÖK) addresses the ethical use of generative AI tools in scientific research and academic publication. Prepared by a commission of expert academics and reviewed by universities before adoption by YÖK's General Assembly in May 2024, the guide aims to help institutions understand and evaluate risks and opportunities in generative AI, and to develop measures protecting scientific integrity and trust in science. It establishes eight core ethical values applicable to AI-assisted academic work: transparency, integrity, diligence, justice and respect, privacy, confidentiality, accountability, and contributing to an ethical climate. The guide provides specific rules for researchers, students, and administrators on disclosing AI use, copyright considerations, and data protection in academic publication contexts.

title: Yeshiva University: Rome Call for AI Ethics Signing (Hiroshima)
abstract: Rabbi Dr. Ari Berman, President of Yeshiva University, signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at the 'AI Ethics for Peace: World Religions commit to the Rome Call' conference in Hiroshima, Japan, July 8-10, 2024. The YU delegation included Rabbi Meir Soloveichik and Rabbi Daniel Feldman. Berman described it as 'an important mandate born of universal moral principles that transcend faith, nationality, and profession.' YU joined alongside the Chief Rabbinate of Israel, representing Judaism at this expanded multifaith commitment alongside other world religions, technology executives from Microsoft, Google, and Cisco, and governmental representatives.

title: Ethics AI Islam Summit Statement (Zaytuna)
abstract: Zaytuna College hosted the Ethics, AI + Islam Summit as part of Muslim Tech Week, bringing together Islamic scholars, ethicists, and technologists to develop recommendations for AI governance grounded in Islamic ethics. The summit addressed questions of Islamic law (fiqh) applications to AI, the protection of human dignity in algorithmic systems, and the role of the Muslim scholarly tradition in global AI governance.

title: Zambia National AI Strategy 2024-2026
abstract: Zambia's National AI Strategy 2024-2026, launched on Independence Day (October 24, 2024) by the Ministry of Technology and Science, addresses six pillars — policy and regulation, human capital, infrastructure, research and innovation, industry development, and international collaboration — with the aim of boosting GDP by nearly 8% by 2030, developed with support from Finland and the Tony Blair Foundation.

title: AACC-FLI-AI-Faith-Consultation
abstract: Two-day consultation held July 8-9, 2025 in Nairobi, gathering 25 African church leaders (bishops and archbishops) to explore AI opportunities, risks, and challenges. Co-organized by AACC and Future of Life Institute. Key outcomes: (1) establishment of permanent AACC AI and Faith Working Group; (2) training programs for church leaders; (3) incorporation of AI learning in seminaries and Christian education curricula; (4) development of policy briefs for governments and the African Union; (5) creation of an African-centered ethical framework for AI development and use. Identified risks include cultural/religious identity erosion, disinformation, job displacement, and compromised human moral control. AACC General Secretary Rev. Dr. Fidon Mwombeki stated: 'We faith leaders need to pay attention to the moral and ethical implications of its development and use.'

title: AARP AI Policy Positions
abstract: AARP's formal policy positions on AI, adopted through the AARP Board of Directors in the 2025–2026 Policy Book. Notes that AI is manifesting new forms of discrimination, fraud, disinformation, and harmful financial practices targeting older adults. Calls for two core solutions: (1) greater government resources and technical expertise for agency oversight of AI industry; (2) dynamic and flexible regulatory standards developed in partnership among policymakers, consumer groups, and industry. Emphasizes that older adults prefer AI integrated into existing products, not replacing human interaction.

title: ABA Statement on AI Innovation in Financial Services
abstract: ABA Congressional statement positioning banking as a model for responsible AI deployment at scale. Argues existing federal regulatory frameworks — supervision, examination, and enforcement — already govern AI in banking, and no additional AI-specific legislation is needed. Cites $25.1 trillion industry scale, $19.7 trillion in deposits, $13.2 trillion in loans.

title: ACLU Statement on Trump's Attack on State AI Regulation
abstract: Formal ACLU statement criticizing President Trump's December 2025 executive order targeting state AI regulation, which instructs federal agencies to deny funding to states with AI regulations deemed more than 'minimally burdensome.' The ACLU argues the order is constitutionally problematic, undermines public safety, and violates Supreme Court precedent. Calls on Congress to pass the AI Civil Rights Act of 2025.

title: ACRL AI Competencies for Academic Library Workers
abstract: Approved by the ACRL Board of Directors in October 2025. A formal institutional framework from the Association of College and Research Libraries (a division of ALA) outlining essential competencies for academic library workers engaging with artificial intelligence. Developed to address AI's growing integration into higher education. The competencies span five key areas: (1) Ethical Considerations — addressing equity, fairness, privacy, accountability, and broader societal impacts; (2) Knowledge and Understanding — building foundational AI literacy; (3) Analysis and Evaluation — assessing AI tools' benefits, risks, and appropriateness; (4) Use and Application — implementing AI for efficiency and innovation while prioritizing accessibility. Emphasizes that adoption should be selective and values-aligned rather than indiscriminate, and is designed as an adaptable foundation that institutions can customize for specific job functions.

title: ADL Report: Generating Hate — AI Bias in LLMs
abstract: Formal institutional research report from the ADL Center for Technology and Society, co-produced with Builders for Tomorrow. Evaluated GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama through 34,400 total queries across six categories of antisemitism and anti-Israel bias. All four models showed concerning biases. Formal policy recommendations for AI developers include pre-deployment testing with civil society partners, NIST Risk Management Framework adoption, and bias benchmarking. For governments: regulatory frameworks requiring independent audits, AI safety investment, and mandatory disclaimers on controversial topics.

title: AFL-CIO Workers First AI Principles
abstract: The AFL-CIO's Workers First Initiative on AI establishes eight guiding principles for the responsible development and deployment of artificial intelligence in workplaces, covering strengthening labor rights, advancing guardrails against harmful workplace AI, supporting copyright protections, developing worker-centered workforce training, institutionalizing worker voice in AI research and development, requiring transparency and accountability, modeling best practices in government procurement, and protecting civil rights and democratic integrity.

title: AHA: Pragmatic AI Evaluation in Healthcare
abstract: This advisory outlines pragmatic, risk-based evaluation and monitoring strategies for AI tools to improve patient safety and outcomes. It addresses the urgent gap between rapid AI deployment in cardiovascular and stroke care and traditional evaluation frameworks, proposing risk-proportionate approaches. The advisory encourages health systems to integrate AI governance into existing quality assurance programs.

title: AHA: TRUE-AIM AI Research Quality Standards
abstract: This journal-level quality assurance initiative establishes standardized reporting requirements for AI research submitted to AHA journals. Authors provide responses using predefined single-choice answers (yes/partially/no/not applicable) or short answers across methodological dimensions, aiming to promote high-quality, reproducible AI research with generalizable, inclusive models that minimize potential for harm.

title: Amnesty International: AI Action Summit Regulation Call
abstract: Published ahead of the February 2025 Paris AI Action Summit, Amnesty International calls for binding and enforceable international AI regulation to curb AI-driven harms. The statement cites evidence of grave consequences from unchecked AI, including lethal autonomous weapons, facial recognition used for mass surveillance, and risk-scoring algorithms deployed in migration and the public sector. Amnesty urges summit participants to prioritize human rights-centered, enforceable frameworks over voluntary commitments.

title: Amnesty International Algorithmic Accountability Toolkit
abstract: Amnesty International's Algorithmic Accountability Toolkit provides civil society organizations, journalists, and activists with practical methods to investigate and hold accountable automated decision-making systems deployed by governments. Drawing on Amnesty's Algorithmic Accountability Lab investigations across Denmark, Sweden, Serbia, France, India, the UK, the US, the Occupied Palestinian Territory, and the Netherlands, the toolkit covers eight areas: project scoping, goals, ethics, information access, human rights research methodology, technical auditing, advocacy, and accountability mechanisms. It emphasizes a sociotechnical approach centering affected communities and human rights law over AI ethics frameworks.

title: AIA AI Policy Resolution (2025)
abstract: Passed with 99% approval at the AIA 2025 Annual Business Meeting on June 5, 2025, sponsored by the AIA Strategic Council. This resolution establishes governance frameworks and promotes ethical adoption of AI in architectural practice. Key mandated initiatives include: development of formal AI Usage Policies for the profession; creation of an AI Education and Exchange Platform; establishment of an AI Ethical Framework integrated into the Code of Ethics; and incorporation of AI considerations into AIA's 2026–2030 Strategic Plan. The resolution balances innovation opportunity with responsibility, addressing ethical concerns while safeguarding the profession's integrity and the public's welfare.

title: AICPA Guidelines for Responsible Use of AI in Forensic and Valuation Services
abstract: Non-authoritative guidelines issued by the AICPA Forensic and Valuation Services Executive Committee in October 2025. Developed by the AICPA FVS Artificial Intelligence Workgroup, the document addresses specific risks and professional responsibilities associated with using AI tools in forensic and valuation service engagements, with particular attention to the AICPA Professional Code of Conduct and the 'Compliance With Standards Rule.' Includes common-sense reminders such as verifying that AI use aligns with engagement terms, confidentiality agreements, and professional judgment requirements. Emphasizes exercising professional judgment, maintaining data security, and ensuring the reasonableness of AI-generated results. Note: Guidelines are non-authoritative and do not supersede existing professional standards. Document is members-only.

title: Comply To Fly? TSA Facial Recognition Report
abstract: By Joy Buolamwini, Sushma Raman, and Andrea Dean. Based on hundreds of travelers' testimonies from AJL's Freedom Flyers Campaign, documents how the TSA's facial recognition program at U.S. airports — originally described as a voluntary pilot — has expanded to over 250 airports with inconsistent informed consent practices and opt-out implementation, effectively making participation mandatory for many travelers.

title: ALECSO AI Ethics Charter
abstract: The ALECSO Charter on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence was adopted at a high-level virtual meeting on June 17, 2025, following a drafting process begun in February 2025. Developed with experts from across Arab member states, it is the first Arab reference framework focused on the ethical use of AI in education, culture, and science. Core principles include justice, transparency, privacy, cultural diversity, and digital sovereignty. An official English translation was subsequently issued. The charter was presented at the first Arab AI Forum in August 2025.

title: AWS Responsible AI Policy
abstract: AWS's Responsible AI Policy governs use of all AWS AI/ML services, prohibiting intentional disinformation, privacy violations, non-consensual synthetic media, exploitation of minors, and weaponization without human control. For consequential decisions affecting fundamental rights, health, or safety, users must implement human oversight and risk mitigation measures.

title: AI Now 2025 Landscape Report: Artificial Power
abstract: The 2025 AI Now Landscape Report examines how post-AI-boom integration of AI everywhere grants technology companies and tech oligarchs power far exceeding their financial resources, arguing that AI systems are being deployed on the public rather than by them. The report transitions from diagnosis to action, providing strategies for organizers, lawmakers, and citizens to reshape AI's trajectory toward serving public interests.

title: ANPD NT 12/2025 — Automated Decisions Consultation Results
abstract: Consolidação das 124 contribuições recebidas na Tomada de Subsídios sobre IA e Revisão de Decisões Automatizadas (novembro 2024 – janeiro 2025), abrangendo mais de 2.000 páginas. Cobre cinco blocos temáticos: princípios da LGPD, bases legais, direitos dos titulares, boas práticas e diretrizes regulatórias. Parte do Item 7 da Agenda Regulatória da ANPD (biênio 2025-2026) sobre regulação do art. 20 da LGPD.

title: APU AI Policy
abstract: Azusa Pacific University launched a campus-wide AI policy at the start of fall 2025 semester. Developed by the AI Stewardship Group co-chaired by VP for Academic Services Louise Huang and CIO Joe Desuta, the policy permits AI use for brainstorming, research support, and editing with instructor permission and proper disclosure. It is explicitly grounded in APU's Christ-centered mission, framing responsible AI use as Christian stewardship and using 'Christian ethics to form boundaries around the use of AI.'

title: ARC Roundtable on AI and Religion in Asia
abstract: The 15th International Roundtable on 'The Media and Religion & Spirituality in Asia' was held November 1-2, 2025, at Payap University, Chiang Mai, Thailand. Organized by the Asian Research Center for Religion and Social Communication (ARC) in partnership with the Department of Peace Studies, International College, the roundtable brought together experts and scholars from five continents to examine how artificial intelligence reshapes faith, communication, and moral consciousness across Asia.

title: ASEAN Expanded GenAI Guide 2025
abstract: Expansion of the 2024 ASEAN AI Governance Guide specifically addressing generative AI risks not covered by traditional AI governance, with updated principles and best practices for responsible development and deployment of generative AI systems across the ASEAN region.

title: Joint Statement on Ethics and Artificial Intelligence (Rome)
abstract: Joint statement signed at a summit hosted by the American Security Foundation in Rome on October 22-23, 2025. Calls for AI that respects human dignity and remains under human control. Outlines five essential principles: accuracy, transparency, privacy and security, human dignity, and the common good. Demands independent evaluation of AI systems, safeguards to protect children and vulnerable people, and vigilance that AI never replaces human relationships or moral responsibility. Signatories include Kristine Torjesen (BioLogos), Rev. Dr. Walter Kim (National Association of Evangelicals), Rev. Johnnie Moore (Congress of Christian Leaders), Gabriel Salguero (National Latino Evangelical Coalition), Micah Goodman (Shalom Hartman Institute), and Rabbi David Bashevkin (18Forty).

title: ASME Position Statement on AI Use for ASME External Content
abstract: Issued March 5, 2025 by ASME Executive Director/CEO Thomas Costabile, P.E., FASME (formal date February 14, 2025 on the PDF), this position statement establishes requirements and expectations for accountability and disclosure of AI tool use in ASME external content, including journal articles, conference proceedings, and other publications. Key requirements include transparency about AI involvement, human oversight of AI-generated materials, legal compliance (privacy, IP, anti-discrimination laws), and prohibition on use of generative AI in journal submissions without disclosure. ASME intends to convert this statement into a formal policy. Contact: aipolicy@asme.org.

title: Australia National AI Plan 2025
abstract: Australia's National AI Plan sets a three-pillar agenda: capturing opportunities through AI infrastructure and capability investment, spreading benefits through workforce training and public service AI adoption, and keeping Australians safe through legislative guardrails and international engagement. The plan includes consolidation of SME support under the National AI Centre, appointment of Chief AI Officers across all agencies, and an accelerated automated decision-making legal framework.

title: BCS AI Ethics and Professional Registrations
abstract: BCS report based on a YouGov survey of 2,000+ UK adults finding 85% support for mandatory professional registration for IT professionals working on public-impacting systems. Recommends mandatory public sector registration, a public register for high-impact AI roles, and government leadership on ethical standards.

title: Burkina Faso National AI Action Plan 2026-2028
abstract: Lancé le 27 août 2025 par le Ministère de la Transition Digitale, des Postes et des Communications Électroniques (MTDPCE), ce plan d'action national sur l'IA 2026-2028 s'articule autour de six axes : infrastructure numérique et connectivité, gouvernance des données, développement des compétences, cadre institutionnel et juridique éthique pour une IA responsable, applications sectorielles prioritaires (santé, agriculture, éducation, administration), et coopération internationale. Il est mis en œuvre par le SPIVTEN et s'inscrit dans la SN@DEN 2018-2027.

title: Bahrain General Policy for the Use of AI
abstract: Published July 30, 2025 (Version 1.0). Approved at the Ministerial Committee for ICT meeting on May 20, 2025. Four pillars: legal compliance, responsible use and adoption, public education and awareness, and international cooperation. Mandatory for all government entities from approval date. Explicitly references and adopts the GCC Guiding Manual on AI Ethics alongside Bahrain's Personal Data Protection Law and cybersecurity legislation.

title: Bahrain General Policy for Use of AI
abstract: Bahrain's national policy framework for public-sector AI adoption, covering four pillars: legal compliance (including alignment with PDPL and the GCC AI Ethics Manual), international and local cooperation, awareness and education, and responsible ethical deployment. The policy mandates human control over consequential decisions and requires government entities to conduct impact assessments, aligned with Bahrain Economic Vision 2050 and Bahrain Vision 2030.

title: Bar Council Updated Guidance on Generative AI (November 2025)
organization: Bar Council of England and Wales
abstract: An updated version of the Bar Council's generative AI guidance for barristers, reflecting recent High Court rulings on AI misuse and rapid technological developments. Developed collaboratively by the Bar Council's IT Panel, Ethics Committee, and Regulation Panel, the guidance addresses newer platforms (Harvey, Perplexity, Microsoft Copilot), enhanced cybersecurity risks, intellectual property considerations, and emerging AI-related platforms. The core principle remains that barristers retain full responsibility for their work and cannot outsource professional judgment to AI systems. Inns of Court libraries and authoritative legal research services are positioned as primary sources, with AI as a supplementary tool.

title: Brennan Center: Agenda to Strengthen US Democracy in the Age of AI
abstract: Comprehensive policy agenda addressing how AI poses risks to electoral integrity and democratic governance. Outlines recommendations spanning government capacity building, transparency standards, corporate accountability, voter protection, and election security measures. Authors: Mekela Panditharatne, Lawrence Norden, Joanna Zdanys, Daniel I. Weiner, Yasmin Abusaif.

title: Brennan Center: Dangers of Unregulated AI in Policing
abstract: Examines risks posed by AI-driven data fusion tools adopted by police departments. Identifies three primary concerns: bias and inaccuracy (systems trained on historically biased arrest data), surveillance expansion (enabling mass monitoring of constitutionally protected activity), and lack of transparency (vendors invoking IP protections while police lack audit capacity). Recommends municipalities adopt OMB standards requiring pre-deployment testing, impact assessments, and ongoing monitoring. Authors: Rachel Levinson-Waldman and Ivey Dyson.

title: CAUT Policy Statement on Generative AI
abstract: Comprehensive policy statement providing guidance to academic staff associations on collective agreement language and advocacy regarding AI. Key positions: institutions must maintain autonomy from single AI providers; AI systems must not reproduce harmful biases; faculty, librarians, and archivists' work can never be replaced by AI automation; staff choice to use or avoid AI constitutes protected academic freedom; institutions must update IP policies to protect staff image, voice, and works; academic staff associations must participate with voting rights in institutional AI policy committees; AI cannot be deployed for surveillance or staff monitoring; additional workload from AI-related academic integrity issues requires compensation.

title: CGI.br Nota Pública — PL 2338/2023 AI Regulation
abstract: Nota pública do CGI.br expressando apoio à regulação de IA no Brasil com foco em PL 2.338/2023. Endossa abordagem baseada em riscos, defende a ANPD como autoridade central do Sistema Nacional de Regulação e Governança de IA (SIA), e recomenda regulação proporcional e assimétrica que não prejudique pequenos desenvolvedores e instituições científicas. Destaca a necessidade de transparência para identificar e mitigar vieses.

title: CBCK Warning on AI-Generated Religious Content
abstract: The Catholic Bishops' Conference of Korea issued a mid-November 2025 alert warning Catholics against fabricated AI-generated videos presenting false statements as authentic Church teachings. The bishops warn that deepfake manipulation of clergy images and voices has misled believers and damaged institutional credibility. Catholics are advised to rely exclusively on official sources (Vatican communications, diocesan outlets, accredited Catholic media). South Korean law prohibits such modifications without consent, with penalties up to 5 years imprisonment or 50 million won in fines.

title: CCA General Assembly AI Consultation
abstract: At the CCA's 15th General Assembly international study consultation in Cyberjaya, Malaysia, Rev. Dr. Yoon-Tae Kim (Professor of Mission Studies, Daejeon Theological University; Senior Pastor, Presbyterian Church in Korea) warned that 'there may be situations in which we trust and seek the advice and guidance of Artificial Intelligence rather than the Holy Spirit.' He highlighted risks of algorithmic bias, surveillance, privacy erosion, and job displacement, urging churches to develop 'a missionary response to AI' and participate in algorithm development to foster ethical AI creation.

title: CCBE Guide on the Use of Generative AI by Lawyers
abstract: The CCBE's guide on generative AI for lawyers addresses the growing adoption of GenAI within the legal profession, acknowledging benefits in efficiency, case management, research, and cost reduction while emphasizing significant professional concerns. Key risks identified include data privacy (client data retained by AI systems without user knowledge), hallucinations producing false case citations, algorithmic bias, system opacity, IP ownership ambiguity, and cybersecurity exposure. The guide specifies professional ethics obligations: lawyers must not input sensitive client data into AI systems, must independently verify all AI-generated outputs, must be aware of algorithmic bias, and must transparently inform clients about AI tool usage. The CCBE concludes that responsible GenAI use with appropriate safeguards can support legal work while preserving foundational professional standards.

title: CCI AI and Competition Market Study
abstract: Commissioned in April 2024 and published in 2025, this study documents India's AI market growth from $3.2B (2020) to $6.05B (2024) projected to $31.94B by 2031 and analyzes concentration patterns in cloud infrastructure (AWS 32.6%, Azure 20.8%, GCP 11.5%). The report identifies risks including self-learning pricing algorithms producing tacit collusion, discriminatory data access, and foundation model lock-in, and proposes regulatory interventions. Study methodology combines literature review, database analysis, structured interviews, and stakeholder surveys conducted through MDIS.

title: Christianity in the Age of AI Appeal
abstract: Open letter from evangelical leaders to US President Trump calling for preservation of American AI leadership while ensuring safe and responsible development. Signed by Rev. Johnnie Moore (Congress of Christian Leaders), Rev. Samuel Rodriguez (NHCLC), Doug Clay (Assemblies of God General Superintendent), Tim Clinton (AACC President), and others. Calls for a presidential advisory council of people of faith and ethicists. Affirms rapid development of beneficial AI tools but opposes autonomous smarter-than-human machines. Emphasizes that AI's ethical implications demand guidance rooted in centuries of religious tradition.

title: CCOO Guide for Union Intervention in Algorithmic Management 2025
abstract: Guía formal publicada por la Secretaría Confederal de Acción Sindical y Empleo y la Secretaría Confederal de Estudios y Formación Sindical de CCOO. Proporciona orientación práctica para la intervención sindical en la gestión algorítmica en los centros de trabajo. Incluye diez demandas fundamentales para la negociación colectiva sobre transparencia, responsabilidad, proporcionalidad, imparcialidad, acceso a datos y control humano de las decisiones laborales.

title: CELAM AI Ethics Encounter Declaration 2025
abstract: Regional declaration resulting from the Latin American Encounter on AI Ethics ('Ética de la Inteligencia Artificial: Una mirada desde Latinoamérica'), organized by CELAM's AI Working Group (created March 28, 2025). The declaration was signed by 250 university rectors from public and private institutions across Latin America, establishing shared ethical principles for AI development, use, and teaching. Centered on human dignity, social justice, and planetary sustainability, inspired by Pope Francis's teachings. Participants included educators, researchers, social leaders, communicators, ecclesial communities, and experts in theology, philosophy, social sciences, and technology.

title: CELAM AI Pastoral Document
abstract: Unprecedented pastoral document on AI from CELAM, elaborated by an interdisciplinary team of theologians, philosophers, bioethicists, educators, economists, engineers, and communicators. Presented by Mons. Lizardo Estrada Herrera (CELAM Secretary General). Addresses ethical, anthropological, and theological dimensions of AI. Covers impact on labor, education, healthcare, politics, and communication.
