Recommends training bishops in digital ethics, using AI for pastoral purposes without replacing human encounter, promoting regulations protecting human rights, investigating AI impacts in Catholic universities, and including vulnerable populations. Emerged from CELAM's 39th General Assembly mandate, with working meetings in Mexico City and Bogotá (2024-2025). Presented to Pope Leo XIV in May 2025.

title: CEM Prudencia ante la IA 2025
abstract: Pastoral reflection published on the CEM website calling for prudence regarding artificial intelligence. Authored by José Sols, Professor of Theology at Universidad Iberoamericana, Mexico City. Warns that AI can be 'beneficial but also extremely dangerous for human life' and must be approached with intelligence and prudence, never with excessive confidence. Core argument centers on humans as 'relational beings' (yo soy yo-con-los-demás) — AI should not replace human connection. Raises concerns about unconscious delegation of human tasks, loss of essential human professions, and substitution of meaningful relationships with AI companions. Published on official CEM platform as part of their public messaging.

title: Church of Greece Hierarchy AI Proposals (2025)
abstract: At the second regular session of the Holy Synod of the Hierarchy of the Church of Greece on October 8, 2025, presided by Archbishop Ieronymos, Metropolitan Nikolaos of Mesogaia and Lavreotiki presented 'Artificial Intelligence: The Church Facing Emerging New Anthropology' with 10 formal proposals. The Hierarchy formally approved the presentation. Key proposals: (1) understand AI deeply — technically, philosophically, and ethically; (2) avoid conspiracy theories while recognizing biblical patterns; (3) utilize technology for pastoral enrichment and witness; (4) create Church-specific algorithms guided by Orthodox principles; (5) present Orthodox theology in contemporary, accessible language; (6) preserve sacred mystery; (7) emphasize prayer's transformative power; (8) demonstrate lived faith; (9) recognize the Church possesses divine answers to contemporary challenges; (10) the Church exists to witness God's Kingdom, not resist technological progress.

title: CGSLB Digital Transition and AI Study 2025
abstract: Étude exhaustive de la CGSLB sur l'impact de la transition numérique et de l'IA sur l'économie, le marché du travail et l'emploi en Belgique. Basée sur une enquête auprès de milliers de membres et délégués de la CGSLB (intervalle de confiance de 99%). Formule des propositions politiques concrètes pour encadrer la transition numérique.

title: CGT Call for Democratic Regulation of AI at the AI Action Summit
abstract: Déclaration CGT (6.2.2025) pour le Sommet IA de Paris. Exige régulation démocratique via OIT/ONU, investissement public en infrastructure IA souveraine, protection contre surveillance algorithmique, convention OIT sur l'IA. 'L'IA est un outil au service des êtres humains.'

title: CIS IPA Model Law on Artificial Intelligence Technologies 2025
abstract: Первый региональный законодательный акт в области ИИ для постсоветского пространства. Принят Межпарламентской Ассамблеей СНГ 18 апреля 2025 года. Разработан Объединённым институтом проблем информатики НАН Беларуси, победившим в открытом конкурсе в мае 2023 г. Регулирует публичные отношения в сфере ИИ на всех стадиях жизненного цикла: проектирование, исследование, разработка, эксплуатация, обслуживание. Рекомендован государствам — участникам СНГ для использования при разработке национального законодательства.

title: Cameroon National AI Strategy (SNIA 2040)
abstract: Adopté le 8 juillet 2025 à Yaoundé au terme des Concertations Nationales sur l'Intelligence Artificielle (CONIA 2025), ce document stratégique définit la vision du Cameroun pour l'IA à l'horizon 2040 : devenir un hub continental de référence grâce à sept piliers, dont la gouvernance (création d'une autorité camerounaise de l'IA, loi-cadre avec enjeux éthiques), l'IA multilingue (modèle 'GPT Cameroun'), l'infrastructure souveraine, la formation (60 000 talents en IA dont 40% de femmes), les applications sectorielles, la coopération régionale et le financement.

title: Bhutan CMB BuddhaBot Launch
abstract: Bhutan's Central Monastic Body partnered with Kyoto University (Prof. Seiji Kumagai) and Teraverse Co. to launch BuddhaBot-Plus, an AI-powered Buddhist dialogue system trained on ancient Buddhist texts using Sentence BERT and ChatGPT technology. The pilot project deploys BuddhaBot to ~200 monks at Tango Buddhist College and the Institute of Science of Mind for a six-month trial, with full rollout expected by 2027. Secretary Choten Dorji stated the CMB will 'use the prototype as a benchmark to refine BuddhaBot's capabilities, particularly in relation to Buddhist texts.' This represents the first nation-level Buddhist institutional adoption of AI for monastic education.

title: CNJ Resolução 615/2025 — AI Governance in Judiciary
abstract: Estabelece diretrizes abrangentes para desenvolvimento, uso e governança de soluções de IA no Poder Judiciário. Classifica sistemas em alto risco (análise preditiva de sentenciamento, avaliação de provas) e baixo risco (tarefas administrativas, pesquisa de precedentes). Proíbe sistemas que não permitam supervisão humana, que usem traços de personalidade para fins penais ou que gerem discriminação ilícita. Cria o Comitê Nacional de Inteligência Artificial do Judiciário (CNIAJ). Entra em vigor 120 dias após publicação (14 de julho de 2025).

title: COSATU 8th CC Declaration — AI and 4IR
abstract: The COSATU 8th Central Committee (342 delegates, 1.5 million members) adopted a declaration with two AI-specific provisions: (1) Page 6: 'The 8th CC noted that Artificial Intelligence (AI) and technology are being implemented in workplaces without transparency and consultations with workers. The Federation calls for trade union oversight and bargaining on the use of AI and workers' data in workplaces, while also advocating for job security, reskilling, and upskilling programmes.' (2) Section 4.4.4 (Page 8): Resolution on education calling for 'opportunities to be created for young workers, including through internships and investing in their skilling and upskilling to equip them in this era of AI and the 4th Industrial Revolution. Related to this, drive a national 4IR and AI response strategy to protect vulnerable workers and advocate for collective agreements to secure at-risk jobs and invest in new job-rich economic sectors.' These positions embed AI concerns within the broader declaration rather than constituting a standalone AI policy.

title: CPSU Draft AI Principles
abstract: Draft principles on AI in the workplace for Australian public servants. Calls for strong regulation, worker voice, transparency, and protections to ensure AI does not deepen inequalities. Based on 2024 worker survey.

title: Sant'Egidio Rome 2025 Forum on AI Ethics and Innovation
abstract: Forum 9 at Sant'Egidio's 'Daring Peace' International Meeting for Peace in Rome, October 27, 2025, at Sala Petrassi, Auditorium Parco della Musica. Chaired by Archbishop Vincenzo Paglia (President, Pontifical Academy for Life). Speakers included Abu al-Qasim al-Dibaji (World Pan-Islamic Jurisprudence Organization, Kuwait), Silvia Calandrelli (RAI Director for Sustainability), Aurelien Hamelle (TotalEnergies, France), Gianmatteo Manghi (CEO Cisco Italy), Rudiantara (Vice Chairman of Indonesian Mosque Council), Eric Salobir (Human Technology Foundation), and Oded Wiener (Chief Rabbinate of Israel). Multi-faith, multi-stakeholder forum addressing AI ethics and innovation. Part of the broader meeting attended by 296 representatives from 35 countries, including Italy's President Mattarella. The meeting's Peace Appeal (October 28) did not specifically mention AI. Speaker texts available in Italian, English, Arabic, and French.

title: China AI Content Labeling Rules 2025
abstract: Required AI-generated content (text, images, audio, video) distributed on Chinese platforms to carry visible explicit labels and embedded implicit watermarks, covering all major Chinese platforms including WeChat, Douyin, Weibo, and e-commerce channels.

title: DRC National AI Strategy (Draft Process 2025)
abstract: Le 8 octobre 2025, la RDC a officiellement lancé les travaux de la Commission interinstitutionnelle chargée d'élaborer le Plan National du Numérique 2026-2030 (PNN 2) et la première Stratégie nationale de l'Intelligence Artificielle. Soutenue par l'ARPTC et l'ADN, cette initiative mobilise 1,5 milliard USD (1 Md public + 500 M de partenaires internationaux). Les secteurs prioritaires sont la santé, l'agriculture, l'éducation, la sécurité et l'administration publique. Le document est en cours de rédaction.

title: Dar al-Ifta Fatwa on AI for Fatwas
abstract: Issued by Dr. Nazir Mohammad Ayyad. Rules that it is impermissible in Islamic law to rely on AI applications to obtain fatwas. AI applications fall short in essential standards of quality, scholarly, and Islamic legal precision, with answers that may be right, wrong, or contradictory to established rulings. AI lacks the human qualities of taqwa, scholarly depth, contextual understanding, and accountability required of a mufti.

title: Antiqua et Nova — Vatican Note on AI and Human Intelligence
abstract: A 117-paragraph doctrinal note co-issued by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith and the Dicastery for Culture and Education addressing the theological and philosophical distinctions between artificial and human intelligence. The document argues AI performs tasks through computational logic while human intelligence involves embodied, relational, and spiritual dimensions. Covers AI ethics challenges in healthcare, education, labor, warfare, and privacy. Asserts that moral responsibility grounded in human dignity must guide AI development, and that humans bear ultimate responsibility for technology's consequences. The most theologically authoritative Vatican AI document since the Rome Call itself.

title: Antiqua et Nova: AI and Human Intelligence
organization: Dicastery for Doctrine of the Faith / Dicastery for Culture and Education
abstract: A 118-paragraph joint document from the Vatican's Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith and Dicastery for Culture and Education addresses the ethical, anthropological, and theological dimensions of AI across education, labor, health, warfare, and human relations. The document affirms that AI is a human tool that cannot replace spiritual, embodied, or relational human intelligence, and warns against personifying AI as a grave ethical violation.

title: DIEESE Study on AI and the World of Work
abstract: Estudo intersindical do DIEESE sobre inteligência artificial e o mundo do trabalho. Examina os objetivos da regulação da IA, impactos no mercado de trabalho e preocupações dos trabalhadores incluindo plágio, vigilância, riscos à privacidade e demissões. Publicado em colaboração com o Ministério do Trabalho e Emprego.

title: Suwon Diocese Priests AI Study Program
abstract: The Catholic Diocese of Suwon launched a study program for priests to understand and engage with artificial intelligence for parish life, reflecting growing institutional interest among Asian Catholic dioceses in practical AI literacy for clergy.

title: EC Guidelines on Prohibited AI Practices 2025
abstract: European Commission guidelines clarifying the eight categories of prohibited AI practices under Article 5 of the AI Act, including social scoring, real-time biometric identification in public spaces, subliminal manipulation, and exploitation of vulnerabilities. Issued ahead of the February 2025 enforcement date for prohibitions.

title: ECLAC Caribbean AI Readiness Brief 2025
abstract: ECLAC policy brief addressing the governance gap for AI in Caribbean states, calling on governments to mitigate documented AI harms, anticipate emerging risks, and harness AI's potential for advancing the SDGs. The brief identifies key threats including economic disruption, cultural erosion, misinformation, and surveillance, and provides recommendations linked to eight SDGs for inclusive and rights-respecting AI governance.

title: ECLAC Digital Traps Report 2025
abstract: ECLAC flagship institutional report (March 2025) arguing that digital transformation and AI adoption can help Latin America and the Caribbean overcome structural development traps of low growth, high inequality, and weak institutions. Recommends a coordinated regional digital agenda with targets for 2026, integrating AI strategies with productive development policies and addressing connectivity, skills, innovation, and governance gaps.

title: 101 Million Reasons to Build Better AI
abstract: EDF advocacy infographic and resource arguing that AI systems must be designed inclusively to serve 101 million people with disabilities in Europe. Addresses both benefits and risks of AI for disabled people, with recommendations for policymakers (to promote inclusive AI frameworks and protect disability rights in digital systems) and for developers (to create accessible and usable AI systems). Warns that without inclusive design, AI can undermine trust in digitalisation and create negative experiences for disabled people.

title: Disability-Inclusive AI Act: Implementation Guide (v2, updated)
abstract: Version 2 of EDF's disability-inclusive AI Act implementation toolkit. Integrates post-adoption European Commission guidance documents covering prohibited AI systems, AI system definitions, obligations for general-purpose AI, incident reporting requirements, and the General-Purpose AI Code of Practice. Authored by Ness James (EDF). Available in Word and PDF formats.

title: EFJ Position on AI in Journalism
abstract: Adopted by the EFJ Steering Committee in Brussels on September 30, 2025. The EFJ represents 320,000 journalists across Europe through member unions. The position paper acknowledges AI's dual nature — capable of assisting with translation and fact-checking while simultaneously enabling misinformation generation and threatening media pluralism. Six core principles are established: (1) Human Editorial Control — media employers and workers must define AI use conditions; AI cannot generate content without human review; (2) Journalist Protection — express consent required before using journalist work for AI training; proper crediting and fair compensation must be ensured; (3) Transparency — 'AI-generated content must be labelled to be clearly identified' by both audiences and journalists; (4) Equitable Access — AI tool investments must reach all journalists including freelancers and local reporters; (5) Ethical Standards — AI use in newsrooms must comply with core journalistic principles including truth, impartiality, accountability, and source protection; (6) Journalist Governance — organizations representing journalists must actively participate in AI system development through social dialogue mechanisms. Available in English, French, Croatian, and German.

title: ELCA 2025 Churchwide Assembly Motion J on AI
abstract: Adopted 584-15 at the 2025 ELCA Churchwide Assembly (July 28-Aug 2, Phoenix, AZ). Refers to the Office of the Presiding Bishop, Theological Discernment, Service and Justice and ELCA Advocacy, in consultation with the Office of the Treasurer and Information Technology, consideration of the ethics and morality surrounding artificial intelligence, including creating safeguards in both privacy and usage, as well as developing a 'faith and AI' study guide. This is a distinct action from the 2024 ELCA Church Council AI Issue Paper already in the vault.

title: Patriarch Bartholomew - Council of Europe Address on AI
abstract: Patriarch Bartholomew addressed the Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe in Strasbourg on AI governance. He acknowledged AI's 'immense potential for positive transformation in environmental protection, education, and healthcare' while noting 'inherent risks' including privacy invasions, rising inequalities, and institutional compromise. He declared that 'implementation of artificial intelligence requires respect of individual dignity, safeguard of fundamental freedoms, and advancement of social equity.' He endorsed the HUDERIA methodology for AI impact assessment and called for integrating Christian values alongside human rights frameworks.

title: Patriarch Bartholomew - Robotocracy Address at Athens University
abstract: At an event at Athens University, Patriarch Bartholomew warned against 'the impending robotocracy,' stating that Orthodox tradition preserves 'valuable human-centric wisdom' essential for navigating technological change. He declared: 'In a world of rapid change, an upheaval of values, and dizzying technological advances — from artificial intelligence to the impending robotocracy — our Church proclaims that there is nothing more sacred than the human being.' He emphasized the 'absolute priority of the person over systems' and the need for education that 'cultivates and supports the spiritual nature of humanity.'

title: EPPC AI Action Plan
abstract: Public comment submitted by Clare Morell (EPPC Fellow) to the White House in response to its request for input on developing the AI Action Plan. Argues the US must balance AI innovation with consumer protection, particularly for children. Recommends clarifying that Section 230 immunity does not apply to generative AI, prohibiting AI generation of illegal content, directing FTC investigation of AI companies for deceptive marketing, and restricting generative AI products to users 18 and older.

title: EPPC Comment on AI in Education
abstract: Public comment by Clare Morell and Chloe Lawrence submitted to the U.S. Department of Education via the Federal eRulemaking Portal. Addresses the Department's proposed priority on 'Advancing Artificial Intelligence in Education.' Supports expanding computer science and AI literacy courses but cautions against broad integration of AI tools into general instruction. Cites research that AI in education creates 'cognitive costs,' reduces critical thinking, and undermines learning outcomes, especially for struggling students.

title: EPPC Model Legislation on AI Chatbots
abstract: Model legislative language by Chloe Lawrence and Clare Morell to address risks posed by AI companion chatbots to children. Requires age verification for AI chatbot access, citing that 72% of American teens have used AI companions. Proposes shifting the burden of protecting children from parents to technology companies, establishing enforceable legal requirements rather than voluntary industry standards.

title: ETUC AI for Workers Not Just Profit 2025
abstract: Adopted at the ETUC Executive Committee meeting of 4-5 March 2025, this position paper represents ETUC's comprehensive updated stance on AI in the workplace. The document identifies challenges including AI-driven automation and generative AI potentially causing job displacement, deskilling, and precarious employment without appropriate regulations. Key demands include: AI must not reinforce societal biases worsening inequalities in hiring, access to training, promotion, and dismissals; binding EU legislation on AI at work within the forthcoming Quality Jobs Act; and upholding worker rights against AI-driven workplace surveillance. The paper calls on the European Commission to bring forward binding legislation responding to the widespread unregulated rollout of AI in European workplaces, citing impacts including unfair automated dismissals, invasive surveillance, opaque algorithmic decision-making, and AI-driven work intensification.

title: Egypt National AI Strategy Second Edition
abstract: The second edition of Egypt's National AI Strategy (first edition: 2021) builds on evolved priorities and global AI trends. Six pillars: Governance, Technology, Data, Infrastructure, Ecosystem, and Talent. Targets: ICT contribution to GDP raised to 7.7%, 250+ AI-driven startups, 30,000+ AI specialists trained, Cairo positioned as global AI hub, $42.7B annual AI value by 2030. Key innovation: shift toward 'Sovereign AI' using locally developed models trained on Egyptian data, with sectoral flagships in smart irrigation, traffic management, medical imaging, and Arabic language processing.

title: FCA AI Sprint Summary
abstract: FCA summary of its January 2025 AI Sprint industry convening event. Identifies four core AI governance themes and commits to 'Supercharged Sandbox' and regulatory clarity actions. Represents forward-looking FCA priorities for AI governance in UK financial services.

title: FCA AI and the FCA: Our Approach
abstract: FCA's official regulatory philosophy on AI in UK financial services. States no new AI-specific regulations will be introduced; relies on existing Consumer Duty and SM&CR frameworks. Principles-based, outcomes-focused approach. Three goals: safe innovation, risk-benefit balance, and UK competitiveness.

title: FCNL AI Legislative Policy
abstract: Section III.2.12 of FCNL's overarching legislative policy statement addresses AI as a rapidly developing technology embedded in medicine, research, education, communications, and daily life. Calls for federal legislation protecting the public (especially children and vulnerable groups), prevention of harmful bias in AI training, protection of privacy and intellectual property, opposition to autonomous AI weapons, mitigation of negative employment/economic effects, and addressing AI's excessive demands on energy, water, and land. Democracy must be protected from AI manipulation. Derived from Quaker discernment processes.

title: FFTN Statement on Moral Guardrails in AI
abstract: Statement issued by the Faith, Family and Technology Network calling for moral guardrails in AI development. Signatories include Notre Dame Law professors Daniel Slate and Paolo Carozza. FFTN brings together scholars, technologists, policymakers, religious leaders, and advocates anchored in pro-faith, pro-family, and pro-technology principles. Emphasizes that AI's moral compass should not be dictated solely by technology developers, advocates for meaningful human-in-the-loop AI safeguards, and addresses AI companions, age verification, and child digital safety. Network has hosted weekly discussions featuring speakers including Jaron Lanier and Audrey Tang.

title: Fo Guang Shan 11th Humanistic Buddhism Symposium on AI
abstract: Fo Guang Shan's 11th Symposium on Humanistic Buddhism (December 21, 2025) gathered 400+ scholars from 13 countries on the theme 'Artificial Intelligence and the Propagation of Religion.' Venerable Miao Fan stated 'technological advancement must not come at the expense of humanistic concern,' emphasizing compassion and human values must remain central. Papers addressed human-AI interaction, AI translation of Buddhist texts, and digital preservation. The symposium complements Fo Guang Shan's practical AI initiatives including Vurbo.ai Buddhist 4.0 (multilingual real-time translation system launched April 2025) and the FoGuang.AI multilingual database developed with UC Berkeley.

title: Fo Guang Shan Vurbo.ai Buddhist Translation System Launch
abstract: Fo Guang Shan's International Buddha Light Association launched Vurbo.ai Buddhist 4.0, a specialized AI translation system processing 10,000+ Buddhist technical terms for multilingual real-time subtitle translation. Developed with IPEVO, it uses a Chinese-English-multilingual intermediary strategy to preserve semantic accuracy of complex Buddhist philosophical concepts. Venerable Jue Pei described it as 'a significant milestone for technology-enabled religious modernization' enabling 'accurate dharma understanding across multilingual international gatherings.' This represents a concrete institutional commitment to ethical AI deployment in religious contexts, prioritizing accuracy of sacred terminology.

title: FGTB Brochure AI 10 Questions for Works Councils
abstract: Guide formel de la FGTB fournissant aux représentants des travailleurs 10 questions clés à poser sur la mise en œuvre de l'IA au sein des conseils d'entreprise (CE) et des comités pour la prévention et la protection au travail (CPPT). Vise à outiller les délégués syndicaux pour comprendre et surveiller le déploiement de l'IA dans leurs entreprises.

title: Folkekirken AI-vejledning (IT Guidance)
abstract: Official AI guidance issued by Folkekirkens IT for Church of Denmark employees. Covers safe and responsible use of free AI assistants (ChatGPT, Copilot) in church work. Addresses data protection, personal information safeguarding, distinguishing AI from traditional software, and security responsibilities. Directed at individual employees and security officers responsible for digital conduct across the Kirkenettet (church network). Supplements existing data protection protocols. Available as downloadable PDF.

title: FIP Statement of Policy: AI in Pharmacy Practice
abstract: FIP Statement of Policy establishing principles for responsible and ethical AI integration in pharmacy practice worldwide. Recognizes AI (including machine learning, generative AI, robotics, and predictive analytics) as an opportunity to enhance pharmacy efficiency, medication safety, and personalized care. Requires strong governance, ethical frameworks, patient privacy protection, bias mitigation, and transparency. Calls for AI tools to complement pharmacist expertise without undermining professional judgment or patient trust. Includes 11 actionable recommendations for pharmacists on AI literacy, multidisciplinary collaboration, AI system evaluation, and continued patient-centered practice.

title: FABC Pastoral Guidelines for AI in Asia (Draft)
abstract: A three-day gathering in Hong Kong (Dec 10-12, 2025) organized by FABC-OSC brought together 7 bishops, 15+ priests, and lay media professionals from across Asia to reflect on 'Artificial Intelligence and Pastoral Challenges in Asia.' Sessions covered AI's impact on humanity and the Church, Catholic media, semantic search tools, online verification, and AI principles for evangelization. Cardinal Stephen Chow called AI 'a gift from God' while Dr. Paolo Ruffini (Vatican Dicastery for Communication) cautioned that 'artificial intelligence must never replace us.' The participants began drafting the region's first set of Pastoral Guidelines for AI in Asia to assist dioceses in responding to technological challenges.

title: FABC-OSC Human-Centered AI Communication Statement
abstract: Fr. John Mi Shen, Executive Secretary of the FABC Office of Social Communication, stated that while AI is transforming the media landscape, Church communication must remain centered on people, not technology. Key position: 'AI is only a tool. The heart of communication remains the human person. Relationships, discernment, and authenticity cannot be automated.'

title: Danish Church Digitalization Strategy 2026-2030
abstract: Joint digitalization strategy of the Danish National Church (Folkekirken) and the Ministry of Ecclesiastical Affairs for 2026-2030. Addresses responsible use of new technologies including artificial intelligence, with focus on establishing responsibility frameworks, processes, data ownership, security, accessibility, and data coherence. Emphasizes human-centered technological solutions. Developed through workshops, meetings, and interviews with church representatives from late 2024 to May 2025.

title: FSA AI Discussion Paper 2025
abstract: The FSA AI Discussion Paper (AIの健全な活用に向けた議論の整理) Version 1.0 was published March 4, 2025 following the FSA AI Public-Private Forum (June–December 2024) in which financial institutions and regulators shared knowledge on AI adoption. The paper addresses: (1) the current state of AI utilization in Japanese financial institutions (approximately half already use generative AI), (2) AI-specific risk factors including hallucination, model opacity, third-party dependencies, and cybersecurity, (3) AI governance integration into existing risk management frameworks, and (4) regulatory approaches balancing innovation with consumer protection. It flags areas where existing regulations may need updating and invites public comment. Version 1.1 was published March 2026.

title: FWCC Holding Position on AI
abstract: Agreed at the annual gathering of the Central Executive Committee in Seoul, October 2025. Acknowledges that 'the use of AI is increasing significantly, including by Quakers' and that the global Quaker community is 'thinking hard about how to navigate this.' Described as a 'holding position' reflecting the early stage of Quaker discernment on AI. FWCC is the international umbrella body connecting Quaker yearly meetings worldwide.

title: Azerbaijan AI Strategy 2025-2028
abstract: Первая комплексная национальная стратегия Азербайджана в области ИИ, утверждённая указом Президента Ильхама Алиева 19 марта 2025 года. Охватывает развитие инфраструктуры данных и высокопроизводительных вычислений, подготовку кадров, создание нормативно-правовой базы для ответственного и этичного применения ИИ (запланировано принятие закона об ИИ к 2027 году), привлечение инвестиций и поддержку стартапов. Детальный план действий включает институциональные ответственности, сроки и целевые показатели.

title: GEW Resolution on AI in Education 2025
abstract: Beschluss des 30. GEW-Gewerkschaftstags: Die GEW warnt davor, dass Automatisierung als Lösung für den Lehrkräftemangel präsentiert wird. Lernprozesse dürften nicht von Algorithmen bestimmt werden — das Primat der Pädagogik muss gewahrt bleiben. KI-Systeme müssen im Sinne guter Arbeit, guter Bildung, sozialer Gerechtigkeit und Demokratie gestaltet werden. Transparenz, Datenschutz, Mitbestimmung und gemeinnützige, offene Technologien sind entscheidend.

title: Brazil PL 6237/2025 — National AI Governance System
abstract: PL do Poder Executivo enviado ao Congresso em dezembro de 2025 para instituir o Sistema Nacional para Desenvolvimento, Regulação e Governança de IA (SIA). Cria o Conselho Brasileiro de Inteligência Artificial (CBIA), a ANPD como autoridade coordenadora para setores sem regulador próprio, o Comitê de Regulação e Inovação em IA (CRIA) e o Comitê de Especialistas e Cientistas (CECIA). Resolve vício de iniciativa no PL 2338/2023, pois atribuir competências à ANPD exige proposta do Executivo.

title: Archbishop Elpidophoros - AI and Theology Conference Address
abstract: Archbishop Elpidophoros of America delivered invocation and welcome remarks at the Orthodox Observer AI & Theology conference 'Do the Divine and the Digital Intersect?' at Hellenic College Holy Cross in Brookline, MA. He described AI as a 'coming tech tsunami' and called on the Church to 'strengthen our Ark of Salvation.' The conference emphasized that what was once a novelty 'has become now an emergency' and that the faithful are called to meet AI 'not with fear, but with vigilance, humility, and an unwavering commitment to humanity.'

title: 10th International Fatwa Conference Communique
organization: General Secretariat for Fatwa Authorities Worldwide
abstract: 16-point communique from 80+ country conference. Key AI recommendations: establish ethical AI frameworks rooted in Islamic principles (truthfulness, privacy, anti-falsification); integrate AI into fatwa research while maintaining human oversight; train muftis in dual Sharia-technical competencies; develop multilingual AI fatwa tools; strengthen digital fatwa platforms; combat digital extremism; educate communities about verified vs. unaccredited AI sources. Chaired by Egypt's Grand Mufti.

title: Taiwan Church AI Bootcamp (GOOD TV)
abstract: GOOD TV partnered with AI coaches in September 2025 to launch the 'Church AI Bootcamp,' drawing participation from over 1,000 people representing 100+ interdenominational churches across Taiwan. The bootcamp equipped churches to use AI for ministry efficiency, content quality, pastoral care, and teamwork. Pastor Zhe-ming Su of Truth Church (Taipei) noted 'AI can greatly reduce the time spent on administration, freeing up more hours for pastoral care and relationships.' Sessions emphasized that amid the AI wave, churches must stay mission-focused and not allow technology to replace authentic spiritual practice. Events held in Taipei (Sep), Taichung (Nov), and Kaohsiung (Jan 2026).

title: Hastings Center Issue Brief: Functional Genomics and AI Ethics
abstract: This Hastings Center Issue Brief (January 2025) examines ethical issues arising at the intersection of functional genomics and artificial intelligence, with particular attention to concerns about generalizability in functional genomics research that often relies on small, specific datasets, and the ethical tensions associated with bench science in early stages of the AI lifecycle that have received less attention than downstream applications.

title: Luxembourg Declaration on AI and Human Values
abstract: Adopted at the 2025 Humanists International General Assembly in Luxembourg and drafted by Humanists UK, this declaration establishes ten ethical principles for AI development and governance grounded in humanist values: human judgment must not be displaced by AI, benefits must serve the common good rather than concentrate power, AI must be democratically accountable and transparent, citizens must be protected from algorithmic discrimination and surveillance, creators deserve fair compensation, and AI's ultimate purpose must be human flourishing and equality. It calls upon governments, corporations, and international bodies to adopt these principles through concrete policies and international agreements.

title: Histadrut — Age of AI and Labor Op-Ed
abstract: Authored by Hila Shienok (Director of Policy) and Peter Lerner (Director General, International Relations) of the Histadrut. Sets out a 7-point AI policy framework: (1) transparency — companies must disclose AI decision-making, automated tasks, and data collection; (2) joint worker-management oversight committees to monitor AI for bias; (3) meaningful privacy consent for worker data; (4) mandatory reskilling for displaced workers; (5) collective agreements protecting against precarious gig work; (6) four-day workweek to distribute productivity gains; (7) preserving face-to-face workplace interactions. Core argument: AI should 'close the gap between the powerful and the powerless' rather than widen inequality.

title: Hungary National AI Strategy 2025-2030
abstract: Hungary's Government published its renewed AI Strategy for 2025-2030 on September 3, 2025, updating the 2020-2030 strategic framework to reflect major technological and regulatory changes including the EU AI Act. The strategy was developed with the Artificial Intelligence Coalition (formed in 2018, comprising 400 representatives from business, science, and government) and other stakeholders. It sets six strategic pillars — regulation and security, infrastructure, education, data economy, R&D, and AI applications — and targets quantitative indicators including 15% AI-induced GDP growth, 26% average corporate productivity increase, and 1 million higher value-added AI-supported jobs by 2030 relative to 2020. State Secretary Richárd Szabados announced an annual review mechanism. The strategy is accompanied by Act LXXV of 2025, which establishes the national framework for enforcing the EU AI Regulation.

title: ICC Harmonised AI Standards
abstract: ICC policy paper addressing the problem of divergent AI regulations creating market fragmentation and compliance cost burdens for businesses, especially SMEs. Proposes eight actions for policymakers: (1) strategic alignment in standards development reflecting market needs; (2) expert participation from domestic and local stakeholders; (3) global industry-driven standards prioritized over national approaches; (4) multistakeholder collaboration through transparent processes; (5) leveraging existing standards in regulation; (6) public procurement use of standards to support SME market access; (7) company participation support via funding and training; (8) awareness building for implementation capacity. Argues that overlapping standardization efforts and inconsistent terminology create complexity rather than simplification.

title: ICC Recommendations for Inclusive AI
abstract: ICC formal policy paper arguing that AI holds immense potential to accelerate economic prosperity but roughly one-third of the global population remains offline, creating risk of uneven benefit distribution. Identifies seven primary policy actions: (1) infrastructure investment in clean energy, broadband, and data centers; (2) expanded access to quality public data; (3) digital education and workforce training; (4) support for local innovation ecosystems and linguistic diversity; (5) national AI strategies aligned with international ethical frameworks; (6) updated regulatory systems for data governance and cybersecurity; (7) AI standards integration into public procurement. Frames inclusive AI as essential to unlocking AI's full potential, with particular attention to Global South challenges.

title: ICCR Investor Statement: AI in Healthcare Regulation
abstract: Formal investor statement from ICCR members opposing a provision in the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' that would impose a 10-year moratorium on state and local AI regulations. Argues such a measure would nullify over 60 existing state-level AI regulations. Cites specific healthcare AI failures: UnitedHealth/Optum algorithm denying rehabilitation care, IBM Watson Health unsafe cancer treatment recommendations, Cigna automated claim denials, CVS/Aetna AI fraud detection errors, Teladoc AI chatbot safety risks. Calls for maintaining state and local regulatory authority over AI in healthcare.

title: ICCR Shareholder Resolution: Microsoft AI HRDD (Customer Misuse)
abstract: ICCR-coordinated shareholder resolution requesting Microsoft publish a report assessing the effectiveness of its human rights due diligence processes in preventing, identifying, and addressing customer misuse of Microsoft AI and cloud products. Cites concerns about alleged use of Microsoft technologies in Gaza operations and Microsoft's incubator program supporting Chinese startups whose surveillance tools were later used against Uyghurs. Lead filer: Religious of the Sacred Heart of Mary (Aaron Acosta); co-filers include Benedictine Sisters of Mount St. Scholastica, Azzad Asset Management, and Benedictine Sisters of Baltimore.

title: Ayatollah Makarem Shirazi on AI and Seminary
abstract: Grand Ayatollah Naser Makarem Shirazi, one of the most senior Shia marjas (religious authorities) in Qom, stated that seminaries and students should be pioneers in utilizing modern tools and technologies such as artificial intelligence, which can assist students and facilitate major scientific and research work. He expressed satisfaction that the seminary is now a pioneer in using AI rather than falling behind. Drawing an analogy to past opposition to loudspeakers and telephones, he urged clergy not to stand against new technologies but to be first to use these tools to promote religion. He also cautioned about the negative effects of improper use of AI in schools, warning that unregulated use can weaken students' capabilities, creativity, and critical thinking. The statement addresses AI's application in Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) as distinct from its use in other fields, emphasizing that Islamic knowledge from the Ahl al-Bayt requires scholarly rigor.

title: IEEE Recommended Practice for Indigenous Peoples Data Provenance
abstract: The first international standard addressing appropriate disclosure of Indigenous peoples' relationships to data, covering historical collections and all future datasets created by or derived from Indigenous peoples and their territories. Developed through consultation with Indigenous stakeholders coordinated by GIDA in 2023, and published by IEEE in November 2025 as an open-access standard.

title: Law Society Practice Guidance: Generative AI — the Essentials
organization: Law Society of England and Wales
abstract: The Law Society's practice guidance on generative AI provides solicitors and law firm staff with a broad overview of the opportunities and risks of generative AI tools. It covers the core technology, the regulatory landscape, and professional duties that remain unchanged even when AI is used to support legal work. The guidance addresses ethical considerations, data risks, confidentiality, and compliance obligations under the SRA Standards and Regulations, and is directed primarily at small and medium-sized law firms.

title: IFLA Statement on Copyright and Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Agreed by the IFLA Governing Board on 4 April 2025 and published 29 April 2025. Prepared by IFLA's Advisory Committee on Copyright and other Legal Matters in collaboration with IFLA's Artificial Intelligence Special Interest Group. Designed to help IFLA member libraries navigate copyright issues related to AI and support development of relevant library programs. Calls for advocacy in favour of limitations and exceptions that enable text-and-data mining of legitimately acquired or accessed content, and for access to the widest possible datasets as a defence against bias and error. States that copyright should not be used as a blunt force tool for addressing ethical issues. Endorses the ICOLC statement on artificial intelligence and licensing. Outlines practical steps for governments and libraries including building capacity and awareness, making ethical choices around AI tools, and developing guidance and monitoring capacity.

title: IIFA Resolution 258 on AI
abstract: The IIFA's 26th session in Doha established that the default ruling on AI development and use is permissibility (ibahah), subject to six ethical and legal conditions: lawful purposes, benefit maximization, respect for beliefs and religions, information protection, security safeguards, and transparency. Recommends further study on granting AI legal personality and organizing specialized symposiums on AI ethics. References the ICESCO Riyadh Charter.

title: Iran AI Tools for Islamic Sciences
abstract: Iran's Islamic Institute of Science, Civilization, and Mysticism unveiled three AI-powered knowledge-based products for advancing Islamic sciences. Ceremony attended by senior clerics including Ayatollah Abolfazl Mobasheri Boushehri and Ayatollah Mohammad Ali Arafi. Part of broader initiative to strengthen global presence of Iranian religious institutions through modern technology integration. Tools designed to revolutionize Islamic scholarship and jurisprudence using artificial intelligence.

title: IPI General Assembly Resolution on AI and Human-Made Journalism 2025
abstract: Adopted by unanimous vote of IPI members on October 15, 2025 at the 74th Annual General Assembly, and presented at the IPI World Congress on October 25, 2025 in Vienna, Austria. The resolution emphasizes that human-made journalism remains 'a cornerstone of democratic societies' that cannot be replaced by AI for core functions like newsgathering and fact-checking. Three critical AI threats are identified: (1) algorithm-driven content curation by tech companies promoting divisive material; (2) AI-generated disinformation and deepfakes undermining truth; (3) training generative AI on journalistic content 'without consent, credit, or compensation.' The resolution acknowledges that 'AI can make journalism more sustainable and improve access to reliable, independent news and information when used responsibly,' but urges policymakers and technology companies to 'prioritize and safeguard the conditions in which human-made journalism can survive and thrive.'

title: ISACA: Leveraging COBIT for AI System Governance
abstract: ISACA white paper exploring the application of the COBIT governance framework to AI system governance. Addresses challenges including algorithmic bias, transparency, and regulatory compliance, demonstrating how COBIT's five domains and governance components provide a structured approach to developing AI systems that are secure, resilient, and compliant with best practices across the AI lifecycle. Formally issued by ISACA as an institutional document under ISACA copyright.

title: IST-AIF Religious Voices and Responsible AI Initiative
organization: Institute for Security and Technology / AI and Faith
abstract: Joint initiative launched October 30, 2025, to equip pastors, imams, and religious educators with practical tools to help their communities navigate AI's risks, opportunities, and ethical use. Deliverables include modular curricula on AI fundamentals and safety/ethics, congregation-ready discussion guides, short videos and reading lists, and a civic engagement hub. Funded by Future of Life Institute's 'Multistakeholder Engagement for Safe and Prosperous AI' grant program. Pilot year targets non-denominational evangelical Christian churches in Southern California and mosques in the San Francisco Bay Area and Seattle.

title: Data & Society: Red-Teaming in the Public Interest
abstract: Offers a vision for red-teaming in the public interest: a process that goes beyond system-centric testing of already-built systems to consider the full range of ways the public can be involved in evaluating generative AI harms. Draws on 26 semi-structured interviews and participant observation at three public red-teaming events. Raises methodological questions (how and when to red-team, who should participate, how results should be used) and conceptual questions (whose interests are being protected). Collaborative project between Data & Society and AI Risk and Vulnerability Alliance (ARVA). Authors: Ranjit Singh, Jacob Metcalf, Emnet Tafesse, Briana Vecchione, Beth Duckles, Borhane Blili-Hamelin.

title: ITUC AI Implications for Trade Unions 2025
abstract: ITUC's comprehensive report on the implications of artificial intelligence for trade unions globally. The report brings together research on the effects of AI deployment on job quality and fundamental labour rights, exploring wider societal challenges including AI's influence on public service delivery, social protection, the environment and development. Key findings include that AI systems are accelerating job fragmentation and intensification, algorithmic systems are increasingly used to manage work schedules, pay and dismissals with lack of transparency or accountability, and AI is reinforcing labour market inequalities particularly affecting low-skilled workers and women. Core ITUC demands include: full union involvement in AI design and deployment decisions; transparent human-centred technology upholding rights and safety; a binding ILO Convention on platform work; and urgent action by governments, employers and international institutions to place workers and unions at the centre of the AI-driven future of work.

title: IslamQA Ruling on AI Fatwas
abstract: Published July 27, 2025 on IslamQA.info. Rules that 'it is not permissible for one to seek a fatwa from artificial intelligence and trust the answer that AI generated by itself, and act on the fatwa it gave.' AI does not meet the shariah conditions required for a fatwa that can be followed, including being Muslim, being an adult, being of sound mind and good character, having knowledge of sharia, and being smart and able to derive rulings and understand realities of life. AI fatwas are based on information fed into the system through computer programs that accumulate information from different sources with varying accuracy. Qualified Islamic scholars may use AI as a research tool to gather references and summaries. Ordinary Muslims might use AI for general understanding only, not for actionable religious rulings.

title: JACL Statement Endorsing AI Civil Rights Act
abstract: Official JACL organizational statement endorsing the AI Civil Rights Act introduced by Senator Markey and Representative Clarke. JACL frames AI discrimination as a direct civil rights concern for its communities, citing research on algorithmic bias in healthcare (50% less likely to recommend care for Black patients) and mortgage lending (50% higher denial rates for AAPI applicants). JACL positions: prohibit discriminatory algorithm software; require pre- and post-deployment auditing; mandate impact assessments; ensure robust enforcement via FTC, state AGs, and private right of action. Authored by JACL Policy with contact information for VP of Public Affairs.

title: JAKIM-Zetrix Shariah AI Framework LOI
abstract: LOI signed between JAKIM Director General Dato' Dr. Sirajuddin Suhaimee and Zetrix AI Director Datuk Mohd Jimmy Wong Abdullah, witnessed by PM Anwar Ibrahim. Establishes the world's first framework for Shariah compliance, certification, and governance in AI. Three pillars: Shariah Certification and Governance for AI systems; Global Advocacy positioning Malaysia as centre of excellence for Islamic AI; JAKIM's Official Channel on NurAI (world's first Shariah-aligned LLM) for Islamic legal rulings and halal certification.

title: Jogye Order AI Robot Monk Experiment
abstract: The Jogye Order of Korean Buddhism, South Korea's largest Buddhist order, introduced humanoid robots to temples, describing it as 'an experiment in how traditional religion can communicate with the digital age.' The Order maintains that Buddhist practice remains the domain of human beings and AI adoption must not undermine tradition, reflecting a cautious but engaged approach to AI integration.

title: Jogye Order AI and Buddhism Seminar
abstract: The Jogye Order's Monastic College professors convened at Dongguk University in Seoul to explore AI applications within Buddhist contexts. Ven. Jahong introduced 'Dharma Mitra,' an AI platform tailored for Buddhist studies with semantic search capabilities for the CBETA database. Ven. Boil, director of the AI Buddhism Research Institute, warned against overreliance on AI in spiritual practice, stating 'We must be wary of attempts to replace artistic inspiration or religious insight with AI' and called for 'a more restrained and mindful approach.' The Order's position: embrace AI as a practical research tool while maintaining vigilance against compromising authentic spiritual disciplines.

title: KCC Generative AI Service User Protection Guidelines
abstract: 챗봇·딥페이크 등 생성형 AI 서비스 이용자 피해 방지를 위해 사업자가 준수해야 할 4대 기본 원칙과 6개 실천 방안을 제시한 가이드라인. 2025년 3월 28일 시행. 이용자 인격권 보호, 의사결정 과정 투명성 확보, 다양성 존중, 입력 데이터 수집·이용 관리, 문제 해결 책임과 참여, 건전한 유통·배포 노력 등을 포함한다.

title: KVKK Generative AI Personal Data Guide
abstract: The KVKK's Generative AI and Personal Data Protection Guide (15 Questions) evaluates personal data processing activities conducted through generative AI systems — systems that produce human-like content from natural language inputs — within the framework of Turkey's Law No. 6698 on Personal Data Protection. It clarifies distinctions between generative and conventional AI, defines concepts such as deep learning and deepfakes, and addresses risks including hallucinations, biased outputs, and security threats. It provides direction on identifying controller/processor responsibilities, implementing transparency measures, securing data subject rights, and applying privacy-by-design principles. The guide is addressed to both data controllers using generative AI and individuals/parents protecting their privacy.

title: Kenya AI Strategy 2025-2030
abstract: Formally launched in March 2025 following public validation consultations in January 2025, Kenya's National AI Strategy 2025-2030 provides a comprehensive governance framework for ethical and inclusive AI development, developed with UNESCO support and aligned with Kenya's Digital Economy Blueprint.

title: Kenya National AI Strategy 2025-2030
abstract: Sets out Kenya's roadmap for AI development, oversight, and alignment with socio-economic priorities through 2030, covering AI governance, infrastructure, talent development, and sectoral applications.

title: Kuwait National AI Strategy 2025-2028
abstract: A phased four-year strategic roadmap for integrating AI across Kuwait's economy and society, aligned with Kuwait Vision 2035. The draft strategy organizes around five pillars—establishing Kuwait as an AI hub, sector transformation (healthcare, energy, education, transport), AI governance and privacy, workforce development, and innovation ecosystem—with implementation in three stages culminating in full public and private sector integration by 2028.

title: LDS General Handbook AI Guidance
abstract: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints updated its General Handbook (Section 38.8.47) on December 16, 2025 to include binding guidance on artificial intelligence. The update states that AI 'cannot replace the gift of divine inspiration or the individual work required to receive it' and that 'interactions with AI cannot substitute for meaningful relationships with God and others.' It affirms that AI can enhance learning, facilitate communication, and foster innovation, but cautions members not to use AI to create or disseminate anything false, misleading, illegal, or harmful. The handbook is the church's authoritative policy document governing all units worldwide.

title: Lausanne AI and Transhumanism Gap Summary
abstract: Collaborative consensus summary from Lausanne Movement networks specifically addressing AI and transhumanism. Envisions AI enhancing gospel accessibility through advanced translation, mentorship for church leaders, and overcoming language barriers. Warns that evangelism is relational and against over-reliance on AI that could undermine discipleship's personal dimensions. Calls for collaboration between technology developers, Christian leaders, and ethicists to ensure AI functions as a supportive tool rather than replacement for human spiritual guidance.

title: LO Sweden Consultation Response on AI Commission Roadmap 2025
abstract: Formellt remissyttrande från LO över AI-kommissionens Färdplan för Sverige. LO konstaterar att AI redan är en del av vardagen på arbetsplatserna men ställer sig kritiskt till att arbetsmarknadens parter inte var representerade vid framtagandet av färdplanen. Kräver formell representation för arbetsmarknadens parter i AI-politiken.

title: State of AI Ethics Report Vol. 7
abstract: The seventh volume of MAIEI's State of AI Ethics Report series, subtitled 'AI at the Crossroads,' surveys the rapidly evolving AI ethics landscape as of 2025, covering governance developments, emerging risks from frontier models, and the global policy environment.

title: Marburger Bund Resolution on AI in Medicine
abstract: Beschluss der 145. Hauptversammlung des Marburger Bundes in Leipzig, Mai 2025. Kernforderungen: nachhaltige und intensivierte Investitionen in Digitalisierung und KI; Anwendung von KI in der Medizin unter ethischen Leitlinien; Sicherstellung von Datenschutz und Datenqualität; Verringerung der Abhängigkeit von nichteuropäischen KI-Anbietern. Grundsatz: 'KI ist kein Ersatz für ärztliche Verantwortung und Urteilskraft. Medizin ist kein Algorithmus — sie ist Menschlichkeit.' KI-basierte Auswertungen und Befunde müssen ärztlich validiert und interpretiert werden.

title: Maryland Bishops AI Pastoral Letter
abstract: Pastoral letter signed by seven bishops including Archbishop William E. Lori (Baltimore), Cardinal Robert W. McElroy (Washington), Bishop William Koenig (Wilmington), and four auxiliary bishops. Contains 7 thematic sections covering human dignity, justice and equity, truth and conscience, solidarity, AI in service to the Church, moral imagination, and a concluding invocation. Urges Catholics to be a 'prophetic voice' placing the human person at the heart of technological transformation. Addresses worker displacement, algorithmic bias, manipulation of truth, lethal autonomous weapons. Bishops pledge to promote AI ethics formation, support Catholic technologists, ensure institutional use upholds dignity, and advocate for protective legislation.

title: MGI Estratégia de IA 2025–2027
abstract: Estratégia de Inteligência Artificial do MGI para 2025-2027, estabelecendo princípios, diretrizes e objetivos para uso ético, seguro e eficiente de soluções baseadas em IA no âmbito do ministério. Elaborada pelo Grupo de Trabalho de IA (GT-IA), instituído pela Resolução CGDSI/MGI nº 6 de dezembro de 2024, com oficinas realizadas entre março e maio de 2025. Princípios orientadores: direitos humanos e equidade, transparência e auditabilidade, segurança e proteção de dados, supervisão humana efetiva e sustentabilidade socioambiental.

title: Lawyers' Committee AI Civil Rights Act Campaign
organization: Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
abstract: The Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law champions the AI Civil Rights Act as 'a landmark civil rights bill for the digital age.' The organization frames AI regulation as a civil rights imperative, citing documented harms: mortgage algorithms denying Black applicants approximately 80% more often than comparable white applicants; hiring systems screening out qualified women and people of color; predictive policing disproportionately targeting Black and Latino neighborhoods; insurance and credit algorithms charging higher rates to communities of color. Proposed protections include prohibiting AI-driven discrimination across six sectors (housing, employment, education, healthcare, credit, criminal legal systems), requiring independent audits pre- and post-deployment, mandating transparency in AI decision-making, and establishing enforcement mechanisms for individuals, state attorneys general, and federal regulators. Bill text (S3308) dated December 2, 2025.

title: Leading Civil Rights Organizations Respond to AI Executive Order
organization: Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
abstract: Joint statement by eight major civil rights organizations (led by Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law) opposing Executive Order 14179, which seeks to preempt state-level AI regulation. The coalition argues the order creates regulatory gaps that endanger civil rights protections and disproportionately harms Black communities through unchecked algorithmic bias. Signatories include: Lawyers' Committee (Damon T. Hewitt), National Urban League (Marc H. Morial), National Action Network (Rev. Al Sharpton), NAACP (Derrick Johnson), NAACP LDF (Janai Nelson), Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights (Maya Wiley), National Council of Negro Women (Shavon Arline-Bradley), National Coalition on Black Civic Participation (Melanie L. Campbell).

title: Paris AI Action Summit Statement 2025
abstract: Declaration signed by 62 parties (including China, India, EU, African Union; not including US and UK) at the Paris AI Action Summit, outlining principles for open, transparent, ethical, safe, and trustworthy AI, overcoming digital divides, sustainable AI, and equitable labour market outcomes.

title: MSIT-KISA AI Security Guide
abstract: 과기정통부와 KISA가 공동 발간한 AI 모델 개발·서비스 단계의 보안 안내서. AI 모델 개발 단계 6개 분야 55개, 서비스 제공 단계 5개 분야 44개 등 총 113개 요구사항을 담고 있으며, 외부 사이버 위협으로부터 AI 모델과 서비스를 보호하기 위한 리스크 관리·데이터 암호화·실시간 모니터링·API 보안·백업 체계 등을 포함한다.

title: Mind & Life Dialogue XXXIX: Minds, AI, and Ethics
organization: Mind & Life Institute / Office of the Dalai Lama
abstract: Three-day dialogue at Thekchen Choling, Dharamsala (Oct 14-16, 2025) convened 120+ scientists, scholars, contemplative practitioners, business leaders, and policymakers to examine AI's potential to alleviate suffering, advance equity, and support human and planetary flourishing, alongside its risks to health, education, work, politics, and climate. The Dalai Lama emphasized that 'life is underpinned by mind' and that consciousness remains foundational. Key positions included: compassion as essential to AI governance ('AI cannot love, but it can help us love better'), maintaining human oversight, and the need for transparency and educator involvement in AI governance. No binding declaration was issued; the dialogue served as a high-level exploratory forum.

title: Church of Greece LOGOS AI Platform Launch
organization: Metropolis of Nea Ionia (Church of Greece)
abstract: The Metropolis of Nea Ionia of the Church of Greece launched LOGOS, the first Orthodox Christian AI platform, developed in collaboration with the HERON ICT Research Laboratory at the University of the Aegean. Metropolitan Gabriel stated the initiative 'contributes to international conversations on how Christian communities might navigate the opportunities and risks of AI with spiritual integrity, ethical responsibility, and pastoral sensitivity.' LOGOS provides scriptural interpretation with patristic commentary, teachings of Church Fathers, liturgical explanations, and prayer guidance. It explicitly does not replace clergy, pastoral counseling, or sacramental life.

title: MUI Position on AI and Religious Authority
abstract: Position statement by KH Cholil Nafis, MUI Dakwah and Ukhuwah Division Chair, presented at the 10th International Conference of Fatwa Institutions in Cairo. AI cannot serve as a mujtahid or mufti because it lacks human consciousness, a prerequisite for issuing fatwas. AI may assist scholars as a research tool for more accurate jurisprudential analysis, but cannot independently issue religious rulings. A fatwa must result from deep ijtihad by scholars meeting requirements of knowledge, honesty, justice, and understanding of contemporary reality.

title: Mufti WP Tinta Mufti on AI Permissibility
abstract: Tinta Mufti Bil. 10/2025 oleh Ustaz Ahmad Fauwaz bin Fadzil, Jabatan Mufti Wilayah Persekutuan, menetapkan bahawa penggunaan teknologi AI adalah harus (permissible) selagi ia membawa manfaat dan tidak melanggar prinsip Syariah. Analisis berdasarkan Maqasid al-Shariah dan kaedah fiqhiyyah (hukum asal sesuatu adalah keharusan). Empat syarat etika penggunaan AI: (1) niat baik dan tujuan bermanfaat, (2) memelihara maruah manusia dan melarang deepfake/manipulasi, (3) mengelakkan penipuan dan aplikasi AI yang merosakkan, (4) mencegah mudarat terhadap agama, nyawa, akal, maruah, harta, dan keselamatan.

title: India AI Governance Guidelines 2025
abstract: The capstone AI governance document from MeitY/IndiaAI Mission advocates a lightweight adaptive regulatory approach leveraging existing laws rather than enacting new AI-specific legislation. The seven sutras are: Trust; People First; Innovation over Restraint; Fairness and Equity; Accountability; Understandable by Design; and Safety, Resilience, and Sustainability. The institutional architecture includes an AI Governance Group (AIGG) for inter-agency policy coordination, a Technology and Policy Expert Committee (TPEC) for advisory functions, and an AI Safety Institute (AISI) for technical research and standards. Built upon the January 2025 draft report following extensive multi-stakeholder consultation (650+ inputs).

title: MeitY AI Governance Guidelines Report
abstract: Released for public consultation on January 6, 2025, this report by the Subcommittee on AI Governance and Guidelines Development (constituted November 9, 2023 under the PSA Advisory Group) examines key AI governance issues, conducts a gap analysis of existing frameworks, and recommends a whole-of-government approach. Key proposals include an inter-ministerial AI Coordination Group led by the PSA and MeitY, a technical Secretariat as advisory body, and guidance for sectoral regulators. The report received over 650 inputs and was revised to produce the final November 2025 guidelines. Public consultation closed January 27, 2025.

title: NRC-AI-God-in-the-Engine
abstract: Interfaith report published by the Nigeria Religious Coalition on Artificial Intelligence, comprising the Christian Council of Nigeria (CCN), Institute of Church and Society Ibadan, and Jama'atu Nasril Islam (JNI). Launched at a media unveiling in Lagos on October 22, 2025. Calls on the Nigerian government to safeguard African culture and values in AI adoption, warns against AI erosion of religious identity, and advocates for stronger ethical frameworks and regulatory policies. Rev. O. Kolade Fadahunsi stated: 'our intention is to moderate what Artificial Intelligence we absorb and ethically guide its usage to protecting shared values and cherished space.' The coalition engaged 500 stakeholders in multi-sector advocacy for faith-informed AI governance.

title: NRC-AI-Regulation-Call-2025
abstract: Joint statement from Nigerian religious leaders calling for urgent AI regulation. Bishop S. Oluwole Onaleke warned that 'AI is reshaping society, influencing decisions, and even altering human interactions' and cautioned against treating technology as neutral. Bishop David Adekoya asserted 'AI is here to stay, but we must not lose our IQ' and emphasized regulating through 'intelligence and wisdom.' Participants included CCN-Institute of Church and Society, JNI, and FOMWAN representatives. The coalition urged the Nigerian government to enact policies ensuring AI development aligns with ethical standards and national interests.

title: NAE Position: Faith and AI Regulation
abstract: The National Association of Evangelicals formally opposed a federal moratorium on state-level AI regulation, arguing that diverse state approaches to AI governance serve the public good and protect human dignity. The position reflects evangelical convictions that AI policy must remain accountable to ethical and theological frameworks.

title: NAM AI Code of Conduct for Health and Medicine
abstract: National Academy of Medicine (NAM) Special Publication establishing formal guidance for responsible AI development and deployment in healthcare and biomedicine. Comprises six commitments — advance humanity, ensure equity, engage impacted individuals, improve workforce well-being, monitor performance, and innovate and learn — alongside ten supporting principles. Addresses risks including algorithm transparency, data privacy, bias mitigation, and equitable access. Intended as a touchstone for organizations implementing AI in healthcare contexts. Preceded by an April 2024 discussion draft that invited public comment; this May 2025 publication represents the final adopted framework. Note: This supersedes the 2024 discussion draft (same URL base) which was not a final statement.

title: NAM AI Policy Positions
abstract: NAM's formal AI policy hub articulating the organization's official positions on AI governance for manufacturing. Six core policy positions: (1) review existing regulations to identify rules inhibiting AI development rather than creating new AI-specific ones; (2) regulate use not tools — context-specific, risk-based approaches; (3) right-size compliance — workable, balanced rules relying on industry standards and self-assessments over audit-heavy regimes; (4) transparency guidance — tasks NIST with helping AI deployers understand model development and training data; (5) preserve competition — ensure access to both open-source and proprietary AI models for manufacturers of all sizes; (6) coordinate across jurisdictions — align federal and state approaches given 40 states have enacted 110+ AI laws. Accompanied by companion report 'The Manufacturing Roadmap to AI & Energy Dominance' (Oct 2025).

title: NAR Letter to OSTP on AI Regulatory Reform
abstract: NAR's formal response to the White House OSTP Request for Information on Regulatory Reform for Artificial Intelligence. Covers state AI/privacy laws, copyright, and fair housing. Calls for uniform federal standards, a safe harbor for compliant real estate professionals, and ethical AI principles including fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, and human oversight.

title: NASEM: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work
abstract: Three-year NASEM Consensus Study Report examining how recent AI advances — particularly generative AI — could reshape the U.S. workforce and economy. Presents 11 key findings, including classifying AI as a general-purpose technology akin to electricity and steam power. Explores productivity gains, job stability, equity, and income inequality implications. Emphasizes that AI is 'a tool with the potential to enhance human labor' but that beneficial outcomes are not guaranteed. Advocates for augmenting collective capabilities, enhancing human well-being, and equipping the workforce to meet future challenges. Highlights research opportunities and data needs to help workers and policymakers adapt. 200 pages.

title: NASEM: Foundation Models for Scientific Discovery
abstract: NASEM Consensus Study Report examining how AI foundation models — large-scale systems trained on massive datasets — have the potential to transform scientific discovery and innovation. Conducted at the request of the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE), the report explores how foundation models can complement traditional computational methods to advance scientific discovery, highlights successful use cases, and recommends strategic approaches and investments to support DOE's mission. Released December 16, 2025.

title: NASEM: Machine Learning for Safety-Critical Applications
abstract: NASEM Consensus Study Report examining how machine learning can be safely integrated into physical systems where failures could cause harm — including self-driving cars, power grids, and healthcare. Finds that extending rigorous safety engineering practices to include ML components brings significant challenges, as current approaches focus too heavily on algorithmic performance metrics rather than system-level safety. Proposes research priorities for improving safety assessment and evaluation, and recommends a shift in research methodologies, professional education, and engineering practice. Released at a public webinar September 25, 2025.

title: NCAI Comments on AI Action Plan RFI
abstract: Formal 11-page submission from NCAI to the NSF/OSTP in response to the federal Request for Information on developing an AI Action Plan (90 Fed. Reg. 9,088). NCAI presents nine overarching policy recommendations to ensure AI policies recognize Tribal digital sovereignty, self-determination, and data governance rights. Key recommendations include: (A) Recognize Tribal Digital Sovereignty to Protect Tribal Rights in AI Policy, (B) Address Limited Tribal Data Representation to Improve AI Systems, (C) Strengthen Tribal Consent Processes for Efficient Data Utilization, (D) Enhance Government-to-Government Consultation with Tribal Nations, (E) Overcome Barriers to AI Adoption by Investing in Strategic Infrastructure in Indian Country, (F) Implement Efficient Cultural Protection Mechanisms, (G) Support Strategic Tribal Workforce Development, (H) Develop Balanced Regulatory Approaches, and (I) Incorporate Tribal Security Considerations in National Defense. Cites multiple prior NCAI resolutions including NC-24-008 on Tribal Digital Sovereignty.

title: NEU AI in Schools Checklist
abstract: The National Education Union published an AI in Schools Checklist in May 2025, providing guidance for union representatives negotiating AI implementation in schools. The checklist covers five key areas: ensuring staff and union consultation before technology adoption, preventing workload increases, providing adequate training, protecting privacy and data, and establishing ongoing monitoring systems. The NEU's core principle is that AI must not be used to replace posts, change workforce structure, or undermine interaction with pupils. Any efficiencies gained should benefit teaching quality rather than enable cost-cutting. The document functions as a practical negotiation tool with benchmark questions for evaluating AI implementations.

title: Nieman Foundation Generative AI Policy
abstract: The Nieman Foundation's Generative AI Policy (last updated September 30, 2025) applies to staff, contractors, and all freelance contributors working with its three publications. Core provisions: (1) No AI-drafted content — 'We do not publish stories drafted or edited using generative AI tools'; (2) Human vetting required for AI-assisted research, transcriptions, translations, social media text, and copyedits before publication; (3) Image restrictions — AI image generators are prohibited for photorealistic depictions of real people or places; any AI-generated illustrations must be clearly labeled with generation methods disclosed; (4) Transparency mandate — 'Any AI-generated material in our publications or on our websites (images, translations, etc.) will be clearly identified for readers'; (5) Harvard alignment — the policy references broader Harvard institutional guidelines emphasizing AI should 'augment human creativity and decision-making, not replace it.' The policy anchors its approach in core journalistic principles of truth, accuracy, and transparency. It acknowledges that 'generative AI tools, overseen carefully by humans, can be useful and beneficial,' while maintaining strict publication standards.

title: NOW and Artificial Intelligence Advocacy Position
abstract: NOW's official AI advocacy position published August 15, 2025 (last updated January 8, 2026). Frames AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery and deepfakes as feminist civil rights issues, noting that 99% of deepfake targets are women or girls. NOW advocates for: legal protections against AI-generated non-consensual imagery; the No FAKES Act; the American Privacy Rights Act; comprehensive federal AI regulation; anti-bias safeguards with consistent human oversight. The position document connects AI harms to all six of NOW's core issue areas (reproductive rights, economic justice, ending violence against women, racial justice, LGBTQIA+ rights, constitutional equality). Accompanied by downloadable legislative tracking PDF.

title: NRB Resolution on AI and Emerging Technology
abstract: Board resolution adopted at the NRB 2025 International Christian Media Convention (nearly 6,000 attendees). Urges Christians to exercise responsibility and wisdom in utilizing AI and other emerging technologies, to acknowledge the limitations of technological enhancements and interventions, and to oppose efforts to use AI technology for anti-human and immoral purposes. One of nine formal resolutions approved by the NRB Board of Directors on February 24, 2025. NRB represents over 1,100 Christian media organizations.

title: NUJ AI Position — Journalism Before Algorithms
abstract: The National Union of Journalists published a comprehensive position paper on AI in January 2025 titled 'Artificial Intelligence: Journalism Before Algorithms.' The NUJ demands urgent regulatory oversight of AI development with mandatory engagement from journalists and creators whose work is used in training models. The union opposes AI-generated content as journalism and calls for stronger protections against copyright infringement, IP violations, and misuse of journalists' likenesses. General Secretary Laura Davison stated that 'AI must only be used as an assistive tool with human oversight.' The position paper also establishes that journalistic images should not contain AI-generated elements or deletions.

title: NIFEA Theological Communique on AI and 4IR
abstract: Theological communique and action plan issued following a consultation in Pocheon-si, South Korea (27-29 August 2025), organized by the NIFEA initiative. 45 church leaders, theologians, and academics from six continents participated. Asserts that human dignity as created in God's image cannot be 'replaced by data, commodified by algorithms, or rendered disposable by automation.' Warns that AI within capitalism's profit framework creates exploitative patterns threatening jobs and environmental integrity. Calls on churches to establish platforms examining digital technology impacts, governments to ensure equitable AI governance and data sovereignty, and the tech industry to prioritize common good over profit.

title: NZ AI Strategy: Investing with Confidence
abstract: New Zealand's first national AI strategy aims to accelerate private sector AI adoption and innovation in line with OECD AI principles, positioning New Zealand competitively among OECD nations after being notably the only OECD member without a published AI strategy prior to its release. The strategy supports the government's 'Going for Growth' agenda and promotes smarter use of AI to boost productivity and living standards.

title: NZ Public Service AI Framework
abstract: New Zealand's Public Service AI Framework provides a structured, OECD-aligned approach for government agencies to adopt AI responsibly and safely, emphasising human-centred values, transparency, and accountability to improve public service delivery. The framework supports leaders, decision-makers, and practitioners across the public service and builds on the Cabinet commitment in June 2024 to adopt OECD AI principles.

title: NZCTU AI in the Workplace Resource
abstract: Comprehensive resource guide for NZ trade unions on AI in the workplace, published April 2025. Articulates 8 foundational principles: Te Tiriti alignment, worker voice, job quality, rights protection, just transition, human oversight, data sovereignty, right to disconnect. Provides template collective agreement clauses and AI impact assessment framework.

title: Nepal National AI Policy 2025
abstract: Nepal's National AI Policy 2025 (2082 in the Bikram Sambat calendar), approved by Cabinet and drafted by the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology, establishes a comprehensive framework for developing a human-centric AI ecosystem across six pillars: ecosystem building, education and research, sectoral applications, entrepreneurship, public services, and ethics and risk management. The policy targets training 5,000 AI professionals within five years, achieving top-50 global AI readiness ranking, and establishing AI excellence centres in all seven provinces, governed by an AI Governance Council and National AI Centre.

title: OIC Tehran Declaration on Ethical AI
abstract: Adopted at the Second Ministerial Meeting of the OIC-15 Dialogue Platform in Tehran, this declaration establishes a collective vision of 'Trustworthy and ethical artificial intelligence for sustainable development' among 15 scientifically advanced OIC member states. The declaration commits member states to cooperation in AI ethics, governance, and technology development aligned with Islamic values.

title: OIC-15 Tehran Declaration on AI
abstract: Adopted unanimously at the second Ministerial Meeting of the OIC-15 Dialogue Platform in Tehran (May 2025) under the theme 'Trustworthy and Ethical Artificial Intelligence for Sustainable Development,' this declaration commits member states (including Pakistan, Iran, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Indonesia, Malaysia, Qatar, Brunei, Tunisia, Kazakhstan) to regional AI collaboration in education, research, infrastructure, ethics, and global competitiveness. COMSTECH was tasked with developing a comprehensive AI policy for all OIC member states.

title: PASS Consensus Statement on AI, Justice and Democracy
abstract: Consensus statement from a March 4-5, 2025 workshop co-hosted by the Pontifical Academy of Social Sciences and COPAJU (Pan-American Committee of Judges for Social Rights). Over 60 participants including US policymakers. Addresses AI's impact on justice systems, democratic citizenship, and work dignity. Five core principles: human dignity, fairness and equity, transparency and accountability, human oversight, and inclusivity. Recommends interdisciplinary collaboration, ethical frameworks prioritizing human dignity, AI education, international regulatory cooperation, and responsible deployment for global challenges. Responds directly to Pope Francis's call for 'algor-ethics'.
