title: PASS Digital Rerum Novarum Final Statement
abstract: Final statement from the October 16-17, 2025 seminar at the Casina Pio IV, Vatican City, convening approximately 50 global experts. Draws on Catholic social teaching to frame AI as a tool that must serve human dignity. Key principles: reject the technocratic paradigm that subordinates human dignity to efficiency; 'What does it mean to be human?' as the foundational governance question; implement 'ethics by design' and 'integral human development by design'; create incentives aligning AI with human flourishing. Seminar also launched the Latin American AI Network for Integral Human Development under PASS auspices, involving institutions including the University of Notre Dame, UN Development Programme, University of Buenos Aires AI Lab, and the National Center for Artificial Intelligence of Chile.

title: PAV-FIAMC Statement on AI and Medicine
abstract: Final statement issued November 12, 2025 from the international congress 'AI and Medicine: The Challenge of Human Dignity' (Rome, November 10-12, 2025), co-organized by the Pontifical Academy for Life and the International Federation of Catholic Medical Associations. Asserts that certain dimensions of patient care cannot be automated and must involve human compassion: 'Some profound dimensions of patient care cannot be replaced by optimised numerical procedures and autonomous robots.' Concludes that 'AI cannot make us forget that medicine is not just a science or a technique, but a human way of supporting patients in their suffering.' Congress included 26 speakers and concluded with a papal audience with Pope Leo XIV.

title: Panoptykon AI Act Implementation Guidelines Statement
abstract: Panoptykon Foundation argues that the European Commission's AI Act guidelines should place fundamental rights as the central framework for meaningful enforcement, noting the final AI Act contains 'grave loopholes' in protections particularly for policing and migration. The statement responds to the Commission's consultation that closed December 11, 2024.

title: Qualcomm Signs Rome Call for AI Ethics
abstract: On June 13, 2025, Qualcomm President and CEO Cristiano Amon signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at the Pontifical Academy for Life in Vatican City. Qualcomm committed to developing reliable and secure AI technologies aligned with Rome Call principles of transparency, inclusion, accountability, impartiality, reliability, security and privacy. The signing complements Qualcomm's Responsible AI Principles and its mission to create platforms that respect privacy, minimize harm, and advance societies.

title: Salesforce Signs Rome Call for AI Ethics
abstract: On October 27, 2025, Sabastian Niles, President and Chief Legal Officer of Salesforce, signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at the Pontifical Academy for Life in Vatican City. Salesforce committed to building trusted and ethical AI at every stage of development, deployment, and application.

title: PAS-WCF Final Statement on AI and Children
abstract: Joint final statement from a March 21-22, 2025 workshop on AI risks and opportunities for children. Co-issued by the Pontifical Academy of Sciences, World Childhood Foundation (chaired by Queen Silvia of Sweden), and the Pontifical Gregorian University. Identifies six AI risk categories for children including algorithmic engagement maximization, data exploitation, behavioral manipulation, and sexual exploitation. Outlines 19 numbered recommendations across six pillars: evidence-based understanding, research partnerships, actionable protective measures, stakeholder responsibilities, ethical AI governance, and anticipatory governance. Guiding principle: 'Child Safety First'.

title: Pakistan National AI Policy 2025
abstract: Pakistan's National AI Policy 2025, approved by the Federal Cabinet on 30 July 2025, establishes a six-pillar framework covering AI innovation, public awareness, secure systems, sectoral transformation, infrastructure, and international partnerships, with targets to be achieved by 2028. The policy aims to transform Pakistan into a knowledge-based economy while emphasizing ethical and responsible AI use, and establishes an AI Council chaired by the federal IT minister and a National Artificial Intelligence Fund (NAIF) to support research and development.

title: RBI FREE-AI Framework
abstract: The FREE-AI Committee was constituted December 6, 2024 following RBI's Statement on Developmental and Regulatory Policies. After extensive surveys and consultations with Indian and international financial institutions and regulators, the committee released this landmark framework addressing AI risks including bias, opacity, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and systemic financial instability. The six pillars are: Infrastructure, Policy, Capacity, Governance, Protection, and Assurance. The 26 recommendations include AI innovation sandboxes, indigenous financial AI model development, governance structures, audit mechanisms, and incident reporting. Committee includes Debjani Ghosh (former NASSCOM President) and other industry/academic leaders.

title: RCGP Position on AI in General Practice
abstract: RCGP Council paper (September 2025) on AI in general practice affirming that AI has potential to support general practice but requires coordinated national and regional action for safe roll-out. Makes eight formal recommendations covering regulatory frameworks (urgent publication of UK AI device regulatory framework expected in 2026), national guidance, commissioner involvement, education integration, clinician design participation, supplier support, implementation timelines, and evaluation standards. Accompanied by December 2025 Nuffield Trust joint research report.

title: Patriarch Kirill - AI Duma Address (AI More Dangerous Than Nuclear)
abstract: Patriarch Kirill addressed the Russian State Duma, describing AI as 'more dangerous than nuclear energy' and calling for strict public and government control over AI in Russia. He warned that at the end of days, the Antichrist will gain power with public support, likening uncontrolled AI to a gateway for such developments. He urged MPs to restrict deepfake technologies and develop a sovereign Russian AI system, arguing that Western AI technologies serve as 'instruments of political manipulation.'

title: Religions for Peace World Council Istanbul AI Thematic Dialogue
abstract: Thematic dialogue at the Religions for Peace World Council meeting in Istanbul (first in-person meeting in six years), examining how religious and faith traditions can guide ethical development and governance of AI. The Council warned against AI-driven dehumanization and structural injustice, determining that faith communities must play a central role in AI governance, particularly concerning autonomous weapons. Elder Gerrit W. Gong proposed a 'Faith Community AI Evaluation' framework. The Council also launched the Shared Sacred Flourishing Initiative. No separate signed AI declaration was issued; AI themes were integrated into the broader final statement.

title: RTS Generative AI Policy
abstract: RTS established a default AI policy allowing limited use of AI for specific research queries (e.g., identifying Bible verses using a specific word) or finding reading leads, which need not be disclosed. However, AI may not be used to generate key writing components (thesis/topic sentences, outlines, critical engagement), write full sentences or paragraphs, or complete online discussion posts, response papers, quizzes, or examinations. The policy frames illegitimate AI use as a violation of the 9th commandment (bearing false witness). Professors may modify the default policy including denying AI use altogether.

title: RenAIssance Foundation Mission 2024 Annual Report
abstract: Annual implementation report documenting RenAIssance Foundation activities throughout 2024. Documents the Hiroshima 'AI Ethics for Peace' event (July 9-10, 2024) involving eleven world religions and sixteen new signatories; Future Talks 2024 events in Dublin, Barcelona, and Marseille; Vatican AI ethics presentations; new corporate and institutional signatories including Cisco and Providence; and RenAIssance scientific director Prof. Paolo Benanti's appointment to the UN Secretary-General's Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence.

title: Bank of Russia AI Ethics Code for Financial Organizations 2025
abstract: Кодекс устанавливает пять принципов применения ИИ финансовыми организациями: человекоцентричность (информирование клиентов, право на отказ от взаимодействия с ИИ), справедливость (недискриминация по национальности, языку, расе, религии), прозрачность (раскрытие информации о рисках ИИ, маркировка ИИ-контента), безопасность (контроль качества данных и систем ИИ, конфиденциальность персональных данных), ответственное управление рисками (ответственное лицо, система хранения сведений о рисках). Кодекс носит рекомендательный характер.

title: Salvation Army UK AI Guardrails
organization: Salvation Army UKI Moral and Social Issues Council
abstract: Foundational guidance for everyone connected to The Salvation Army (employees, volunteers, leaders, members) on engaging with AI technology. Grounded in six core values: boldness, passion, compassion, respect, integrity, and mutual accountability. Described as 'parameters rather than restrictive rules' for decision-making. Predates the formal AI Policy (August 2025) and serves as broader starting point for the faith community.

title: Salvation Army UK AI Policy
abstract: Formal AI policy for all Salvation Army personnel (staff, corps leaders, volunteers, third parties) in the UK Territory. Developed by IT and Digital Section with AI Working Group and territorial leadership. Signed off by policy management group and Cabinet. Core principles: transparency, no AI for decision-making about people, data protection, fact-checking AI content, labeling AI-generated content. Values AI as a productivity tool but emphasizes human authorship for fundraising and case studies. Training materials in development with William Booth College.

title: Swiss EA AI and Church Working Paper
abstract: 78-page working paper by the Swiss Evangelical Alliance examining theological, ethical, and legal dimensions of artificial intelligence for faith communities. Developed by a specialist group of seven including two theologians, software engineers, computer science experts, a business consultant, and a futurist. Covers data protection, copyright, responsible AI implementation, and practical guidelines. Warns AI could be misused if unchecked but holds promise when guided by Christian values of honesty, integrity, and charity. Invites churches to develop their own AI guidelines based on the paper.

title: SECAM-2025-2050-Roadmap-Digital
abstract: 12-point pastoral roadmap adopted at the 20th SECAM Plenary Assembly in Kigali, Rwanda (July 30 - August 4, 2025). Pillar 9, 'Mission in the Digital Environment,' directly addresses AI: 'the rapidly emerging artificial intelligence forces us to train young people to be witnesses of the faith in this new areopagus, a new place of evangelization.' The bishops call for defining 'evangelical criteria for ethical use of digital technology,' ensuring youth familiarity with computing and digital tools, and creating Catholic digital infrastructure including websites for parish participation. While not an AI-specific statement, it represents the collective position of Catholic bishops across Africa and Madagascar on AI and digital technology for the next 25 years.

title: SGPC AI Sub-Committee Formation
abstract: Approved during the SGPC interim committee meeting on Oct 13, 2025 (publicly announced Feb 2, 2026), the SGPC formed a dedicated 8-member sub-committee of AI technology experts to examine artificial intelligence and its implications for Sikh doctrine and practice. Members include Dr. Harjinder Singh Sandhu (USA), Harmeet Shah Singh and Dr. Sarabjot Singh (UK), professors from Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Khalsa College Patiala, Guru Nanak Engineering College Ludhiana, and the SGPC System Administrator. The mandate includes establishing connections with major AI firms, drafting guidelines to prevent AI-generated content that violates Sikh maryada (code of conduct) or disrespects guru tradition, and recommending monitoring mechanisms for digital platforms. SGPC Secretary Pratap Singh stated: 'Videos and other content that hurt Sikh sentiments through artificial intelligence are continuously increasing on social media.'

title: SGPC Letter to AI Platforms on Gurbani Misrepresentation
abstract: The SGPC, the apex elected body managing Sikh gurdwaras, sent formal communiqués to operators of 15+ AI platforms including ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Grok, Gemini AI, Meta, Google, DALL-E 2, Midjourney, and others. SGPC President Harjinder Singh Dhami raised concerns about incorrect depictions of Gurbani (Sikh holy verses), misrepresentation of Sikh Gurus and religious figures, improper presentation of Sikh symbols, and potential harm to younger generations. The SGPC also urged Indian Union Home Minister Amit Shah to formulate a concrete national policy addressing AI-generated religious misrepresentation.

title: SHRM Position on AI Executive Order
abstract: Formal SHRM position statement on the Trump Administration's AI Executive Order, issued December 12, 2025. SHRM advocates for a regulatory framework that protects workers while promoting ethical innovation, calling on the federal government to establish nationwide AI governance standards focused on workplace applications. Key recommendations include: establishing clear workplace AI standards addressing recruitment, performance evaluation, workforce monitoring, scheduling, pay decisions, and job displacement risks; incorporating HR expertise into federal advisory councils; harmonizing federal rules across agencies; and providing implementation support. SHRM expresses readiness to partner with the Trump Administration, lawmakers, and business leaders to develop 'a federal workplace AI framework that supports both organizational performance and worker well-being.'

title: Sri Lanka Buddhist-AI Research Initiative
abstract: President Ranil Wickremesinghe announced LKR 1 billion (approx. USD 3.3M) will be allocated for research on the relationship between the Buddha's teachings and Artificial Intelligence. The announcement was made at the 100th anniversary celebration of the Sri Lanka Buddhist Society Moratuwa, framing Buddhism as a resource for ethical AI development.

title: Saudi Menarat Al-Haramain Fatwa Robot v2
organization: Saudi Presidency of Two Holy Mosques
abstract: The Presidency of Religious Affairs launched the second version of the Menarat Al-Haramain (Beacon of the Two Holy Mosques) Robot at the Grand Mosque in Makkah. AI-powered robot provides instant fatwa services in 15 languages using a pre-verified database of religious rulings. For complex inquiries, facilitates direct video calls with qualified Islamic scholars. Features Islamic decorative design inspired by mosque architecture. Launched by Sheikh Abdulrahman Al-Sudais.

title: Serbia AI Strategy 2025-2030
abstract: Serbia's successor national AI strategy for 2025–2030, leveraging digitization as a catalyst for innovation and competitiveness and aiming to reduce the development gap with advanced economies through AI deployment across all major sectors. Builds on the first strategy's institutional infrastructure including the Office for Artificial Intelligence and the Institute for Artificial Intelligence Research and Development.

title: Tianfeng: Chinese Church Riding the AI Wave
abstract: Published in Tianfeng (the official magazine of China's state-sanctioned Protestant church), minister Yang Nengzhao of Canaan Garden Church (Zhongshan City, Guangdong) frames AI as an inevitable and welcome development. He argues 'We cannot hold back the wave, but we can choose to ride it,' acknowledging that AI will significantly impact clergy roles and pastoral authority. Invoking Romans 12:2, the piece emphasizes spiritual renewal through adaptation aligned with God's will, reflecting characteristic TSPM optimism about discerning divine purpose within historical-technological change.

title: Tzohar Position Paper: AI and Halachic Rulings
abstract: Official institutional position paper from Israel's Tzohar Center for Jewish Ethics, authored by Rabbi Yuval Cherlow and Rabbi Yaron Moskowitz (published 15 Heshvan 5786 / November 6, 2025). Establishes that AI systems should function as 'a complementary support tool — and not as an independent halachic authority.' Six primary objections: (1) halacha requires human rabbinic authority that AI lacks; (2) halachic decisions demand integration of Torah knowledge, moral responsibility, and understanding of reality that algorithms cannot replicate; (3) AI responses mimic rabbinic style superficially but lack authentic moral responsibility; (4) present systems produce incorrect answers, imprecise quotes, and irrelevant sources; (5) AI cannot participate in the evolving relationship between tradition and contemporary circumstances; (6) rabbinic expertise involves intuitive elements unavailable to computational systems. Permits AI for research, source analysis, and learning support only. Calls for clear regulation requiring strict disclosure standards and prevention of misrepresentation.

title: Faithful Futures 2025 Ecumenical AI Conference
abstract: The second annual ecumenical AI gathering (September 2-5, 2025, Minneapolis) expanded from three to four mainline denominations — adding the United Methodist Church to the Episcopal Church, ELCA, and PCUSA. Capped at 75 in-person participants, the conference featured keynotes, scenario engagement exercises (including a 'Faithful Futures 2035' social simulation), and collaborative cohort work producing practical tools, prototypes, and theological reflections on AI in ministry. Cohorts recommended that denominations issue formal AI position papers, engage in interfaith/interdisciplinary coalitions, create certification standards or 'faith audits' for AI technologies, and present co-written principles at bodies like UNESCO and AI Safety Summits. No formal joint declaration was adopted, but the conference represents the most significant organized Protestant multi-denominational AI engagement to date.

title: Tanzania AI Ethics Use Guidelines
abstract: Published by Tanzania's Ministry of Communication and Information Technology, these guidelines establish ethical principles for AI development and use in Tanzania, complementing the country's completed UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment and supporting the forthcoming National AI Strategy.

title: TUAC Submission on OECD AI and Digitalisation Policy (2025 MCM)
abstract: Formal TUAC submission (C/MIN(2025)13) to the 2025 OECD Ministerial Council Meeting. It argues that without urgent and comprehensive policy action, AI advancement will undermine human rights and exacerbate inequalities. Key demands include: legislative safeguards protecting workers' intellectual property rights and ensuring compensation for their creativity; protections through social dialogue and collective bargaining not undermined by digital trade agreements; data sovereignty requirements for sensitive personal information; and prevention of digital offshoring to jurisdictions with weaker labour protections.

title: UCAP-2025-AI-Human-Values
abstract: Congress of the Union of the African Catholic Press held August 10-17, 2025 at GIMPA, Accra, Ghana, with 100+ participants from 20+ African nations. Theme: 'Balancing Technological Progress and the Preservation of Human Values in the Age of Artificial Intelligence.' Archbishop Julien Kabore (Vatican representative in Ghana) stated: 'AI must serve the human person, but not be subordinate' and 'AI may process data, generate text, or mimic thoughts, but it cannot love.' Bishop Matthew Kwasi Gyamfi (GCBC President) warned against reducing human life to data or replacing authentic relationships with simulations. The congress affirmed that human dignity derives from being 'created in the image and likeness of God' and urged media professionals to resist misinformation with integrity.

title: UGT Portugal Motion on AI and Social Dialogue
abstract: Moção aprovada pelo Secretariado Nacional da UGT promovendo o diálogo social por uma inteligência artificial ao serviço das pessoas. Aborda como a IA transformará os locais de trabalho e o emprego. Exige que os trabalhadores tenham o direito de ser consultados e incluídos na implementação da IA, garantindo que o uso da IA seja concebido com segurança, equidade e dignidade.

title: Notre Dame DELTA Framework for AI Ethics
abstract: The DELTA framework for AI ethics was unveiled at the Notre Dame Summit on AI, Faith, and Human Flourishing (September 22-25, 2025), attended by 200 invited leaders from tech, faith, academia, media, and government. Created by philosopher Meghan Sullivan, DELTA comprises five principles rooted in Christian values: Dignity, Embodiment, Love, Transcendence, and Agency. Designed to be accessible to people of all faith perspectives. Supported by a record $50.8 million grant from Lilly Endowment Inc. No joint declaration was issued; the summit served as the framework's launch and multi-sector dialogue platform.

title: UNI Europa Telecom AI Declaration 2025
abstract: Joint social partner declaration between UNI Europa (representing telecommunications workers across Europe) and Connect Europe (employers) calling for ethical and fair adoption of AI in the telecommunications sector. Acknowledges the beneficial potential of AI but insists that focus on innovation and productivity alone is insufficient. Demands human-in-command AI use to prevent discrimination and misuse, stronger social dialogue and collective bargaining when new technologies are introduced, and investment in both hard and soft skills for the workforce.

title: USCCB AI Principles Letter to Congress
abstract: Joint letter from multiple USCCB committee chairmen (led by Bishop William D. Byrne, Chairman of the Committee on Communications) to the U.S. Congress offering ethical principles and policy recommendations on AI. Centers on dignity of the human person, care for the poor, and respect for the truth. Addresses AI's impact on family life, labor and the economy, healthcare, education, political and civic life, warfare, energy, and the environment. Calls for a regulatory framework informed by ethical principles, human oversight, well-defined accountability, and promotion of fair democratic processes. Distinct from the USCCB Labor Day 2024 statement which focused on AI and worker dignity.

title: EO 14179: Removing Barriers to US AI
abstract: Rescinded Biden's EO 14110 and directed development of an AI Action Plan within 180 days to promote AI free from ideological bias, focusing on human flourishing, economic competitiveness, and national security.

title: VHPA-Preserving-Hindu-Ethos-AI
abstract: Editorial in VHPA's Hindu Vishwa magazine arguing that Hindu Americans must proactively publish authentic content about Hindu Dharma so AI training datasets represent Hinduism accurately. Warns that generative AI tools trained on biased or hostile sources risk institutionalizing Hinduphobia. Calls for digital preservation of scriptures, oral traditions, and cultural practices. Frames this as a 'civilizational calling.' Not a formal policy statement but an organizational publication articulating a position on AI and Hindu representation.

title: VU Amsterdam GenAI Education Framework
abstract: Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam's Executive Board approved a formal framework for generative AI in education in April 2025. The framework establishes seven key principles: AI as learning support (not replacement), AI literacy requirements for all students and lecturers, scientific integrity and transparency mandates, course-level clarity on AI permissions, data protection safeguards, formative assessment over AI detection tools, and sustainability/ethics consciousness. The framework will be regularly updated with support from the VU Education Lab and AI Competence Network.

title: WEA AI Ethics and Trust Research Paper
abstract: Research paper presented at the World Evangelical Alliance General Assembly 2025 in Seoul, South Korea (October 2025). Authored by McGrath, Huizinga, Dyer, and Graves. Provides a biblically informed, practical, and responsible response to AI's ethical challenges. Proposes a four-point framework: Assure responsible tool design, Align AI with Kingdom principles, Protect human identity/truth/justice/creation, and Use technology to advance mission. Calls on delegates to commit to using AI responsibly to fulfill the Great Commission. Accompanied by interactive TRUST GPT tools.

title: WCC Multi-Faith AI Communique: Transform AI
abstract: A multi-denominational theological communique from six major Christian world communions, issued following a consultation in South Korea with 45 church leaders from six continents, calls for a 'spiritual revolution' against AI's role in deepening global inequality. The document warns that AI within global capitalism creates 'digital empire' perpetuating colonial patterns, and calls for churches, governments, and tech companies to prioritize human dignity over profit.

title: WCO Report on Adoption of AI and ML in Customs
abstract: A comprehensive 59-page formal WCO deliverable produced through the Smart Customs Project (financed by the Customs Cooperation Fund of China). The report provides in-depth insights into AI/ML adoption in customs administrations globally, covering: technical frameworks, governance, risk management, business processes, capacity building, ethical considerations, cybersecurity, and the Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approach for transparent and ethical AI decisions. Two versions released — a public edition and a members-only version with case studies. Expands on the WCO/WTO Study Report on Disruptive Technologies (June 2022). Accompanied by an AI/ML Readiness Self-Assessment Tool and a China Customs case study (January 2025).

title: WEA Seoul Declaration
abstract: Declaration adopted at the 14th General Assembly of the World Evangelical Alliance in Seoul, addressing seven clusters of theological initiatives. Commits delegates to pursuing 'human-centered, ethical development in technology, including the discerning and redemptive use of media in a rapidly changing digital age.' Acknowledges the rapid emergence of AI into the public sphere as a defining challenge. Not AI-specific but includes technology/AI commitments within a broader evangelical agenda.

title: WMA Statement on AI in Medical Care (2025)
abstract: Adopted by the 76th WMA General Assembly in Porto, Portugal, October 2025. Establishes the 'Physician-in-the-Loop' (PITL) principle requiring physicians to retain final authority over AI-generated outputs in clinical care. Contains 14 foundational principles covering human-centricity, transparency, safe deployment, equitable implementation, and data governance. Supersedes the 2019 statement.

title: WMA Statement on AI in Medical Care
abstract: Comprehensive WMA statement establishing 14 foundational principles for AI in healthcare including human-centricity, physician-in-the-loop authority, accountability, transparency, and equitable implementation. Emphasizes 'augmented intelligence' framing that positions AI as supporting rather than replacing physician judgment.

title: World Russian People's Council - Call for Orthodox AI
abstract: The World Russian People's Council, a body closely linked to the Russian Orthodox Church and headed by Patriarch Kirill, called for creation of an 'Orthodox' AI rooted in traditional spiritual values. Deputy chairman Mikhail Ivanov argued Western AI models trained on 'alien values' could distort interpretations. Konstantin Malofeev called for a sovereign Russian AI trained on 'correct' sources including the Gospel, Domostroi, Dostoevsky, Pushkin, and works of Ivan Ilyin and Alexander Dugin. The proposal frames AI as a tool that should serve Russian Orthodox civilization rather than Western liberal values.

title: Waseda Buddhist AI Ethics Symposium
organization: Waseda University / KAKENHI Buddhist AI Ethics Project
abstract: A public symposium at Waseda University (December 13, 2025) organized by a KAKENHI-funded research project on 'Buddhist Teachings and Ethical Issues of AI' led by Prof. Shigeki Moro of Hanazono University. The symposium examined AI's societal impact from religious, philosophical, cultural, and educational perspectives. Moro's broader research program examines AI through Buddhist concepts including buddha-nature, the six realms of existence, and non-self doctrine, producing scholarship on 'AI, Buddha-Nature, Ethics' and 'Does AI Belong to the Six Realms?'

title: Wheaton College AI Policy
abstract: Wheaton College adopted a campus-wide AI policy for the 2025-26 academic year after approximately two years of development. CIO Alan Wolff chaired the faculty and staff AI Council that developed the policy, explicitly stating they prayed for guidance so the policy would 'align with Wheaton's Christian mission.' The policy encourages AI for editing, brainstorming, and task organization while restricting sensitive data input and deepfake creation. An AI Council subset continues monitoring with annual reviews and event-driven updates.

title: WorkersDecide.tech — AI Chaos Prevention Committee
abstract: A multi-organization initiative launched by the AI Chaos Prevention Committee, comprising organizers from Tech Workers Coalition, Turkopticon, CODE-CWA, Amazon Employees for Climate Justice, DAIR, the Times Tech Guild, and Collective Action in Tech. The site provides organizing tools and resources for workers to resist top-down AI mandates and demand 'Implementation Committees' as a workplace organizing structure. It includes a Workers' Inquiry Toolkit for collective reflection on working conditions under AI. The initiative positions workers — not employers — as the decision-makers on what AI tools to use in the workplace.

title: AAJC From Translations to Chatbots AI Governance Report
abstract: Community-based research report from Asian Americans Advancing Justice — AAJC documenting AI's impacts on AANHPI communities and articulating a governance framework responsive to those communities' needs. Intended as a guide for policymakers, community leaders, and funders making decisions on regulatory priorities, community investments, and AI governance frameworks. Six key findings address AI literacy gaps, workplace transformation for limited English-proficient workers, AI-generated misinformation harms, generational divides in chatbot adoption, data transparency demands, and the underrepresentation of AANHPI perspectives in AI governance. Full PDF at https://www.advancingjustice-aajc.org/sites/default/files/2026-02/From%20Translations%20to%20Chatbots%20AANHPI%20Perspectives%20on%20AI_3.pdf

title: ABA Joint Letter on NIST AI Risk Management Framework
abstract: Coalition letter from 10 financial industry associations including ABA urging preservation of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. Argues the RMF provides a common language, flexible governance foundation, global interoperability, and serves as a cross-sector standard for trustworthy AI development.

title: AFSC Palantir AI Surveillance Campaign
abstract: AFSC campaign against Palantir Technologies, a company that uses AI to weaponize data for deportations, military strikes, and mass surveillance. Calls for institutions (universities, hospitals, corporations) to divest from Palantir. Includes mapping tool showing Palantir's institutional connections. Focuses on AI-enabled surveillance harms to immigrants, Palestinians, and communities of color. By Kenny Morris, AFSC campaigns strategist.

title: AIA Position Statement on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Issued January 29, 2026 by the AIA AI Task Force, this formal position statement addresses AI's role in architecture, emphasizing 'upholding architectural values and professional accountability, protecting the public interest, and leveraging AI to enhance design excellence, sustainability, and innovation.' The statement frames AI as amplification rather than automation. Accompanied by a companion document, 'Guidance for the Responsible Use of AI by Architecture and Design Firms,' which provides practical recommendations covering professional responsibility, data governance, workforce development, and business resilience.

title: ANPD NT 1/2026 — Grok AI and Digital Violence
abstract: Nota técnica da ANPD sobre o sistema Grok da plataforma X (antigo Twitter) e possíveis violações à LGPD, afirmando que conteúdo sintético gerado por IA que referencie pessoas identificáveis constitui tratamento de dados pessoais. Emitida em resposta ao uso de Grok para gerar imagens sexualizadas não consensuais de mulheres, crianças e adolescentes. Acompanhada de Recomendação Conjunta ANPD/MPF/Senacon exigindo medidas imediatas da plataforma.

title: CBCP AI in Evangelization Seminar
abstract: The CBCP Episcopal Commission on Social Communications held a seminar on January 20, 2026 at Pope Pius XII Catholic Center, Manila. Archbishop Rex Andrew Alarcon (ECSC Chair) framed the discussion as addressing 'a change of epoch.' Executive Secretary Edwin Lopez emphasized 'Technology serves. Communion saves,' warning against treating AI as a substitute for pastoral presence. Core positions: AI tools must assist rather than replace human discernment; evangelization requires relational encounter, not automation; technological progress without moral reflection undermines human dignity. The Vatican document Antiqua et Nova was referenced as a guiding framework.

title: Thai Bishops AI Ethics Formation Seminar
abstract: The 2026 Annual Regional Seminar in Nakhon Phanom Province, northeastern Thailand, brought together nearly 200 priests, 5 bishops, and delegates from neighboring Laos. Fr. Joseph Anucha Chaiyadej (Secretary General, Office of Social Communication) stated: 'Artificial intelligence does not possess true intelligence, consciousness, or comprehension. AI is not the subject but the object.' He urged clergy: 'Don't let algorithms write your story; use technology to serve humanity.' The Vatican document Antiqua et Nova was presented as a 'moral compass' for clergy in digital ministry.

title: CFM Resolução 2454/2026 — AI in Medicine
abstract: Resolução do CFM que normatiza o uso de IA na medicina em todo o território nacional. Assegura ao médico o direito de usar IA como ferramenta de apoio, mantendo responsabilidade final pelas decisões clínicas. Proíbe delegação de diagnósticos, prognósticos ou decisões terapêuticas à IA. Classifica sistemas em quatro níveis de risco (baixo, médio, alto, inaceitável). Exige registro no prontuário e informação ao paciente. Publicada no DOU em 27/02/2026 e entra em vigor após 180 dias.

title: CNBB AI Pastoral Mission Course 2026
abstract: Free online extension course launched jointly by CNBB, SBCC (Brazilian Society of Catholic Scientists), and UFN (Franciscana University) on February 24, 2026. Six-module curriculum covering: the Church's mission and AI's societal impact; foundational AI concepts; synodality within digital culture; communication and evangelization strategies using AI; ethical principles for responsible AI deployment; and integration seminar with guidance for advanced modules. Sessions held Wednesdays 8-10 PM. Represents the Church's institutional commitment to dialogue between faith and science amid contemporary AI challenges.

title: Common Cause Letter on Pentagon AI Surveillance Demands
abstract: Coalition letter co-signed by Common Cause (VP of Policy Carol Evans), The Alliance for Secure AI, and Young Americans for Liberty, addressed to Congressional committee chairs and members. Urges Congress to investigate Secretary Hegseth's ultimatum pressuring Anthropic to remove AI safeguards against 'mass domestic surveillance' and 'fully autonomous weapons systems.' Requests witness testimony from Hegseth, document production from DoD and major AI companies, and a recurring Congressional reporting requirement on classified AI deployments, constitutional compliance, and usage policies.

title: Dar al-Ifta Fatwa Banning AI Quran Interpretation
abstract: Dar al-Ifta issued a fatwa prohibiting the use of AI applications including ChatGPT for interpreting the Holy Quran. Relying independently on AI-generated Quranic interpretations is impermissible because it risks subjecting the Quran to conjecture, inaccuracies, and misrepresentation. Muslims should refer to authoritative tafsir works, qualified scholars, and trusted religious institutions. Issued by Dr. Nazir Mohammad Ayyad.

title: Ecuador EFIA 2026
abstract: Ecuador's national AI strategy, presented March 10, 2026 by President Daniel Noboa's government, structured around three pillars: AI governance (ethical and transparent frameworks), capacity and technology (human talent and digital infrastructure), and AI adoption and development (research, innovation, and public-private applications). Developed with technical support from the IDB and UNESCO.

title: Egypt-SCIA-36th-Cairo-Document-AI-Professions
abstract: وثيقة القاهرة حول الإسلام وفلسفة العمران، صادرة عن المؤتمر الدولي السادس والثلاثين للمجلس الأعلى للشؤون الإسلامية (19-20 يناير 2026)، بمشاركة أكثر من 180 عالمًا ومفكرًا من أكثر من 50 دولة. تناول المحور الخامس مستقبل المهن في عصر الذكاء الاصطناعي. أوصى المؤتمر بتوجيه تقنيات الذكاء الاصطناعي لخدمة الإنسانية ضمن أطر أخلاقية وإنسانية، وأكد أن الإنسان يظل الهدف المحوري للعمل المهني وليس التكنولوجيا. تم اعتماد الوثيقة للتعميم على المنظمات الدولية بما فيها الأمم المتحدة واليونسكو.

title: Pro-Human AI Declaration
abstract: Broad cross-spectrum coalition declaration with 40+ organizational signatories asserting the importance of humans and human values as AI becomes more powerful. Five major demands: (1) keeping humans in charge, prohibiting superintelligence development until proven safe; (2) avoiding concentration of power, preventing AI monopolies; (3) protecting the human experience including family/community bonds and child safety; (4) preserving human agency and liberty, no AI personhood; (5) developer accountability with criminal penalties. Faith-based signatories include Congress of Christian Leaders, G20 Interfaith Forum Association, National Association of Evangelicals (Rev. Johnnie Moore, Walter Kim), Rabbi Geoff Mitelman (Sinai and Synapses), and Fr. Michael Baggot (Pontifical Athenaeum Regina Apostolorum).

title: FO Communiqué on AI Summit and Worker Rights
abstract: Communiqué de FO publié lors du Sommet pour l'action sur l'IA à Paris. Demande une législation nationale stricte et une directive européenne pour réglementer le déploiement de l'IA sur les lieux de travail, le soutien au dialogue social et à la négociation collective, la protection des travailleurs dans toute la chaîne de valeur de l'IA, un cadre de transition socialement juste, et l'opposition à la vague de déréglementation.

title: Armenia Signing of CoE Framework Convention on AI 2026
abstract: 27 января 2026 года Армения подписала Рамочную конвенцию Совета Европы об искусственном интеллекте, правах человека, демократии и верховенстве закона. Конвенция является первым международным юридически обязательным договором в области ИИ (принята 5 сентября 2024 г.) и направлена на обеспечение совместимости систем ИИ с правами человека, демократическими ценностями и верховенством закона.

title: GHPGVN HCMC AI Training for Monks
abstract: Khoa boi duong ve Tri tue nhan tao (AI) va Chuyen doi so do Ban Thong tin - Truyen thong GHPGVN TP.HCM to chuc tai Viet Nam Quoc Tu vao ngay 9-10 thang 1 nam 2026. Hon 700 tang ni va Phat tu tham du. Chuong trinh gom 2 chu de: 'Tri tue nhan tao tu goc nhin chanh niem: Hieu de phuc vu va hoang phap' va 'Chuyen doi so va doi moi sang tao (Phung su nhan sinh trong ky nguyen so)'. Cac phat thu co Hoa thuong Thich Nhat Hy (Pho Ban Tri su), Hoa thuong Thich Thien Quy (Pho Thu ky), va dai dien Ban Ton giao Chinh phu. 23 chung chi khen thuong va chung chi hoan thanh khoa hoc duoc trao.

title: Korean Pathway Preaching Conference on AI
abstract: The 'Pathway Preaching Conference' held February 26 at Good Shepherd Church in Seongnam (south of Seoul) brought together pastors, associate ministers, and seminary students under the theme 'In the Age of AI, How Can Preaching Survive?' Speakers emphasized that while AI may generate polished sermons, it cannot embody lived faith, suffering, or spiritual encounter. The conference reflected growing concern among Korean Protestant clergy about AI's impact on pastoral ministry.

title: Khamenei Istifta on AI-Generated Ghina
organization: Office of the Supreme Leader of Iran
abstract: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Supreme Leader of Iran, issued a religious edict (istifta) declaring it forbidden for men to listen to AI-generated songs featuring female voices if the content is considered 'ghina' — a term in Islamic jurisprudence referring to melodious or sensual singing. The fatwa stresses there is 'no difference' whether the voice originates from a real person or artificial intelligence. This extends traditional Shia Islamic rulings on music and female voice to AI-generated content, representing one of the first formal applications of traditional jurisprudential categories to AI-generated media.

title: CFA Institute Response to FCA Mills Review on AI in Retail Financial Services
abstract: CFA Institute's formal response to the FCA Mills Review on AI in retail financial services. Advocates functional regulation based on consumer outcomes, tiered governance proportionate to AI autonomy, clearer Consumer Duty guidance for AI services, and international alignment via IOSCO and FSB.

title: Data & Society: The Big AI State
abstract: Policy brief analyzing major priorities of the Trump administration's AI agenda, finding that the US government is engaging in extraordinary levels of market intervention in order to bolster the AI sector. Assesses implications for AI governance, oversight, and public interest.

title: Jogye Order Robot Monk Statement
abstract: South Korea's first AI robot monk debuted at Bongeunsa temple in Seoul's Gangnam District (Jan 27-28, 2026), greeting visitors, explaining Buddhist practices such as 108 prostrations, and answering questions in Korean and English. The robot's training data — Buddhist scriptures, sermons, temple information, religious etiquette — was vetted by Dongguk University religious experts. A Jogye Order spokesperson described it as 'an experiment in how traditional religion can communicate with the digital age,' stipulating that while robots can play supporting roles, Buddhist practice remains the domain of human beings and AI adoption must not undermine tradition or symbolic integrity.

title: Korean Religious Women AI Ethics Lecture
abstract: A lecture on 'Faith in the Face of Technology: AI Ethics' held February 24, 2026, at Jeongdong Franciscan Church Cathedral in Seoul drew approximately 250 participants, primarily women religious. Fr. James Bang Jong-woo (Catholic University of Korea professor) stated: 'Artificial intelligence mimics human communication, and for this reason, it must be used carefully and responsibly.' He framed AI as 'evidence of humanity's ability to participate responsibly in God's creative work.' Sister Jung Yun-jin emphasized the need for deeper spiritual awareness amid rapid digital development.

title: Morocco AI Roadmap 2030 (Maroc IA 2030)
abstract: Lancée le 12 janvier 2026 lors d'un symposium national à Rabat, cette feuille de route opérationnelle 'Maroc IA 2030' s'appuie sur les conclusions des Assises nationales de l'IA de juillet 2025. Elle s'articule autour de cinq axes : indépendance technologique, confiance publique dans les applications IA, développement de l'expertise nationale, innovation locale, et couverture territoriale équilibrée. Elle prévoit la création d'Instituts Jazari dans les 12 régions du Royaume et un laboratoire de recherche conjoint avec Mistral AI.

title: Morocco Maroc IA 2030
abstract: Launched January 12, 2026 in Rabat by Minister Ghita Mezzour. Branded 'AI Made in Morocco.' Translates outcomes of the National AI Conference (July 2025) into an operational roadmap. Five priorities: technological independence, building trust in AI, developing national skills, supporting local innovation, and balanced territorial coverage. Central initiative: launch of JAZARI ROOT, founding nucleus of national AI centres of excellence (Jazari Institutes). Targets: MAD 100B added value to GDP, 50,000 AI jobs, 200,000 AI-skilled graduates by 2030. Combined impact with Digital Morocco 2030: ~240,000 digital jobs and $10B GDP contribution.

title: NRC-AI-Media-Training-2026
abstract: Media engagement event in Lagos on February 17, 2026, organized by the Nigeria Religious Coalition on Artificial Intelligence (NRC-AI). The coalition raised alarm over AI-powered platforms spreading 'misleading claims about religion, particularly among young people.' CCN General Secretary Evans Onyemara warned the trend 'appears to be deliberate' and could 'create confusion and weaken long-held beliefs.' Kolade Fadahunsi emphasized AI use must 'reflect Nigeria's religious and cultural realities rather than undermine them.' Called for inclusive, value-driven framework to regulate AI development and deployment, and urged government to include faith-based organizations in AI policy formulation.

title: NCD Statement on AI Bias Against Disabled People (Senate, 2026)
abstract: Official NCD statement submitted for the congressional record by Acting Chairman Neil Romano, warning the Senate that AI systems risk developing and perpetuating biases against people with disabilities. Topics addressed: AI use in clinical trial screening and participant selection, AI in healthcare diagnostics and treatment decisions, the need to include disabled populations in AI training datasets, and how AI may learn implicit and explicit bias about disability from healthcare providers. Recommends regulations requiring AI healthcare systems to incorporate disability data, disability cultural competence training, and review of healthcare AI applications to prevent bias.

title: National PTA Position Statement on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: The National PTA's formal position statement on artificial intelligence, adopted by its Board of Directors and scheduled for review in 2031. The statement recognizes AI's growing integration into education and family life and urges PTA members at all levels to advocate for: meaningful oversight and transparency of AI technologies; rigorous data protection measures including data minimization and prohibition on retaining children's photos; AI literacy training for students, educators, and families; age-appropriate safety features and content moderation; independent bias audits and risk assessments; school-level AI use inventories; equitable access to vetted AI tools; and environmental sustainability for AI data center infrastructure. The statement also addresses dangers including deepfakes, CSAM, children's over-reliance on AI for emotional support, and algorithmic bias in school discipline and placement decisions.

title: NU SOUL.md AI Ethics Framework
abstract: Draft AI ethics framework grounded in NU's Aswaja an-Nahdliyah tradition (Al-Asy'ari and Al-Maturidi theological schools). Seven sections: theological foundation; four core characteristics (tawassuth/moderation, tawazun/balance, i'tidal/uprightness, tasamuh/tolerance); five ethical pillars (truthfulness, trustworthiness, justice, cooperation, consistency); ethical boundaries from Maqasid al-Shariah; authority relations (AI as tool, not authority); behavioral guidelines; knowledge methodology. Guided by NU maxim: 'Preserving good traditions while adopting better innovations.' Explicitly not a fatwa.

title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance Responsible AI
abstract: First internationally agreed, government-backed due diligence guidance for responsible AI, backed by all OECD member countries plus 17 partner governments and the EU, providing a whole-of-value-chain framework for multinational enterprises supplying or using AI systems.

title: Public Citizen Generative Influence: AI Lobbying Transparency Report
abstract: Public Citizen report analyzing five years of federal lobbying disclosure data to document corporate capture of AI policy. Finds over 3,500 lobbyists (26% of all federal lobbyists) reported lobbying on AI issues in 2025, a 265% increase from 2022. Documents that 82% of AI lobbyists represent corporate interests vs. 18% for non-corporate entities. Warns that Congress has failed to regulate AI meaningfully. Recommends rejecting blanket preemption measures, building sector-specific guardrails, investing in regulatory technical capacity, and protecting workers, children, and communities from AI harms.

title: RCP View on Digital and AI Report
abstract: RCP formal policy report (January 15, 2026) examining requirements for the NHS digital transformation ('analogue to digital shift'). Draws on June 2025 all-member snapshot survey (70% of UK physicians support widespread AI implementation; 68% say NHS lacks digital infrastructure). Makes ten recommendations including modernizing NHS digital infrastructure, establishing a centralized registry of approved AI tools, involving clinicians in solution design, developing regulatory safeguards, and integrating digital/AI competencies into medical education. Emphasizes AI should support rather than replace clinical judgment.

title: SIGNIS Indonesia AI and Faith Assembly
abstract: The 52nd SIGNIS Indonesia Assembly (Feb 24 - Mar 1, 2026, Bandung, West Java) themed 'Artificial Intelligence in the Light of Faith' brought together 32 members. Mgr. Agustinus, Chairman of the Social Communications Commission of the Indonesian Bishops' Conference, stated that 'AI must serve as a tool to convey the living word (dabar), not replace the human role' and called for communication that brings 'energy, meaning, and transformative power.' The assembly elected new leadership for 2026-2030 and emphasized that the Church need not fear AI as long as it remains anchored in Christ's identity and mission.

title: Lakewood Roshei Yeshiva: Emergency Asifa Declaration on AI Dangers
abstract: Emergency gathering of approximately 20+ senior Haredi rabbinical leaders convened January 5, 2026, at Ateres Chana/Bais Faiga Hall in Lakewood, NJ. Led by Rav Elya Ber Wachtfogel (Rosh Yeshiva, South Fallsburg) with attendees including Rav Malkiel Kotler, Rav Dovid Schustal, Rav Yeruchem Olshin, Rav Yisroel Neuman, Rav Yitzchok Sorotzkin, and others representing Hasidic, Lithuanian, and Sephardic communities. Concluded with a formal declaration establishing: (1) a designated yom tefillah and communal fast day with Krias HaTorah of Vayechal; (2) directive that roshei yeshivos, roshei kehillos, chadarim, and Bais Yaakov schools address AI dangers; (3) call to eliminate use of calling or texting open AI; (4) practical steps including introduction of phones/lines that block AI access. A second follow-up asifa was also held.

title: TUC 10-Union Joint Statement on Educator Voice in AI
abstract: Ten UK education unions — AEP, EIS, GMB, NAHT, NASUWT, NEU, UCAC, UCU, UNISON, and Unite — issued a joint declaration in January 2026 through the TUC calling for educator voice in AI and EdTech adoption. The statement declares that 'technology is not a panacea, it is a tool' and demands educator involvement at three levels: policy formation (consultation and legislative input), technology design (early involvement to ensure sound pedagogy), and local implementation (collective bargaining and procurement decisions). The unions emphasize that AI must be shaped by educators' expertise and professional judgment rather than imposed top-down. The statement represents millions of educators across the UK education system.

title: United Sikhs UK Appeal to Akal Takht on AI Gurbani
abstract: United Sikhs UK, through representatives Harmeet Shah Singh and AI researcher Dr. Sarabjot Singh, submitted a formal appeal to Sri Akal Takht Sahib (the supreme Sikh temporal authority) and met with the Akal Takht Jathedar and SGPC President. The appeal documented that AI platforms branded as 'SikhGPT' are generating fabricated Gurbani verses, altering shabads (hymns), and presenting synthetic language as authentic scripture from Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji. United Sikhs UK submitted evidence including screenshots of fabricated verses and textual errors, and formally requested: immediate investigation into platforms using Sikh GPT branding, disclosure of app creators' identities, show-cause notices, suspension of problematic apps/websites pending inquiry, and an official advisory warning the community against unverified AI tools. This appeal directly prompted the SGPC to form its 8-member AI sub-committee.
